## 1. HfO₂/ZrO₂ Ferroelectric Superlattice - Latest Research (2025-2026)

### Breakthrough: Self-Rectifying Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction
- **Source:** [Materials Horizons RSC](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2024/mh/d4mh00519h)
- **Structure:** HfO₂/ZrO₂/HfO₂ superlattice (HZH SL) + Al₂O₃ + TiO₂
- **Key Achievement:** 6nm HZH SL suppresses non-ferroelectric phases, increases remnant polarization (Pᵣ)
- **Performance:** Integrated array >7k devices, 10% read margin, image recognition >92%

### Sub-Nanosecond FeFET Switching
- **Source:** [Nano Letters (ACS)](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/acs.nanolett.2c04706)
- **Achievement:** Sub-nanosecond switching in Si:HfO₂ FeFETs
- **Significance:** Enables high-speed neuromorphic computing

### Nanosecond Pulsed Laser Annealing
- **Source:** [ACS Applied Materials](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.4c10002)
- **Method:** Nanosecond pulsed laser annealing (NLA) for local crystallization
- **Result:** Ultrathin (3.6nm) HZO films crystallized into ferroelectric orthorhombic phase

### Roadmap Paper (2023)
- **Source:** [APL Materials](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/apm/article/11/8/089201/2908480/Roadmap-on-ferroelectric-hafnia-and-zirconia-based)
- **Content:** Comprehensive roadmap on ferroelectric hafnia/zirconia materials and devices

## 5. Challenges Identified

### Material Challenges (from Roadmap Paper)
1. **Endurance** - Limited cycling before degradation
2. **Retention** - Long-term data stability
3. **Wake-up Effect** - Initial cycling required for full polarization
4. **High Switching Voltages** - Energy consumption concern

### Superlattice Solutions
- Interface engineering controls defects
- Layer thickness ratios affect ferroelectric properties
- Single-layer thickness relates to interfacial non-ferroelectric layers

## 6. Preisach Model Implementation

### Foundational Paper: Bartic et al. (2001)
- **Source:** [ResearchGate PDF](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/30047270_Preisach_model_for_the_simulation_of_ferroelectric_capacitors)
- **Also:** [Jülich Research Center](https://juser.fz-juelich.de/record/36264/files/4398.pdf)

### Key Concepts
- Ferroelectric adaptation of Preisach theory (originally for ferromagnetics)
- Distribution of coercive voltages with physical meaning for ferroelectrics
- Experimental data fitted by analytic function
- 2D distribution calculated using linear approximation
- Validated with Spectre circuit simulator

### Implementation: PFECAP Verilog-A Model
- **Source:** [GitHub - DavidTobar456/pfecapRevision](https://github.com/DavidTobar456/pfecapRevision)
- **Method:** Hyperbolic tangent for hysteresis loop (Bo Jiang et al., 1997)
- **Features:**
  - Computationally efficient non-linear switching
  - Minor hysteresis loop support
  - LIFO structure for dominant extreme values

### Simulation Features
- Closed subcycle simulation (locus curves)
- Memory wipe-out (turning point erasure)
- Rate-dependent and thermal-drift behaviors

---

## 7. Landau-Khalatnikov Equation - Numerical Methods

### Key Papers
1. **Theoretical Analysis** - [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1007570420303543)
2. **2D Domain Patterns** - [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0096300323006409)
3. **Time-Fractional Model** - [ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/365516629_Time-fractional_Landau-Khalatnikov_model_applied_to_numerical_simulation_of_polarization_switching_in_ferroelectrics)
4. **Equivalent Circuit** - [IEEE Xplore](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/1226538/)
5. **Circuit Model** - [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/cond-mat/0108189)

### Numerical Methods
| Method | Description | Application |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Implicit Finite Difference** | Combined with iterative procedure | Initial-boundary value problems |
| **Finite Element Method** | For domain pattern formation | 2D/3D domain visualization |
| **Time-Fractional Scheme** | Caputo derivative approximation | Memory effect modeling |

### Key Results
- Absolutely stable monotonic scheme (2nd order accuracy)
- Hysteresis loops for BaTiO₃ match experimental data
- Domain pattern formation under varying boundary conditions
- FRAM device-array co-analysis

## 8. Vulkan Compute Shaders for Physics

### Tutorial Resources
| Resource | URL | Notes |
|----------|-----|-------|
| **Vulkan Tutorial** | [vulkan-tutorial.com/Compute_Shader](https://vulkan-tutorial.com/Compute_Shader) | Particle systems, SSBOs |
| **Vulkan Docs** | [docs.vulkan.org](https://docs.vulkan.org/tutorial/latest/11_Compute_Shader.html) | Official documentation |
| **Vulkan Guide** | [vkguide.dev](https://vkguide.dev/docs/gpudriven/compute_shaders/) | GPU-driven rendering |
| **Sascha Willems** | [GitHub](https://github.com/SaschaWillems/Vulkan) | C++ examples |

### Key Concepts
- GLSL → SPIR-V compilation via `glslc`
- `std140` memory layout for buffer mapping
- Work groups: Dispatch groups of thread blocks (1D, 2D, or 3D)
- Memory barriers for synchronization
- Shader Storage Buffer Objects (SSBOs) for data

### Physics Simulation Pattern
```glsl
// Particle/Cell struct
struct Cell {
    vec3 polarization;
    float potential;
};

layout(std140, binding = 0) buffer CellBuffer {
    Cell cells[];
};

// Dispatch: ceil(numCells / workGroupSize)
```

### Recommended: vgpu (Cogent Core)
- **Used by:** Emergent neural network simulation framework
- **Features:**
  - Each Pipeline = 1 compute shader (like CUDA kernel)
  - System Memory organization for shader data structures
  - High-level Vulkan abstraction

### Code Generator

- Generates Go bindings from Vulkan XML specification

## 10. FerroX: GPU Phase-Field Framework

### Overview
- **Source:** [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0010465523001029)
- **arXiv:** [arxiv.org/abs/2210.15668](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.15668)
- **Type:** Massively parallel, 3D phase-field simulation

### Equations Solved
1. **TDGL:** Time-Dependent Ginzburg-Landau for polarization
2. **Poisson:** Electric potential distribution
3. **Charge:** Carrier densities in semiconductors

### Implementation
- **Framework:** AMReX (scalable manycore/GPU)
- **Acceleration:** MPI + CUDA
- **Performance:** 15× speedup over CPU (node-by-node)
- **Scaling:** Near-perfect to 512 GPUs

### Applications
- Domain-wall negative capacitance (NC)
- MFIM devices
- MFISM devices

### Relevance to Ferroelectric CIM
FerroX provides a reference implementation for:
- TDGL solver architecture
- GPU parallelization strategies
- Domain visualization techniques

# ITERATION 2

## 13. ADC/DAC Design for CIM Peripherals

### Key Challenge
ADC/DAC converters contribute ~50% of total CIM macro energy and are susceptible to non-linearities, leakage, and process variations.

### Design Approaches

| Approach | Description | Energy Savings |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Quantized Interval ADC** | Weight-flip-store (WFS) coding | Optimized quantization |
| **P2O-DAC** | Partial power-off using input sparsity | ~50% DAC power |
| **ADC-less (Light-CIM)** | Fully analog tiles, no ADC/DAC | ADC only 2.5% of power |
| **FP-ADC** | Floating-point adaptive range | FP8 support |

### Energy Efficiency Benchmarks

| Design | Node | TOPS/W |
|--------|------|--------|
| Near-CIM analog | 55nm | 44.3 |
| P-8T SRAM CIM | 28nm | 50.07 |
| AFPR-CIM | RRAM | 19.89 TFLOPS/W |

### Key Insight
- Low-throughput accelerators: Fewer ADCs → reduce area
- High-throughput accelerators: More ADCs → reduce energy
- ADC resolution trades off with computations per convert

### Sources
- [SRAM CIM Review](https://arxiv.org/html/2411.06079v2)
- [ADC/DAC Modeling](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.06553)
- [Light-CIM](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025ITCAD..44..602Z/abstract)

## 14. Noise-Aware Training (IBM AIHWKit)

### IBM Analog Hardware Acceleration Kit
- **GitHub:** [github.com/IBM/aihwkit](https://github.com/IBM/aihwkit)
- **Docs:** [aihwkit.readthedocs.io](https://aihwkit.readthedocs.io/)
- **Language:** Python/PyTorch with CUDA C++ backend

### Features
- Device-to-device systematic variations
- Cycle-to-cycle noise
- ADC/DAC discretization
- Stochastic update pulse trains
- PCM-like noise models

### Hardware-Aware Training Pattern

```python
from aihwkit.nn import AnalogLinear
from aihwkit.simulator.configs import InferenceRPUConfig
from aihwkit.inference import PCMLikeNoiseModel

# Configure realistic noise
rpu_config = InferenceRPUConfig()
rpu_config.noise_model = PCMLikeNoiseModel()

# Create analog layer
layer = AnalogLinear(784, 256, rpu_config=rpu_config)
```

### Noise Injection Approaches

| Method | Result | Source |
|--------|--------|--------|
| **Variance-Aware** | 79.3% → 97.6% (CIFAR-10) | [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.16183) |
| **Bayes-Optimized** | 10-100× improvement | [Nature Comms Eng](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44172-023-00074-3) |
| **Hardware-Aware Retraining** | Iso-accuracy with FP | [Nature Comms](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-40770-4) |

## 17. STDP Implementation in FeFET

### Spike-Timing Dependent Plasticity
STDP is a biological learning rule where synaptic weight changes based on relative timing of pre/post-synaptic spikes.

### FeFET STDP Mechanisms
- Channel conductance modulated by ferroelectric polarization
- Pulse gate voltage enables multi-valued conductance
- Three-terminal structure allows concurrent learning
- Symmetric and asymmetric STDP achievable

### Performance Benchmarks (2024)

| Device | Endurance | Energy | Gmax/Gmin | Accuracy |
|--------|-----------|--------|-----------|----------|
| 2D SnS₂ FeFET | 10⁷ cycles | 48 aJ/spike | >10⁵ | 94% MNIST |
| FE-SBFET | 10⁵ pulses | ~2 fJ/spike | - | - |
| HZO FeFET | - | 1 µs response | - | - |

### Key Advantages
- 10,000× faster than biological synapses (~1 µs vs ~10 ms)
- Sub-femtojoule energy per spike
- Non-volatile weight storage

### Sources
- [2D SnS₂ FeFET - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11040367/)
- [FE-SBFET - ACS](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.1c07505)

## 18. FerroX TDGL Solver Details

### Governing Equation
```
∂P/∂t = -Γ δF/δP
```
Where:
- P = polarization vector
- Γ = kinetic coefficient
- F = total free energy (Landau + gradient + electrostatic)

### Numerical Methods

| Component | Method | Accuracy |
|-----------|--------|----------|
| Spatial | Finite Difference | 2nd order (1st at interfaces) |
| Temporal | Predictor-Corrector | 1st or 2nd order |
| Poisson | MLMG Multigrid | High accuracy |

### Boundary Conditions

| Type | Equation | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| Extrapolation | λ dP/dz - P = 0 | Surface effects |
| Free | dP/dz = 0 | Unconstrained |
| Zero | P = 0 | Fixed boundary |

### GPU Parallelization (AMReX)
- **Strategy:** MPI + CUDA hybrid
- **Data Movement:** Minimal (ghost cells only)
- **Scaling:** Near-perfect to 512 GPUs
- **Speedup:** 15× vs CPU (node-by-node)

### Key Insight for Implementation
FerroX uses fixed-point iteration for MFISM devices where charge depends on potential, converging to 10⁻⁵ tolerance.

### Sources
- [FerroX arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2210.15668)
- [GitHub](https://github.com/AMReX-Microelectronics/FerroX)

## 20. Multi-Level Analog States in FeFET

### Achievable States

| Device | States | Bits | Method |
|--------|--------|------|--------|
| HZO FeFET | 32 | 5 | 50mV pulse steps |
| Ferroelectric FET | 200+ | ~7.6 | Space charge + oxidation |
| 2D FeFET | >128 | >7 | HZO + 2D semiconductor |

### Ferroelectric CIM 30-State Claim
Dr. Tour's presentation mentions 30 discrete states, consistent with literature showing 32-state (5-bit) operation via incremental pulse schemes.

### Key Requirements for Multi-Level
1. **Linearity:** Equal ΔG per pulse
2. **Symmetry:** Potentiation = Depression
3. **Retention:** Long-term stability
4. **Endurance:** >10⁷ cycles

### Physics of Partial Polarization
Multi-level achieved by stopping domain wall propagation at intermediate points, creating mixed "up/down" domain states.

### Sources
- [Multi-timescale synaptic weight](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43246-023-00342-x)
- [FeFET analog states](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9720631/)

## 17. Crossbar Array Sneak Path Mitigation (Iteration 4)

### The Sneak Path Problem
In passive crossbar arrays, unselected cells create parallel current paths that corrupt read operations. This is a critical challenge for scaling beyond small arrays.

### Mitigation Techniques

#### 1. Ferroelectric Capacitive Memory (FCM) Arrays
FCMs inherently avoid sneak paths because:
- Device resistance >> interconnect resistance (no IR drop)
- Capacitive readout uses charge sensing, not current
- No selector transistors needed

**Key Reference:** [Springer - Ferroelectric capacitive memories](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40580-024-00463-0)

#### 2. Metal-Insulator-Ferroelectric-Semiconductor (MIFS)
- Scalable to 145×145 arrays without selectors
- TER ratio ~911%
- Built-in tunneling barrier suppresses leakage

**Key Reference:** [ACS - Selector-less FTJs](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.1c14952)

#### 3. Self-Rectifying Bilayer Devices
- HfOx bilayer reduces sneak current by ~20%
- Read margin improved from 0.1V to 2.5V
- Nonlinear I-V characteristic naturally suppresses parallel paths

**Key Reference:** [PMC - Sneak Path Current Solutions](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7823919/)

#### 4. Biasing Schemes
- Apply intermediate voltages (V/2 or V/3) to unselected cells
- Effective at large read/write voltages
- Prevents disturbance to stored data

### Optimal Array Sizing
- Unit crossbar: 128×128 to 256×256
- Larger arrays partitioned into tiles
- Line resistance dominates at larger scales

---

## 18. ADC Quantization & Noise-Aware Training (Iteration 4)

### The ADC Bottleneck
ADCs dominate power consumption in analog CIM accelerators. High-precision ADCs (8-12 bits) are energy-expensive.

### Key Techniques

#### 1. Partial-Sum Quantization (PSQ) Training
Train networks aware of quantization at the partial-sum level:
- **Result:** ADC resolution reducible to 2-3 bits
- **Precision:** Binary or ternary partial sums demonstrated
- **Energy:** 40-64× ADC energy savings

**Key Reference:** [ACM - Extreme Partial-Sum Quantization](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3528104)

#### 2. Hardware-Aware Training (HWA)
IBM AIHWKit approach:
```python
# Simulate 8-bit ADC discretization
rpu_config.forward.out_res = 1/256

# Add weight noise during training
rpu_config.forward.w_noise = 0.02
```
Re-training with hardware awareness greatly improves analog inference accuracy.

**Key Reference:** [IBM AIHWKit Documentation](https://aihwkit.readthedocs.io/en/stable/hwa_training.html)

#### 3. Dynamic Quantization Range Control
- Adaptive quantization ranges per MVM operation
- Exploits that different layers have different dynamic ranges
- Compatible with standard quantization-aware training

**Key Reference:** [ACM - Dynamic Quantization Range Control](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3498328)

#### 4. ADC-Less Hybrid Approaches (HCiM)
- Combine analog MAC with digital accumulation
- Eliminate ADC entirely for intermediate values
- Final conversion at network output only

**Key Reference:** [HCiM: ADC-Less Hybrid Accelerator](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.13577v1)

### Compute-Aware SNR (CSNR)
- 3-bit ADC precision reduction with 6 dB CSNR gain
- Algorithm-hardware co-design essential
- Quantization-aware training vital for device variability

## 20. P-E Curve Plotting Implementation (Iteration 4)

### Design for Go + Vulkan Demo

The P-E (Polarization vs Electric Field) hysteresis curve is the canonical visualization for ferroelectric behavior.

#### Data Structure (implemented in render.go)
```go
type HysteresisPlot struct {
    Points      []Point2D  // Historical trajectory
    EMin, EMax  float64    // Electric field axis limits
    PMin, PMax  float64    // Polarization axis limits
    CurrentE    float64    // Current operating point
    CurrentP    float64    // Current polarization
    LineColor   Color      // Curve color (blue)
    MarkerColor Color      // Current point marker (red)
}
```

#### Key Features
1. **Circular buffer** - Keep last 10,000 points for performance
2. **Normalized coordinates** - Convert E,P to screen space [0,1]
3. **Divergent colormap** - Red(+P) → White(0) → Blue(-P)
4. **Real-time updates** - Add points during simulation

#### Visualization Elements
- **Axes:** E (horizontal), P (vertical) with gridlines
- **Curve:** Line strip connecting historical points
- **Marker:** Current operating point highlighted
- **Cell display:** Adjacent ferroelectric cell color-coded by P

### Plot Utilities Added
See `demo1-hysteresis/pkg/render/render.go` and new `plot.go` file.

## 21. Neural Network Weight Mapping Algorithms (Iteration 4)

### The Mapping Challenge
Large neural networks exceed single crossbar capacity (typically 128×128 to 256×256). Mapping requires partitioning layers across multiple arrays while minimizing data movement.

### Key Algorithms

#### 1. COMPASS Compiler Framework (2025)
State-of-the-art compiler for resource-constrained crossbar-based PIM accelerators:
- Handles networks exceeding on-chip capacity
- Optimal partitioning algorithm considering:
  - Data dependence between layers
  - Core utilization
  - Write instructions to minimize latency
- **Result:** 1.78× throughput improvement, 1.28× energy-delay savings

**Key Reference:** [COMPASS Framework](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.06780v1)

#### 2. Heuristic Weight Remapping (2024)
For irregular sparse neural networks:
- Greedy remapping scheme
- 70-94% utilization of sparsity
- 1.3× improvement over naive compression

**Key Reference:** [Semantic Scholar - Weight Remapping](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/a4465e8d6b08330bc378af0531fa7f975233082a)

#### 3. Simple Packing Algorithm (November 2024)
- Determines optimal number of physical tiles
- Fixed optimal array dimensions
- Compared to binary linear optimization (bin-packing)
- Optimum depends on tile capacity vs peripheral circuit scaling

**Key Reference:** [ArXiv - Simple Packing Algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.04814)

#### 4. Hierarchical Partitioning
For large-scale networks with sparse connectivity:
- Compute-balanced mapping
- Memory-efficient parallel processing
- Low-latency execution
- Dense synaptic storage with minimal routing

**Key Reference:** [PMC - Hierarchical Partitioning](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8842996/)

### Mapping Strategies

#### Layer-by-Layer Mapping
- Each layer maps to one or more crossbars
- Clear data flow between tiles
- Simple but may underutilize resources

#### Folded Mapping
- Large weight matrices folded across multiple arrays
- Accumulate partial results
- Better utilization for large layers

#### Variation-Tolerant Mapping
- Account for device-to-device variation
- Two-phase mapping: initial placement + fine-tuning
- Reduce stuck-at-fault (SAF) impact

**Key Reference:** [ACM - Variation Tolerant Mapping](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3585518)

### Off-Chip vs On-Chip Training
- **Off-chip training:** Weights trained in software, mapped to crossbar for inference
- Avoids frequent conductance updates
- Mapping errors inevitable but manageable
- More suitable for stable, long-term inference

## 23. FerroX Integration Possibilities (Iteration 4)

### What is FerroX?
FerroX is a massively parallel, 3D phase-field simulation framework for modeling ferroelectric materials. Developed at Lawrence Berkeley National Lab as part of the MicroEleX suite.

**GitHub:** [AMReX-Microelectronics/FerroX](https://github.com/AMReX-Microelectronics/FerroX)

### Physics Equations Solved
1. **TDGL (Time-Dependent Ginzburg-Landau):** Ferroelectric polarization dynamics
2. **Poisson's Equation:** Electric potential distribution
3. **Semiconductor Charge Equation:** Carrier densities in semiconductor regions

### Key Capabilities
- 3D simulation of MFIM and MFISM devices
- Negative capacitance (NC) effect modeling
- Domain wall dynamics
- Polycrystalline multi-phase hafnia/zirconia films
- Second-order accurate in space and time

### Performance
- **15× GPU speedup** (node-by-node comparison)
- Excellent scaling on NERSC systems
- Supports CUDA, HIP, SYCL backends

### Build Options
```
Backend: NOACC | OMP (default) | CUDA | SYCL | HIP
Precision: SINGLE | DOUBLE
Features: Embedded boundaries, SUNDIALS ODE, MPI
```

### Integration with Ferroelectric CIM-Vis
Possible integration approaches:

1. **Data Pipeline:** Export FerroX simulation results → Import into Go visualizer
2. **FFI Binding:** Call FerroX C++ functions from Go via cgo
3. **REST API:** Wrap FerroX in a microservice, call from Go
4. **Shared Memory:** Use memory-mapped files for real-time data exchange

**Recommended:** Data pipeline approach for initial integration
- Export polarization fields as HDF5 or binary files
- Import into Go for Vulkan visualization
- Keep simulation and visualization decoupled

### Key References
- [FerroX Paper - Computer Physics Communications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0010465523001029)
- [arXiv Preprint](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.15668)
- [MicroEleX Documentation](https://amrex-microelectronics.github.io/)

## 24. Pulse Programming for Multi-Level States (Iteration 4)

### Multi-Level Cell (MLC) Operation
FeFETs can store multiple bits per cell using intermediate polarization states (partially polarized domains).

### Key Programming Techniques

#### 1. Incremental Step Pulse Programming (ISPP)
Standard approach:
- Apply initial V_Prog
- Measure I at V_Read
- If I ≠ I_Target, increment V_Prog by 0.05V
- Repeat until target reached

**Result:** Precise VT state adjustment for reliable multi-bit storage

#### 2. Logarithmic ISPP (L-ISPP)
Improved version from 2024 research:
- Logarithmic voltage increments
- Reduces programming energy consumption
- Improves learning accuracy for neuromorphic computing
- Trade-off: dynamic range vs energy consumption

**Key Reference:** [ScienceDirect - L-ISPP Pulse Programming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1567173924001755)

#### 3. Program/Verify Algorithm
```
1. Apply V_Prog_initial
2. Read current at V_Read
3. If I_Measured != I_Target:
   V_Prog += 0.05V
   Goto step 1
4. Store final V_Prog as calibration
```

### Demonstrated Performance
| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Multi-level states | 4-bit (16 levels) | 2024 Nanoscale |
| Retention time | >2000s @ 4-bit | Double-gate FeFET |
| Endurance | 10⁷ cycles | 2D FeFET SnS2 |
| Gmax/Gmin ratio | >10⁵ | HZO-based devices |
| Analog states | >7-bit (>128 levels) | Advanced linearity |

### Device Requirements for Neuromorphic
1. **Linearity:** Equal ΔG per pulse
2. **Symmetry:** Potentiation ≈ Depression
3. **Dynamic Range:** High Gmax/Gmin
4. **Low Variability:** Consistent across array
5. **Endurance:** >10⁶ cycles minimum

### Dual-Bit FeFET (2025)
Recent Nature paper demonstrates enhanced storage and endurance with dual-bit architecture.

**Key Reference:** [Nature - Dual-Bit FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00030-8)

# Iteration 5

## 25. Landau-Devonshire Free Energy Model for HfO₂/ZrO₂ (Iteration 5)

### Theoretical Framework
The Landau-Ginzburg-Devonshire (LGD) free energy functional describes ferroelectric phase transitions:

```
F = ∫[α₀(T-Tc)P² + βP⁴ + γP⁶ - E·P + κ(∇P)²] dV
```

Where:
- `P` = polarization order parameter
- `α₀` = Landau coefficient (temperature-dependent)
- `β, γ` = higher-order coefficients
- `κ` = gradient energy coefficient (domain wall energy)
- `E` = applied electric field

### Universal Curie Constant for HfO₂
Experimentally determined from multiple dopant types:
- **Curie constant:** C = 5.8 × 10⁻⁷ ± 0.46 × 10⁻⁷ K·C·V⁻¹·m⁻¹
- **Landau α₀:** 1.72 × 10⁶ ± 0.138 × 10⁶ V·m·K⁻¹·C⁻¹
- Independent of dopant concentration and type

**Key Reference:** [Universal Curie constant - Nano Energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2211285520302901)

### Size Effects in HZO Films
Unique "double size effect" in HfO₂/ZrO₂:
1. **Thickness dependence:** Effective coefficients aₚ(x,h), aₐ(x,h) vary with film thickness h
2. **Grain size coupling:** Average grain size R depends on thickness
3. **Critical thickness:** ~5 nm for ferroelectric HZO, ~4 nm for undoped HfO₂

### Depolarization Field Effects
In ultrathin (<5 nm) Hf₀.₅Zr₀.₅O₂:
- Depolarization fields drive system to stable paraelectric phase
- Metastable ferroelectric phase coexists
- Explains "pinched" hysteresis behavior

**Key Reference:** [Depolarization in HZO - Nature Comms Physics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42005-022-00951-x)

### Recent Comprehensive Theory (January 2025)
arXiv:2601.06267 provides:
- Higher-power LGD functional
- Trilinear and biquadratic couplings
- Polar, nonpolar, and antipolar order parameters
- Strain-dependent critical sizes

**Key Reference:** [arXiv:2601.06267 - Landau-type Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.06267)

---

## 26. Domain Wall Dynamics in HfO₂/ZrO₂ (Iteration 5)

### Domain Wall Types
Two distinct 180° domain wall types in orthorhombic HfO₂:

| Type | Structure | Migration Barrier |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Soft DW** | Continuous polar layers | Low (~0.38 eV) |
| **Hard DW** | Alternating nonpolar/polar | High (~0.80 eV) |

Soft DW barrier is **10× lower** than hard DW and nucleation barrier.

### Switching Mechanisms

#### Nucleation-Limited Switching (NLS)
- Dominant in polycrystalline HZO films
- Rate-limited by domain nucleation, not propagation
- Enables extraction of minimum switching time

#### Nucleation-and-Growth
Previously thought impossible due to high DW energy barriers. New research reveals:
- **Topological DWs:** Up to 35× lower energy barriers
- **Shift-inside (SI):** 0.38 eV barrier
- **Shift-across (SA):** 0.80 eV barrier

**Key Reference:** [Low barrier topological DWs - ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S136970212100256X)

### Anisotropic Switching
3D anisotropic ferroelectric switching along principal axes:
- **b-direction:** Non-polar layer suppresses inter-layer interaction
- **a-direction:** Lower growth barrier than nucleation
- **Coercive field:** Theory predicts ~1 MV/cm (matches experiment)

Previous homogeneous models predicted ~10 MV/cm (10× too high).

**Key Reference:** [3D Anisotropic Switching - Adv. Funct. Mater.](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adfm.202500390)

### Domain Wall Motion Dynamics
Phase-field simulations reveal:
- 180° polarization switching is nucleation-dominated
- Hard DWs constrain switching
- DW migration energy determines switching speed

**Key Reference:** [Phase-field DW dynamics - ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1359645425008134)

### Direct Atomic-Scale Visualization
90° domain walls and migrations observed in Hf₀.₅Zr₀.₅O₂ thin films via atomic-scale imaging.

**Key Reference:** [Atomic-scale DW visualization - ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2588842023001050)

### Key PNAS Paper (2025)
"Nucleation mechanism of multiple-order parameter ferroelectric domain wall motion in hafnia" provides comprehensive understanding of DW nucleation.

**Key Reference:** [PNAS - DW nucleation mechanism](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2406316122)

## 29. Go Vulkan Bindings Research (Iteration 5)

### vgpu / Cogentcore Framework

**Repository:** [cogentcore/core/gpu](https://github.com/cogentcore/core/tree/main/gpu)

Key features:
- High-level Vulkan interface in Go
- Uses github.com/goki/vulkan bindings
- Pipeline-based compute shader management
- System manages multiple pipelines and memory

### gosl - Go as Shader Language

**Repository:** [cogentcore/lab/gosl](https://github.com/cogentcore/lab/tree/main/gosl)

Enables writing GPU shaders in Go:
- Converts Go code to HLSL
- Uses DirectX shader compiler (dxc)
- Outputs SPIR-V for Vulkan

```go
//gosl start
func myKernel(idx int, data []float32) {
    data[idx] = data[idx] * 2.0
}
//gosl end
```

### Current Status
Cogentcore has switched to WebGPU (wgpu) instead of Vulkan, so vgpu is no longer actively maintained. However, it remains functional for compute workloads.

### Alternative: Direct Vulkan Bindings
- **go-vk:** https://github.com/bbredesen/go-vk (Vulkan 1.1-1.3)
- **vulkan-go:** https://github.com/vulkan-go/vulkan

### Mac Installation
Requires Vulkan SDK from https://vulkan.lunarg.com/sdk/home (homebrew molten-vk is insufficient).

### Key References
- [vgpu Documentation](https://pkg.go.dev/cogentcore.org/core/vgpu)
- [gosl Package](https://pkg.go.dev/cogentcore.org/core/vgpu/gosl)
- [Vulkan Compute Tutorial](https://vulkan-tutorial.com/Compute_Shader)

## 30. Compute Shader Design for CIM (Iteration 5)

### MVM Shader (mvm.comp)
Matrix-vector multiplication kernel:
```glsl
layout(local_size_x = 256) in;

// One thread per output row
// Each thread computes dot product: y[i] = sum(W[i,j] * x[j])
void main() {
    uint rowIdx = gl_GlobalInvocationID.x;
    float sum = 0.0;
    for (int j = 0; j < cols; j++) {
        sum += weights[rowIdx * cols + j] * inputs[j];
    }
    outputs[rowIdx] = sum;
}
```

### Non-Idealities Modeled
1. **DAC quantization:** Input voltage discretization
2. **ADC quantization:** Output current discretization
3. **Device noise:** Per-cell noise factors
4. **Temporal noise:** Time-varying read noise

### Performance Considerations
- Workgroup size: 256 threads
- Coalesced memory access for weights
- Shared memory for reduction (softmax)

## 31. Retention and Endurance Characteristics (Iteration 5)

### State-of-the-Art Endurance

| Device Type | Endurance | Key Innovation | Reference |
|-------------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| HZO Superlattice MFM | **>10¹² cycles** | Superlattice structure | Peng et al. |
| Zr-doped HfO₂ FeFET | 2×10⁷ cycles | Ultra-thin ITO channel | Nature Comms 2024 |
| α-IGZO FeFET + HfO₂ IL | >10⁷ cycles | 2nm HfO₂ interfacial layer | Sci. China 2025 |
| HZO + ZrO₂ seed | Standard | Enhanced ferroelectric properties | PMC 2019 |

### Key Challenge: Interfacial Layer (IL) Stress
- MFIS gate stack IL experiences stress during polarization switching
- Leads to fatigue during cycling operation
- Short retention from depolarization field

### Solutions: Interfacial Layer Engineering
Replace SiO₂ IL with high-k materials:
- **Al₂O₃:** Improved electric field coupling
- **HfO₂:** Better interface characteristics
- **La₂O₃:** Reduced electron trapping
- **ZrO₂ seed layer:** Enhanced remanent polarization

### Retention Performance
- **ZrO₂ seed FeFET:** 10-year extrapolated retention of 0.9V (vs 0.8V without seed)
- **α-IGZO FeFET:** >10 years retention with 0.85V MW after 10⁷ cycles

### Key References
- [Advanced Electronic Materials 2025](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aelm.202400686)
- [Nature Communications 2024 - ITO FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46878-5)
- [Science China 2025 - HfO₂ IL](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11432-024-4429-7)
- [HZO Superlattice Endurance](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/357111874)

## 32. Flash Joule Heating Synthesis (Iteration 5)

### Overview
Flash Joule Heating (research sample) is an ultrafast, scalable synthesis method invented by external research group's group at external research institution (2019). Uses a jolt of electricity to instantaneously transform materials.

### Key 2024 Developments

#### Electric Field Effects (JACS, June 2024)
- Deconvoluted thermal vs electrical contributions to graphene synthesis
- Electric field in precursor catalyzes graphene formation
- Three-step phase transition control:
  1. Amorphous carbon → Turbostratic graphene
  2. Turbostratic graphene → Ordered graphene
  3. Ordered graphene → Graphite

**Reference:** [JACS - Electric Field Effects](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/jacs.4c02864)

#### Kilogram-Scale Synthesis (ACS Nano, 2024)
- "Kilogram Flash Joule Heating Synthesis with an Arc Welder"
- Eddy, Tour et al.
- Industrial-scale graphene production

#### Flash-Within-Flash (Nature Chemistry, August 2024)
New FWF method:
- Gram-scale production in seconds
- >50% reduction in energy, water, and GHG emissions
- Enables synthesis of diverse solid-state compounds

**Reference:** [ScienceDaily - FWF Method](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2024/08/240816173928.htm)

### Applications
1. **Waste conversion:** Plastic bottles, food waste, tires → Graphene
2. **2D materials:** Boron nitride (2D BN), boron carbon nitride
3. **Turbostratic graphene:** Misaligned layers for easy dispersion

### Relevance to Ferroelectric CIM
research sample could enable scalable synthesis of:
- Ferroelectric thin films
- HfO₂/ZrO₂ precursors
- Electrode materials for CIM devices

### Key References

# Iteration 6

## 33. On-Chip Learning Algorithms for FeFET (Iteration 6)

### The Challenge
Traditional backpropagation requires:
- All weights stored in memory
- Sequential access in backward pass
- Significant memory/compute overhead
- Long-range data dependencies

### Solutions for FeFET-Based Training

#### 1. Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA)
Overcomes BP's long-range dependencies:
- Random fixed feedback weights
- Local weight updates
- Better suited for parallel hardware

**Reference:** [Springer - On-Chip DNN Training with DFA](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-19568-6_11)

#### 2. Hybrid Precision Synapse (HPS)
Combines FeFET with volatile memory:
- Non-volatile for weight storage
- Volatile for gradient accumulation
- Accelerates in-situ training

**Reference:** [ACM - Hybrid Precision Synapse](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3473461)

#### 3. Analog FeFET Synapse
Experimentally demonstrated:
- **5-bit synapse** with symmetric potentiation/depression
- **45× tunable conductance range**
- **75 ns update pulse**
- Partial polarization switching for analog weight update

**Reference:** [IEEE - FeFET Analog Synapse](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8268338/)

### Architecture

```
[Input] → [FeFET Crossbar Array] → [Neuron Circuitry]
                ↓                         ↓
           VMM operation         Forward propagation
                                 Backpropagation
                                 Weight update
```

### CrossSim Validation
Sandia CrossSim simulator used for BP algorithm validation:
- MNIST dataset testing
- Non-ideal FeFET conductance modeling
- Noise effects on training accuracy

### 3D FeNAND Architecture
3D ferroelectric NAND arrays proposed:
- Vertical layer allocation for hidden layers
- Area-efficient NN implementation
- Successful vector-matrix multiplication

**Reference:** [Nature Communications - 3D FeFET Array](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-36270-0)

### Endurance Optimization for Training
Programming pulse optimization critical:
- Shorter stress times → improved endurance
- Electric field frequency management
- Trade-off: training iterations vs device lifetime

---

## 34. Negative Capacitance (NC) Effects in HfO₂/ZrO₂ (Iteration 6)

### Concept
Ferroelectric NC can enable sub-60 mV/decade subthreshold swing (SS), overcoming the Boltzmann limit for energy-efficient transistors.

### Physics
NC arises from the S-shaped polarization curve where:
```
C_FE = dP/dE < 0  (in unstable region)
```

When stabilized with series capacitor:
```
1/C_total = 1/C_FE + 1/C_oxide
```

If |C_FE| > C_oxide, effective capacitance amplification occurs.

### Experimental Results

| Device | SS (mV/dec) | On/Off | Notes |
|--------|-------------|--------|-------|
| ZrOₓ NC-FET | **45.06** | - | ±1V VGS range |
| MoS₂/HZAO | **22.3** | 9.4×10⁶ | Hysteresis-free |
| Standard MOSFET | 60 (limit) | - | Boltzmann limit |

**Reference:** [PMC - ZrOx NC-FET](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7855158/)

### Key Materials

#### HfZrAlO (HZAO)
- Al incorporation enables simultaneous NC + positive capacitance
- Hysteresis-free operation
- Compatible with 2D channel materials (MoS₂)

**Reference:** [MDPI - Steep-Slope MoS₂ NC-FET](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-4991/12/24/4352)

### Challenges

1. **Stabilized NC:** Still controversial, not unambiguously demonstrated
2. **Hysteresis:** Often accompanies transient NC effects
3. **Quantum capacitance:** Limiting factor for sub-thermionic SS
4. **Trap density:** Low-trap MOSFETs receive limited NC benefit

### Design Considerations
- Gate voltage biasing in NC window critical
- S-shaped polarization region function of FE thickness
- Need for excellent electrostatic integrity

### Applications
- **Steep-slope logic:** Beyond-Boltzmann energy efficiency
- **Voltage scaling:** Reduce subthreshold/overdrive losses
- **GAA/Nanosheet:** Compatible with advanced architectures

**Reference:** [Nature Communications - NC-FET Limitations](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-019-13797-9)

---

## 35. ALD Fabrication of HfO₂/ZrO₂ Ferroelectrics (Iteration 6)

### Overview
Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) is the industrial standard for producing ferroelectric HfₓZr₁₋ₓO₂ (HZO) thin films with precise thickness control at the atomic scale.

### Key Process Parameters

#### Temperature Windows
| Material | ALD Window | GPC Error |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| HfO₂ | 210-270°C | <0.006 nm/cycle |
| ZrO₂ | 180-270°C | <0.006 nm/cycle |
| HZO | 210-270°C | Composite |

#### Oxygen Sources
- **H₂O:** Mitigates TiN oxidation damage
- **O₃:** Enables stoichiometric HfO₂ formation
- **Hybrid H₂O + O₃:** Best of both approaches

### State-of-the-Art Results (2024-2025)

#### ZrO₂-Starting Process Breakthrough
5 nm HZO at 450°C achieves:
- **Pr:** 19.6 µC/cm²
- **Speed:** <100 ns switching
- **Endurance:** >5×10⁹ cycles
- **Voltage:** 0.9-1.6 V operation

**Reference:** [ACS - Region-Selective Oxygen Vacancy](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.5c08743)

#### New Cocktail Precursor (FER-1)
- Hf(dmap)₄ + Zr(dmap)₄ (1:1 mol%)
- Overcomes ZrO₂ thermal stability limits
- Enables higher deposition temperatures

**Reference:** [ACS - Liquid Cocktail Precursor](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.4c21964)

### Nanolaminate Structures
HfO₂/ZrO₂ multilayer films at 250°C:
- Individual layer thickness: 1 nm optimal
- **Pr:** 10.2 µC/cm²
- **εᵣ:** 23
- **Leakage:** 1000× reduction

**Reference:** [Springer - Thickness-dependent Nanolaminates](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10853-020-05680-6)

### Electrode Materials
- **TiN:** Most common electrode for HZO research
- **Epitaxial TiN:** Best crystalline phase control
- Electrode nature affects HZO crystallization during annealing

**Reference:** [ScienceDirect - Epitaxial TiN Bottom Electrodes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0925838824033036)

### Process Variants

#### Direct vs Remote Plasma ALD
Both techniques studied for memory applications:
- Direct plasma: Higher deposition rates
- Remote plasma: Reduced ion damage

**Reference:** [PMC - Direct and Remote Plasma ALD](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10005305/)

### Typical ALD Sequence
```
1. Precursor pulse (Hf or Zr organometallic)
2. Purge (N₂ or Ar)
3. Oxidant pulse (H₂O, O₃, or hybrid)
4. Purge
5. Repeat for desired thickness
6. Post-deposition anneal (PDA) 400-600°C
```

## 36. Demo 2 MNIST Loader Implementation (Iteration 6)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/data/mnist.go`

Features:
- Automatic download from Yann LeCun's website
- Gzip decompression
- IDX file format parsing
- Normalized pixel values (0-1)
- One-hot encoding utilities
- Batch loading support

### API
```go
// Create dataset
mnist := data.NewMNISTDataset("./data")

// Download if needed
mnist.Download()

// Load into memory
mnist.Load()

// Get batch
batch := mnist.GetBatch(0, 64, true) // training batch

// Inference
for _, img := range batch {
    output := network.Forward(img.Pixels)
    predicted := data.ArgMax(output)
    if predicted == img.Label {
        correct++
    }
}
```

### Dataset Statistics
- Training: 60,000 images
- Test: 10,000 images
- Image size: 28×28 = 784 pixels
- Classes: 10 (digits 0-9)

# Iteration 7

## 37. Ferroelectric Tunnel Junctions (FTJ) (Iteration 7)

### Concept
FTJs exploit tunneling electroresistance (TER) - the change in tunnel current based on ferroelectric polarization direction. Polarization modulates the barrier height/width.

### State-of-the-Art Performance

| Metric | Value | Device | Reference |
|--------|-------|--------|-----------|
| TER ratio | ~10⁵ | 2.5nm barrier | Literature |
| TER ratio | 834 | HZO epitaxial | ACS 2021 |
| TER ratio | >400 | ZrO₂ tunnel | 2024 |
| ON/OFF | 5.8×10² | Pt/HZO/TiO₂/TiN | ACS 2024 |
| Speed | 20 ns | HZO FTJ | |
| Speed | 50 ns | With TiO₂ IL | |
| Endurance | 2×10⁸ cycles | With TiO₂ IL | |
| Retention | >10⁵ s (10 yr ext.) | @ 160°C | |
| States | 128 (symmetric) | Synaptic | |

### Key Advantages for CIM

1. **Self-rectifying:** Nonlinear I-V enables selector-free crossbars
2. **4F² cell size:** Minimum footprint
3. **Low conductance:** Voltage-driven (not current)
4. **CMOS compatible:** Standard fab processes
5. **Energy efficient:** Tunneling mechanism

### FTJ as Artificial Synapse
- 128-state conductance manipulation
- Cycle-to-cycle variation: 2.75%
- Pattern recognition accuracy: >95.5%
- Speech recognition demonstrated

**Reference:** [ACS - HZO FTJ Speech Recognition](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.5c01547)

### Architecture: Pt/HZO/TiO₂/TiN
TiO₂ interlayer benefits:
- Improved HZO/TiN interface
- Enhanced endurance (2×10⁸)
- Better ON/OFF ratio

**Reference:** [ACS - High-Speed HZO FTJ Memristor](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.1c18165)

### FTJ vs FeFET Comparison

| Feature | FTJ | FeFET |
|---------|-----|-------|
| Readout | Current tunneling | Channel conductance |
| Endurance | 10⁸ cycles | 10⁷-10¹² cycles |
| Speed | 20-50 ns | <100 ns |
| Cell size | 4F² | 6F² |
| Selector | Self-rectifying | May need selector |

**Reference:** [Advanced Intelligent Systems - FTJ Memristors for CIM](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aisy.202300554)

---

## 38. CMOS BEOL Integration (Iteration 7)

### The Challenge
Back-End-Of-Line (BEOL) integration requires:
- Thermal budget: **400-450°C max**
- Compatible with metal interconnects (Cu, Al)
- No damage to underlying transistors

### Why HZO is Ideal
- Lower crystallization temperature than other HfO₂ dopants
- Wide doping window
- CMOS-compatible precursors
- Scalable to sub-5nm thickness

### Nanosecond Laser Annealing (NLA)
Breakthrough approach (2024):
- Local crystallization of **3.6 nm HZO**
- Heat confined above Si substrate
- Preserves BEOL thermal budget
- Enables ultrathin ferroelectric films

**Reference:** [ACS - NLA for Ultrathin HZO](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.4c10002)

### Industry Demonstrations

#### CEA-Leti FeRAM
- 130nm CMOS BEOL integration
- Si-doped HfO₂ capacitors
- Ultralow-power, fast, high-endurance
- More environmentally friendly than PZT

**Reference:** [IEEE - BEOL Integrated FeRAM](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9854909/)

#### Fraunhofer IPMS
- mmWave varactor applications
- Optimized ferroelectric HfO₂ integration
- CMOS technology compatibility

### Flexible Electronics (2024)
FeFET on flexible substrate:
- Thermal budget: <400°C
- HZO + ultra-thin ITO channel
- Memory window: 2.78V
- Endurance: 2×10⁷ cycles

**Reference:** [Nature Communications - Flexible FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46878-5)

### Integration Approaches

| Approach | Temperature | Advantages |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Standard PDA | 400-600°C | Simple, proven |
| Flash anneal | <450°C | Fast, localized |
| NLA | Room temp substrate | Ultrathin, BEOL safe |
| Remote plasma | <400°C | Reduced damage |

### Key Reference
[Advanced Electronic Materials 2025 - HfO₂ Game-Changer](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aelm.202400686)

---

## 39. Ferroelectric Characterization Techniques (Iteration 7)

### Overview
Characterizing ferroelectricity in HfO₂-based thin films requires specialized techniques due to:
- Nanoscale thickness (<10 nm)
- Weak piezoresponse
- Parasitic electrostatic effects
- Complex domain structures

### Key Characterization Methods

#### 1. Piezoresponse Force Microscopy (PFM)
- Based on **converse piezoelectric effect**
- Measures local piezoelectric hysteresis loops
- Nanoscale spatial resolution
- Challenges: electrostatic artifacts, backswitching

**Reference:** [Nature Communications - PFM and Nanoferroics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-019-09650-8)

#### 2. PUND Switching Spectroscopy PFM (2024)
Improved methodology:
- **P**ositive **U**p **N**egative **D**own pulse sequence
- Removes electrostatic contribution
- Step-mode instead of pulse-mode
- Better for semiconductor ferroelectrics and relaxors

**Reference:** [PubMed - PUND SSPFM](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38181439/)

#### 3. Direct Piezoelectric Force Microscopy (DPFM)
- Measures **direct piezoelectric effect**
- Complementary to PFM
- Useful for verification

#### 4. Nano-PUND
- Measures **polarization charges** directly
- Alternative to piezoelectric-based methods

### Deep Learning Enhanced PFM (2023)
For ultrathin HfO₂:
- DNN + Deep Denoising Autoencoder + PCA
- Sensitivity: **0.3 pm** (previously ~1 pm limit)
- Enables low-excitation measurements
- Applicable to 10 nm HZO films

**Reference:** [npj Computational Materials - DL for PFM](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41524-023-00982-0)

### Electrical Characterization

| Method | Measures | Information |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| P-E loop | Polarization vs Field | Pr, Ec, coercivity |
| PUND | Switching charge | True Pr (removes leakage) |
| C-V | Capacitance vs Voltage | Memory window, NC |
| I-V | Current vs Voltage | Leakage, TER |
| Retention | Polarization vs Time | Data stability |
| Endurance | Pr vs Cycles | Fatigue behavior |

### HfO₂-Specific Challenges
- **d₃₃ sign variations:** Can change within single device
- **Thickness sensitivity:** Properties vary with film thickness
- **Electrode effects:** Bottom/top electrode materials matter
- **False positives:** Amorphous HfO₂ can show hysteretic PFM artifacts

### Key Reference
[ACS - Imprint Effects Quantification](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsaelm.4c00875)

---

## 40. Demo 2 Accuracy Evaluation Module (Iteration 7)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/evaluation/accuracy.go`

Features:
- Classification accuracy tracking
- Cross-entropy loss calculation
- Confusion matrix generation
- Per-class precision/recall/F1
- Macro-averaged F1 score
- Inference timing statistics

### API
```go
// Create metrics tracker
metrics := evaluation.NewMetrics(10) // 10 classes

// Update with predictions
metrics.Update(predicted, actual, probabilities)

// Or with timing
metrics.UpdateWithTiming(predicted, actual, probs, inferenceTime)

// Get results
fmt.Println(metrics.String())
metrics.PrintConfusionMatrix()
metrics.PrintPerClassMetrics()
```

### Metrics Computed
- **Accuracy:** Correct / Total
- **Precision:** TP / (TP + FP) per class
- **Recall:** TP / (TP + FN) per class
- **F1 Score:** 2 * P * R / (P + R)
- **Macro F1:** Average F1 across classes
- **Cross-entropy Loss:** -log(p_correct)

# Iteration 8

## 41. Energy Efficiency: CIM vs GPU Comparison (Iteration 8)

### TOPS/W Benchmarks

| Technology | TOPS/W | Notes |
|------------|--------|-------|
| GPU (NVIDIA) | 0.1-5 | Traditional digital |
| SRAM CIM | 50 | 28nm, 2024 |
| FeFET CIM | **26.3-111.8** | 22nm FDSOI |
| FeFET Binary | **1169** | Binary operations |
| FeFET 8b/4b | 36.5 | 8-bit act, 4-bit wt |
| 2D FeFET | 26.3 | MoS₂ channel |
| SK hynix FeRAM | ~20 | Integer ops |

### Best-in-Class FeFET Performance
- **Energy:** 0.03 fJ/bit
- **Efficiency:** 111.8 TOPS/W (charge-domain)
- **Accuracy:** 89% CIFAR-10 (VGG-8)
- **Technology:** 22nm FDSOI, 28nm HKMG

**Reference:** [Science Advances - 2D FeFET CIM](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.adp0174)

### Why FeFET CIM is Efficient

1. **No data movement:** Compute in place
2. **Voltage-driven:** Not current (low power)
3. **Analog MAC:** One operation vs many digital
4. **High ON/OFF:** Clean signal separation
5. **CMOS compatible:** No exotic fab

### Energy Reduction Potential
- **90% reduction** in neural network data transfers
- **70,000× reduction** vs GPU for LLM acceleration
- **100× speedup** for in-memory workloads

**Reference:** [Science Advances - CMOS-compatible FeFET CIM](https://www.science.org/doi/full/10.1126/sciadv.abm8537)

### Ferroelectric CIM Claims Context
Dr. Tour's claim of 10⁶× energy reduction is aggressive but directionally supported by:
- CIM eliminates memory-compute data bus (dominant energy)
- Analog MAC vs digital multiply-accumulate
- Realistic near-term: 100-1000× improvement

---

## 42. Device Variability and Yield (Iteration 8)

### Variability Types

| Type | Definition | Typical Values |
|------|------------|----------------|
| Cycle-to-Cycle (C2C) | Same device, different cycles | 0.3-5% |
| Device-to-Device (D2D) | Different devices, same fab | 0.5-10% |
| Wafer-scale | Across wafer | Varies |

### State-of-the-Art (2024)

#### 2D MoS₂ FeFET Platform
Best reported variability:
- **C2C variation:** ~0.3%
- **D2D variation:** ~0.5%
- **Wafer yield:** 96.36%
- **Endurance:** >10¹² cycles

**Reference:** [Science Advances - 2D FeFET](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.adp0174)

#### 28nm HKMG FeFET
Experimental measurements show:
- Variation is function of conductance state
- Higher variation at intermediate states
- Lower variation at saturation states

### Reliability Challenges

1. **Memory window (MW):** Reduces with scaling
2. **Polycrystalline FE:** Grain boundaries cause variation
3. **Process variation:** Pronounced at smaller nodes
4. **Weight deviation:** Stored ≠ expected values

### Mitigation Strategies

#### 1. Probabilistic Deep Learning
Bayesian Neural Networks (BNN):
- Model variation as probability distribution
- Train with expected variation
- Maintain accuracy under device non-idealities

**Reference:** [arXiv - Variation-Resilient FeFET](https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.15444)

#### 2. Write-Verify Schemes
- Program and check
- Iterative refinement
- Trade-off: time vs precision

#### 3. Error-Correcting Codes
- Redundancy in weight storage
- Tolerate some bit errors

### Variation as Feature: PUF
FeFET C2C variation enables:
- Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF)
- Hardware security applications
- Unique device fingerprints

**Reference:** [Nature Communications - FeFET PUF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-55380-x)

---

## 43. System Architecture & Peripheral Circuits (Iteration 8)

### The Peripheral Circuit Problem
Even with efficient CIM arrays, peripheral circuits dominate energy:
- **ADC/DAC:** Major energy overhead
- **Sense amplifiers:** Signal conditioning
- **Row/column drivers:** Address decoding
- **Controllers:** Timing, sequencing

### Solutions for FeFET CIM

#### 1. All-Digital Peripheral Design
- Eliminate analog ADC/DAC overhead
- Digital accumulation of results
- Trade-off: precision vs energy

#### 2. Ferroelectric Memcapacitor
Benefits over memristor:
- **Zero static power** consumption
- **Ultra-low dynamic energy:** ~31 fJ/inference
- **No selectors needed:** Self-rectifying
- **No sneak current:** Capacitive readout
- **Simplified peripherals:** Fewer components

**Reference:** [ScienceDirect - HfO₂ Memcapacitor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2211285525003702)

#### 3. Hybrid CIM Platform
Monolithic integration of:
- Boolean logic + triggers (digital)
- Multi-level cell arrays (analog)
- Best of both worlds: precision + efficiency

**Reference:** [Science Advances - Hybrid CIM](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.adp0174)

### FeFET vs ReRAM Architecture

| Aspect | FeFET | ReRAM |
|--------|-------|-------|
| Read/write energy | Lower | Higher |
| Current | Voltage-driven | Current-driven |
| Sneak path | Less severe | Major issue |
| Selector | Often not needed | Required |
| VMM | Efficient | Efficient |

### System Block Diagram

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Controller                 │
├─────────┬───────────┬─────────┬────────┤
│   DAC   │  Row      │ Column  │  ADC   │
│  Array  │  Drivers  │ Sense   │ Array  │
├─────────┴───────────┴─────────┴────────┤
│                                         │
│      ┌───────────────────────┐         │
│      │   FeFET Crossbar      │         │
│      │   (Weight Storage)    │         │
│      │                       │         │
│      │   W[i,j] = G[i,j]    │         │
│      │                       │         │
│      │   y = Σ W·x          │         │
│      └───────────────────────┘         │
│                                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           Output Buffers                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Key References
- [Nature Reviews - Ferroelectric Neuromorphic](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44287-025-00222-1)
- [ACM - FeFET Power-Efficient Architecture](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3240765.3240770)
- [Intelligent Computing - FE Devices](https://spj.science.org/doi/10.34133/2022/9859508)

## 44. Crossbar Heatmap Visualization (Iteration 8)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/visualization/heatmap.go`

Features:
- Configurable colorschemes (viridis, plasma, coolwarm)
- GPU-ready vertex generation
- Cell highlighting for debugging
- ASCII fallback for terminal
- Statistics computation

### API
```go
// Create heatmap
heatmap := visualization.NewCrossbarHeatmap(64, 64, nil)

// Set conductance data from crossbar
heatmap.SetData(crossbar.GetConductanceMatrix())

// Generate GPU vertices
vertices := heatmap.GenerateVertices()

// Or print ASCII version
heatmap.PrintASCII()

// Get statistics
min, max, mean, std := heatmap.GetStats()
```

### Colorschemes
- **Viridis:** Perceptually uniform, good for magnitudes
- **Plasma:** High contrast, bright colors
- **Coolwarm:** Diverging, good for signed values

## 45. 2D Ferroelectric Materials (Iteration 9)

### Overview
Two-dimensional (2D) ferroelectric materials offer unique advantages over bulk ferroelectrics:
- **Van der Waals interfaces:** Clean, atomically sharp junctions
- **Scalability:** Intrinsic thinness without ferroelectricity degradation
- **Integration:** Easy stacking with 2D semiconductors (MoS₂, WSe₂)
- **Dual polarization:** Some materials show coupled in-plane/out-of-plane switching

### Key 2D Ferroelectric Materials

#### α-In₂Se₃ (Indium Selenide)
Primary candidate for 2D ferroelectric devices:

| Property | Value | Notes |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Type | Semiconductor FE | ~1.3 eV bandgap |
| Polarization | In-plane + Out-of-plane | Coupled switching |
| Write speed | 40 ns | Ultra-fast |
| Energy/event | 234 fJ (excitation) / 40 fJ (inhibition) | Low power |
| Switching voltage | ~0.3 V | Ultra-low |
| Retention | >10⁴ s | Good stability |
| Endurance | >225 cycles | Needs improvement |

**Polymorphism:** β, β', ε, and γ phases show distinct sliding ferroelectric behaviors.

**Stacking effects (Nature Communications 2024):**
- **3R stacking:** In-plane domain wall movement → large hysteresis window
- **2H stacking:** Out-of-plane domain wall motion → small hysteresis window

**References:**
- [ScienceDirect - α-In₂Se₃ FSJ](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0042207X2400321X)
- [Nature Communications - Stacking Effects](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-54841-7)
- [AIP - Phase Transition Review](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/apr/article/11/2/021314/3289204)

#### CuInP₂S₆ (CIPS)
Layered insulating ferroelectric:

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Type | Insulating FE |
| Operating temp | Room temperature |
| Thickness range | 4-400 nm stable |
| Application | Gate dielectric for FeFETs |

**1T1M Architecture (Nano Letters 2024):**
Using CuInP₂S₆ + MoS₂ + h-BN:
- Resistance tunability: 10⁶
- Sneak current: 120 fA
- Static power: 12 fW

**Reference:** [Nano Letters - 1T1M Architecture](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.4c06118)

#### All-vdW FeFET Structures
Complete van der Waals stacking:
- **Gate:** α-In₂Se₃ (ferroelectric)
- **Insulator:** h-BN
- **Channel:** MoS₂, WSe₂

Advantages:
- No interfacial states
- Reduced short-channel effects
- Preserved ferroelectricity in ultra-thin films

**Reference:** [Springer - All-vdW FeFET](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11432-022-3617-2)

### 2D Ferroelectric Device Applications

#### In-Sensor Computing
Integration of:
- Sensing (optical, mechanical)
- Memory (non-volatile states)
- Processing (in-memory compute)

Eliminates von Neumann bottleneck for edge AI.

#### Logic-in-Memory
SnS/α-In₂Se₃ heterojunctions enable:
- 4 distinct resistance states from polarization interplay
- Reconfigurable logic operations
- Stable cycling endurance

**Reference:** [ACS AMI - Logic-in-Memory](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.5c16543)

### Current Challenges
1. **Endurance:** Limited cycle life (hundreds vs billions needed)
2. **Phase control:** Ferroelectric dynamics not fully understood
3. **Large-scale synthesis:** Reproducibility at wafer scale
4. **Reliability:** Long-term retention degradation mechanisms

---

## 46. FeFET Spiking Neural Networks (Iteration 9)

### Why SNNs on FeFET?
Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are more biologically realistic and energy-efficient:
- **Event-driven:** Only compute when spikes occur
- **Temporal coding:** Information in spike timing
- **Low power:** No continuous computation

FeFET advantages for SNNs:
- **Intrinsic dynamics:** Polarization switching mimics membrane potential
- **Ultra-low energy:** ~2 fJ/spike achievable
- **Non-volatility:** Weights preserved without refresh
- **Electric field driven:** Lower energy than current-driven memories

### Neuron Models on FeFET

#### Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF)
Membrane potential equation:
```
τ du/dt = f(u) + Σ wᵢIᵢ
```
Where:
- τ = membrane time constant
- u = membrane potential (stored as FE polarization)
- wᵢ = synaptic weights
- Iᵢ = input currents

FeFET implementation:
- Membrane potential = ferroelectric polarization state
- Threshold crossing = abrupt Vₜ modulation
- Spike = drain current increase

**Reference:** [Frontiers - FeFET SNN](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnins.2020.00634/full)

#### Double-Gate MPB-TFT Neuron (2024)
Morphotropic Phase Boundary thin-film transistor:
- Eliminates capacitors and reset circuits
- Enhanced area and energy efficiency
- All-ferroelectric SNN system

**Accuracy:** 94.9% on image classification

**Reference:** [Adv. Science - All-FE SNN](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/advs.202407870)

### Synapse Characteristics

#### FE-SBFET Synapse
Ferroelectric Schottky Barrier FET:
- Single-crystalline NiSi₂ contacts
- Hf₀.₅Zr₀.₅O₂ ferroelectric layer
- Silicon-on-insulator substrate

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Energy/spike | ~2 fJ |
| Endurance | 10⁵ cycles |
| C2C variation | ~1% |
| LTP/LTD | Demonstrated |

**Reference:** [ACS AMI - FE-SBFET Synapse](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.1c07505)

### Learning Algorithms

#### Surrogate Gradient (SG) Learning
Challenge: Spike function is non-differentiable.

Solution: Replace spike gradient with smooth surrogate:
```
σ'(u) = normalized negative part of fast sigmoid
```

Implementation: SuperSpike method enables backpropagation through spike timing.

#### MNIST Performance (28nm FeFET)

| Weight Precision | Accuracy |
|-----------------|----------|
| 32-bit (full) | 95.1% |
| 6-bit | 95.4% |
| 5-bit | 91.0% |
| 3-bit | Requires noise |

Dramatic accuracy drop below 5-bit highlights scaling challenges.

### 2D Material SNNs (2024-2025)

#### WSe₂ Impact Ionization Neuron
- Precise biological spiking behavior
- Energy: 2 pJ/spike
- Combined with 2D FeFET synapse

**Reference:** [ACS Nano - 2D SNN](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsnano.4c11081)

#### Bio-Inspired DG-FeFET (2025)
Double-Gate FeFET for astrocyte/dendrite emulation:
- Top gate: ferroelectric (synaptic weight programming)
- Back gate: non-ferroelectric (gain modulation)
- Emulates tripartite synapse dynamics

**Reference:** [arXiv - DG-FeFET Astrocyte](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.14466v1)

### Energy Comparison

| Technology | Energy/Spike |
|------------|--------------|
| Biological neuron | ~10 fJ |
| FeFET synapse | ~2 fJ |
| CMOS digital | ~10 pJ |
| ReRAM | ~100 fJ |

FeFET approaches biological efficiency.

## 47. Weight Import/Export Utilities (Iteration 9)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/weights/weights.go`

Features:
- JSON and binary file formats
- Layer-wise weight storage
- Quantization support for hardware deployment
- Weight statistics and normalization
- Compatible with trained PyTorch/TensorFlow models

### API

```go
// Create model weights container
model := weights.NewModelWeights("mnist_mlp", 3)

// Add layers from trained model
model.AddLayer("fc1", weightsMatrix, biasVector)
model.AddLayer("fc2", weightsMatrix2, biasVector2)

// Save to file
model.SaveJSON("model.json")
model.SaveBinary("model.bin")

// Load from file
loaded, _ := weights.LoadJSON("model.json")

// Get layer weights as matrix for crossbar programming
layer, _ := loaded.GetLayer("fc1")
matrix := layer.ToMatrix()

// Quantize for hardware
layer.QuantizeWeights(8, true) // 8-bit symmetric

// Normalize to [0,1] for crossbar
scale, offset := layer.NormalizeWeights()
```

### File Formats

#### JSON Format
Human-readable, good for debugging:
```json
{
  "name": "mnist_mlp",
  "version": "1.0",
  "layers": [{
    "name": "fc1",
    "shape": [128, 784],
    "dtype": "float64",
    "data": [...],
    "bias": [...]
  }]
}
```

#### Binary Format
Compact storage (float32):
- Magic header: 0x46455745 ("FEWE")
- Layer count
- Per-layer: name, shape, data, bias

~50% smaller than JSON.

### Quantization Support

Hardware-aware quantization:
- Symmetric or asymmetric
- Configurable bit width (1-16)
- Per-tensor quantization
- Scale and zero-point tracking

## 49. Analog In-Memory Computing for Transformers (Iteration 10)

### The Attention Bottleneck
In Large Language Models (LLMs), attention mechanisms consume 70-80% of inference energy due to:
- KV caching data transfer
- Softmax computations
- Quadratic complexity with sequence length

### AIMC Attention Architecture (Nature Computational Science 2025)

Revolutionary approach using gain-cell memories:

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Latency vs RTX 4090 | 300× faster |
| Latency vs Jetson Nano | 7,000× faster |
| Energy savings | 5 orders of magnitude |
| Single token latency | 65 ns per attention head |
| Energy per token | 6.1 nJ per attention head |

**Architecture:**
- Two arrays perform consecutive dot-products (Q·K^T, then φ(S)·V)
- Charge-to-pulse converter eliminates ADCs
- Sliding Window Attention limits memory to M recent tokens

**Evaluation:**
- ChatGPT-2 (124M parameters)
- Cross-entropy loss: 3.1 (vs 3.0 software baseline)
- Comparable downstream task performance

**Reference:** [Nature Computational Science - AIMC Attention](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00854-1)

### FeFET Challenges for Attention
Non-volatile memories face limitations for attention:
- **Slow writes:** Weights change every token in KV cache
- **High write energy:** Frequent updates consume power
- **Limited endurance:** Many write cycles per sequence

**Current approach:** FeFET for Q/K/V linear projections only, not dynamic attention scores.

### Hybrid Analog-Digital Solutions (2024)
65nm CMOS prototype:
- Analog CIM prunes 75% of low-score tokens at ultra-low power
- Digital processor handles remaining 25% precisely
- Maintains accuracy while reducing energy

**Reference:** [IEEE - Hybrid Attention](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10719540/)

---

## 50. Multi-Level Cell Programming Schemes (Iteration 10)

### MLC FeFET Fundamentals
Multi-level cell operation stores multiple bits per device:
- 2-bit: 4 conductance levels
- 3-bit: 8 levels (demonstrated in 1k FeFET arrays)
- Higher precision requires write-verify schemes

### Programming Mechanisms

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Pulse width | 200 ns |
| Voltage step | 40 mV |
| Levels achieved | Up to 7 (1k array) |

**Key insight:** Final conductance depends on pulse amplitude, independent of initial state - enables deterministic learning.

**Reference:** [arXiv - Analog Weight Update](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.01186)

### Write-Verify Scheme
1. Apply programming pulse
2. Read threshold voltage
3. Compare to target
4. If not matched, apply corrective pulse
5. Repeat until convergence or max retries

Achieves precise VT control at cost of throughput.

### First MLC FeFET Crossbar Demo (Nature Communications 2023)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Array size | 32×32 cells |
| States per cell | 4 (2-bit) |
| MNIST accuracy | 96.64% |
| CIFAR-10 accuracy | 91.55% |
| Energy efficiency | 885.4 TOPS/W |

**Cell structure:** 1FeFET-1R with external 1MΩ resistor limits current variability.

**Reference:** [Nature Comm - MLC FeFET Crossbar](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-42110-y)

### Dual-Bit FeFET Innovation (2025)
Novel segmented gate structure:
- Localized domain control
- Independent switching regions
- Eliminates complex pulse schemes
- No staircase voltage needed

**Reference:** [npj - Dual-Bit FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00030-8)

### Current Percolation Path Challenges
CPPs dominate MLC state transitions:
- Device-to-device variation
- Asymmetric program/erase
- Requires calibrated write schemes

---

## 51. Hardware-Aware Training (Iteration 10)

### Why Hardware-Aware Training?
Analog CIM introduces non-idealities:
- Weight quantization
- Update noise
- Asymmetric potentiation/depression
- Conductance drift

Training must account for these to maintain accuracy.

### Training Stages for AIMC
1. **Forward propagation:** Input × Weights with read noise
2. **Backward propagation:** Gradient computation
3. **Weight update:** Outer product of activations × gradients

### Key Techniques

#### Noise Injection Training
Inject Gaussian noise during software training:
```
W_effective = W + N(0, σ_device)
```
Forces network to find robust solutions.

#### Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)
Simulate limited conductance states during training:
- 4-bit: May need noise injection for stability
- 5-bit: ~91% MNIST accuracy
- 6-bit: ~95% accuracy (near full precision)

#### On-Chip vs Off-Chip Training

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| Off-chip | Full precision, fast | Accuracy drop on deployment |
| On-chip | Adapts to device | Endurance wear, slow |
| Hybrid | Best of both | Complex calibration |

### IBM AIHWKIT
Open-source toolkit for hardware-aware training:
- Device models (PCM, RRAM, Flash)
- Noise simulation
- PyTorch integration
- Supports MLPs, CNNs, RNNs

**Reference:** [AIHWKIT Documentation](https://aihwkit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/analog_ai.html)

### XBTorch Framework (2025)
Recent crossbar modeling tool:
- Device-level noise during weight updates
- Compatible with NeuroSim device models
- Faster than low-level simulation

**Reference:** [arXiv - XBTorch](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.07086)

---

## 52. FPGA Prototyping for FeFET (Iteration 10)

### Why FPGA Emulation?
FeFET manufacturing/integration insufficient for large-scale application. FPGA offers:
- Quick prototyping
- Design space exploration
- Algorithm validation before ASIC

### FPGA-Based Memristor Emulation

| Platform | Resource Usage | Application |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| Xilinx ZYNQ-7000 | <1% | CNN with 512 params |
| Various | Varies | 90% MNIST accuracy |

**Methods:**
- Cubic spline interpolation
- Polynomial approximations
- Hyperbolic tangent functions

### FeFET vs Other Memory Technologies on FPGA

FeFET advantages for emulation:
- **Current-source behavior:** Less prone to IR drop/noise
- **High on/off ratio:** >10⁵
- **Low programming power:** <0.1 fJ
- **Memory window:** Up to 2V

**Reference:** [IEEE - FeFET FPGA Emulation](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9912067/)

### Hybrid FPGA-Memristor Systems
Architecture:
```
CMOS Image Sensor → FPGA (Preprocessing) → Memristor Crossbar → FPGA (Readout)
```
FPGA handles digital interfacing, crossbar performs analog MAC.

### 3D Ferroelectric Arrays
3D FeNAND architecture for area-efficient NNs:
- Vector-matrix multiplication demonstrated
- Pattern classification achieved
- Vertical integration for density

**Reference:** [Nature Comm - 3D FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-36270-0)

## 53. Training Utilities Implementation (Iteration 10)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/training/training.go`

Features:
- Hardware-aware backpropagation
- Momentum-based SGD optimizer
- Weight quantization simulation
- Update noise injection
- Asymmetric potentiation/depression
- MLC programming simulation

### Trainer API

```go
// Create trainer with layer dimensions
dims := []int{784, 128, 64, 10}  // MNIST MLP
config := training.DefaultTrainingConfig()
config.QuantizeBits = 6
config.UpdateNoise = 0.02
trainer, _ := training.NewTrainer(dims, config)

// Train batch
loss := trainer.TrainBatch(inputs, targets)

// Evaluate
accuracy := trainer.Evaluate(testInputs, testLabels)

// Export weights for crossbar
weights := trainer.GetWeights()
```

### MLC Programmer API

```go
// Create MLC programmer (2-bit = 4 levels)
mlc := training.NewMLCProgrammer(2)

// Get pulse parameters
voltage, width, numPulses := mlc.ComputePulseParams(currentG, targetG)

// Simulate programming with noise
finalG, success := mlc.SimulateProgramming(currentG, targetG)

// Get valid quantized levels
levels := mlc.GetQuantizedLevels()  // [0, 0.333, 0.667, 1.0]
```

### Hardware-Aware Training Config

```go
type TrainingConfig struct {
    LearningRate   float64  // Base learning rate
    WeightDecay    float64  // L2 regularization
    Momentum       float64  // SGD momentum

    // Hardware constraints
    WeightClipMin  float64  // Min conductance (0.0)
    WeightClipMax  float64  // Max conductance (1.0)
    UpdateNoise    float64  // σ for weight update noise
    QuantizeBits   int      // Bits for weight quantization
    AsymmetryRatio float64  // Potentiation/depression ratio
}
```

## 54. FeFET Reliability: Endurance & Retention (Iteration 11)

### Endurance Limitations
Current Si-based FeFETs face significant endurance challenges:

| Technology | Typical Endurance | Target |
|------------|-------------------|--------|
| Si FeFET | 10⁴ - 10⁶ cycles | 10¹⁰+ |
| HZO superlattice | 10⁹ - 10¹⁰ cycles | 10¹² |
| Optimized HZO | 10⁸ under bipolar stress | Higher |

### Primary Degradation Mechanisms

#### 1. Charge Trapping
- Electrons and holes accumulate at interfaces
- Asymmetric de-trapping dynamics
- Trapped electrons de-trap during positive voltage
- Holes remain trapped when returning to 0V

**Key insight:** Increase in donor trap density is primary cause of fatigue.

#### 2. Interface Degradation
Primary reliability issues stem from:
- Ferroelectric/interlayer interface
- Interlayer/semiconductor interface
- Not the ferroelectric material itself

#### 3. Breakdown Mechanisms
- Defect redistribution (not generation) triggers hard breakdown
- Main failure mechanism limiting write endurance
- Progressive degradation before catastrophic failure

**Reference:** [Frontiers - HZO Breakdown](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/electronics/articles/10.3389/felec.2022.1091343/full)

### Retention Challenges
- Depolarization field degrades retention
- Inferior to FeRAM but acceptable for NVM
- Typically >10 years at room temperature for optimized devices

### Optimization Strategies

#### Nitridation Process
- Improves interfacial layer quality
- Suppresses unrecoverable electron trapping
- Main cause of endurance enhancement

#### Superlattice Engineering
Recent advances (Nature Comm 2025):
- Stable ferroelectricity up to 100nm thickness
- >10⁹ switching cycles
- Low coercive field: ~0.85 MV/cm

**Reference:** [Nature Comm - HZO Superlattice Stability](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-61758-2)

#### 2024-2025 State of the Art
- Sub-5V write voltages achieved
- <10ns switching speed
- On/off ratios up to 10⁸ cycles under optimized stress

**Reference:** [AIP JAP - HfO₂ FeFET Review](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/jap/article/138/1/010701/3351745)

---

## 56. ANN-to-SNN Conversion (Iteration 11)

### Why Convert ANN to SNN?
- SNNs more energy-efficient on neuromorphic hardware
- Event-driven computation
- Temporal coding capability
- But harder to train directly

### Conversion Methods

#### 1. Rate Coding Conversion
Map ANN activations to SNN firing rates:
```
firing_rate = f(activation)
```
Simple but requires many timesteps.

#### 2. Direct Training with Surrogate Gradients
Use smooth gradient approximations during backprop:
```
spike_gradient ≈ fast_sigmoid'(membrane_potential)
```

#### 3. Hybrid ANN-SNN Approaches
Combine strengths of both:
- ANN for feature extraction
- SNN for temporal processing

### Ferroelectric ANN-SNN Systems

#### HfZrOx Artificial Synapses/Neurons
- Ferroelectric and antiferroelectric devices
- CNN-SNN hybrid networks
- Improved 3D image recognition: 82.3% → 92.7%

**Reference:** [Nano Letters - HfZrOx Synapses](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.5c02889)

#### All-Ferroelectric SNN (2024)
- DG MPB-TFT LIF neurons
- 94.9% classification accuracy
- No capacitors or reset circuits needed

**Reference:** [Adv. Science - All-FE SNN](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/advs.202407870)

### SNN Toolbox
Automates ANN-to-SNN conversion:
- Pre-trained model input
- Automatic spike rate mapping
- Compatible with neuromorphic hardware
- Supports multiple SNN simulators

**Reference:** [SNN Toolbox Docs](https://snntoolbox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/intro.html)

### Anti-Ferroelectric Neurons
- Polarization/depolarization based
- 37 fJ/spike power consumption
- Eliminates capacitors and additional circuits
- Highly scalable

---

## 57. Batch Normalization for Analog CIM (Iteration 11)

### The BN Challenge
Batch normalization is essential for deep networks but challenging for analog CIM:
- Requires division operations
- Running statistics storage
- Per-channel normalization

### Solutions

#### 1. BN Fusion with Linear Layers
Most effective for inference:
```
W_fused = γ/√(σ² + ε) × W
b_fused = γ/√(σ² + ε) × (b - μ) + β
```

Pre-compute during model deployment, no runtime overhead.

#### 2. In-Memory BN (Binary Networks)
For BNN accelerators:
- Integrate BN on resistive crossbar
- Suppress sense amp errors
- Area/energy efficient

**Reference:** [ACM - In-Memory BN](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3287624.3287718)

#### 3. Batchnorm Fine-tuning
Real-time calibration:
- Adjust BN parameters for device non-idealities
- Nearly training-less solution
- Mitigates IR drop and read noise

**Reference:** [arXiv - Batchnorm Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18416)

### Alternative Normalizations

| Type | Use Case | Advantage for CIM |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| Layer Norm | Transformers, RNNs | No batch dependency |
| RMS Norm | LLaMA, modern LLMs | Simpler, no mean subtraction |
| Group Norm | Small batches | Stable statistics |

### Implementation Note
Modern algorithms (post-2014) have shown some normalization layers unnecessary, enabling fully analog accelerators.

---

## 58. Normalization Layer Implementation (Iteration 11)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/normalization.go`

Features:
- Batch Normalization with fusion support
- Layer Normalization for transformers
- RMS Normalization for modern LLMs
- Quantized BN for hardware deployment

### API Examples

```go
// Create batch normalization
bnConfig := layers.DefaultBatchNormConfig(128)
bn := layers.NewBatchNorm(bnConfig)

// Fuse with linear layer for CIM deployment
fusedWeights, fusedBiases := bn.FuseWithLinear(weights, biases)

// Get scale/shift for in-memory fusion
scale, shift := bn.GetFusionScaleShift()

// Quantized BN for efficient deployment
qbn := layers.NewQuantizedBatchNorm(bn, 8)  // 8-bit
output := qbn.ForwardQuantized(input)

// Layer normalization (transformers)
ln := layers.NewLayerNorm(512)
output := ln.Forward(input)

// RMS normalization (modern LLMs)
rms := layers.NewRMSNorm(512)
output := rms.Forward(input)
```

### BN Fusion Formula
For linear layer Y = WX + b followed by BN:
```
scale[i] = γ[i] / √(var[i] + ε)
shift[i] = β[i] - scale[i] × mean[i]

Y_fused = scale × (WX + b - mean) + β
        = (scale × W)X + (scale × (b - mean) + β)
```

Single fused operation, no runtime BN overhead.
