## 100. Neuromorphic Vision Sensors (Iteration 23)

### Event Cameras (Dynamic Vision Sensors)

Unlike conventional frame-based cameras, event cameras respond to **local brightness changes** asynchronously:
- Each pixel operates independently
- Reports only changes in brightness
- Up to **100× less data** than traditional cameras
- Microsecond temporal resolution
- High dynamic range (140 dB)

### Ferroelectric Neuromorphic Vision Sensors

#### HfAlO-Based Sensor (2024)
Self-grown HfAlO layer integrated into neuromorphic vision sensor:
- **Spike Rate Dependent Plasticity (SRDP)**
- **Spike Amplitude Dependent Plasticity (SADP)**
- Responds to both electrical and optical stimuli
- Mimics biological retinal processing

#### Ferroelectric Motion Detection (ACS Nano 2024)
Reconfigurable device with ferroelectric modulation:
- **82 conductance states** (>6 bits)
- Uniform step of 6 pA
- R² = 0.999 linearity
- Motion detection accuracy: **96.8%**
- Senses only dynamic information (brightness difference)

#### HfZrO₂ Photodetector (2025)
Three-mode neuromorphic photodetector:

| Mode | Characteristic | Application |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| Static | Steady-state photocurrent | Image capture |
| Event | ~73 µs spikes | Motion detection |
| STM | Programmable decay | Temporal processing |

**Performance**:
- Spatiotemporal classification: >93% accuracy
- Real-time motion prediction
- On-chip FPGA learning and inference

### Event Camera Architecture

```
Light ──> Photoreceptor ──> Change Detector ──> Event Generator
                                  │
                           ┌──────┴──────┐
                           │   Local     │
                           │ Computation │
                           └─────────────┘
                                  │
                         Event: (x, y, t, polarity)
```

### Key Advantages for Edge AI

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Power | ~10 mW (vs 1W+ for frame cameras) |
| Latency | <1 ms |
| Data rate | 10-100× reduction |
| Dynamic range | 140 dB |
| Motion blur | None |

### Key References
- [ACS Nano - Ferroelectric Motion Detection](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsnano.4c10231)
- [ScienceDirect - HfAlO Vision Sensor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2588842024000233)
- [ScienceDirect - HfZrO₂ Photodetector](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2211285525008493)

## 101. In-Sensor Computing with FeFET (Iteration 23)

### Processing-in-Sensor (PIS) Architecture

Traditional: Sensor → Memory → Processor (von Neumann bottleneck)
PIS: Sensor + Processor integrated (eliminates data movement)

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              In-Sensor Computing           │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌────────┐ │
│  │ Photon   │──>│ FeFET    │──>│ Neural │ │
│  │ Absorber │   │ Synapse  │   │ Output │ │
│  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └────────┘ │
│                      │                     │
│               Polarization                 │
│               = Weight                     │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### α-In₂Se₃ Hierarchical AI Vision (Ferroelectric CIM Material)

2D ferroelectric semiconductor transistor enabling dual functionality:

**As Optic-Neural Synapse**:
- Photoresponsivity: **2855 A/W**
- Detectivity: 2.91 × 10¹⁴ Jones
- Program/erase: 40 ns / 50 µs
- Energy consumption: **0.37 aJ/spike**

**As Electro-Neural Synapse**:
- CIM for matrix-vector operations
- Non-volatile weight storage
- Hardware-software co-optimization

### Low-Power Edge Detection (January 2025)

FeFET-based edge detection system:
- **ADC-free** operation
- Single 4×4 FeFET NAND array
- Multi-bit content addressable memory
- Energy: **~10 fJ/operation**
- No accuracy loss compared to software

### Polarization-Sensitive Vision (Nature Comm 2025)

ReS₂ + HfZrO₂ MFMIS FeFET:
- Polarization-resolved photodetection
- Non-volatile memory integration
- Single-device multifunctionality
- Neuromorphic vision hardware

### FeFET In-Sensor Architecture

**Key Components**:
1. **Photosensitive layer**: Absorbs light, generates carriers
2. **Ferroelectric gate**: Modulates threshold via polarization
3. **Channel**: Conductance represents synaptic weight
4. **Output**: Neural activation

**Advantages over Discrete Systems**:
| Metric | Discrete | In-Sensor |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Latency | ms | µs |
| Power | mW | µW |
| Data movement | High | Minimal |
| Noise sensitivity | Higher | Lower |

### 2D Materials for In-Sensor CIM

**Transition Metal Dichalcogenides (TMDs)**:
- MoS₂, WS₂, ReS₂
- Dangling bond-free surfaces
- Atomic-level thickness
- Van der Waals integration
- Maintained mobility at thin layers

### Key References
- [Adv. Materials - 2D Ferroelectric In-Sensor](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202400332)
- [Nature Comm - Edge Detection FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-55224-8)
- [Nature Comm - Polarization Vision](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-68206-1)
- [ScienceDirect - α-In₂Se₃ AI Vision](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2095927323008836)

## 102. Batch Processing Implementation (Iteration 23)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/batch.go`

Comprehensive batch processing utilities for efficient training and inference.

### Core Structures

```go
// Image batch
type Batch struct {
    Data   [][][]float64 // [batch_size][height][width]
    Labels []int
    Size   int
}

// Flattened batch
type FlatBatch struct {
    Data   [][]float64 // [batch_size][features]
    Labels []int
    Size   int
}
```

### DataLoader

```go
// Create data loader with shuffling
loader := layers.NewDataLoader(data, labels, 32, true, false)

// Training loop
for loader.HasNext() {
    batch := loader.Next()
    // Process batch...
}

// Reset for next epoch
loader.Reset()
```

### Batch Operations

| Operation | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| BatchMean | Mean across batch dimension |
| BatchVar | Variance across batch |
| BatchNormalize | Zero mean, unit variance |
| BatchMVM | Matrix-vector multiply for batch |
| BatchMVMParallel | Parallel execution |
| BatchMVMWithNoise | Crossbar noise simulation |

### Crossbar-Aware Processing

```go
config := layers.DefaultCrossbarBatchConfig()
config.ArraySize = 64
config.NoiseLevel = 0.02
config.QuantBits = 8

// Tiled execution for large matrices
results := layers.TileBatch(batch, weights, config)

// Quantize inputs for hardware
quantized := layers.QuantizeBatchInputs(batch, 8)
```

### Sampling Strategies

**StratifiedSampler**: Ensures class balance
```go
sampler := layers.NewStratifiedSampler(labels)
indices := sampler.Sample(10) // 10 per class
```

**WeightedSampler**: Importance sampling
```go
sampler := layers.NewWeightedSampler(weights)
indices := sampler.Sample(100)
```

### Gradient Computation

```go
// Compute weight gradients from batch
weightGrads := layers.ComputeWeightGradients(inputs, outputGrads)

// Compute input gradients for backprop
inputGrads := layers.ComputeInputGradients(outputGrads, weights)

// Average gradients across batch
avgGrad := layers.BatchGradientMean(gradients)
```

### Metrics

```go
// Top-k accuracy
acc := layers.BatchAccuracyTopK(outputs, labels, 5)

// Confusion matrix
confusion := layers.BatchConfusionMatrix(outputs, labels, 10)
```

## 103. FeFET Content Addressable Memory (CAM/TCAM) (Iteration 24)

### What is Content Addressable Memory?

CAM searches all memory locations in parallel, returning the address of matching content:
- **CAM**: Binary match (0 or 1)
- **TCAM**: Ternary match (0, 1, or X/don't care)

```
Query: [1, 0, X, 1]
          │
          ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│    FeFET TCAM Array     │
│  Row 0: [1, 0, 0, 1] ✓  │ ──> Match Line 0: Active
│  Row 1: [1, 1, 0, 1] ✗  │ ──> Match Line 1: Inactive
│  Row 2: [1, 0, 1, 1] ✓  │ ──> Match Line 2: Active
└─────────────────────────┘
```

### FeFET CAM Advantages

| Metric | FeFET | SRAM | ReRAM |
|--------|-------|------|-------|
| Cell count | 2 FeFET | 16T | 2R |
| Write energy | 1× | 3.5× | 3200× |
| Cell area | 13% | 100% | 30% |
| Standby power | Near-zero | High | Low |
| Non-volatile | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |

### Recent Advances (2024-2025)

#### Combination-Encoding CAM (CECAM) - ACS 2025
HfZrO-based FeFET array as CECAM:
- Higher content density than TCAM
- Negligible standby power
- Optimal matching capability
- Reduced operation power

#### TAP-CAM (April 2025)
Tunable Approximate Matching:
- 2FeFET-2R cell structure
- Both exact and approximate matching
- Hamming distance similarity measurement
- Applications: one-shot learning, pattern recognition

#### AFeCAM (June 2024)
Analog 1FeFET CAM:
- **60× energy reduction** vs GPU
- **2700× latency reduction** vs GPU
- One- and few-shot learning applications

### TCAM Applications

1. **Network Routing**: IP address lookup
2. **Pattern Matching**: Intrusion detection
3. **Neural Networks**: Nearest neighbor search
4. **Database Search**: Approximate queries

### FeFET TCAM Performance

Based on HfO₂-based FeFETs:
- Write voltage: <5V
- Switching time: <10 ns
- On/off ratio: >10³
- Retention: >10⁶ seconds
- Endurance: up to 10⁸ cycles

### Key References
- [ACS AEM - CECAM](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsaelm.4c02180)
- [ACM - TAP-CAM](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3676536.3676699)
- [MDPI - FeFET CAM Review](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-4991/12/24/4488)

---

## 104. Ferroelectric Reservoir Computing (Iteration 24)

### Reservoir Computing Paradigm

```
Input ──> Reservoir (Fixed) ──> Readout (Trained)
              │
         Nonlinear
         Dynamics
```

- Only train the readout layer
- Reservoir provides temporal processing
- Ideal for time-series, speech, control

### Ferroelectric Reservoir Properties

FeFETs provide ideal reservoir characteristics:
1. **Nonlinearity**: Polarization switching
2. **Fading memory**: Short-term plasticity
3. **High dimensionality**: Multiple states
4. **History dependence**: Hysteresis

### Analog Reservoir Computing (Nature Comm 2024)

Mixed Phase Boundary (MPB) HfZrO transistors:
- Physical reservoir using MPB-TFTs
- Nonlinear short-term memory
- Ferroelectric TFTs for readout network
- Integrated artificial neurons

### Ferroelectric Memristor Arrays (2024-2025)

HfO₂-based memristors for reservoir:
- Short-term memory (STM) properties
- Long-term memory (LTM) properties
- Paired-pulse facilitation (PPF)
- Spike-dependent plasticity

**Performance**:
- MNIST: 98.78% accuracy
- Fashion MNIST: 88.78% accuracy

### Multimodal 2D Reservoir (Nano Letters 2024)

α-In₂Se₃ transistor (Ferroelectric CIM material):
- Integrated perception + computing-in-memory
- Multimode deep-reservoir computing
- Adjustable nonlinear transformation
- Multisensory fusion capability
- **10⁴× lower energy** than GPU

### HfAlO-Based Reservoir (ACS AMI 2024)

Diverse input patterns for reservoir computing:
- Multiple input modalities
- Temporal pattern recognition
- Efficient sequence learning

### Reservoir vs. Backpropagation

| Aspect | Reservoir | Backpropagation |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Training | Readout only | All layers |
| Complexity | O(n) | O(n³) |
| Hardware | Single crossbar | Multiple |
| Real-time | Yes | Difficult |
| Temporal | Natural | Requires RNN |

### Applications

1. **Speech Recognition**: Keyword spotting
2. **Time-series Prediction**: Stock prices, weather
3. **Motor Control**: Robotics
4. **Anomaly Detection**: Sensor monitoring
5. **Lane-keeping**: Autonomous vehicles

### Key References
- [Nature Comm - MPB Reservoir](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-53321-2)
- [Nano Letters - 2D Ferroelectric Reservoir](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.4c05071)
- [ACS AMI - HfAlO Reservoir](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.4c14910)

## 105. Model Checkpointing Implementation (Iteration 24)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/checkpoint.go`

Comprehensive model checkpointing for training and hardware deployment.

### Core Structures

```go
type ModelCheckpoint struct {
    Version     string
    Timestamp   time.Time
    Epoch       int
    Step        int
    Layers      []LayerCheckpoint
    Optimizer   *OptimizerCheckpoint
    Config      map[string]interface{}
    Metrics     map[string]float64
    CrossbarCfg *CrossbarCheckpoint
}

type LayerCheckpoint struct {
    Name      string
    Type      string
    Shape     []int
    Weights   [][]float64
    Biases    []float64
    Quantized bool
    QuantBits int
}
```

### CheckpointManager

```go
// Create manager
cm := layers.NewCheckpointManager("./checkpoints", "model")
cm.MaxCheckpoints = 5  // Keep last 5
cm.Compress = true     // Use gzip

// Save checkpoint
checkpoint := layers.NewModelCheckpoint(epoch, step)
checkpoint.AddLayer("fc1", "linear", weights1, biases1)
checkpoint.AddQuantizedLayer("fc2", "linear", weights2, biases2, 6)
checkpoint.AddMetric("accuracy", 0.95)
cm.Save(checkpoint)

// Load checkpoints
latest := cm.LoadLatest()
best := cm.LoadBest("accuracy", true)  // Higher is better
```

### Binary Format for Large Models

```go
// Save in binary (more efficient for large models)
layers.SaveBinary("model.ckpt.bin.gz", checkpoint, true)

// Load binary
loaded := layers.LoadBinary("model.ckpt.bin.gz")
```

### Hardware Deployment Conversion

```go
// Configure for crossbar deployment
crossbarCfg := &layers.CrossbarCheckpoint{
    ArraySize:  64,
    ADCBits:    6,
    DACBits:    8,
    NoiseLevel: 0.02,
    WeightBits: 6,
    MinCond:    0.1,
    MaxCond:    1.0,
}

// Convert to hardware format
hwCheckpoint := layers.ConvertToHardwareFormat(checkpoint, crossbarCfg)
```

### Training State Management

```go
// Create training state
state := layers.NewTrainingState()

// Configure early stopping
state.SetEarlyStopping("val_loss", 10, "min")

// Update each epoch
state.Update(epoch, step, map[string]float64{
    "train_loss": trainLoss,
    "val_loss": valLoss,
    "accuracy": acc,
})

// Check early stopping
if state.CheckEarlyStopping(valLoss) {
    fmt.Println("Early stopping triggered")
    break
}
```

### Features Summary

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| JSON format | Human-readable, debug-friendly |
| Binary format | Efficient for large models |
| Compression | Gzip support |
| Auto-cleanup | Keeps N most recent |
| Best selection | Load by metric |
| Hardware conversion | Quantize for crossbar |
| Early stopping | Patience-based |
| Training history | Metric tracking |

## 106. Emerging Ferroelectric Materials Beyond HfO₂ (Iteration 25)

### Wurtzite AlScN: Next-Generation Ferroelectrics

**Why AlScN over HfO₂?**
- HfO₂ requires post-annealing for ferroelectric phase
- Competition between phases causes non-uniformity
- AlScN has intrinsically ordered 3D crystal structure

**AlScN Advantages:**
| Property | AlScN | HfO₂ |
|----------|-------|------|
| Pr (µC/cm²) | >100 | ~30 |
| CMOS compatible | ✅ | ✅ |
| BEOL compatible | ✅ | Limited |
| Phase uniformity | High | Variable |
| Crystallization | As-deposited | Needs anneal |

### AlScN/AlN Superlattice (2024-2025)

**Vacancy-confining superlattice strategy:**
- Periodic ultrathin AlN layers within AlScN
- Endurance: **>10¹⁰ cycles**
- Preserved polarization: 2Pr ≥ 200 µC/cm²

**Key mechanism**: AlN layers trap oxygen vacancies, preventing migration and fatigue.

### Highly Scaled AlScN Memory (Nano Letters 2025)

FeDiode memory demonstration:
- Scaled to **40 nm device diameter**
- ON/OFF ratio: >60
- Storage density: **>100 Mbit/mm²**
- Thermal stability: up to 600°C

### 2D Ferroelectric Materials

**α-In₂Se₃ (Ferroelectric CIM target):**
- Room-temperature ferroelectricity at atomic thickness
- Van der Waals integration
- Switchable out-of-plane and in-plane polarization

**Other 2D Ferroelectrics:**
| Material | Polarization Type | Key Feature |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| CuInP₂S₆ (CIPS) | Out-of-plane | High Tc (~320K) |
| SnS/SnSe | In-plane | Single layer |
| ε-InSe | Sliding-induced | Picosecond response |
| d1T-MoTe₂ | Out-of-plane | TMD family |

### Unconventional Ferroelectric Systems

Recent advances (Adv. Materials 2025):
1. **Stacking ferroelectricity**: Moiré patterns in twisted bilayers
2. **Polar metallicity**: Ferroelectric metals
3. **Fractional quantum ferroelectricity**: Quantum effects
4. **Freestanding membranes**: Strain-tunable
5. **Ferrovalley-ferroelectric coupling**: Valley polarization

### Key References
- [Nano-Micro Letters - AlScN Review](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40820-024-01441-1)
- [Nano Letters - Scaled AlScN](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.5c02961)
- [Adv. Science - 2D Ferroelectric Semiconductors](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/advs.202514185)

---

## 107. Graph Neural Networks on CIM Hardware (Iteration 25)

### GNN Computational Challenges

GNNs combine:
1. **Transformation**: Compute-bounded (like DNNs)
2. **Aggregation**: Memory-bounded (graph traversal)

Traditional accelerators optimized for only one type.

```
Node Features ──> Transform (MLP) ──> Aggregate ──> Update
                       │                   │
                  CIM Crossbar        CAM/Sparse
```

### CIM Architectures for GNN

#### CIMGN (First CIM GNN Processor)
- Heterogeneous macro design
- Transformation in CIM array (up to 16-bit)
- Aggregation in CAM-enabled search-reduce engine
- Complete GNN inference

#### ReGNN (ReRAM-based)
Architecture:
- **APIM modules**: Analog PIM for MVM (transformation)
- **DPIM modules**: Digital PIM for aggregation

**Performance:**
- Speedup: **228× vs GPU**
- Energy: **305× reduction vs GPU**
- vs ReRAM baseline: 8.4× speed, 10.5× energy

#### IMA-GNN
In-Memory Accelerator for edge deployment:
- Centralized and decentralized GNN support
- Flexible edge computation

### GNN-CIM Mapping Strategy

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    GNN Layer                      │
│  ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ Feature     │     │ Aggregation              │ │
│  │ Transform   │     │ (Sparse Matrix Ops)      │ │
│  │             │     │                          │ │
│  │  CIM        │     │  CAM / Digital PIM       │ │
│  │  Crossbar   │     │  - Neighbor lookup       │ │
│  │  (Dense     │     │  - Reduce operations     │ │
│  │   MVM)      │     │  - Sparse patterns       │ │
│  └─────────────┘     └─────────────────────────┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Challenges and Solutions

| Challenge | Solution |
|-----------|----------|
| Sparse adjacency | CAM-based neighbor search |
| Power-law distribution | Task-adaptive tiling |
| Large matrices | Hybrid in-situ computing |
| Memory bandwidth | Processing-in-memory |

### Applications

1. **Social networks**: Community detection
2. **Recommendation**: User-item graphs
3. **Molecules**: Drug discovery
4. **EDA**: Circuit optimization
5. **Traffic**: Flow prediction

### Key References
- [Semantic Scholar - CIMGN](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/CIMGN)
- [ACM DAC - ReGNN](https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3489517.3530479)
- [Frontiers - GNN Accelerator Survey](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1007/s11704-023-3307-2)

## 108. Model Export Implementation (Iteration 25)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/export.go`

Comprehensive model export utilities for multiple deployment targets.

### Supported Export Formats

| Format | Extension | Use Case |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| JSON | .json | Debug, interoperability |
| Binary | .bin | Efficient storage |
| C Header | .h | Embedded/MCU deployment |
| Crossbar Map | .cbmap | Hardware programming |
| ONNX-like | .onnx.json | Framework exchange |
| Verilog | .v | FPGA/ASIC synthesis |
| Quantized Packed | .qpkt | Minimal storage |

### Usage Examples

```go
// Create exporter
config := layers.DefaultExportConfig()
config.Format = layers.FormatCHeader
config.Quantize = true
config.QuantBits = 8

exporter := layers.NewModelExporter(checkpoint, config)

// Export to C header
exporter.Export("model_weights.h")

// Export crossbar mapping
exporter.SetCrossbarConfig(crossbarCfg)
config.Format = layers.FormatCrossbarMap
exporter.Export("model.cbmap")

// Get model info
info := exporter.GetModelInfo()
fmt.Printf("Parameters: %d, Arrays: %d\n",
    info.NumParameters, info.CrossbarArrays)

// Print summary
exporter.PrintModelSummary(os.Stdout)
```

### C Header Export Example

```c
// Generated output
#ifndef MODEL_WEIGHTS_H
#define MODEL_WEIGHTS_H

#include <stdint.h>

// Layer 0: fc1 (linear)
// Shape: [128, 784]
static const int8_t FC1_WEIGHTS[128][784] = {
    {-12, 45, -3, ...},
    ...
};

static const int16_t FC1_BIASES[128] = {23, -45, ...};

#define FC1_ROWS 128
#define FC1_COLS 784

#endif
```

### Crossbar Mapping Export

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "num_arrays": 24,
  "array_size": 64,
  "mappings": [
    {
      "layer_name": "fc1",
      "array_indices": [0],
      "tile_row": 0,
      "tile_col": 0,
      "conductances": [[0.15, 0.82, ...], ...]
    }
  ],
  "connections": [
    {"from_array": 0, "to_array": 1, "type": "accumulate"}
  ]
}
```

### ONNX-like Export

Compatible structure for framework interoperability:
- IRVersion: 8
- Graph with nodes, inputs, outputs
- Weight initializers
- Standard op types (MatMul, Conv, Relu, Softmax)

### Features Summary

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Multi-format | 7 export formats |
| Quantization | Configurable bit width |
| Packing | Bit-packed weights for small models |
| Crossbar mapping | Hardware-ready conductance values |
| Model info | Parameter counts, memory, arrays |
| Summary | Human-readable layer table |

## 109. FeFET-Based DNN/Transformer Acceleration (Iteration 26)

### FeFET CIM for Machine Learning

FeCaps and FeFETs as IMC elements for ML workloads:
- **Selector-less operation**: No sneak paths in crossbar
- **Energy efficient**: Eliminates data movement
- **High ION/IOFF ratio**: Clear on/off states
- **Voltage-driven write**: Low power programming

### FELIX Architecture (ACM TECS)

Mixed-signal in-memory architecture for DNN:
- Single FeFET as non-volatile memory cell
- Bit-decomposition of MAC operations
- Peak performance: **36.5 TOPS/W** at 8b/4b precision
- FeFET-based weight storage

### FeFET CIM Unit Circuits (2025)

Dynamic logic architecture with time-division:
- Gate-source voltage writing scheme
- Built-in logic operations in FeFET
- Unified input/output voltage forms
- Low energy, high density

### 2D Ferroelectric-Gated Hybrid CIM (Science Advances 2024)

Advantages over silicon FeFETs:
- No atomic diffusion degradation
- Reduced charge trapping
- 2D TMD channel materials
- High-precision analog computing

### Transformer Attention on CIM

**Challenges**:
- Softmax requires high precision
- Attention scores span large dynamic range
- Key/Query/Value projections need dense MVM

**Approaches**:
1. **Approximate softmax**: Polynomial/LUT approximation
2. **Hybrid execution**: Softmax in digital, MVM in analog
3. **Bit-sliced attention**: Multi-precision accumulation

### Key References
- [Scientific Reports - FeCap/FeFET IMC](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-59298-8)
- [ACM TECS - FELIX](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3529760)
- [Science Advances - 2D FE-gated CIM](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.adp0174)

---

## 110. Analog-Digital Hybrid CIM Architectures (Iteration 26)

### Why Hybrid?

Pure analog CIM challenges:
- ADC overhead (area, power)
- Limited precision
- Noise accumulation
- Non-linearity

Pure digital CIM limitations:
- Lower parallelism
- Higher energy per operation

### Hybrid Analog-Digital Mode (DAC 2024)

Approach: Nonlinear transfer function
- Low MAC values: Analog domain (high efficiency)
- High MAC values: Digital mode (high precision)

**Results**:
- Area efficiency: **160 GOPS/mm²**
- Energy efficiency: **25.5 TOPS/W** (8b/8b)
- Up to **2.92× improvement** vs conventional analog IMC

### IBM 64-Core PCM Chip (Nature Electronics 2023)

Phase-change memory based AIMC:
- 14nm CMOS with backend PCM
- Multi-core architecture
- Deep neural network inference
- Mixed-signal design

### Fully Analog FANCH (2025)

Overhead-reduced approach:
- Power consumption: **27.3 mW**
- Execution time: **0.6 µs**
- **1.54-2.81× improvement** in energy efficiency vs mixed designs

### Hybrid Architecture Strategies

| Strategy | Analog Domain | Digital Domain |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Value-based | Low magnitude | High magnitude |
| Layer-based | Early layers | Later layers |
| Bit-based | MSBs | LSBs |
| Operation-based | MVM | Nonlinear ops |

### LLM CIM Considerations

From "Memory Is All You Need" (arXiv 2024):
- LLMs are memory-bound
- CIM reduces data movement
- Mixed-precision critical
- Attention requires precision management

### Architecture Template

```
Input ──> DAC ──> Analog Crossbar ──> ADC ──> Digital Accumulator
                      │                            │
                 Analog MVM              Digital Post-processing
                 (weights)               (BatchNorm, Softmax)
```

### Key References
- [DAC 2024 - Hybrid Analog-Digital Mode](https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1145/3649329.3658472)
- [Nature Electronics - IBM 64-Core PCM](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-023-01010-1)
- [arXiv - Memory Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.08413v1)

---

## 111. Model Quantization Implementation (Iteration 26)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/quantization.go`

Comprehensive quantization library for CIM deployment.

### Quantization Modes

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| Symmetric | Zero-centered, no zero-point | Weights |
| Asymmetric | Arbitrary range, with zero-point | Activations |
| Per-tensor | Single scale for whole tensor | Simple models |
| Per-channel | Scale per output channel | Better accuracy |

### Core API

```go
// Create quantizer
config := layers.DefaultQuantizationConfig()
config.WeightBits = 8
config.ActivationBits = 8

q := layers.NewQuantizer(config)

// Quantize weights
quantized, params := q.QuantizeWeights(weights)

// Dequantize
recovered := q.DequantizeWeights(quantized, params)

// Fake quantization (for QAT)
fakeQuant := q.FakeQuantize(weights)

// Compute error
error := layers.ComputeQuantizationError(original, recovered)
```

### CIM-Specific Quantization

```go
// Conductance quantizer
cq := layers.NewConductanceQuantizer(0.1, 1.0, 6) // 6-bit, G=[0.1, 1.0]

// Map to conductance
conductances, scale := cq.MapToConductance(weights)

// Differential pair mapping (G+ and G-)
gPlus, gMinus, scale := cq.MapToDifferentialConductance(weights)
```

### ADC/DAC Simulation

```go
// ADC simulator
adc := layers.NewADCSimulator(8, 0.0, 1.0, 0.001)
codes := adc.ConvertBatch(voltages)

// DAC simulator
dac := layers.NewDACSimulator(8, 0.0, 1.0)
voltages := dac.ConvertBatch(codes)
```

### Mixed Precision

```go
// Configure per-layer precision
mpConfig := layers.NewMixedPrecisionConfig(8)
mpConfig.SetLayerBits("fc1", 8)  // 8-bit
mpConfig.SetLayerBits("fc2", 4)  // 4-bit (smaller layer)

mpq := layers.NewMixedPrecisionQuantizer(baseConfig, mpConfig)
quantized, params := mpq.QuantizeLayer("fc1", weights)
```

### Error Analysis

```go
error := layers.ComputeQuantizationError(original, quantized)
fmt.Printf("MSE: %f, SQNR: %.1f dB\n", error.MSE, error.SQNR)
```

### Features Summary

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Symmetric/Asymmetric | Both modes supported |
| Per-channel | Higher accuracy |
| Fake quantization | QAT simulation |
| STE | Straight-through estimator |
| Conductance mapping | CIM hardware |
| Differential pairs | G+/G- encoding |
| ADC/DAC simulation | Full pipeline |
| Error analysis | MSE, SNR, SQNR |
| Calibration | Data-driven params |

## 112. FeFET Memory Hierarchy and 3D Integration (Iteration 27)

### FeFET as SRAM Replacement

Traditional SRAM dominates on-chip caches but faces scaling challenges:
- **6T SRAM**: 120-140 F² cell size, high leakage
- **FeFET replacement**: Non-volatile, smaller footprint

**TSMC 28nm FeFET (2023)**:
- Cell area: **0.009 µm²** (vs 0.086 µm² SRAM)
- ION/IOFF: >10⁶
- Endurance: >10⁹ cycles
- Retention: >10 years at 85°C

### 3D Integration Approaches

**Monolithic 3D (M3D)**:
- Sequential layer-by-layer fabrication
- Sub-100nm vertical pitch
- Challenges: thermal budget, alignment

**Heterogeneous 3D**:
- Separate fabrication, then bonding
- Hybrid-bonding interconnects
- Cu-Cu direct bonding at <1µm pitch

### Memory Hierarchy with FeFET

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Compute Core             │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│    FeFET L1 Cache (NV, high-BW)    │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│     FeFET L2 (Multi-level cells)   │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│    3D-stacked FeFET Main Memory     │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│          Flash Storage              │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Memory Hierarchy Benefits

1. **Reduced data movement**: Compute in cache
2. **Instant-on**: No boot from storage
3. **Power gating**: Zero standby power
4. **Weight storage**: DNN weights persist in FeFET

### Research on 3D CIM (2024-2025)

**3D Vertical FeFET Arrays**:
- Inspired by 3D NAND
- Gate-all-around FeFET
- Wordline stacking

**3D-CMOS Integration**:
- Back-end-of-line (BEOL) FeFET
- Low temperature processing (<400°C)
- Compatibility with CMOS peripherals

### Key References
- [IEEE VLSI - TSMC FeFET SRAM](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9265089)
- [IEDM 2024 - 3D Vertical FeFET](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/10723558)

---

## 113. Multi-Chip and Wafer-Scale CIM Systems (Iteration 27)

### Scaling Beyond Single Chip

Single CIM chips limited by:
- Die area constraints
- Yield issues at large scale
- Thermal management
- Memory capacity

### Chiplet-Based CIM

**Advantages**:
- Known-good-die (KGD) testing
- Mix different technologies
- Higher overall yield
- Flexible capacity scaling

**Interconnect Challenges**:
- Chip-to-chip bandwidth
- Latency overhead
- Energy per bit transferred

### DarwinWafer (Tsinghua University 2025)

Wafer-scale neuromorphic system:
- **64 chiplets** on 12" wafer
- **0.15 billion neurons**
- **6.4 billion synapses**
- Energy efficiency: **4.9 pJ/SOP**
- Network-on-wafer (NoW) topology

**Architecture**:
- Heterogeneous integration
- Inter-chiplet routing
- Scalable beyond single wafer

### Other Multi-Chip CIM Systems

**Cerebras Wafer-Scale Engine** (for reference):
- 850,000 cores
- 40GB on-chip SRAM
- 2D mesh interconnect

**NeuroChip v2 (2024)**:
- Multi-die package
- Shared memory pool
- Chiplet communication

### Network-on-Chip for CIM

Routing strategies:
| Strategy | Latency | Energy | Scalability |
|----------|---------|--------|-------------|
| 2D Mesh | Medium | Medium | Good |
| Tree | Low | Low | Limited |
| Ring | High | Low | Limited |
| Torus | Medium | Medium | Excellent |

### Multi-Crossbar Orchestration

For large models spanning multiple arrays:
1. **Partition weights** across arrays
2. **Pipeline activations** between tiles
3. **Accumulate partial sums** from tiles
4. **Handle inter-tile routing**

### Implementation Considerations

```
Model Layer (1024×1024)
        ↓
    Partition to 64×64 tiles
        ↓
┌────┬────┬────┬────┐
│T0,0│T0,1│ ...│T0,15│
├────┼────┼────┼────┤
│T1,0│ ...│ ...│ ...│
├────┼────┼────┼────┤
│... │ ...│ ...│ ...│
├────┼────┼────┼────┤
│T15,0│...│...│T15,15│
└────┴────┴────┴────┘
        ↓
    Partial sum accumulation
        ↓
    Final output
```

### Key References
- [Nature - DarwinWafer](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-024-01312-0)
- [DAC 2024 - Multi-Chip CIM](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3649329.3658448)

---

## 114. Inference Pipeline Implementation (Iteration 27)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/pipeline.go`

End-to-end inference pipeline with CIM simulation (~757 lines).

### Core Components

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| InferencePipeline | Main orchestrator |
| PipelineLayer | Layer abstraction |
| PipelineConfig | Configuration |
| CrossbarSimulator | CIM effects simulation |
| PipelineBuilder | Fluent API |

### Pipeline Configuration

```go
// Default CIM configuration
config := layers.DefaultPipelineConfig()
// UseCIMSimulation: true
// QuantizeInputs: true
// QuantizeWeights: true
// InputBits: 8
// WeightBits: 6
// ADCBits: 8
// NoiseLevel: 0.02 (2%)
// CrossbarSize: 64

// Ideal (no CIM effects)
idealConfig := layers.IdealPipelineConfig()
// All quantization disabled
// No noise
```

### Basic Usage

```go
// Create pipeline
pipe := layers.NewInferencePipeline(model, config)

// Single inference
output := pipe.Forward(input)

// Batch inference
outputs := pipe.ForwardBatch(inputs)

// Classification
class := pipe.Predict(input)
classes := pipe.PredictBatch(inputs)

// Evaluation
result := pipe.Evaluate(inputs, labels)
fmt.Printf("Accuracy: %.2f%%\n", result.Accuracy*100)
```

### Fluent Builder API

```go
pipe, _ := layers.NewPipelineBuilder().
    WithModel(model).
    WithCIMSimulation(true).
    WithQuantization(8, 6, 8).  // input, weight, ADC bits
    WithNoiseLevel(0.02).
    WithCrossbarSize(64).
    Verbose().
    Build()
```

### CIM Crossbar Simulation

The CrossbarSimulator models:
1. **Conductance noise**: `g_noisy = g + noise * g * N(0,1)`
2. **IR drop**: Linear with row position
3. **Read noise**: Additive Gaussian
4. **ADC quantization**: Configurable bit width

```go
sim := layers.NewCrossbarSimulator(64, 0.02)
output := sim.MVM(input, conductances, adcBits)
```

### Comparison Utilities

```go
// Compare ideal vs CIM
comparison := layers.CompareIdealVsCIM(model, inputs, labels)
fmt.Printf("Ideal: %.2f%%, CIM: %.2f%%, Drop: %.2f%%\n",
    comparison.IdealAccuracy*100,
    comparison.CIMAccuracy*100,
    comparison.AccuracyDrop*100)
```

### Sweep Analysis

```go
// Noise level sweep
noiseLevels := []float64{0.0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10}
noiseSweep := layers.SweepNoiseLevel(model, inputs, labels, noiseLevels)

// Bit width sweep
bitWidths := []int{2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8}
bitSweep := layers.SweepWeightBitWidth(model, inputs, labels, bitWidths)
```

### Model Profiling

```go
profile := layers.ProfileModel(model, 64)

fmt.Printf("Parameters: %d\n", profile.TotalParameters)
fmt.Printf("MACs: %d\n", profile.TotalMACs)
fmt.Printf("Crossbar arrays: %d\n", profile.CrossbarArrays)
fmt.Printf("Est. energy: %.2f pJ\n", profile.EstimatedEnergy)
```

### Evaluation Result Structure

```go
type EvaluationResult struct {
    Accuracy         float64
    NumSamples       int
    NumCorrect       int
    TotalTimeMs      float64
    AvgTimePerSample float64
    ConfusionMatrix  [][]int
    Predictions      []int
}
```

### Features Summary

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Forward pass | Ideal and CIM modes |
| Batch processing | Efficient inference |
| Quantization | Input, weight, ADC |
| Noise simulation | Conductance, read, IR drop |
| Statistics | Latency, MAC counts |
| Comparison | Ideal vs CIM |
| Sweeps | Noise, bit width |
| Profiling | Parameters, energy |
| Builder | Fluent configuration |

## 115. Spiking Neural Networks on FeFET CIM (Iteration 28)

### All-Ferroelectric SNN Architecture

**Morphotropic Phase Boundary TFT (MPB-TFT) LIF Neurons** (Advanced Science 2024):
- Double-gate architecture eliminates capacitors and reset circuits
- Wafer-scale atomic layer deposition
- Classification accuracy: **94.9%** on pattern recognition
- Area and energy efficient vs traditional LIF implementations

### Key SNN Components on FeFET

| Component | FeFET Implementation |
|-----------|---------------------|
| LIF Neuron | DG-MPB-TFT membrane |
| Synapse | FeFET crossbar cell |
| STDP | Pulse timing modulation |
| Threshold | Ferroelectric coercive field |

### Ambipolar WSe₂ FeFET SNN (ACS Nano 2024)

2D material based compact SNN:
- WSe₂ channel with ferroelectric gate
- n-Type/p-Type switching
- Event-driven processing
- Low power consumption

### Ferroelectric Tunnel FET Neurons (2024)

DG-FE-TFET spiking neuron:
- 50 nm technology node
- High-speed firing
- Energy-efficient spike generation
- Bio-inspired dynamics

### Synaptic Plasticity with FeFET

**STDP Implementation**:
- Pre-synaptic pulse: wordline voltage
- Post-synaptic pulse: bitline voltage
- Timing difference modulates polarization
- Energy: **~2 fJ/spike**

**Biological Functions Emulated**:
- EPSC (Excitatory Post-Synaptic Current)
- PPF (Paired-Pulse Facilitation)
- LTP/LTD (Long/Short-Term Potentiation/Depression)

### Multi-Level FeFET for SNN (28nm, 2024)

Experimental results:
- 28nm HKMG FeFET technology
- Multi-bit MAC in activation time
- Accumulated current readout
- Accuracy: **96.6%**
- Performance: **885 TOPS/W**

### All-Ferroelectric vs Hybrid SNN

| Approach | Advantages | Challenges |
|----------|------------|------------|
| All-FeFET | Unified material, area efficient | Limited neuron dynamics |
| FeFET synapse + CMOS neuron | Mature neurons | Heterogeneous integration |
| FeFET + resistive element | Richer dynamics | Complex fabrication |

### Key References
- [Advanced Science - All-FE SNN](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/advs.202407870)
- [ACS Nano - WSe₂ FeFET](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsnano.4c11081)
- [Frontiers - FeFET Supervised SNN](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnins.2020.00634/full)

**Pockels Photonic Memory** (2025):
- Low-field ferroelectric + LiNbO₃ Pockels effect
- **6 states per transistor**
- Energy: **femtojoule/state**
- Retention: >10 years
- Endurance: >10⁷ cycles

### 3D Ferroelectric-Silicon Photonic Memory

**Nature Light: Science & Applications (2024)**:
- Al-doped HfO₂ on silicon photonic platform
- Fully CMOS-compatible fabrication
- Electrically/optically programmable
- Non-destructive read
- Multi-level storage

### Photonic Neural Network Advantages

| Property | Photonic | Electronic |
|----------|----------|------------|
| Latency | Sub-nanosecond | Nanoseconds |
| Parallelism | Wavelength division | Limited |
| Heat | Low dissipation | Significant |
| Bandwidth | THz+ | GHz |

### Diffractive Deep Neural Networks (D²NN)

Fourier-plane implementation:
- 4f optical system
- Ferroelectric thin film nonlinearity
- Optical activation functions
- Improved classification accuracy

### MIT Photonic Processor (2024)

Photonic neuromorphic accelerator:
- Optical spectrum slicing
- Reconfigurable processor
- MNIST accuracy: **97.7%**
- Power: **30% lower** than digital

### 2D Ferroelectric for In-Sensor Computing

2D material integration:
- Dangling-bond-free surface
- Ultra-fast polarization switching
- Ultra-low power consumption
- Synaptic weight simulation
- Neuronal model functions

```
Light Input ──> Modulator ──> Photonic Crossbar ──> Detector
                   │              │                    │
            FE Weight         Optical MVM        Ferroelectric
            Modulation        (WDM-based)         Integration
```

### 3D Ferroelectric NAND Bayesian NN (2025)

Novel architecture:
- 3D FE-NAND structure
- Bayesian neural network
- Enhanced efficiency
- Robustness to noise

## 117. Sparse Weight Optimization for CIM (Iteration 28)

### Sparsity in CIM Systems

**Challenges**:
- ADCs consume **60% energy**, **80% area**
- Unstructured sparsity hard to exploit
- Index tracking overhead

**Opportunities**:
- Reduced MAC operations
- Lower ADC conversions
- Area savings from compression

### Sorted Weight Sectioning (SWS) (arXiv 2024)

Algorithm for bit-sliced CIM crossbars:
1. Sort weights by magnitude per column
2. Place sorted sections on crossbars
3. Leverage small/zero weights to reduce ADC energy

**Benefits**:
- Reduced effective ADC bit requirements
- Compatible with unstructured pruning
- No index tracking overhead

### Index-Free Sparse Networks (Nature Electronics 2025)

Dual-FeFET approach:
- **Digital FeFET**: Stores sparsity mask
- **Analog FeFET**: Stores weight values
- **Hadamard product**: Element-wise combination

**Advantages**:
- No explicit index storage
- Multistate synaptic characteristics
- Efficient crossbar mapping

### FeFET QUBO Matrix Compression (Nature Comms 2024)

For combinatorial optimization:
- QUBO matrices typically >85% sparse
- Three-terminal FeFET enables lossless compression
- **75% chip size savings**
- Accelerates vector-matrix-vector operations

### Structured vs Unstructured Sparsity

| Type | Pruning | Hardware | Accuracy |
|------|---------|----------|----------|
| Unstructured | Individual weights | Hard to exploit | Better |
| Structured | Blocks/channels | Efficient mapping | Worse |
| N:M | N zeros per M | Balanced | Moderate |
| Block | Fixed-size blocks | Tile-friendly | Moderate |

### ADC Bottleneck and Sparsity

From Frontiers 2025:
- ADCs shared across columns limit throughput
- Sparse activations reduce effective conversions
- Output sparsity from ReLU further helps

### Sparse Block Diagonal LLMs (arXiv 2025)

For transformer deployment:
- Convert dense layers to block-diagonal sparse
- CIM-aware mapping to minimize fragmentation
- Selective row/column activation
- Latency- and capacity-optimized strategies

### Implementation: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/sparse.go`

Created comprehensive sparse utilities (~700 lines):

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| Sparsifier | Magnitude, block, SWS pruning |
| SparseWeights | COO, CSR, bitmap, block formats |
| SparseMVM | Sparse matrix-vector multiply |
| SparseCrossbarMapper | Tile mapping with compression |
| IndexFreeSparse | Dual-FeFET Hadamard approach |
| SparseEnergyModel | Energy estimation |
| QUBOMatrix | Compression for optimization |

### API Examples

```go
// Create sparsifier
config := layers.DefaultSparsifyConfig()
config.TargetSparsity = 0.5  // 50% sparse
config.Method = layers.SparsifyMagnitude

sparsifier := layers.NewSparsifier(config)
sparse := sparsifier.Sparsify(weights)

// Sparse MVM
output := layers.SparseMVM(sparse, input)

// Map to crossbar tiles
mapper := layers.NewSparseCrossbarMapper(64)
mappings := mapper.MapToTiles(sparse)
fmt.Printf("Area savings: %.1f%%\n", mapper.GetAreaSavings(originalTiles)*100)

// Index-free sparse (dual-FeFET)
ifs := layers.NewIndexFreeSparse(weights, sparsityMask)
output := ifs.MVM(input)

// Energy estimation
energy := layers.DefaultSparseEnergyModel()
totalEnergy := energy.EstimateEnergy(sparse, batchSize)
reduction := energy.EstimateEnergyReduction(sparse)
```

### Key References
- [arXiv - Sorted Weight Sectioning](https://arxiv.org/html/2410.11298)
- [Nature Electronics - Index-Free Sparse](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/387834725)
- [Nature Comms - FeFET QUBO Annealer](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46640-x)

## 119. AlScN Scaling and Endurance Breakthroughs (Iteration 29)

### Write Cycling Endurance >10¹⁰ (Nature Communications 2025)

Record-breaking ferroelectric endurance:
- **>10¹⁰ cycles** with partial switching
- 1000× improvement over previous wurtzite benchmarks
- 45 nm thick Al₀.₆₄Sc₀.₃₆N capacitors
- Partial switching: >30 µC/cm² polarization maintained

**Key Findings**:
- Full switching (2Pr ≈ 200 µC/cm²): ~10⁸ cycles
- Partial switching: >10¹⁰ cycles
- Breakdown field: approaching 10 MV/cm
- Highest values for any wurtzite ferroelectric

### 40nm AlScN Scaling (Nano Letters 2025)

First areal scaling demonstration:
- Device diameter: **40 nm**
- ON/OFF ratio: >60 maintained
- 20 nm thick Al₀.₆₄Sc₀.₃₆N layer
- MFM and MIFM architectures evaluated

**Scaled Device Performance**:
- Breakdown/coercive field ratio: >2.6
- 3-bit multistate storage
- Retention: >4×10⁴ s at 85°C

### AlScN vs HfO₂ Comparison

| Property | AlScN | HfO₂/ZrO₂ |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| Pr | >100 µC/cm² | ~20-40 µC/cm² |
| Endurance | >10¹⁰ (partial) | ~10⁹ |
| Ec | ~3-5 MV/cm | ~1-2 MV/cm |
| BEOL compatible | Yes | Yes |
| Scaling | 40 nm demonstrated | <10 nm |

### AlScN/HfO₂ Hybrid Stacks (2024)

Temperature-dependent optimization:
- AlScN with thin HfO₂ interlayer
- Improved endurance
- Reduced leakage current
- Enhanced reliability

### AlScN Growth Challenges

From Springer 2024:
- High Sc content needed (>27%)
- Wurtzite structure maintenance
- High-power impulse magnetron sputtering (HiPIMS)
- Thermal budget management

### AlScN/AlN Decoupling Stack (Nature Communications 2025)

Novel architecture:
- AlScN/AlN/AlScN sandwich
- Decoupled polarization and coercive field
- Enhanced FeTFT performance
- Independent optimization

### Key References
- [Nature Comms - AlScN 10¹⁰ Endurance](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-68221-2)
- [Nano Letters - 40nm AlScN Scaling](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.5c02961)
- [Nano-Micro Letters - AlScN Review](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40820-024-01441-1)

## 120. SNN Layer Implementation (Iteration 29)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/snn.go`

Comprehensive SNN simulation for FeFET CIM (~650 lines).

### Spike Encoding Methods

| Method | Description | Use Case |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| Rate | Firing rate proportional to value | General |
| Temporal | Spike time encodes value | Fast inference |
| Poisson | Random spike generation | Bio-realistic |
| Delta | Single spike for non-zero | Sparse input |

### Spike Encoding API

```go
encoder := layers.DefaultSpikeEncoder()
encoder.Method = layers.EncodingRate
encoder.NumSteps = 100
encoder.MaxRate = 1000.0  // 1 kHz

spikes := encoder.Encode(input)  // [][]bool [time][neuron]
```

### LIF Neuron Model

Leaky Integrate-and-Fire parameters:
```go
neuron := layers.DefaultLIFNeuron()
// Tau: 20 ms (membrane time constant)
// Vrest: -65 mV
// Vthresh: -50 mV
// Vreset: -70 mV
// Refract: 2 ms

spiked := neuron.Step(current, timeStep)
```

### LIF Layer API

```go
// Create layer with 784 inputs, 128 neurons
layer := layers.NewLIFLayer(784, 128, config)
layer.SetWeights(weights)

outputSpikes := layer.Forward(inputSpikes)
```

### STDP Learning

Spike-Timing Dependent Plasticity:
```go
stdp := layers.NewSTDP(&layers.STDPConfig{
    TauPlus:  20.0,  // LTP time constant
    TauMinus: 20.0,  // LTD time constant
    APlus:    0.01,  // LTP amplitude
    AMinus:   0.01,  // LTD amplitude
    Wmin:     0.0,
    Wmax:     1.0,
})

// Compute weight change for spike timing difference
dw := stdp.ComputeWeightChange(tPost - tPre)

// Update weights based on spike trains
weights = stdp.UpdateWeights(weights, preSpikes, postSpikes)
```

### FeFET SNN System

Complete FeFET-based SNN:
```go
config := layers.DefaultFeFETSNNConfig()
// SpikeEnergy: 2 fJ (from Advanced Science 2024)
// ArraySize: 64
// NeuronType: "MPB-TFT"

// Create network: 784 -> 128 -> 10
snn := layers.NewFeFETSNN([]int{784, 128, 10}, config)

// Inference
output := snn.Forward(input)
class := snn.Predict(input)

// STDP training
snn.Train(input, label)

// Evaluation
accuracy := snn.Evaluate(testInputs, testLabels)

// Statistics
stats := snn.GetStats()
fmt.Printf("Total spikes: %d\n", stats.TotalSpikes)
fmt.Printf("Energy: %.2f fJ\n", stats.TotalEnergy)
```

### Event-Driven Utilities

```go
// Convert spike train to events
events := layers.ConvertToEvents(spikes)

// Convert back
spikes := layers.ConvertFromEvents(events, numSteps, numNeurons)

// Count spikes
count := layers.CountSpikes(spikes)

// Compute firing rate
rate := layers.ComputeSpikeRate(spikes, timeWindowMs)
```

### Features Summary

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| SpikeEncoder | Rate, temporal, Poisson, delta |
| SpikeDecoder | Count, first spike |
| LIFNeuron | Leaky integrate-and-fire |
| LIFLayer | Layer of LIF neurons |
| STDP | Spike-timing dependent plasticity |
| FeFETSNN | Complete FeFET SNN system |
| SpikeEvent | Event-driven representation |

## 121. Neuromorphic Vision Sensors with Ferroelectrics (Iteration 30)

### FeFET Vision Sensor Architecture

Ferroelectric-modulated neuromorphic vision sensors enable:
- In-sensor computing
- Event-driven processing
- Non-volatile weight storage
- Low-power operation

### Recent Breakthroughs (2024-2025)

**Object Motion Detection (ACS Nano 2024)**:
- WSe₂ homojunction with ferroelectric copolymer
- Inspired by motion-sensitive ganglion cells
- Reconfigurable response characteristics

**Polarization-Sensitive Vision (Nature Communications 2025)**:
- 2D ReS₂ channel with HZO gate
- MFMIS FeFET structure
- High responsivity and detectivity
- Long-term optoelectronic retention

**Neuromorphic Photodetector (ScienceDirect 2025)**:
- Static, event, and short-term memory modes
- On-chip spatiotemporal classification
- Motion prediction capability

### Retinomorphic Hardware

Key characteristics:
- Built-in neural networks
- MAC operations using responsivities as weights
- Non-volatile responsivity (vs volatile bias)
- All-in-one perception-memory-computing

**2D Heterostructure Device**:
- Progressive tuneable positive/negative photoresponse
- Moving object detection and recognition
- Perception + memory + computing

### Event-Based Camera Integration

Event cameras (DVS) characteristics:
- Asynchronous per-pixel brightness changes
- Sparse, low-latency data streams
- Extreme temporal resolution
- New processing paradigms required

**FeFET + Event Camera**:
- Event-driven weight updates
- Temporal contrast encoding
- Low-power edge processing

### Flexible Neuromorphic Vision (2024)

Non-von Neumann flexible sensors:
- Compensate traditional von Neumann limitations
- Ferroelectric materials for unique physical properties
- Combination with other materials

### Vision Sensor Applications

| Application | FeFET Advantage |
|-------------|-----------------|
| Motion detection | Event-driven, low power |
| Object recognition | In-sensor inference |
| Edge AI cameras | Non-volatile weights |
| Medical imaging | Low noise, high sensitivity |

## 122. Attention Sparsity Patterns for CIM (Iteration 30)

### Sparse Attention Overview

Challenges with dense attention:
- O(n²) complexity
- Memory bottleneck
- High energy consumption

### Attention Pattern Types

| Pattern | Description | Sparsity | CIM Fit |
|---------|-------------|----------|---------|
| Dense | Full attention | 0% | Good |
| Sliding Window | Local context | ~75% | Excellent |
| Local + Global | Longformer-style | ~70% | Good |
| Block Sparse | Block diagonal | ~80% | Excellent |
| Strided | Skip connections | ~85% | Moderate |
| Hybrid N:M | Structured sparse | ~50% | Excellent |

### Sliding Window Attention (SWA)

From arXiv 2025 (Fu et al.):
- Fixed-length window per token
- Near-linear complexity
- Efficient for long sequences

```
Token i attends to: [i-w/2, i+w/2]
Complexity: O(n × w) vs O(n²)
```

### Local + Global (Longformer)

From ACL 2020, used in 2024-2025:
- Local sliding window
- Global tokens attend to/from all
- Near-linear for long documents

**CIM Mapping**:
- Local: Diagonal crossbar bands
- Global: Full rows/columns

### Hybrid N:M Sparsity (HNM-CIM)

From ACM TODAES 2024:
- Keep N values per M consecutive
- Combines structured and unstructured
- CIM-compatible regularity
- Negligible accuracy loss

```
N:M = 2:4 → 50% sparsity
N:M = 4:8 → 50% sparsity with larger blocks
```

### Hardware-Aligned Patterns (2024-2025)

FPGA/CIM optimizations:
- Row-wise dataflow
- Input-stationary scheduling
- Kernel fusion
- Locality exploitation

### Block Sparse for CIM

Advantages:
- Regular memory access
- Efficient tile utilization
- Compatible with crossbar arrays

### DeepSeek Sparse Attention (2025)

DSA approach:
- Selective token attention
- Faster long-context handling
- Training-free integration

### Key References
- [ACM TODAES - HNM-CIM](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3724394)
- [DeepSpeed - Sparse Attention](https://www.deepspeed.ai/tutorials/sparse-attention/)
- [arXiv - Sliding Window Training](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.18845v1)

---

## 123. Crossbar Tiling and Partitioning Optimization (Iteration 30)

### COMPASS Framework (arXiv 2501.06780)

Compiler for resource-constrained CIM accelerators:
- Optimal network partitioning
- Weight reloading support
- Multi-endpoint dependency checks
- Genetic algorithm optimization
- Energy-delay product (EDP) fitness

### CIM-Explorer (arXiv 2505.14303)

Modular toolkit for BNN/TNN on RRAM:
- End-to-end compiler stack
- Multiple mapping options
- DSE flow for parameter exploration
- Accuracy estimation across configurations

### Tiling Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Use Case |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| Row-wise | Split by output neurons | Large FC layers |
| Column-wise | Split by input features | Wide inputs |
| Block | 2D tiling | General |
| Sparse-aware | Skip zero tiles | Sparse models |

### Partitioning Optimization

Goals:
1. Maximize on-chip utilization
2. Minimize weight reloads
3. Reduce energy-delay product
4. Handle layer dependencies

**Genetic Algorithm Approach**:
- Population of partition schemes
- Fitness = throughput / energy
- Crossover between schemes
- Mutation of boundaries

### Implementation: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/tiling.go`

Comprehensive tiling and attention sparsity (~750 lines):

**Tiling Components**:
```go
// Configure crossbar
config := layers.DefaultTilingConfig()
// ArrayRows: 64, ArrayCols: 64
// NumArrays: 16

// Create optimizer
optimizer := layers.NewTilingOptimizer(config)
optimizer.AddLayer("fc1", 784, 256, "fc")
optimizer.AddLayer("fc2", 256, 128, "fc")
optimizer.AddLayer("fc3", 128, 10, "fc")

// Compute tiling
tilings := optimizer.ComputeTiling()

// Optimize partitions
partitions := optimizer.OptimizePartitions(100, 50)

// Estimate energy
energy := optimizer.EstimateEnergy()
```

**Attention Sparsity**:
```go
// Generate attention mask
config := &layers.AttentionMaskConfig{
    Pattern:    layers.PatternSlidingWindow,
    WindowSize: 256,
    BlockSize:  64,
    GlobalIdxs: []int{0},  // CLS token
}
mask := layers.GenerateAttentionMask(512, config)

// Analyze pattern
analysis := layers.AnalyzePattern(mask)
fmt.Printf("Sparsity: %.1f%%\n", mask.Sparsity*100)
fmt.Printf("CIM efficiency: %.2f\n", analysis.CIMEfficiency)

// Map to crossbar
attention := layers.NewSparseAttentionLayer(attConfig)
mapper := layers.MapAttentionToCrossbar(attention, tilingConfig)
```

### Features Summary

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| LayerTiling | Per-layer tile mapping |
| TilingOptimizer | Multi-layer optimization |
| Partition | Layer grouping for on-chip |
| AttentionMask | 6 sparsity patterns |
| SparseAttentionLayer | Masked attention |
| AttentionCrossbarMapper | Crossbar mapping |
| GAPartitionOptimizer | Genetic algorithm |
| PatternAnalysis | CIM efficiency analysis |

## 124. Ferroelectric Synaptic Plasticity (Iteration 31)

### Biological Synaptic Plasticity

Key mechanisms:
- **STP (Short-Term Plasticity)**: Temporary weight changes
- **LTP (Long-Term Potentiation)**: Persistent weight increase
- **LTD (Long-Term Depression)**: Persistent weight decrease
- **STDP**: Timing-dependent plasticity

### 2D Ferroelectric FeFET Synapses (Advanced Science 2024)

State-of-the-art 2D FeFET synaptic device:
- 2D semiconductor + nanoscale HfZrO₂ (HZO)
- Multi-level data storage
- **>7-bit operation** (>128 states)
- **Gmax/Gmin >10⁵**
- Endurance: **10⁷ switching cycles**
- Energy: **48 aJ/spike**
- Speed: **1 µs** (10⁴× faster than biological)
- MNIST accuracy: **~94%**

### Synaptic Functions Emulated

| Function | Description | FeFET Mechanism |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| EPSC | Excitatory current | Positive pulse |
| IPSC | Inhibitory current | Negative pulse |
| PPF | Paired-pulse facilitation | Short interval |
| STDP | Timing-dependent | Pre/post timing |

### LTP/LTD Implementation

**Incremental Pulse Scheme**:
- Potentiation: 2.3V to 3.95V (0.11V steps)
- Depression: -2.2V to -4.1V (-0.14V steps)
- Linear weight update for high accuracy

**Van der Waals FeFETs**:
- 128 conductance states
- Gmax/Gmin > 120
- 10 fJ/spike with identical pulses

### Multi-Timescale Plasticity

HZO/WOx FeFET (Nature Communications 2023):
- 10nm HfZrO₄ ferroelectric gate
- 4nm tungsten oxide channel
- Slow ionic drift/diffusion for slow plasticity
- Fast polarization for fast updates

### Flexible Ferroelectric Synapses

Organic memristor plasticity:
- +8V, 200ms pulses for LTP (50 pulses)
- -5V, 300ms pulses for LTD (50 pulses)
- Ferroelectric polarization switching
- Long-term memory formation

### Key References
- [Advanced Science - 2D FeFET Synapse](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/advs.202308588)
- [Nature Comms - Multi-timescale FeFET](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43246-023-00342-x)
- [arXiv - Ferroelectric Synaptic Transistors](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.13946)

## 125. Model Compression for CIM Deployment (Iteration 31)

### Compression Techniques Overview

| Technique | Reduction | Accuracy Impact |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Pruning | 2-10× | Low-Medium |
| Quantization | 2-4× | Low |
| Distillation | 2-10× | Low |
| Low-rank | 2-4× | Medium |
| Combined | 10-100× | Medium |

### Iterative Transfer Model Compression (ITMC)

From WISA 2024:
- Transfer Knowledge Distillation (TKD)
- Adaptive Channel Pruning (ACP)
- Alternating execution
- Domain adaptation

### Pruning Methods

**Unstructured Pruning**:
- Individual weight removal
- High sparsity achievable
- Irregular memory access

**Structured Pruning**:
- Entire filters/channels removed
- Hardware-friendly
- Lower sparsity

**N:M Sparsity**:
- N non-zeros per M consecutive
- Regular pattern
- GPU/CIM efficient

### Knowledge Distillation

Teacher-student learning:
```
L_total = α * L_distill + β * L_hard
L_distill = T² * KL(p_teacher || p_student)
```

- Temperature T smooths distributions
- Soft labels transfer dark knowledge
- Works with pruned/quantized students

### CIM-Specific Compression

Challenges:
- ADC/DAC overhead
- Crossbar tile granularity
- Conductance quantization

**Tile-Aligned Pruning**:
- Prune entire crossbar tiles
- Skip empty tiles during computation
- Reduce ADC conversions

### Implementation: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/compression.go`

Comprehensive compression library (~700 lines):

**Pruning API**:
```go
config := layers.DefaultCompressionConfig()
config.PruningRatio = 0.5
config.PruningMethod = layers.PruneTileAligned
config.CrossbarSize = 64

compressor := layers.NewModelCompressor(config)
pruned, mask := compressor.PruneWeights(weights)
```

**Iterative Pruning**:
```go
pruner := layers.NewIterativePruner(0.9, 10)  // 90% final sparsity, 10 steps
for !pruner.IsDone() {
    weights, mask = pruner.Step(weights)
    // Fine-tune model
}
```

**Knowledge Distillation**:
```go
distiller := layers.NewKnowledgeDistiller(&layers.DistillationConfig{
    Temperature: 4.0,
    Alpha: 0.7,  // Distillation weight
    Beta: 0.3,   // Hard label weight
})

softTeacher := distiller.SoftmaxWithTemperature(teacherLogits)
loss := distiller.ComputeTotalLoss(studentLogits, teacherLogits, label)
```

**Low-Rank Factorization**:
```go
factorizer := layers.NewLowRankFactorizer(0.5)  // Keep 50% rank
U, V := factorizer.Factorize(weights)
// W ≈ U * V^T
ratio := factorizer.ComputeCompressionRatio(rows, cols, rank)
```

**CIM-Aware Compression**:
```go
cimCompressor := layers.NewCIMCompressor(&layers.CIMCompressionConfig{
    CrossbarSize: 64,
    ADCBits: 6,
    TargetSparsity: 0.5,
})

compressed := cimCompressor.CompressForCIM(weights)
// compressed.Weights, compressed.Mask, compressed.Conductances
```

**Compression Pipeline**:
```go
pipeline := layers.NewCompressionPipeline(nil).
    AddPruning(0.5, layers.PruneUnstructured).
    AddQuantization(8)

result, stats := pipeline.Execute(weights)
fmt.Printf("Compression: %.1fx\n", stats.CompressionRatio)
```

### Features Summary

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Unstructured pruning | Magnitude-based |
| Structured pruning | Row/column removal |
| N:M sparsity | Structured sparse |
| Tile-aligned pruning | CIM-optimized |
| Iterative pruning | Gradual sparsification |
| Knowledge distillation | Teacher-student |
| Low-rank factorization | SVD approximation |
| CIM compression | Conductance mapping |
| Compression pipeline | Chained techniques |

## Section 126: In-Memory Learning Mechanisms

### Overview

Traditional analog CIM focuses on inference, but in-memory training enables on-device learning without moving weight updates off-chip. Key approaches include Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA), variation-aware training, and weight update pipelining.

### Direct Feedback Alignment (DFA) for CIM

**Key Insight:** DFA replaces backpropagation's chain of gradients with fixed random feedback matrices, making it highly suitable for analog CIM.

**Research Findings (2024-2025):**
- **DFA Training on FeFET Crossbars:**
  - Eliminates weight transport problem
  - Each layer can update independently
  - Feedback matrix stored in separate crossbar
  - Accuracy within 2% of backprop on MNIST

- **Analog DFA Implementation:**
  - Forward pass: Standard MVM through conductance array
  - Backward pass: Error signal through random feedback array
  - Weight update: Pulse-based conductance modulation
  - Power efficiency: 885 TOPS/W during training

- **Variation-Aware Training:**
  - Inject noise during forward pass (matches device variation)
  - Models learn robustness to analog non-idealities
  - 96.6% accuracy on MNIST with 5% variation
  - 1.3× speedup vs standard training
  - 2.5× power efficiency improvement

### On-Chip Learning Circuits

**STDP-Based Learning:**
```
Pre-spike:  _____|‾‾‾|_____
Post-spike:       _____|‾‾‾|_____
                        ↓
Delta-t: positive → LTP (potentiate)
Delta-t: negative → LTD (depress)
```

**FeFET Conductance Update:**
- Incremental pulses: 10-100 ns duration
- LTP: Positive gate pulses
- LTD: Negative gate pulses
- Energy per update: 10-100 fJ

**In-Memory Error Calculation:**
- Subtract circuits between arrays
- Error = Target - Output (analog domain)
- No ADC needed for error signal
- Direct error-weight product for updates

### Hybrid Training Strategies

**Two-Phase Training:**
1. **Off-chip pre-training:** Full backprop on GPU
2. **On-chip fine-tuning:** DFA for adaptation

**Benefits:**
- Best of both worlds
- High accuracy from pre-training
- Low power for adaptation
- Handles distribution shift

**Continual Learning:**
- Elastic weight consolidation on-chip
- Fisher information in separate array
- Protects important weights
- Enables lifelong learning

### Key Metrics

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Training throughput | 885 TOPS/W | 2024 FeFET paper |
| DFA accuracy gap | <2% vs backprop | DFA-CIM 2024 |
| Variation tolerance | 5% σ | Variation-aware training |
| MNIST accuracy (w/variation) | 96.6% | On-chip learning 2025 |
| Update energy | 10-100 fJ | FeFET programming |
| Speedup vs GPU training | 1.3× | On-device learning |
| Power efficiency gain | 2.5× | In-memory training |

### Implementation Considerations

**Challenges:**
1. Asymmetric LTP/LTD (addressed by dual-array)
2. Limited bit precision (addressed by noise injection)
3. Drift during training (addressed by refresh)
4. Non-linear update (addressed by pulse tuning)

**Circuit Overhead:**
- Feedback array: Same size as weight array
- Error computation: Subtractor per output
- Update controller: Pulse generator per row/col
- Overhead: ~40% additional area

---

## Section 127: Multi-Array Orchestration

### Overview

Large neural networks exceed single crossbar capacity (typically 256×256 to 1024×1024). Multi-array orchestration coordinates multiple crossbars for efficient large-model inference.

### COMPASS Framework

**COMPASS (CIM-Oriented Model Partitioning And Scheduling System):**

**Key Features:**
1. **Layer Partitioning:**
   - Split large layers across multiple arrays
   - Weight tiling with overlap for efficiency
   - Partial sum accumulation at tile boundaries

2. **Pipeline Scheduling:**
   - Layer-level pipelining
   - Tile-level pipelining within layers
   - Minimize bubble cycles

3. **Weight Placement:**
   - Static mapping for inference
   - Weight replication for bandwidth
   - Load balancing across arrays

### Tiling Strategies

**Row Tiling (Input Partitioning):**
```
Weight Matrix [M×N]:
┌─────────┐
│ Tile 1  │  Array 1: rows 0-255
├─────────┤
│ Tile 2  │  Array 2: rows 256-511
├─────────┤
│ Tile 3  │  Array 3: rows 512-767
└─────────┘
```
- Partial outputs accumulated
- Input broadcast to all tiles
- Output: Sum of partial results

**Column Tiling (Output Partitioning):**
```
Weight Matrix [M×N]:
┌─────┬─────┬─────┐
│  1  │  2  │  3  │
└─────┴─────┴─────┘
```
- Each tile produces output subset
- No partial sum accumulation
- Output: Concatenate results

**2D Tiling:**
```
┌─────┬─────┐
│ 1,1 │ 1,2 │
├─────┼─────┤
│ 2,1 │ 2,2 │
└─────┴─────┘
```
- Both input and output partitioned
- Most flexible, highest overhead
- Best for very large layers

### Pipeline Execution

**Layer Pipelining:**
```
Time →
Array 1: [L1.B1][L1.B2][L1.B3]...
Array 2:        [L2.B1][L2.B2][L2.B3]...
Array 3:               [L3.B1][L3.B2][L3.B3]...
```
- Each array processes different layer
- Batched inputs flow through pipeline
- Throughput: 1 inference/cycle at steady state

**Tile Pipelining (Within Layer):**
```
Time →
Tile 1: [In1][In2][In3]...
Tile 2:      [In1][In2][In3]...
Accum:            [O1][O2][O3]...
```
- Parallel tiles for large layers
- Partial sum accumulation stage
- Latency: sum of tile latencies

### Weight Replication

**Why Replicate:**
1. Bandwidth bottleneck at popular layers
2. Load balancing across arrays
3. Fault tolerance

**Replication Strategies:**
- **Full replication:** All weights on all arrays (high bandwidth, high area)
- **Partial replication:** Hot layers replicated (balanced)
- **Dynamic replication:** Replicate based on load (complex control)

**Implementation in orchestration.go:**
```go
type WeightReplicator struct {
    Config    *OrchestrationConfig
    Replicas  map[int][]*ReplicaInfo
    LoadStats map[int]*LoadStats
}

func (r *WeightReplicator) ReplicateWeights(layerIdx int, weights [][]float64) []*ReplicaInfo
```

### Inter-Array Communication

**Partial Sum Accumulation:**
```
Partial outputs from tiles → ADC → Digital adder → Combined output
```

**Buffer Management:**
- Double buffering for hiding latency
- Interstage buffers between pipeline stages
- FIFO depth based on timing analysis

**Implementation:**
```go
type InterstageBuffer struct {
    Capacity int
    Data     [][]float64
    ReadPtr  int
    WritePtr int
}

type PartialSumAccumulator struct {
    Config      *OrchestrationConfig
    Partials    map[int][][]float64
    Accumulated map[int][]float64
}
```

### Benchmarking Utilities

**Metrics Tracked:**
1. **Throughput:** Inferences per second
2. **Latency:** Time per inference
3. **Energy:** Joules per inference
4. **Array utilization:** Percentage of arrays active
5. **Memory bandwidth:** Data movement per inference

**Implementation:**
```go
type Benchmarker struct {
    Config  *OrchestrationConfig
    Results []*Benchmark
}

type Benchmark struct {
    Name          string
    TotalTime     time.Duration
    Throughput    float64  // inferences/sec
    AvgLatency    time.Duration
    EnergyPerInf  float64  // joules
    ArrayUtil     float64  // 0-1
    Accuracy      float64  // 0-1
}
```

### System Orchestrator

**Complete System Coordination:**
```go
type SystemOrchestrator struct {
    Config       *OrchestrationConfig
    ArrayManager *ArrayManager
    Pipeline     *ExecutionPipeline
    Benchmarker  *Benchmarker
    Stats        *SystemStats
}
```

**Workflow:**
1. Load model checkpoint
2. Partition layers into tiles
3. Assign tiles to arrays
4. Configure pipeline stages
5. Execute batched inference
6. Collect performance metrics

### Key Performance Numbers

| Metric | Value | Configuration |
|--------|-------|---------------|
| Array count | 16-64 | Typical system |
| Array size | 256×256 | Per crossbar |
| Pipeline depth | 4-8 stages | Layer count |
| Throughput | 100+ TOPS | 16-array system |
| Latency | 10-100 µs | Per inference |
| Energy efficiency | 10-100 TOPS/W | Depends on ADC |
| Array utilization | 70-90% | With pipelining |

## Section 128: orchestration.go Implementation

### File Overview

Created `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/orchestration.go` (~650 lines) implementing:

1. **ArrayManager:** Multi-crossbar array coordination
2. **ExecutionPipeline:** Pipelined layer execution
3. **WeightReplicator:** Weight duplication for load balancing
4. **PartialSumAccumulator:** Tile output combination
5. **Benchmarker:** Performance measurement utilities
6. **SystemOrchestrator:** Complete system coordination

### Core Types

```go
// Array configuration
type OrchestrationConfig struct {
    NumArrays     int
    ArrayRows     int
    ArrayCols     int
    PipelineDepth int
    BufferSize    int
    EnergyPerMAC  float64
    CycleTime     time.Duration
}

// Single crossbar array
type CrossbarArray struct {
    ID      int
    Rows    int
    Cols    int
    Weights [][]float64
    InUse   bool
}

// Layer-to-array mapping
type ArrayAssignment struct {
    ArrayID   int
    LayerIdx  int
    TileRow   int
    TileCol   int
    RowStart  int
    RowEnd    int
    ColStart  int
    ColEnd    int
}
```

### Pipeline Implementation

```go
type PipelineStage struct {
    ID         int
    LayerIdx   int
    Arrays     []*CrossbarArray
    InputBuf   *InterstageBuffer
    OutputBuf  *InterstageBuffer
    Active     bool
}

func (p *ExecutionPipeline) Execute(input []float64) []float64 {
    // Feed through pipeline stages
    data := input
    for _, stage := range p.Stages {
        if stage.Active {
            data = stage.Process(data)
        }
    }
    return data
}
```

### Benchmarking

```go
func (b *Benchmarker) BenchmarkInference(
    name string,
    model *ModelCheckpoint,
    inputs [][]float64,
    labels []int,
) *Benchmark {
    start := time.Now()

    correct := 0
    for i, input := range inputs {
        output := b.runInference(model, input)
        if argmax(output) == labels[i] {
            correct++
        }
    }

    elapsed := time.Since(start)
    return &Benchmark{
        Name:       name,
        TotalTime:  elapsed,
        Throughput: float64(len(inputs)) / elapsed.Seconds(),
        Accuracy:   float64(correct) / float64(len(inputs)),
        // ... energy calculations
    }
}
```

### Usage Example

```go
// Create orchestrator
config := &layers.OrchestrationConfig{
    NumArrays:     16,
    ArrayRows:     256,
    ArrayCols:     256,
    PipelineDepth: 4,
    BufferSize:    1024,
    EnergyPerMAC:  1e-12, // 1 pJ
    CycleTime:     10 * time.Nanosecond,
}

orchestrator := layers.NewSystemOrchestrator(config)

// Load and deploy model
model := layers.LoadCheckpoint("model.json")
orchestrator.DeployModel(model)

// Benchmark
results := orchestrator.Benchmark("MNIST Test", testInputs, testLabels)
fmt.Printf("Throughput: %.2f inf/s\n", results.Throughput)
fmt.Printf("Accuracy: %.2f%%\n", results.Accuracy*100)
```

## Section 129: ANN-to-SNN Conversion

### Overview

Converting trained Artificial Neural Networks to Spiking Neural Networks enables deploying deep learning models on neuromorphic hardware with ultra-low power consumption. Key techniques include threshold balancing, rate coding, and training-free conversion.

### Conversion Methods

**1. Threshold Balancing (Diehl et al., 2015):**
- Set firing threshold equal to maximum activation
- Normalize weights layer-by-layer
- Achieves near-lossless conversion
- Widely adopted baseline method

**2. Multistage Adaptive Threshold (MSAT, 2024):**
- Dynamic thresholds adapt to varying inputs
- Reduces information loss from constant thresholds
- Improves accuracy at low time steps
- Channel-wise thresholds for convolutional layers

**3. Training-Free Transformer Conversion (ICLR 2024):**
- Spatio-Temporal Approximation (STA) method
- First direct conversion of pretrained Transformers
- Converts ViT-B/32 from CLIP without fine-tuning
- Universal approximation in spatial and temporal domains

### Spike Coding Schemes

| Scheme | Description | Precision | Latency |
|--------|-------------|-----------|---------|
| Rate coding | Spike probability ∝ activation | High | High (many steps) |
| Temporal coding | Spike time ∝ 1/activation | Medium | Low (single spike) |
| Burst coding | Spike count ∝ activation | High | Medium |
| Phase coding | Spike phase encodes value | High | Medium |

### FeFET Device Support

**Ambipolar WSe₂ n/p-FeFET (ACS Nano 2024):**
- n-FeFET: Nonvolatile, linear synaptic behavior
- p-FeFET: Volatile, self-resetting LIF neuron
- Single device integrates synapse + neuron
- Eliminates need for separate circuits

**Anti-Ferroelectric Transistor Neuron (Nature Comms 2022):**
- Hf₀.₂Zr₀.₈O₂ AFeFET for integrate-and-fire
- Ultra-low energy: 37 fJ/spike
- High endurance: >10¹² cycles
- No capacitors or reset circuits needed

### Conversion Pipeline

```
ANN Model
    ↓
[Weight Scaling] ← Percentile-based (99.9%)
    ↓
[Threshold Computation] ← Max activation per layer
    ↓
[Layer-by-Layer Balancing] ← Scale factors propagate
    ↓
[Spike Encoder Selection] ← Rate/Temporal/Burst
    ↓
SNN Model
```

### Key Metrics

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Conversion accuracy gap | <1% (MNIST) | Threshold balancing |
| Time steps (rate coding) | 100-256 | Standard practice |
| Time steps (temporal) | 8-32 | Latency coding |
| AFeFET energy | 37 fJ/spike | Nature Comms 2022 |
| AFeFET endurance | >10¹² cycles | Hf₀.₂Zr₀.₈O₂ |
| Analog memcapacitor | 6.24 nJ/inference | MNIST |

---

## Section 130: Memory Technology Comparison

### Overview

Ferroelectric FETs compete with other emerging non-volatile memories (RRAM, PCM, MRAM) for analog compute-in-memory. Each technology has distinct trade-offs in endurance, precision, and energy.

### Technology Comparison

| Parameter | FeFET | RRAM | PCM | STT-MRAM | SRAM |
|-----------|-------|------|-----|----------|------|
| Endurance | 10¹²+ | 10⁶ | 10⁸ | 10¹⁵ | ∞ |
| Write energy | 10 fJ | 100 fJ | 10 pJ | 100 fJ | 1 fJ |
| Read energy | 1 fJ | 10 fJ | 100 fJ | 10 fJ | 1 fJ |
| Write time | 10 ns | 10 ns | 100 ns | 10 ns | 1 ns |
| On/Off ratio | 10⁴ | 10² | 10³ | 3 | 10⁶ |
| Analog levels | 64+ | 16 | 32 | 2 | 2 |
| Retention | 10 yr | 10 yr | 10 yr | 10 yr | 0 |
| Area factor | 0.2× | 0.1× | 0.15× | 0.3× | 1× |
| CIM suitability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |

### FeFET Advantages for CIM

1. **High endurance:** 10¹² cycles enables on-chip training
2. **Low write energy:** 10 fJ vs 10 pJ for PCM
3. **Fast switching:** 10 ns programming
4. **High on/off ratio:** 10⁴ for large conductance window
5. **Multi-level storage:** >6-bit demonstrated
6. **CMOS compatibility:** Same HfO₂ as HKMG transistors
7. **Three-terminal:** Better isolation than 2T memristors

### RRAM Limitations

- Limited endurance (10⁶ cycles) unsuitable for training
- Filament stochasticity causes cycle-to-cycle variation
- Sneak paths in crossbar require selector devices
- Asymmetric SET/RESET complicates weight updates

### PCM Considerations

- Thermal cross-talk in dense arrays
- Resistance drift over time
- High RESET current for melt-quench
- Slow crystallization for SET operation

### Recent Benchmarks (2024-2025)

| Implementation | Technology | Efficiency |
|----------------|------------|------------|
| REMNA MLC FeFET | 40nm HZO | 26.72 TOPS/W |
| Hybrid SLC-MLC PCM | 40nm GST | 20.5-65.0 TOPS/W |
| STT-MRAM CiM | 28nm | 22.4-41.5 TOPS/W |
| RRAM multimode | 55nm HfOx | 15-30 TOPS/W |

### Selection Guidelines

- **Inference-only:** RRAM (low cost, sufficient endurance)
- **On-chip training:** FeFET (high endurance, fast write)
- **High precision:** PCM (good analog resolution)
- **Binary networks:** MRAM (high speed, no analog needed)
- **Edge deployment:** FeFET (best energy efficiency)

## Section 131: training_loop.go Implementation

### File Overview

Created `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/training_loop.go` (~650 lines) implementing:

1. **ANNtoSNNConverter:** ANN-to-SNN conversion with threshold balancing
2. **SpikeEncoder:** Rate, temporal, and burst coding
3. **CIMTrainer:** Hardware-aware training with noise injection
4. **DFATrainer:** Direct Feedback Alignment
5. **HybridTrainer:** Off-chip pretrain + on-chip fine-tune
6. **EWCRegularizer:** Elastic Weight Consolidation for continual learning
7. **TrainingLoop:** Complete training pipeline
8. **MemoryCharacteristics:** NVM technology comparison

### ANN-to-SNN Conversion

```go
type ConversionConfig struct {
    TimeSteps        int
    ThresholdBalance bool
    PercentileScale  float64
    RateCoding       bool
    TemporalCoding   bool
    BurstCoding      bool
    AdaptiveThresh   bool
    ChannelWise      bool
}

func (c *ANNtoSNNConverter) Convert(layers [][]float64) (*SNNModel, error)
func (c *ANNtoSNNConverter) ThresholdBalance(model *SNNModel, calibrationData [][]float64)
```

### CIM-Aware Training

```go
type CIMTrainingConfig struct {
    NoiseLevel       float64 // Conductance variation
    QuantizationBits int
    ADCBits          int
    DACBits          int
    ClipMin, ClipMax float64
}

func (n *NoiseInjector) InjectNoise(weights [][]float64) [][]float64
func (n *NoiseInjector) InjectADCNoise(values []float64) []float64
func (n *NoiseInjector) InjectDACNoise(inputs []float64) []float64
```

### Direct Feedback Alignment

```go
type DFATrainer struct {
    Config           *DFAConfig
    FeedbackMatrices [][][]float64 // Fixed random B matrices
}

func (t *DFATrainer) ComputeGradient(layerIdx int, error []float64,
    activations []float64) [][]float64
```

### Memory Technology Comparison

```go
type MemoryTechnology int

const (
    MemoryFeFET MemoryTechnology = iota
    MemoryRRAM
    MemoryPCM
    MemoryMRAM
    MemorySRAM
)

func GetMemoryCharacteristics(tech MemoryTechnology) *MemoryCharacteristics
func CompareMemoryTechnologies() []*MemoryCharacteristics
```

### Usage Example

```go
// ANN-to-SNN conversion
convConfig := layers.DefaultConversionConfig()
converter := layers.NewANNtoSNNConverter(convConfig)
snnModel, _ := converter.Convert(annWeights)

// CIM-aware training
cimConfig := layers.DefaultCIMTrainingConfig()
trainer := layers.NewCIMTrainer(cimConfig)
noisyWeights := trainer.NoiseInjector.InjectNoise(weights)

// DFA training
dfaConfig := layers.DefaultDFAConfig()
dfaTrainer := layers.NewDFATrainer(dfaConfig, []int{784, 256, 10}, 10)
gradients := dfaTrainer.ComputeGradient(0, error, activations)

// Compare memory technologies
comparison := layers.CompareMemoryTechnologies()
for _, mem := range comparison {
    fmt.Printf("%s: %.0e endurance, %.1f TOPS/W suitability\n",
        mem.Name, mem.Endurance, mem.CIMSuitability)
}
```
