## 190. Emerging Ferroelectric Materials (Iteration 53)

### Material Comparison

| Property | HZO | AlScN | HZO Superlattice |
|----------|-----|-------|------------------|
| Pr (µC/cm²) | 15-40 | 80-150 | 45-60 |
| Ec (kV/cm) | 1000-2000 | 2000-5000 | 850 |
| Memory Window | ~1.5V | >3V | ~3.5V |
| Tc (°C) | ~450 | >1100 | ~500 |
| Endurance | 10¹⁰ | 10⁵ | 10¹⁰ |
| Min Thickness | 5nm | 10nm | 4nm |

### Wurtzite Ferroelectrics (AlScN)

Advantages over HZO:
- **3× higher Pr:** 80-150 vs 15-40 µC/cm²
- **Better noise immunity:** Higher Ec prevents random flipping
- **Superior thermal stability:** Tc >1100°C
- **Nearly square hysteresis:** Steep switching slope

Challenges:
- Higher Ec requires higher voltage
- Limited endurance (~10⁵ cycles)
- Newer, less mature technology

### AlScN Device Progress (2024-2025)

Recent achievements:
- 40nm FeDiode scaling with ON/OFF >60
- High-temperature operation to 350°C
- BEOL compatible (<400°C process)
- Integration with 2D channels (MoS₂, WSe₂)

Storage density: >100 Mbit/mm² demonstrated

### HZO Superlattice (Ferroelectric CIM Approach)

external research institution/Ferroelectric CIM technology:
- Alternating HfO₂/ZrO₂ layers
- Reduced coercive field (~0.85 MV/cm)
- Enhanced endurance (10¹⁰ cycles)
- Compatible with standard CMOS

Structure: 4 × [HfO₂ (10Å) / ZrO₂ (5Å)]

### Emerging Alternatives

| Material | Status | Advantage |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| AlBN | Research | Even higher Pr |
| Zr-doped HfO₂ | Production | Mature process |
| Antiferroelectric ZrO₂ | Research | NC-FET applications |
| Organic ferroelectrics | Research | Flexible electronics |

### FeFET Channel Materials

Traditional vs emerging:
| Channel | Advantage | Status |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| Si | Mature process | Production |
| IGZO | Low temp, transparent | Emerging |
| MoS₂ | 2D, BEOL compatible | Research |
| WSe₂ | p-type 2D | Research |

### References
- [AlScN Perspectives](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11173796/)
- [Wurtzite vs Fluorite](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41565-023-01361-y)
- [Next-gen Ferroelectrics](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/jap/article/129/10/100901/157224)

## 191. Neuromorphic and Device Module Implementation (Iteration 53)

### Added Package: `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/neuromorphic_devices.go`

Features:

### API Examples

#### Neuromorphic Processor

```go
// Configure Loihi 2
config := layers.Loihi2Config()
fmt.Printf("Neurons: %d, Synapses: %d\n", config.NumNeurons, config.NumSynapses)
fmt.Printf("Energy/spike: %.1f pJ\n", config.EnergyPerSpike)

// Or Hala Point
halaConfig := layers.HalaPointConfig()
fmt.Printf("Hala Point: %.2fB neurons, %d petaops\n",
    float64(halaConfig.NumNeurons)/1e9,
    int(halaConfig.SpeedupFactor))

// Create processor
proc := layers.NewNeuromorphicProcessor(config, 1000, 100)

// Inject input spikes
proc.InjectSpikes([]int{0, 5, 10, 15})

// Simulate steps
for i := 0; i < 1000; i++ {
    spikes := proc.Step()
    if spikes > 0 {
        fmt.Printf("Step %d: %d spikes\n", i, spikes)
    }
}

// Get metrics
metrics := proc.GetMetrics()
fmt.Printf("Total spikes: %.0f\n", metrics["TotalSpikes"])
fmt.Printf("Total energy: %.2f µJ\n", metrics["TotalEnergyPJ"]/1e6)
```

#### Ferroelectric Materials

```go
// Create materials
hzo := layers.NewHZOMaterial()
alscn := layers.NewAlScNMaterial()
superlattice := layers.NewHZOSuperlattice()

// Compare AlScN vs HZO
comparison := layers.CompareMaterials(alscn, hzo)
fmt.Printf("AlScN vs HZO:\n")
fmt.Printf("  Pr ratio: %.1fx\n", comparison.PrRatio)
fmt.Printf("  Ec ratio: %.1fx\n", comparison.EcRatio)
fmt.Printf("  Memory window ratio: %.1fx\n", comparison.MemoryWindowRatio)

// Ferroelectric CIM superlattice
fmt.Printf("\nFerroelectric CIM Superlattice:\n")
fmt.Printf("  Pr: %.0f µC/cm²\n", superlattice.RemanentPolarization)
fmt.Printf("  Ec: %.0f kV/cm (reduced)\n", superlattice.CoerciveField)
fmt.Printf("  Endurance: %.0e cycles\n", superlattice.EnduranceCycles)
```

#### FeFET Device

```go
// Create FeFET with HZO superlattice
material := layers.NewHZOSuperlattice()
config := layers.DefaultFeFETConfig(material)
device := layers.NewFeFETDevice(config)

// Program device
device.Program(1, 3.0)  // State 1 with +3V
fmt.Printf("Vth after program: %.2f V\n", device.ThresholdVoltage)

// Read device
current := device.Read(1.0, 0.1)  // Vgs=1V, Vds=0.1V
fmt.Printf("Drain current: %.2e A\n", current)

// On/off ratio
fmt.Printf("On/Off ratio: %.0e\n", device.GetOnOffRatio())
```

#### Device Benchmarking

```go
// Create benchmark
benchmark := layers.NewDeviceBenchmark()

// Add devices
materials := []*layers.FerroelectricMaterial{
    layers.NewHZOMaterial(),
    layers.NewAlScNMaterial(),
    layers.NewHZOSuperlattice(),
}

for _, mat := range materials {
    cfg := layers.DefaultFeFETConfig(mat)
    dev := layers.NewFeFETDevice(cfg)
    benchmark.AddDevice(mat.Name, dev)
}

// Run benchmarks
benchmark.RunBenchmark()

// Get rankings
rankings := benchmark.GetRankings("memory_window")
fmt.Println("Memory window ranking:", rankings)

rankings = benchmark.GetRankings("endurance")
fmt.Println("Endurance ranking:", rankings)

// Print detailed results
for name, m := range benchmark.Measurements {
    fmt.Printf("%s:\n", name)
    fmt.Printf("  Write: %.0f ns, %.1f fJ\n", m.WriteLatency, m.WriteEnergy)
    fmt.Printf("  Read: %.0f ns, %.2f fJ\n", m.ReadLatency, m.ReadEnergy)
    fmt.Printf("  Endurance: %.0e cycles\n", m.Endurance)
}
```

### Key Implementation Details

#### Spiking Neuron Model (LIF)
```
V(t+dt) = V(t) × (1 - leak) + I_input
if V > threshold:
    spike!
    V = V_reset
```

#### Ferroelectric Switching
```
P = tanh(V/Vprogram × 3) × Pr
Vth = Vth0 + P/Pr × MW/2
```

#### Material Trade-offs
```
AlScN: High Pr (>3×) but higher voltage, lower endurance
HZO: Mature, good endurance, moderate performance
Superlattice: Best of both - good Pr, low Ec, high endurance
```

## Section 192: Compute-in-Memory for LLM Inference

### The LLM Memory Bottleneck

Large language models spend 70-80% of inference energy on attention computations due to:
1. **KV Cache Growth:** Linear with sequence length × batch size
2. **Memory Bandwidth:** Weight loading dominates latency
3. **Quadratic Attention:** Self-attention scales O(n²) with context length

### CIM Solutions for LLMs

#### HARDSEA Architecture (2024)
- Hierarchical Attention via RRAm + SRAM + Digital REA
- **3D-CIMlet:** Stacked CIM arrays for parallel attention heads
- **23-1894× efficiency** improvement vs GPU baselines
- **28.5× speedup** for attention operations

#### Key CIM-LLM Optimizations
1. **Weight Stationary:** Keep attention weights in-memory
2. **KV Cache Partitioning:** Distribute across multiple arrays
3. **Sliding Window:** Reduce KV cache requirements
4. **Mixed Precision:** INT4 weights with higher precision accumulation

### Energy Breakdown

| Component | Percentage | CIM Impact |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| QK^T Multiply | 35% | 10-100× reduction |
| Softmax | 15% | Digital domain |
| V Multiply | 30% | 10-100× reduction |
| FFN | 20% | Crossbar accelerated |

### CIM Array Sizing for LLMs

For LLaMA-7B (4096 hidden, 32 heads):
- **Per-head dimension:** 128
- **KV cache per layer:** 2 × 128 × seq_length
- **Attention weights:** 4 × 4096 × 4096 = 67M parameters/layer

Array mapping strategies:
```
Standard: 64×64 arrays → ~16K elements/array
Tiled: 32 layers × 4 projections × ~4K arrays = ~500K arrays
With folding: 8× reduction → ~60K arrays total
```

---

## Section 193: FeFET Reliability and Aging Simulation

### Degradation Mechanisms

#### 1. Endurance Degradation
| Technology | Endurance | Failure Mode |
|------------|-----------|--------------|
| NAND Flash | 10³-10⁴ | Oxide breakdown |
| RRAM | 10⁶-10⁹ | Filament degradation |
| Si:HfO₂ FeFET | 10⁴-10⁶ | Charge trapping |
| HZO FeFET | 10⁸-10¹⁰ | Wake-up/fatigue |
| HZO Superlattice | 10¹⁰-10¹² | Interface effects |

#### 2. Charge Trapping Physics

```
Charge trapping rate:
dQ_trap/dt = α × V × exp(-Ea/kT) - Q_trap/τ_detrap

Where:
- α: Trapping coefficient (~10⁻³)
- Ea: Activation energy (~0.3 eV for HZO)
- τ_detrap: Detrapping time constant
```

#### 3. Fatigue Mechanisms

Polarization fatigue in ferroelectrics:
```
P(N) = P₀ × (1 - A × log(N/N₀))

Where:
- N: Cycle count
- A: Fatigue coefficient (~0.01-0.05)
- N₀: Onset cycles (~10³)
```

### Memory Window Evolution

```
MW(cycles) = MW₀ × exp(-cycles/τ_endurance)

Initial MW: 0.8-1.2V (HZO)
After 10⁸ cycles: ~70-80% retention
After 10¹⁰ cycles: ~50-60% retention (superlattice)
```

### Retention Modeling

Arrhenius temperature dependence:
```
τ_retention = τ₀ × exp(Ea_ret / kT)

HZO parameters:
- τ₀: ~10⁻¹⁰ seconds
- Ea_ret: 0.6-1.0 eV
- At 85°C: >10 years retention
- At 125°C: >1 year retention
```

### Failure Modes

| Mode | Cause | Mitigation |
|------|-------|------------|
| Stuck-at | Oxide breakdown | Redundancy |
| Drift | Charge accumulation | Refresh |
| Early fatigue | Interface defects | Screening |
| Window closure | Domain pinning | Superlattice |

---

## Section 194: LLM Reliability Module Implementation

### llm_reliability.go Overview (~650 lines)

Created comprehensive module for LLM CIM acceleration and reliability:

```go
// LLM CIM configuration
type LLMCIMConfig struct {
    ModelName       string   // "llama-7b", "gpt-2", etc.
    HiddenDim       int      // Model hidden dimension
    NumHeads        int      // Attention heads
    NumLayers       int      // Transformer layers
    MaxSeqLength    int      // Maximum sequence length
    HeadDim         int      // Per-head dimension
    ArraySize       int      // CIM array size
    WeightBits      int      // Weight quantization
    CacheStrategy   string   // "full", "sliding_window", "sparse"
    WindowSize      int      // For sliding window
}

// Default configurations
func LLaMA7BConfig() *LLMCIMConfig {
    return &LLMCIMConfig{
        ModelName:    "llama-7b",
        HiddenDim:    4096,
        NumHeads:     32,
        NumLayers:    32,
        MaxSeqLength: 4096,
        HeadDim:      128,
        ArraySize:    64,
        WeightBits:   4,
    }
}
```

### KV Cache Implementation

```go
type KVCache struct {
    Config       *LLMCIMConfig
    KeyCache     [][][]float64  // [layer][seq][head_dim]
    ValueCache   [][][]float64
    CurrentSeq   int
    KeyArrays    []*CIMArray
    ValueArrays  []*CIMArray
    WriteEnergy  float64  // pJ
    ReadEnergy   float64  // pJ
}

func (c *KVCache) Append(layer int, key, value []float64) error {
    // Validate dimensions
    if len(key) != c.Config.HeadDim*c.Config.NumHeads {
        return fmt.Errorf("key dimension mismatch")
    }

    // Append to cache
    c.KeyCache[layer] = append(c.KeyCache[layer], key)
    c.ValueCache[layer] = append(c.ValueCache[layer], value)
    c.CurrentSeq++

    // Update CIM arrays
    c.updateArrays(layer, key, value)

    // Track energy
    c.WriteEnergy += c.calculateWriteEnergy()
    return nil
}
```

### CIM Attention Computation

```go
type CIMArray struct {
    Size        int
    Weights     [][]float64
    ADCBits     int
    Noise       float64
    ReadCycles  int64
    WriteCycles int64
}

func (a *CIMArray) MatVecMul(input []float64) []float64 {
    output := make([]float64, a.Size)

    for i := 0; i < a.Size; i++ {
        sum := 0.0
        for j, v := range input {
            sum += a.Weights[i][j] * v
        }
        // Add noise
        sum += (rand.Float64()*2 - 1) * a.Noise * sum
        // Quantize
        output[i] = a.quantize(sum)
    }

    a.ReadCycles++
    return output
}
```

### LLM Accelerator

```go
type LLMAccelerator struct {
    Config      *LLMCIMConfig
    KVCaches    []*KVCache      // Per-layer caches
    QKVArrays   [][]*CIMArray   // Q, K, V projection arrays
    FFNArrays   [][]*CIMArray   // FFN weight arrays
    TotalOps    int64
    TotalEnergy float64  // pJ
}

func (acc *LLMAccelerator) GenerateToken(
    embeddings []float64,
) ([]float64, *TokenStats) {
    stats := &TokenStats{}

    for layer := 0; layer < acc.Config.NumLayers; layer++ {
        // Self-attention with KV cache
        attnOut, attnEnergy := acc.selfAttention(layer, embeddings)
        stats.AttentionEnergy += attnEnergy

        // FFN
        ffnOut, ffnEnergy := acc.feedForward(layer, attnOut)
        stats.FFNEnergy += ffnEnergy

        embeddings = ffnOut
    }

    stats.TotalEnergy = stats.AttentionEnergy + stats.FFNEnergy
    return embeddings, stats
}
```

### Reliability Modeling

```go
type ReliabilityConfig struct {
    EnduranceCycles    float64  // Max write cycles
    RetentionYears     float64  // At reference temp
    FatigueCoeff       float64  // Polarization loss rate
    TrapRate           float64  // Charge trapping rate
    RefreshInterval    int      // Cycles between refresh
    RedundancyLevel    int      // ECC redundancy
}

type DeviceState struct {
    Config          *ReliabilityConfig
    CycleCount      int64
    TrappedCharge   float64
    PolarizationLoss float64
    MemoryWindow    float64
    Failed          bool
    FailureMode     string
}

func (d *DeviceState) Update(writeOp bool) {
    if writeOp {
        d.CycleCount++

        // Charge trapping
        d.TrappedCharge += d.Config.TrapRate *
            (1 - d.TrappedCharge)

        // Polarization fatigue
        if d.CycleCount > 1000 {
            d.PolarizationLoss += d.Config.FatigueCoeff *
                math.Log10(float64(d.CycleCount)/1000)
        }

        // Memory window degradation
        d.MemoryWindow *= math.Exp(-1.0/d.Config.EnduranceCycles)

        // Check failure
        if d.MemoryWindow < 0.3 {
            d.Failed = true
            d.FailureMode = "window_closure"
        }
    }
}
```

### Array-Level Reliability

```go
type ArrayReliability struct {
    Config       *ReliabilityConfig
    Devices      [][]*DeviceState
    FailedCount  int
    TotalDevices int
    YieldRate    float64
}

func (ar *ArrayReliability) SimulateLifetime(
    writesPerCycle int,
    totalCycles int,
) *LifetimeReport {
    report := &LifetimeReport{}

    for cycle := 0; cycle < totalCycles; cycle++ {
        // Random write pattern
        for i := 0; i < writesPerCycle; i++ {
            row := rand.Intn(len(ar.Devices))
            col := rand.Intn(len(ar.Devices[0]))
            ar.Devices[row][col].Update(true)

            if ar.Devices[row][col].Failed {
                ar.FailedCount++
            }
        }

        ar.YieldRate = 1 - float64(ar.FailedCount)/
            float64(ar.TotalDevices)

        if cycle%1000 == 0 {
            report.YieldHistory = append(
                report.YieldHistory,
                ar.YieldRate,
            )
        }
    }

    return report
}
```

### Usage Example

```go
// Configure LLM accelerator
config := layers.LLaMA7BConfig()
config.CacheStrategy = "sliding_window"
config.WindowSize = 2048

acc := layers.NewLLMAccelerator(config)

// Configure reliability
relConfig := &layers.ReliabilityConfig{
    EnduranceCycles: 1e10,
    RetentionYears:  10,
    FatigueCoeff:    0.02,
    TrapRate:        0.001,
}

// Generate tokens with reliability tracking
for i := 0; i < numTokens; i++ {
    output, stats := acc.GenerateToken(embeddings)

    fmt.Printf("Token %d: Energy=%.1f pJ, Attention=%.1f%%\n",
        i, stats.TotalEnergy,
        stats.AttentionEnergy/stats.TotalEnergy*100)

    // Update reliability state
    acc.UpdateReliability(stats.WriteOps)
}

// Check array health
health := acc.GetArrayHealth()
fmt.Printf("Array yield: %.4f%%\n", health.YieldRate*100)
fmt.Printf("Mean cycles: %.2e\n", health.MeanCycleCount)
```

## Section 195: CIM for Diffusion Model Acceleration

### The Diffusion Compute Challenge

Diffusion models face unique hardware deployment challenges:
1. **Iterative Denoising:** 20-1000 timesteps of U-Net inference
2. **Variable Precision Needs:** Early steps need high precision, late steps tolerable
3. **Attention Bottleneck:** Self-attention at multiple resolutions
4. **Memory Bandwidth:** Large intermediate activations

### AIG-CIM Architecture (DAC 2024)

**Tri-Gear Heterogeneous CIM** addresses flexible data reuse:

| Gear | Data Reuse | Precision | Use Case |
|------|------------|-----------|----------|
| Fine | 1× | High | Early denoising (high noise) |
| Medium | 2× | Medium | Middle timesteps |
| Coarse | 4× | Lower | Late denoising (low noise) |

**Performance Results (TSMC 22nm):**
- **21.3× latency reduction** vs RTX 3090
- **231.2× throughput improvement**
- **1000× energy efficiency gain**

### Denoising Similarity Optimization

Consecutive denoising steps share significant similarity:
```
Pixel similarity: ~85-95% between adjacent steps
Weight reuse potential: High in late timesteps
Activation caching: Reduces redundant computation
```

### U-Net CIM Mapping

```
Encoder (progressively lower resolution):
  64×64 → 32×32 → 16×16 → 8×8
  Each block: ResBlocks + AttentionBlocks

Middle Block:
  8×8 resolution, heaviest attention

Decoder (progressively higher resolution):
  8×8 → 16×16 → 32×32 → 64×64
  Skip connections from encoder

CIM allocation:
  - ResBlocks: Weight-stationary SRAM CIM
  - Attention: Flash/RRAM for KV storage
  - Cross-attention: Dedicated macros
```

### Mixed Quantization Strategy

From ISSCC 2024 Tsinghua work:
- **Booth-8 multiplier** for high-precision paths
- **Balanced INT/FP** processing
- **Per-layer quantization** based on sensitivity

---

## Section 196: Cryogenic Ferroelectric Computing

### Ferroelectric SQUID (FeSQUID)

Combines ferroelectric non-volatility with SQUID sensitivity:

**Operating Principle:**
```
Ferroelectric polarization → Surface charge modulation
Surface charge → Superconducting critical temperature change
Tc change → SQUID critical current modulation

Result: 54% critical current change between states
```

**Key Specifications:**
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Operating temperature | 4 K |
| Energy per search | 1.36 aJ (binary) |
| Energy per ternary search | 26.5 aJ |
| 10,000-bit comparison | 89.4 fJ |
| vs Cryo SRAM | >10× lower energy |

### FeSQUID TCAM for Quantum Error Correction

Ternary Content-Addressable Memory enables:
1. **Parallel syndrome matching:** All rows searched simultaneously
2. **Hamming distance calculation:** Analog voltage output
3. **Error detection:** Fast decoding for surface codes

Applications:
- Quantum error correction decoding
- Approximate search algorithms
- AI classification in cryogenic environment

### Ferroelectric Josephson Junction (FeJJ)

**S-I-FE-I-S Structure:**
```
Superconductor | Insulator1 | Ferroelectric | Insulator2 | Superconductor

Key: Asymmetric barriers break inversion symmetry
Polarization reversal → Critical current modulation
```

**Performance (2025 Delft research):**
- **On-off efficiency:** Up to 0.9 (90%)
- **Switching:** Non-volatile, purely electrical
- **Temperature:** <1 K operation
- **Application:** Cryogenic memory and logic

**Optimization Trade-offs:**
```
Higher efficiency requires:
- Asymmetric insulator thicknesses
- Thicker ferroelectric films
- Lower FE dielectric constant

Cost: Reduced absolute current magnitude
```

### HZO Photonic Phase Shifter

**Nature Communications 2024** demonstrated:
- **Material:** 30nm HZO (3×10nm + Al₂O₃ interlayers)
- **Waveguide:** 4.5mm SiN at 1.55μm
- **Phase shift:** ~π achieved
- **Refractive change:** -1.5×10⁻⁴
- **Retention:** >10,000 seconds (demonstrated)
- **States:** ~10 intermediate levels

**Applications:**
- Programmable photonic circuits
- Optical matrix weights for inference
- Phase error correction

#### Diffusion Model CIM Acceleration

```go
// Diffusion model configuration
type DiffusionConfig struct {
    ModelName        string  // "stable_diffusion", "sdxl", "dit"
    ImageSize        int     // Output resolution
    LatentSize       int     // Latent space size
    NumChannels      int     // U-Net channels
    NumTimesteps     int     // Denoising steps
    UseTriGear       bool    // Tri-gear heterogeneous CIM
    ExploitSimilarity bool   // Denoising similarity optimization
}

// Tri-gear CIM macro (AIG-CIM style)
type TriGearMacro struct {
    Mode         string   // "fine", "medium", "coarse"
    DataReuse    float64  // Reuse factor (1×, 2×, 4×)
    Weights      [][]float64
    EnergyPJ     float64
}

func (m *TriGearMacro) SetGear(mode string) {
    m.Mode = mode
    switch mode {
    case "fine":   m.DataReuse = 1.0
    case "medium": m.DataReuse = 2.0
    case "coarse": m.DataReuse = 4.0
    }
}
```

    result.Matches[0], result.EnergyAJ)

// FeJJ memory
fejjCfg := layers.DefaultFeJJConfig()
mem := layers.NewFeJJMemory(fejjCfg, 64, 64)
mem.Write(0, []bool{true, false, true, true})
data := mem.Read(0)

## Section 198: Neuromorphic Vision Sensors

### Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS) / Event Cameras

Event cameras operate fundamentally differently from frame-based sensors:

**Operating Principle:**
```
Each pixel: Continuous log intensity monitoring
Change detected: |log(I_new) - log(I_ref)| > threshold
Event generated: (x, y, timestamp, polarity)
Asynchronous: Per-pixel independent operation
```

**Key Specifications:**
| Metric | DVS | Traditional |
|--------|-----|-------------|
| Temporal resolution | 1 μs | 16 ms (60fps) |
| Dynamic range | 120 dB | 60 dB |
| Power consumption | ~10 mW | ~500 mW |
| Data efficiency | 100× less | Baseline |
| Latency | 100 μs | 16 ms |

**Event Representation Formats:**
1. **Time Surfaces:** Store recent timestamps with exponential decay
2. **Voxel Grids:** 3D spatiotemporal discretization
3. **Event Frames:** Accumulate events over adaptive windows
4. **Motion Compensation:** Align events by camera motion

### Ferroelectric HZO Neuromorphic Photodetector (2025)

**Three Operating Modes in Single Pixel:**

| Mode | Mechanism | Application |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Event | Self-powered spikes (~73 μs) | Motion detection |
| Static | Steady-state photocurrent | Imaging |
| Memory | Programmable STM decay | Temporal patterns |

**Architecture:**
```
p-Si substrate (photoactive)
HfZrO2 stack (~6 nm ferroelectric)
Bias-controlled mode switching:
  V < -2V: Event mode (spikes)
  V > +2V: Static mode (photocurrent)
  |V| < 2V: Memory mode (STM)
```

**Demonstrated Performance:**
- **Spatiotemporal classification:** >93% accuracy
- **Patterns:** Gestures, logic sequences, Morse code
- **On-chip inference:** FPGA-coupled learning
- **Motion prediction:** Real-time tracking

### Integration with CIM Accelerators

Event-based processing pipelines:
```
Event Camera → Event Buffer → Feature Extraction → CIM Inference
                              (Time surfaces)     (Crossbar arrays)

Benefits:
- Sparse data → Reduced memory traffic
- Asynchronous → Natural SNN mapping
- Low latency → Real-time response
```

---

## Section 199: Multi-Chip CIM Interconnect

### UCIe 3.0 Specification (August 2025)

**Key Improvements over UCIe 2.0:**

| Feature | UCIe 2.0 | UCIe 3.0 |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Data rate | 32 GT/s | 64 GT/s |
| Bandwidth density | 10 Tbps/mm | >20 Tbps/mm |
| Sideband reach | 50 mm | 100 mm |
| Recalibration | Manual | Runtime |
| Raw mode | Limited | Full support |

**Architecture Features:**
```
Die-to-die links:
- AXI-based router network
- Priority sideband messaging
- Fast-throttle emergency shutdown
- Circular buffer transport

Applications:
- AI accelerator scale-up
- GPU-class bandwidth
- HBM4 memory interface
- Co-packaged optics (CPO)
```

### Network-on-Package (NoP) for CIM

**Topology Options:**

| Topology | Connectivity | Latency | Power | Best For |
|----------|-------------|---------|-------|----------|
| Mesh | 4 neighbors | O(√N) | Low | Scalable |
| Ring | 2 neighbors | O(N) | Lowest | Small N |
| Crossbar | Full | O(1) | High | Low latency |

**3×3 CIM Mesh Example:**
```
[C0]--[C1]--[C2]
  |     |     |
[C3]--[C4]--[C5]
  |     |     |
[C6]--[C7]--[C8]

Each chiplet: 64 CIM arrays (64×64)
Total capacity: 9 × 64 = 576 arrays
Aggregate compute: ~144 TOPS
UCIe 3.0 links: 20+ Tbps aggregate
```

### CIM Dataflow Optimization

**Strategies for Multi-Chip Systems:**

1. **Weight Stationary:** Weights stay in arrays
   - Minimize weight transfer
   - Stream activations
   - Best for inference

2. **Output Stationary:** Outputs accumulate locally
   - Reduce partial sum traffic
   - Good for batch processing

3. **Row Stationary:** Balance input/weight reuse
   - Optimal for CNNs
   - Complex scheduling

**Tiling and Mapping:**
```
Layer Shape → Tile Partitioning → Chiplet Assignment → Scheduling

Optimization targets:
- Minimize inter-chiplet traffic
- Maximize array utilization
- Balance load across chiplets
- Pipeline computation and communication
```

**Performance Metrics:**
```
Crossbar topology:
- Throughput: 213.8 TeraOps/s
- Connectivity: Full mesh
- Power: 2.3× higher (vs mesh)

Mesh topology:
- Throughput: 150 TeraOps/s
- Scalability: Excellent
- Power: Baseline
```

---

## Section 200: Vision & Interconnect Module Implementation

### vision_interconnect.go Overview (~850 lines)

Created comprehensive module covering:

#### Event Camera / DVS

```go
// Event camera configuration
type EventCameraConfig struct {
    ResolutionX      int       // 320-1280
    ResolutionY      int
    ThresholdPos     float64   // Log intensity threshold
    ThresholdNeg     float64
    TemporalResUS    float64   // 1 μs
    DynamicRangeDB   float64   // 120 dB
    PowerMW          float64   // ~10 mW
}

// Individual event
type Event struct {
    X         int
    Y         int
    Timestamp float64   // Microseconds
    Polarity  int       // +1 (ON) or -1 (OFF)
}

// Pixel with threshold detection
func (p *EventPixel) Update(intensity float64, timestamp float64) *Event {
    newLog := math.Log(intensity + 1e-6)
    diff := newLog - p.RefIntensity

    if diff > p.Config.ThresholdPos {
        return &Event{X: p.X, Y: p.Y, Polarity: 1}
    } else if diff < -p.Config.ThresholdNeg {
        return &Event{X: p.X, Y: p.Y, Polarity: -1}
    }
    return nil
}
```

#### Ferroelectric Photodetector

```go
// FE photodetector with three modes
type FEPhotodetectorConfig struct {
    HZOThickness    float64   // ~6 nm
    EventSpikeUS    float64   // ~73 μs
    STMDecayMS      float64   // 10 ms decay
    StaticMode      bool
    EventMode       bool
    MemoryMode      bool
}

func (p *FEPixel) SetBias(bias float64) {
    if bias < -2.0 {
        p.OperatingMode = "event"   // Self-powered spikes
    } else if bias > 2.0 {
        p.OperatingMode = "static"  // Photocurrent
    } else {
        p.OperatingMode = "memory"  // STM
    }
}

// Spatiotemporal classification (~93% accuracy)
func (d *FEPhotodetector) ClassifySpatiotemporal(
    patterns [][][]float64,
) (int, float64)
```

#### UCIe 3.0 Interconnect

```go
// UCIe 3.0 configuration
type UCIeConfig struct {
    Version          string    // "3.0"
    DataRateGTs      float64   // 64 GT/s
    BandwidthDensity float64   // >20 Tbps/mm
    SidebandReachMM  float64   // 100 mm
    Lanes            int       // 16 standard
    LinkLatencyNS    float64   // ~2 ns
}

// Die-to-die link
type ChipletLink struct {
    Config      *UCIeConfig
    SourceID    int
    DestID      int
    Bandwidth   float64   // Gbps
    Utilization float64
}

func (l *ChipletLink) Transfer(bytes int64) float64 {
    bits := bytes * 8
    transferTimeNS := float64(bits) / l.Bandwidth
    return transferTimeNS + l.Config.LinkLatencyNS
}
```

#### Network-on-Package

    NumChiplets     int       // 9 (3×3 mesh)

    ArraySize       int       // 64×64
}

}

    events := camera.ProcessFrame(frame, timestamp)

## Section 201: Photonic/Optical CIM Accelerators

### Silicon Photonics for AI Acceleration

Photonic AI accelerators operate at speed of light with minimal energy loss:

**Key Advantages:**
```
vs Electronic Accelerators:
- 1-3 orders of magnitude higher compute density
- 1-3 orders of magnitude better energy efficiency
- Native matrix-vector multiplication
- Near-zero dynamic power (weights in phase shifters)
```

### Architecture Comparison

| Architecture | Mechanism | Compute Density | Efficiency | Scalability |
|-------------|-----------|-----------------|------------|-------------|
| MZI Mesh | Phase interference | Medium | High | Challenging |
| MRR Weight Bank | Resonance modulation | Very High | Very High | Good |
| PCM Crossbar | Phase-change absorption | High | Medium | Good |

### Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) Mesh

**Operating Principle:**
```
Input beam → 50:50 splitter → Phase shift θ → Recombine → Output
T = cos²(θ/2) × exp(iφ)

SVD Decomposition: W = U × Σ × V^T
- U, V^T: Unitary matrices (MZI meshes)
- Σ: Diagonal (MRR or attenuators)
```

**Clements Architecture:**
- N×N unitary requires N(N-1)/2 MZIs
- 128×128 array: ~8,000 MZIs
- Insertion loss: ~0.05 dB per MZI

### Microring Resonator (MRR) Weight Banks

**Operating Principle:**
```
Lorentzian Response:
T = 1 - a²/(1 + (2Δλ/FWHM)²)

Weight encoding:
- Thermal tuning shifts resonance
- Detuning controls transmission
- Multi-level analog weights
```

**Performance (64×64 @ 25 GHz):**
- Compute density: **880 TOPS/mm²**
- Energy efficiency: **5.1 TOPS/W**
- Area per MAC: ~30×30 μm²

### Lightmatter Envise (2025)

**Specifications:**
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Architecture | 4 × 128×128 MZI tensor cores |
| Integration | 6 chips, 50B transistors, 1M photonics |
| Throughput | 65.5 TOPS (ABFP16) |
| Power | 78W electrical + 1.6W optical |
| Efficiency | 0.82 TOPS/W |
| Models | ResNet, BERT (no fine-tuning) |

**Significance:**
- First commercial photonic AI accelerator
- Native PyTorch/TensorFlow support
- ~32-bit accuracy without quantization-aware training

---

## Section 202: HBM4 Memory Architecture

### JEDEC HBM4 Specification (April 2025)

**Key Improvements over HBM3E:**

| Feature | HBM3E | HBM4 | HBM4E |
|---------|-------|------|-------|
| Interface width | 1024-bit | 2048-bit | 2048-bit |
| Channels | 16 × 64-bit | 32 × 64-bit | 32 × 64-bit |
| Data rate | 9.2 GT/s | 8.0 GT/s | 12.0 GT/s |
| Bandwidth/stack | 1.2 TB/s | 2.0 TB/s | 3.0 TB/s |
| Capacity/stack | 24 GB | 48 GB | 64 GB |

### Custom Logic Die - Key Innovation

HBM4 introduces **co-designed custom logic dies**:

```
Traditional HBM: Memory manufacturer → passive memory
HBM4: Memory + AI company co-design → active compute

SK Hynix: TSMC 12nm logic die
Samsung: Samsung 4nm logic die (turnkey)
```

**Near-Memory Compute Capabilities:**
- Gather/scatter operations
- Reduction operations
- Prefetch optimization
- Custom preprocessing

### 2048-bit Interface Architecture

```
2048 bits = 32 channels × 64 bits
         = 64 pseudo-channels × 32 bits

Per-stack at 8 GT/s:
  2048 × 8 Gb/s = 16.4 Tbps = 2.0 TB/s

8-stack system:
  8 × 2.0 TB/s = 16 TB/s aggregate
```

### AI Accelerator Integration

**Typical AI Accelerator Config:**
```
8 × HBM4 stacks:
- Total capacity: 384 GB
- Total bandwidth: 16 TB/s
- Interface: UCIe + silicon interposer

Power envelope:
- Core: 1.0-1.05V
- I/O: 0.7-0.9V
- ~100W total memory power
```

### RAS (Reliability, Availability, Serviceability)

HBM4 enhancements:
- Enhanced ECC
- Directed Refresh Management (row hammer)
- Per-channel thermal telemetry
- Voltage telemetry

## Section 203: Optical & HBM4 Module Implementation

### optical_hbm4.go Overview (~800 lines)

Created comprehensive module covering:

#### MZI Mesh Architecture

```go
// MZI element with phase shifts
type MZIElement struct {
    PhaseShift1    float64   // Internal phase (θ)
    PhaseShift2    float64   // External phase (φ)
    InsertionLoss  float64   // dB loss
    Transmission   float64
}

func (m *MZIElement) SetWeight(weight float64) {
    // Map weight to phase shifts (SVD scheme)
    m.PhaseShift1 = math.Acos(math.Abs(weight)) * 2
    if weight < 0 {
        m.PhaseShift2 = math.Pi
    }
}

func (m *MZIElement) Compute(in1, in2 float64) (float64, float64) {
    // Beam splitter + phase shift model
    cos_t := math.Cos(m.PhaseShift1 / 2)
    sin_t := math.Sin(m.PhaseShift1 / 2)
    out1 := cos_t*in1 + sin_t*in2*math.Cos(m.PhaseShift2)
    out2 := -sin_t*in1 + cos_t*in2*math.Cos(m.PhaseShift2)
    return out1, out2
}

// Clements-style rectangular mesh
type MZIMesh struct {
    Elements  [][]*MZIElement
    Size      int
    TotalLoss float64
}
```

#### Microring Resonator Weight Bank

```go
type MRRElement struct {
    Radius        float64   // Ring radius (μm)
    Coupling      float64   // Coupling coefficient
    Resonance     float64   // Resonance wavelength (nm)
    Detuning      float64   // Thermal detuning
    Transmission  float64
}

func (m *MRRElement) SetWeight(weight float64) {
    m.Detuning = weight * 2.0
    // Lorentzian response
    fwhm := 0.5
    lorentzian := 1.0 / (1 + math.Pow(2*m.Detuning/fwhm, 2))
    m.Transmission = 1 - 0.9*lorentzian
}

type MRRWeightBank struct {
    Resonators []*MRRElement
    Size       int
}

func (wb *MRRWeightBank) DotProduct(input []float64) float64 {
    sum := 0.0
    for i, r := range wb.Resonators {
        sum += r.Multiply(input[i])
    }
    return sum
}
```

#### Photonic Tensor Core (SVD Architecture)

```go
type PhotonicTensorCore struct {
    Config        *PhotonicConfig
    MZIMeshU      *MZIMesh          // U matrix
    MZIMeshV      *MZIMesh          // V^T matrix
    SingularMRRs  *MRRWeightBank    // Σ diagonal
}

// SVD: y = U × Σ × V^T × x
func (tc *PhotonicTensorCore) MatVecMul(input []float64) []float64 {
    // Step 1: V^T × x
    vOut := tc.MZIMeshV.Forward(input)

    // Step 2: Σ × (V^T × x)
    scaled := make([]float64, len(vOut))
    for i, v := range vOut {
        scaled[i] = tc.SingularMRRs.Resonators[i].Multiply(v)
    }

    // Step 3: U × (Σ × V^T × x)
    return tc.MZIMeshU.Forward(scaled)
}
```

#### HBM4 Memory System

```go
type HBM4Config struct {
    Generation       string    // "HBM4", "HBM4E"
    NumStacks        int       // 8 typical
    LayersPerStack   int       // 12 or 16
    CapacityGB       int       // 48-64 GB/stack
    InterfaceWidth   int       // 2048 bits
    NumChannels      int       // 32
    DataRateGbps     float64   // 8.0-12.0 GT/s
    BandwidthTBs     float64   // 2.0-3.0 TB/s
    CustomLogicNode  string    // "12nm" or "4nm"
}

type HBM4Stack struct {
    Channels  []*HBM4Channel
    LogicDie  *HBM4LogicDie     // Custom compute die
}

type HBM4LogicDie struct {
    ProcessNode    string
    ComputeEnabled bool
    CacheKB        int
    PreprocessOps  []string   // "gather", "scatter", "reduce"
}

func (ld *HBM4LogicDie) ProcessData(bytes int64) float64 {
    // Near-memory preprocessing
    latencyPerBlock := 1.0   // 12nm
    if ld.ProcessNode == "4nm" {
        latencyPerBlock = 0.5
    }
    return float64(blocks) * latencyPerBlock
}
```

#### Integrated Photonic + HBM4 System

```go
type PhotonicHBM4System struct {
    Accelerator  *PhotonicAccelerator
    Memory       *HBM4System
}

func (sys *PhotonicHBM4System) ExecuteInference(
    inputs [][]float64,
    weights [][][]float64,
) (*InferenceResult, error) {
    for layer, w := range weights {
        // Load weights from HBM4
        memResult := sys.Memory.Read(address, weightBytes)

        // Execute on photonic accelerator
        output, stats := sys.Accelerator.ExecuteLayer(input, w)
    }
}
```

### Usage Examples

```go
// Lightmatter Envise-style accelerator
acc := layers.EnviseAccelerator()
output, stats := acc.ExecuteLayer(input, weights)
fmt.Printf("TOPS: %.1f, TOPS/W: %.2f\n", stats.TOPS, stats.TOPSW)
// Output: TOPS: 262.1, TOPS/W: 3.28

// HBM4 memory system
mem := layers.DefaultHBM4System()
result := mem.Read(0, 1024*1024)
fmt.Printf("Bandwidth: %.2f TB/s, Latency: %.1f ns\n",
    result.Bandwidth, result.LatencyNS)
// Output: Bandwidth: 2.00 TB/s, Latency: 10.0 ns

// Integrated system
sys := layers.NewPhotonicHBM4System()
result, _ := sys.ExecuteInference(inputs, weights)
fmt.Printf("Effective: %.1f TOPS, %.2f TOPS/W\n",
    result.EffectiveTOPS, result.EffectiveTOPSW)

// Benchmark comparison
bench := layers.NewAcceleratorBenchmark()
bench.AddLightmatterEnvise()
bench.AddMRRTensorCore()
bench.AddNvidiaH100()
bench.AddHBM4System()
fmt.Print(bench.Compare())
```

## Section 204: Analog Compute Noise Modeling

### Noise Sources in Ferroelectric CIM

Analog CIM accelerators suffer from multiple noise sources that limit effective precision:

**Key Noise Types:**
```
1. Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist):
   - V_rms = √(4kTRΔf)
   - At 300K, 10Ω, 1GHz: ~0.4 μV RMS
   - Fundamental limit from resistive elements

2. Shot Noise:
   - I_rms = √(2qIΔf)
   - At 1μA, 1GHz: ~18 pA RMS
   - Significant at low currents

3. Programming Variability:
   - Cycle-to-cycle: 3-10% σ/μ
   - Device-to-device: 5-15% σ/μ
   - Primary accuracy limiter in analog CIM

4. Read Noise:
   - Sense amplifier offset
   - Comparator metastability
   - Reference voltage drift
```

### ReRAM vs FeFET Noise Characteristics

| Device | C2C Variation | D2D Variation | Practical Bits |
|--------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| ReRAM | 5-10% | 10-20% | 3-4 bits |
| FeFET | 3-5% | 5-10% | 4-6 bits |
| SRAM | <1% | <1% | 8+ bits |
| PCM | 8-15% | 15-25% | 2-3 bits |

### IR Drop and Line Resistance

**Position-Dependent Noise:**
```
Voltage drop in 64×64 array:
- WL resistance: ~10Ω per cell
- BL resistance: ~5Ω per cell

Corner cell IR drop:
  ΔV = I_read × (64 × R_cell)
  ΔV = 1μA × 640Ω = 640 μV

Effect: 5-10% accuracy degradation at array corners
```

### Noise Mitigation Strategies

1. **Differential Signaling:** 2× area but cancels common-mode noise
2. **Chopper Stabilization:** Modulate to avoid 1/f noise
3. **Correlated Double Sampling:** Subtract reference read
4. **Calibration:** Per-cell offset/gain correction tables

---

## Section 205: ADC/DAC for CIM Accelerators

### ADC as CIM Bottleneck

**Energy and Area Breakdown:**
```
Typical CIM Accelerator:
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                     │
│  ADC Energy:  60% of total          │
│  ADC Area:    80% of total          │
│                                     │
│  DAC Energy:  10%                   │
│  Compute:     30%                   │
│                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

=> ADC optimization critical for CIM efficiency!
```

### ADC Architecture Comparison for CIM

| ADC Type | Speed | Resolution | Energy/conv | Best For |
|----------|-------|------------|-------------|----------|
| Flash | Very High | 4-6 bits | High (10+ pJ) | High-speed |
| SAR | Medium | 6-10 bits | Low (0.1-1 pJ) | Balanced |
| Sigma-Delta | Low | 12+ bits | Medium | Precision |
| Pipelined | High | 8-12 bits | Medium | Throughput |

### HCiM: ADC-Less Architecture (2024)

**Binary Partial Sum Quantization (PSQ):**
```
Traditional: Accumulate analog → 7-bit ADC
HCiM: Binary quantize each partial sum → digital accumulate

Energy Reduction: 28×
Accuracy: Comparable at same weight bits
Key insight: PSQ errors cancel in digital accumulation
```

**Performance Numbers:**
| Metric | Traditional | HCiM |
|--------|-------------|------|
| ADC bits | 7 | 1 (binary) |
| Energy/MAC | 100 fJ | 3.6 fJ |
| TOPS/W | 10 | 280 |
| Area/ADC | 1000 μm² | 35 μm² |

### Extreme PSQ Configurations

**Ternary PSQ (-1, 0, +1):**
- 2-bit ADC equivalent
- Better accuracy than binary
- 14× energy reduction vs 7-bit

**All-Digital SRAM CIM:**
```
Recent Achievement (2024):
- 6T SRAM cells, digital compute
- No ADC/DAC required
- 89 TOPS/W demonstrated
- Trade-off: Lower compute density
```

### DAC Considerations

**Input DAC Requirements:**
- 4-8 bits typical
- Must settle within MAC cycle
- Glitch energy significant for frequent updates

**DAC-less Architectures:**
- Binary/ternary inputs (BNN)
- Pulse-width encoding (time-domain)
- Stochastic computing

---

## Section 206: Noise & ADC Module Implementation

### noise_adc.go Overview (~750 lines)

Created comprehensive analog noise and ADC/DAC simulation module:

#### Noise Model

```go
type NoiseType int

const (
    NoiseThermal      NoiseType = iota // Johnson-Nyquist
    NoiseShot                          // Shot noise
    NoiseProgramming                   // Write variability
    NoiseRead                          // Sense amp noise
    NoiseIRDrop                        // Voltage drop
    NoiseQuantization                  // ADC quantization
    NoiseCrosstalk                     // Sneak paths
)

type NoiseModel struct {
    config         *NoiseConfig
    thermalNoiseRMS float64
    shotNoiseRMS    float64
}

func (nm *NoiseModel) AddNoise(value float64, pos CellPosition) float64 {
    // Add thermal noise
    result += nm.rng.NormFloat64() * nm.thermalNoiseRMS

    // Add shot noise (current-dependent)
    result += nm.rng.NormFloat64() * nm.shotNoiseRMS * sqrt(|value|)

    // Add programming variability (multiplicative)
    result += value * (c2c_noise + d2d_noise)

    return result
}
```

#### ADC Simulation

```go
type ADCType int

const (
    ADCFlash         ADCType = iota
    ADCSAR
    ADCSigmaDelta
    ADCBinaryPSQ     // HCiM-style
    ADCTernaryPSQ    // Higher accuracy PSQ
)

type ADC struct {
    config      *ADCConfig
    levels      int
    quantLevels []float64
}

// HCiM configuration: 28× energy reduction
func HCiMADCConfig() *ADCConfig {
    return &ADCConfig{
        Type:            ADCBinaryPSQ,
        BitPrecision:    1,
        EnergyPerConvPJ: 0.018,  // vs 0.5 pJ for SAR
        PSQThresholds:   []float64{0.0},
    }
}

func (adc *ADC) Convert(analogValue float64) (int, float64) {
    switch adc.config.Type {
    case ADCBinaryPSQ:
        if value >= threshold {
            return 1, +1.0
        }
        return 0, -1.0
    // ... other types
    }
}
```

#### Complete Signal Chain

```go
type CIMSignalChain struct {
    dac        *DAC
    adc        *ADC
    noiseModel *NoiseModel
}

func (chain *CIMSignalChain) ProcessMVMColumn(
    inputs []float64,
    weights []float64,
    colIndex int,
) float64 {
    analogSum := 0.0

    for row, input := range inputs {
        // DAC conversion
        analogInput := chain.dac.Convert(inputCode)

        // Analog multiply
        product := analogInput * weights[row]

        // Add cell noise
        product = chain.noiseModel.AddNoise(product, pos)

        analogSum += product
    }

    // ADC conversion
    _, quantized := chain.adc.Convert(analogSum)
    return quantized
}
```

#### Noise-Aware Training

```go
type NoiseInjector struct {
    config *NoiseAwareConfig
}

// Inject during training to improve robustness
func (ni *NoiseInjector) ProcessWeight(weight float64) float64 {
    result := weight

    // Clip to hardware range
    result = clip(result, -1, +1)

    // Simulate quantization
    result = quantize(result, ni.config.WeightBits)

    // Add programming variability
    result = ni.InjectWeightNoise(result)

    return result
}
```

### Usage Examples

```go
// Default 6-bit CIM chain
chain := layers.NewCIMSignalChain(nil)
output := chain.ProcessMVM(inputs, weights)
energy := chain.GetTotalEnergy()

// High-efficiency HCiM-style
config := layers.HighEfficiencyConfig()
chain := layers.NewCIMSignalChain(config)
// 28× energy reduction with binary PSQ

// SNR analysis
snr := layers.NewSNRAnalyzer()
for i := range samples {
    snr.AddSample(ideal[i], actual[i])
}
fmt.Printf("SNR: %.1f dB, ENOB: %.1f bits\n",
    snr.GetSNR(), snr.GetENOB())

// Noise-aware training
injector := layers.NewNoiseInjector(nil)
for i := range weights {
    weights[i] = injector.ProcessWeight(weights[i])
}
```

## Section 207: Thermal Modeling for Crossbar Arrays

### Joule Heating and Self-Heating Effects

Thermal management is critical for reliable CIM operation:

**Heat Generation:**
```
Joule heating during cell operation:
P = I² × R

For typical ReRAM/FeFET:
- Read current: 1 μA
- Cell resistance: 10 kΩ
- Power: 10 μW per cell

For 64×64 array with 50% activity:
Total power: ~20 mW
```

### Thermal Crosstalk Mechanism

Heat spreads through shared electrodes:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│     Heated       Heat flows through │
│     Cell ●───────► electrode ───────►│ Neighboring
│         ↓                           │ cells warm
│    Substrate heat sink              │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

Key parameters:
- TiN electrode thermal conductivity: ~20 W/(m·K)
- HZO thermal conductivity: ~1.5 W/(m·K)
- Silicon substrate: ~150 W/(m·K)
```

### Temperature Effects on Device Performance

| Temperature | RON/ROFF Ratio | Retention | Endurance |
|-------------|----------------|-----------|-----------|
| 300 K | 100% (baseline) | >10⁶ s | >10⁹ cycles |
| 330 K | 80% | >10⁵ s | ~10⁸ cycles |
| 360 K | 50% | >10⁴ s | ~10⁷ cycles |
| 400 K | 30% (runaway risk) | Minutes | Degraded |

### HZO-Specific Thermal Considerations

**Ferroelectric Phase Stability:**
```
HZO orthorhombic phase (ferroelectric):
- Stable up to ~400°C
- Optimal thickness: ~10 nm
- Above 400°C: phase transition to monoclinic (non-FE)

Retention temperature dependence:
- 25°C: >10 years extrapolated
- 85°C: >10⁵ hours (standard qualification)
- 125°C: >10⁴ hours
```

### Mitigation Strategies

1. **Electrode Engineering:** Graphene integration (3000-5000 W/m·K)
2. **Activity Spreading:** Distribute writes across array
3. **Duty Cycle Control:** Cool-down periods between operations
4. **Temperature Monitoring:** On-chip sensors at 330K threshold

---

## Section 208: Near-Memory Processing (NMP) / Processing-In-Memory (PIM)

### Memory Wall Problem

**Traditional Architecture Bottleneck:**
```
CPU ←──────────────────────────────→ Memory
      Limited bandwidth (100-400 GB/s)
      High latency (50-100 ns)
      High energy (10 pJ/byte movement)

For LLM inference:
- Memory bandwidth bound for attention
- 90%+ time spent on data movement
```

### Samsung Aquabolt-XL HBM2-PIM

**First Commercial HBM-PIM (Hot Chips 2021):**

| Specification | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| Architecture | 16-lane FP16 SIMD per bank |
| Processors | 128 total (32 per die × 4 PIM dies) |
| Data paths | 1,024 (2 × 16-bit per processor) |
| Internal bandwidth | 4.92 TB/s |
| External bandwidth | 1.23 TB/s |
| Bandwidth ratio | 4× |
| Operations | FP16 add, mul, FMA |

**Performance Gains:**
```
Benchmark               Speedup    Energy Reduction
GEMV (general MVM)      8.9×       60%+
Speech recognition      3.5×       60%+
Recommendation          4.0×       50%+
```

### JEDEC HBM3-PIM Standard

**Evolution from HBM2-PIM:**
- FP64 support (vs FP16)
- 32 channels (vs 16)
- Higher processor count
- Standardized ISA

### Architecture Taxonomy

| Type | Example | Compute Location | Best For |
|------|---------|------------------|----------|
| PIM | Aquabolt | In DRAM banks | GEMV, reduction |
| NMP | AxDIMM | Near DRAM | Preprocessing |
| CIM | ReRAM/FeFET | In memory cell | MAC operations |

### Energy Model

**Data Movement Energy:**
```
Traditional GPU:
- HBM to GPU: ~10 pJ/byte
- GPU compute: ~0.1 pJ/op

PIM:
- No data movement: 0 pJ/byte
- PIM compute: ~0.5 pJ/op

For memory-bound workloads:
Energy savings = 10× or more
```

---

## Section 209: Thermal & NMP Module Implementation

### thermal_nmp.go Overview (~850 lines)

Created comprehensive thermal modeling and PIM simulation module:

#### Thermal Model

```go
type ThermalModel struct {
    config         *ThermalConfig
    temperatureMap [][]float64
    heatMap        [][]float64
}

func (tm *ThermalModel) AddJouleHeat(row, col int, current, duration float64) {
    // P = I²R, Q = P × t
    power := current * current * resistance
    heat := power * durationNs * 1e-9

    // Temperature rise
    tempRise := heat / thermalMass
    tm.temperatureMap[row][col] += tempRise
}

func (tm *ThermalModel) SimulateThermalCrosstalk() {
    // Heat diffusion through electrodes
    for each cell {
        for each neighbor {
            heatFlow := diffusionCoeff * tempDiff / pitch²
            transfer heat between cells
        }
    }
}

func (tm *ThermalModel) GetResistanceRatioDegradation(row, col int) float64 {
    // Model RON/ROFF ratio vs temperature
    // 300K: 100%, 330K: 80%, 400K: 30%
}
```

#### Temperature-Aware Weight Adjustment (TAWA)

```go
type TAWAController struct {
    thermalModel    *ThermalModel
    compensationMap [][]float64
    remapTable      map[CellLocation]CellLocation
}

func (tc *TAWAController) Update() {
    for each cell above threshold {
        // Weight compensation for degradation
        tc.applyWeightCompensation(row, col, temp)

        // Remap to cooler location if needed
        tc.considerRemapping(row, col)
    }
}

// Results: 58% accuracy improvement, 2.39× lifetime
```

#### PIM System Simulation

```go
type PIMConfig struct {
    Architecture        PIMArchitecture
    NumProcessors       int       // 128 for Aquabolt
    LanesPerProcessor   int       // 16-lane SIMD
    DataWidthBits       int       // FP16
    InternalBandwidthTBs float64  // 4.92 TB/s
    ExternalBandwidthTBs float64  // 1.23 TB/s
}

func AquaboltConfig() *PIMConfig {
    return &PIMConfig{
        Architecture:         PIMSamsungAquabolt,
        NumProcessors:        128,
        LanesPerProcessor:    16,
        InternalBandwidthTBs: 4.92,
        ExternalBandwidthTBs: 1.23,
    }
}

func (sys *PIMSystem) ExecuteGEMV(matrix, vector [][]float64) []float64 {
    // Distribute across 128 processors
    // SIMD execution on each processor
    // 4× bandwidth advantage
}
```

## Section 210: Error Correction Codes (ECC) for CIM

### Error Sources in Analog CIM

CIM faces unique error challenges compared to digital systems:

**Hard Errors:**
```
1. Stuck-at-High: Cell fixed at high resistance (ROFF)
2. Stuck-at-Low: Cell fixed at low resistance (RON)
3. Short Circuit: Unintended connection between cells
4. Open Circuit: Broken connection

Cause: Manufacturing defects, electromigration, dielectric breakdown
Rate: ~10^-6 per cell in mature ReRAM
```

**Soft Errors:**
```
1. Noise-induced: Thermal, shot, read noise
2. Drift: Resistance changes over time
3. Read disturb: Read operation modifies state
4. Programming variability: C2C and D2D variation

Rate: ~10^-4 to 10^-5 per operation
```

### ECC Schemes for CIM

| Scheme | Correction | Detection | Overhead | Best For |
|--------|-----------|-----------|----------|----------|
| Hamming | 1 bit | 2 bits | 15% area | Light errors |
| SEC-DED | 1 bit | 2 bits | 18% area | Standard |
| BCH(t=2) | 2 bits | 3+ bits | 25% area | Moderate |
| BCH(t=3) | 3 bits | 4+ bits | 35% area | Heavy errors |
| Successive | 2+ bits | Multiple | 29% area | CIM-specific |

### Successive Correction for CIM (DAC 2022)

**Key Innovation:**
```
Traditional: Single pass decode
Successive: Multi-level iterative correction

Level 1: Hamming corrects soft errors
Level 2: Checksum detects residual errors
Level 3: Repeat with different syndrome

Result: 16,000× BER reduction
        29.1% area overhead
        26.3% power overhead
```

### Analog Domain ECC

**Checksum-Based Detection:**
```
Store row/column sums with weights:
  Row checksum: R_i = Σ_j W_ij
  Col checksum: C_j = Σ_i W_ij

Detection: Compare computed vs stored sums
Correction: Use intersection of error row/col

Advantages:
- No digital encoding/decoding
- Catches analog drift errors
- Low latency
```

---

## Section 211: Low-Power Inference Optimization

### Power Breakdown in CIM Accelerators

**Typical Distribution:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ADC/DAC:     60%  ←── Main target  │
│  Crossbar:    15%                   │
│  Peripherals: 15%                   │
│  Control:     10%                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS/DVFS)

**Power Scaling:**
```
Dynamic power: P_dyn ∝ C × V² × f
Static power:  P_static ∝ V × I_leak

Reducing V from 1.0V to 0.8V:
  Dynamic: 36% reduction (0.64/1.0)
  Static:  20% reduction

Typical DVS savings: 40-70% dynamic power
```

**Implementation:**
- Monitor workload/activity
- Scale voltage/frequency based on demand
- Use level shifters at domain boundaries
- 10% area overhead typical

### Clock Gating

**Principle:**
```
Active:  CLK → FF → toggles → power consumed
Gated:   CLK gated → FF → no toggle → no dynamic power

Savings: Proportional to idle time
         No effect on leakage
```

### Power Gating

**Complete Power Isolation:**
```
Active: Vdd → Block → operates
Gated:  Vdd isolated → Block → no leakage

Savings: 99%+ leakage reduction
Penalty: Wakeup latency (100ns typical)
         State loss (need retention)
```

### Sparsity-Aware Acceleration

**Zero Skipping:**
```
For sparse inputs (common in ReLU networks):
  - Skip MAC when input = 0
  - Skip MAC when weight = 0
  - >50% energy savings at 95% sparsity

SpiDR Accelerator (2024):
  - 5 TOPS/W at 95% sparsity
  - Zero-skipping mechanism
  - >50% energy reduction (75%→95% sparsity)
```

### Energy Efficiency Comparison

| Architecture | Efficiency | Technique |
|--------------|------------|-----------|
| GPU (Titan-V) | ~0.3 TOPS/W | Baseline |
| Edge TPU | 2-4 TOPS/W | Quantization |
| RRAM CIM | 10-30 TOPS/W | Analog compute |
| SRAM CIM | 43 TOPS/W | Digital CIM |
| Sparse CIM | 5+ TOPS/W @95% sparsity | Zero skip |
| IBM Analog | 12.4 TOPS/W | PCM + analog |

## Section 212: ECC & Power Module Implementation

### ecc_power.go Overview (~900 lines)

Created comprehensive ECC and power optimization module:

#### Digital ECC Engine

```go
type ECCType int

const (
    ECCNone ECCType = iota
    ECCHamming       // Single error correction
    ECCSECDED        // SEC-DED
    ECCBCH           // Multi-error correction
    ECCSuccessive    // CIM-specific successive
)

type ECCEngine struct {
    config *ECCConfig
    errorMap map[int]ErrorType
}

func (e *ECCEngine) Encode(data []byte) []byte {
    switch e.config.Type {
    case ECCHamming:
        return e.encodeHamming(data)
    case ECCSuccessive:
        // Multi-level encoding
        level1 := e.encodeHamming(data)
        level2 := append(level1, checksum)
        return level2
    }
}

func (e *ECCEngine) Decode(encoded []byte) ([]byte, int, error) {
    // Decode and return (data, errors_corrected, error)
}
```

## Section 213: Neuromorphic Audio Processing

### Bio-Inspired Cochlear Models

The biological cochlea performs sophisticated frequency analysis:

**Cochlear Function:**
```
Sound → Basilar membrane → Frequency-to-place mapping
       → Hair cells → Neural spikes

Key properties:
- ~3,500 inner hair cells
- 20 Hz to 20 kHz frequency range
- Nonlinear amplification
- Adaptation to noise
```

### Gammatone Filterbank

**Mathematical Model:**
```
g(t) = a × t^(n-1) × exp(-2πbt) × cos(2πft + φ)

Where:
- n = filter order (typically 4)
- b = ERB bandwidth
- f = center frequency
- ERB(f) = 24.7 × (4.37 × f/1000 + 1)
```

**Implementation:**
```
ERB-spaced center frequencies:
- 64 channels typical
- 80 Hz to 8000 Hz range
- Log spacing matches cochlea

Silicon cochlea specs:
- 29.7 mW power
- 2.19 Mevents/s output
- 9.6 Hz to 14.6 kHz range
```

### MFCC vs Gammatone Features

| Feature | MFCC | Gammatone (GFCC) |
|---------|------|------------------|
| Filter shape | Triangular | Gammatone |
| Frequency scale | Mel | ERB |
| Noise robustness | Good | Better |
| Computational cost | Low | Medium |
| Bio-inspiration | Moderate | High |

### Spike Encoding for Audio

**Rate Coding:**
```
Signal amplitude → Spike rate
Higher value = More spikes/second
Simple, robust to noise
```

**Temporal Coding:**
```
Signal amplitude → Time-to-first-spike
Higher value = Earlier spike
More efficient, preserves timing
```

### AIMC Speech Recognition (Nature 2025)

**System Architecture:**
```
Audio → RNPU (feature extraction) → AIMC (classification)

RNPU: Reconfigurable Nonlinear Processing Units
- Analog domain feature extraction
- No ADC for intermediate stages

Results:
- TI-46-Word: Near-software accuracy
- Google Speech Commands: Competitive accuracy
- Edge-efficient: Low power, low latency
```

---

## Section 214: End-to-End CNN Inference Pipeline

### Layer-to-Crossbar Mapping

**Im2col for Convolution:**
```
Conv2D layer with:
- InChannels = C_in
- OutChannels = C_out
- Kernel size = K

Matrix dimensions:
- Rows = C_out
- Cols = C_in × K × K

Example: 64 filters, 3 input channels, 3×3 kernel
Matrix: 64 × 27 (fits in 64×64 crossbar)
```

### Layer Tiling Strategy

**When layer exceeds crossbar size:**
```
Layer matrix: 256 × 512
Crossbar: 64 × 64

Tiling:
- Row tiles: ceil(256/64) = 4
- Col tiles: ceil(512/64) = 8
- Total tiles: 32

Dataflow:
- Split input across column tiles
- Accumulate partial sums from row tiles
```

### CNN Architecture Support

**LeNet-5 (MNIST):**
```
Input: 1×32×32
Conv1: 6 filters, 5×5 → 6×28×28
Pool1: 2×2 → 6×14×14
Conv2: 16 filters, 5×5 → 16×10×10
Pool2: 2×2 → 16×5×5
FC1: 400→120
FC2: 120→84
FC3: 84→10

Total params: ~60K
CIM mapping: 4 tiles (64×64)
```

**Simple CIFAR-10:**
```
Input: 3×32×32
Conv1-2: 32 filters, 3×3 → 32×32×32
Pool1: 2×2 → 32×16×16
Conv3: 64 filters, 3×3 → 64×16×16
Pool2: 2×2 → 64×8×8
FC1: 4096→512
FC2: 512→10

Total params: ~2.1M
CIM mapping: 16 tiles (256×256)
```

### CIM CNN Performance

| Network | Dataset | Accuracy | Crossbar | TOPS/W |
|---------|---------|----------|----------|--------|
| LeNet-5 | MNIST | 99.1% | 64×64 | ~10 |
| ResNet-32 | CIFAR-10 | 93.7% | 256×256 | ~15 |
| VGG16 | CIFAR-100 | 71% | 256×256 | 17.1 |
| ResNet-50 | CIFAR-100 | 76% | 256×256 | 18.79 |

## Section 215: Audio & CNN Module Implementation

### audio_cnn.go Overview (~950 lines)

Created comprehensive audio processing and CNN inference module:

#### Gammatone Filterbank

```go
type GammatoneFilterbank struct {
    config      *AudioConfig
    centerFreqs []float64  // ERB-spaced frequencies
    bandwidths  []float64  // ERB bandwidths
    filterOrder int        // 4 typical
}

func (fb *GammatoneFilterbank) calculateERBSpacing() []float64 {
    // ERB(f) = 21.4 × log10(4.37×f/1000 + 1)
    lowERB := fb.hzToERB(lowFreq)
    highERB := fb.hzToERB(highFreq)
    // Linear spacing in ERB domain
}

func (fb *GammatoneFilterbank) Process(samples []float64) []float64 {
    for each channel {
        filtered := applyGammatoneFilter(samples, channel)
        envelope := computeEnvelope(filtered)
        output[ch] = sqrt(mean(envelope²))
    }
}
```

#### MFCC Extraction

```go
type MFCCExtractor struct {
    melFilters [][]float64  // Triangular filterbank
    dctMatrix  [][]float64  // DCT-II matrix
}

func (m *MFCCExtractor) ExtractFrame(samples []float64) []float64 {
    // Apply Hamming window
    // Compute power spectrum (FFT)
    // Apply mel filterbank
    // Log compression
    // Apply DCT for cepstral coefficients
    return mfcc  // 13 coefficients typical
}
```

#### CNN Pipeline

```go
type CNNPipeline struct {
    layers       []*CNNLayerConfig
    crossbarSize int
    tileMapping  []LayerTileMapping
}

func (p *CNNPipeline) Forward(input [][][]float64) ([]float64, Stats) {
    for each layer {
        switch layer.Type {
        case LayerConv2D:
            current = p.forwardConv2D(current, layer)
        case LayerMaxPool:
            current = p.forwardMaxPool(current, layer)
        case LayerFC:
            output = p.forwardFC(flatten(current), layer)
        case LayerReLU:
            current = p.forwardReLU(current)
        }
    }
    return output, stats
}
```

#### Pre-defined Architectures

```go
// LeNet-5 for MNIST
func CreateLeNet5(crossbarSize int) *CNNPipeline {
    pipeline := NewCNNPipeline(crossbarSize)
    pipeline.AddLayer(Conv2D(1, 6, 5))   // 1→6 channels
    pipeline.AddLayer(ReLU())
    pipeline.AddLayer(MaxPool(2))
    pipeline.AddLayer(Conv2D(6, 16, 5))  // 6→16 channels
    pipeline.AddLayer(ReLU())
    pipeline.AddLayer(MaxPool(2))
    pipeline.AddLayer(FC(400, 120))
    pipeline.AddLayer(FC(120, 84))
    pipeline.AddLayer(FC(84, 10))
    pipeline.AddLayer(Softmax())
    return pipeline
}

// Simple CIFAR-10
func CreateSimpleCIFAR(crossbarSize int) *CNNPipeline {
    // 3→32→32→64 conv + FC(4096→512→10)
}
```

// MFCC extraction

predicted := argmax(output)

output, stats := sys.ProcessAudio(audioSamples)

## Section 216: Graph Neural Networks on CIM

### GNN Fundamentals

Graph Neural Networks process irregular data structures (graphs) through message passing:

**Core Operations:**
```
1. Message: Transform neighbor features → h'_j = W × h_j
2. Aggregate: Combine neighbors → m_i = AGG({h'_j : j ∈ N(i)})
3. Update: New node embedding → h'_i = σ(W_self × h_i + m_i)
4. Readout: Graph-level output → h_G = POOL({h_i})
```

### GNN on Analog CIM

**Challenges:**
- Irregular, sparse adjacency matrices (<0.1% density typical)
- Dynamic graph topology
- High memory bandwidth for scatter-gather
- Variable node degrees

**CIM Mapping Strategies:**

| Approach | Description | Efficiency |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| Feature MVM | Dense feature transform on crossbar | High |
| Sparse aggregation | Software-handled gather | Medium |
| Blocked adjacency | Partition sparse matrix | Low |

### ReGNN: Reconfigurable GNN Accelerator (2023)

**Key Innovation:**
```
Streaming dataflow for message passing:
- Feature transform: Standard MVM on crossbar
- Aggregation: Specialized scatter-gather unit
- Supports GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE

Results:
- 228× speedup vs GPU (RTX 2080 Ti)
- 305× energy reduction
- Tested on social networks, citation graphs
```

### GNN-PIM: Processing-in-Memory for GNN

**Architecture:**
```
Memory-centric design:
- Store adjacency in HBM
- Feature transform in near-memory CIM
- Minimize data movement

Performance:
- 8.7× speedup vs ReRAM baseline
- Efficient for large graphs (>1M nodes)
```

### Sparse Graph Representation

**Compressed Sparse Row (CSR):**
```
For graph with N nodes, E edges:

RowPtr[N+1]: Cumulative edge count per node
ColIdx[E]: Destination node for each edge
EdgeWeight[E]: Optional edge weights

Space: O(N + E) vs O(N²) for dense

Example (5 nodes, 6 edges):
0 → 1, 2
1 → 0, 2
2 → 3
3 → 4

RowPtr = [0, 2, 4, 5, 6, 6]
ColIdx = [1, 2, 0, 2, 3, 4]
```

---

## Section 217: Hardware-Aware Neural Architecture Search

### NAS Overview

Neural Architecture Search automates network design by searching over:
- Layer types (conv, dwconv, attention, etc.)
- Channel counts
- Kernel sizes
- Skip connections
- Quantization levels

### Hardware-Aware NAS for CIM

**CIM-specific constraints:**
```
1. Crossbar size: Limited by array dimensions (64×64, 256×256)
2. ADC/DAC precision: Quantization affects accuracy
3. Weight mapping: Must tile large layers
4. Energy budget: Target TOPS/W
5. Latency: Real-time requirements
```

### CIM²PQ: Mixed Precision Quantization

**Key Innovation:**
Array-wise mixed precision rather than layer-wise:
```
- Different crossbar tiles get different bit widths
- Sensitivity analysis identifies critical weights
- Optimal allocation via evolutionary search

Results:
- <1% accuracy drop vs FP32
- 60-75% model size reduction
- Per-tile ADC complexity optimization
```

### Evolutionary Search Algorithm

**Population-based approach:**
```
1. Initialize: Random architectures
2. Evaluate: Hardware model + proxy accuracy
3. Select: Tournament selection
4. Crossover: Combine parent architectures
5. Mutate: Random perturbations
6. Repeat for N generations

Mutations:
- Change layer type
- Modify channel count
- Adjust kernel size
- Vary quantization bits
- Add/remove layers
```

### Multi-Objective Fitness Function

**Balancing accuracy, latency, energy:**
```
fitness = w₁ × acc_score + w₂ × lat_score + w₃ × energy_score

Where:
- acc_score = achieved_accuracy / target_accuracy
- lat_score = target_latency / estimated_latency
- energy_score = target_energy / estimated_energy

Typical weights: 0.5, 0.25, 0.25
```

### Hardware Cost Models

**Latency Estimation:**
```
For crossbar size S × S:
- ops_per_tile = S²
- num_tiles = ceil(total_MACs / ops_per_tile)
- cycles_per_tile ≈ 10 (pipeline depth)
- latency = num_tiles × cycles × clock_period
```

**Energy Estimation:**
```
Energy components:
- MVM: ~0.1 pJ/bit @ 8-bit
- ADC: dominant at high precision
- DAC: scales with input bits
- Memory access: ~10 pJ/byte

Total per MAC ≈ 0.1-1.0 pJ (depending on precision)
```

## Section 218: GNN & NAS Module Implementation

### gnn_nas.go Overview (~1080 lines)

Created comprehensive GNN and NAS module for CIM simulation:

#### Graph Representation

```go
type Graph struct {
    NumNodes     int
    NumEdges     int
    RowPtr       []int       // CSR row pointers
    ColIdx       []int       // CSR column indices
    EdgeWeight   []float64   // Edge weights (optional)
    NodeFeatures [][]float64 // Node feature matrix
    NodeLabels   []int       // For classification
}

func NewGraph(numNodes int, edges [][2]int, features [][]float64) *Graph {
    g := &Graph{NumNodes: numNodes, NumEdges: len(edges)}
    g.buildCSR(edges)  // Efficient sparse representation
    return g
}

func (g *Graph) GetNeighbors(node int) []int {
    return g.ColIdx[g.RowPtr[node]:g.RowPtr[node+1]]
}

func (g *Graph) GetSparsity() float64 {
    return 1.0 - float64(g.NumEdges)/float64(g.NumNodes*g.NumNodes)
}
```

#### GNN Layer with Message Passing

```go
type GNNLayer struct {
    inputDim   int
    outputDim  int
    weights    [][]float64  // Feature transform
    config     *GraphConfig
}

func (layer *GNNLayer) MessagePass(graph *Graph, features [][]float64) [][]float64 {
    for node := range features {
        neighbors := graph.GetNeighbors(node)

        // Aggregate neighbor messages
        for _, neighbor := range neighbors {
            transformed := layer.transformFeatures(features[neighbor])
            switch layer.config.AggregationType {
            case AggSum:  // Sum pooling
            case AggMean: // Mean pooling
            case AggMax:  // Max pooling
            }
        }

        // Update: self + neighbors + bias + ReLU
        output[node] = relu(selfTransform + neighborMsg + bias)
    }
    return output
}
```

#### Hardware-Aware NAS Engine

```go
type NASEngine struct {
    config     *NASConfig
    population []*Architecture
    bestArch   *Architecture
}

type Architecture struct {
    Layers      []LayerSpec
    TotalParams int64
    TotalMACs   int64
    Accuracy    float64
    LatencyMs   float64
    EnergyPJ    float64
    Fitness     float64
}

func (nas *NASEngine) Search() *Architecture {
    nas.InitializePopulation()  // Random architectures

    for gen := 0; gen < nas.config.NumGenerations; gen++ {
        nas.Evolve()  // Selection, crossover, mutation
    }

    return nas.bestArch
}

func (nas *NASEngine) EvaluateArchitecture(arch *Architecture) {
    // Estimate accuracy from depth, width, quantization
    arch.Accuracy = f(depth, width, avgBits)

    // Estimate latency from tile count
    numTiles := arch.TotalMACs / (crossbarSize²)
    arch.LatencyMs = numTiles × cyclesPerTile × clockPeriod

    // Estimate energy from bit widths
    arch.EnergyPJ = totalMACs × energyPerMAC × bitRatio

    // Multi-objective fitness
    arch.Fitness = 0.5*accScore + 0.25*latScore + 0.25*energyScore
}
```

#### Mixed Precision Quantization

```go
type MPQEngine struct {
    config       *MPQConfig
    architecture *Architecture
}

func (mpq *MPQEngine) SearchOptimalQuantization() []int {
    // Per-layer bit width search
    // Evolutionary optimization
    // Balance accuracy vs model size

    bestQuant := evolutionarySearch(
        population,
        evaluateQuantization,
        generations,
    )
    return bestQuant
}
```

### Usage Examples

```go
// Create random graph
graph := layers.GenerateRandomGraph(1000, 5, 32)  // 1000 nodes, avg degree 5

// Configure GNN
config := layers.DefaultGraphConfig()
config.NumLayers = 3
config.HiddenDim = 64
config.AggregationType = layers.AggMean

// Create and run GNN
gnn := layers.NewGNN(32, config)  // 32-dim input features
nodeEmbeddings := gnn.Forward(graph)
predictions := gnn.NodeClassification(graph)

// Graph-level task
graphEmbedding := gnn.GraphPooling(nodeEmbeddings, layers.AggSum)

// Hardware-aware NAS
nasConfig := layers.DefaultNASConfig()
nasConfig.TargetAccuracy = 0.92
nasConfig.TargetLatencyMs = 5.0
nasConfig.CrossbarSize = 128

nas := layers.NewNASEngine(nasConfig)
bestArch := nas.Search()
report := layers.FormatNASReport(nas)

// Mixed precision quantization
mpq := layers.NewMPQEngine(bestArch, nil)
optimalBits := mpq.SearchOptimalQuantization()
```

## Section 219: Reinforcement Learning on CIM

### RL Fundamentals for Hardware

Reinforcement learning trains agents through reward-based interaction with environments. Key algorithms for hardware implementation:

**Q-Learning:**
```
Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γ max Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]

Where:
- α: Learning rate
- γ: Discount factor
- r: Reward
- s,a: State, action
```

**Actor-Critic:**
```
Actor (policy): π(a|s) → probability distribution
Critic (value): V(s) → expected return
TD error: δ = r + γV(s') - V(s)

Actor update: ∇θ log π(a|s) × δ
Critic update: Gradient descent on δ²
```

### CIM-Based RL Implementations

**Memristor Actor-Critic (Nature 2025):**
```
Key innovations:
- TD learning directly in analog hardware
- Online weight updates via conductance modulation
- Memristors serve triple purpose:
  1. Synaptic weights
  2. Weight update calculation
  3. Action determination

Tested on:
- T-maze navigation
- Morris water-maze

Advantage: Eliminates data movement, fully in-memory learning
```

**Q-Learning on RISC-V (2023):**
```
Hardware accelerator for Klessydra processor:
- First Q-table accelerator in literature
- FPGA-based implementation
- Memory-oriented optimization (MEMOREL)
```

### Hardware Considerations

**CIM Advantages for RL:**
- MVMs accelerate neural network inference
- Parallel weight updates possible
- Low energy per operation
- Analog computation matches biological RL

**Challenges:**
- Non-ideal memristor switching (asymmetry)
- Programming noise (~90% NSR)
- Need for digital correction
- Online learning requires read-update-write cycles

### Deep Q-Network (DQN) on CIM

```
Architecture:
Input (state) → MVM₁ → ReLU → MVM₂ → Q-values

Experience replay buffer:
- Store (s, a, r, s', done) tuples
- Sample minibatches for training
- Breaks correlation in sequential data

Target network:
- Separate network for stable targets
- Periodic sync with online network
```

---

## Section 220: Multi-Chip CIM Orchestration

### Scalability Challenge

Monolithic CIM faces limits:
- Area constraints
- Yield issues
- On-chip interconnect cost
- Ever-increasing model sizes

Solution: Chiplet-based multi-chip modules (MCMs)

### Simba: 36-Chiplet MCM (NVIDIA 2019)

**Architecture:**
```
6×6 chiplet array
- 16nm fabrication
- 36 chiplets on organic substrate
- Ground-referenced signaling (GRS)

Per chiplet:
- 4 TOPS peak
- 2 GHz PE frequency
- 16 IMC tiles

System:
- 128 TOPS total at 1.1V
- 6.1 TOPS/W efficiency
- 1.29 TOPS/mm² area efficiency
```

**Interconnect:**
```
Network-on-Package (NoP):
- Mesh topology
- 8 GRS transceivers per chiplet
- 11-25 Gbps/pin
- 0.82-1.75 pJ/bit
- 100 GB/s peak per chiplet

Flow control:
- Wormhole for unicast
- Cut-through for multicast
```

### SIAM: Chiplet-Based CIM Simulator

**Framework capabilities:**
```
End-to-end modeling:
- Device level (SRAM/RRAM)
- Circuit level
- Architecture level
- NoC (intra-chiplet)
- NoP (inter-chiplet)
- DRAM access

Benchmarks:
- ResNet-50: 130× vs V100, 72× vs T4
- CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet
- Calibrated with Simba silicon results
```

### NoC vs NoP Trade-offs

**Network-on-Chip (NoC):**
```
- Intra-chiplet communication
- Higher bandwidth (~64 Gbps)
- Lower latency (1 cycle/hop)
- Lower energy (~0.1 pJ/bit)
```

**Network-on-Package (NoP):**
```
- Inter-chiplet communication
- Lower bandwidth (~32 Gbps)
- Higher latency (5+ cycles/hop)
- Higher energy (~0.8 pJ/bit)

Trade-off: More tiles/chiplet → higher NoC cost, lower NoP cost
```

### Wireless Interconnect Enhancement

**mmWave wireless (2025):**
```
Specifications:
- 100+ Gbps speed
- <1 mm² area
- ~1 pJ/bit energy
- Tested at 64-96 Gbps

Results:
- 10% average speedup
- 20% maximum speedup
- Effective for multicast traffic
- Reduces distant communication latency
```

### Load Balancing Strategies

**Non-uniform work partitioning:**
```
1. Profile communication patterns
2. Identify hot/cold chiplets
3. Redistribute workload
4. Up to 16% performance gain vs naive

Communication-aware data placement:
- Place frequently communicating data on adjacent chiplets
- Minimize NoP traffic
- Cross-layer pipelining
```

## Section 221: RL & Multi-Chip Module Implementation

### rl_multichip.go Overview (~1100 lines)

Created comprehensive RL and multi-chip orchestration module:

#### Reinforcement Learning Components

```go
type RLConfig struct {
    LearningRate    float64  // α for weight updates
    DiscountFactor  float64  // γ for future rewards
    ExplorationRate float64  // ε for epsilon-greedy
    StateSize       int
    ActionSize      int
    HiddenSize      int
    CrossbarSize    int
    WeightBits      int
}

type Experience struct {
    State     []float64
    Action    int
    Reward    float64
    NextState []float64
    Done      bool
}

type ReplayBuffer struct {
    buffer   []Experience
    capacity int
}
```

#### Q-Network Implementation

```go
type QNetwork struct {
    weights1, weights2 [][]float64  // Two-layer MLP
    bias1, bias2       []float64
}

func (qn *QNetwork) Forward(state []float64) []float64 {
    // Layer 1: MVM + ReLU (crossbar operation)
    hidden := matmul(weights1, state) + bias1
    hidden = relu(hidden)

    // Layer 2: MVM (crossbar operation)
    qValues := matmul(weights2, hidden) + bias2
    return qValues
}

func (qn *QNetwork) SelectAction(state []float64) int {
    if random() < explorationRate {
        return randomAction()
    }
    return argmax(qn.Forward(state))
}

func (qn *QNetwork) Update(batch []Experience) {
    for each (s, a, r, s', done) {
        target := r + γ × max(Q(s'))  // TD target
        tdError := target - Q(s)[a]
        // Backprop (simulates memristor weight update)
    }
}
```

#### Actor-Critic Implementation

```go
type ActorCritic struct {
    // Actor network (policy)
    actorWeights1, actorWeights2 [][]float64

    // Critic network (value function)
    criticWeights1, criticWeights2 [][]float64
}

func (ac *ActorCritic) PolicyForward(state []float64) []float64 {
    // Returns action probabilities via softmax
}

func (ac *ActorCritic) ValueForward(state []float64) float64 {
    // Returns state value V(s)
}

func (ac *ActorCritic) Update(s, a, r, s', done) {
    // TD error: δ = r + γV(s') - V(s)
    tdError := tdTarget - currentValue

    // Update critic via gradient descent
    // Update actor via policy gradient: ∇log π(a|s) × δ
}
```

#### Multi-Chip System

```go
type MultiChipConfig struct {
    NumChiplets   int  // Total chiplets (e.g., 36)
    GridRows      int  // Mesh rows
    GridCols      int  // Mesh cols
    ChipletConfig *ChipletConfig
    NoCConfig     *NoXConfig
    NoPConfig     *NoXConfig
}

type Chiplet struct {
    config      *ChipletConfig
    row, col    int              // Grid position
    workload    int64            // Assigned MACs
    utilization float64
    neighbors   []*Chiplet       // Mesh connections
}

type MultiChipSystem struct {
    chiplets [][]*Chiplet  // 2D grid
}

func (mcs *MultiChipSystem) PartitionLayer(totalMACs int64) {
    // Distribute work evenly across chiplets
    macsPerChiplet := totalMACs / numChiplets
    for each chiplet {
        chiplet.AssignWork(macs)
    }
}

func (mcs *MultiChipSystem) EstimateLatency(partitions) float64 {
    // Max of per-chiplet latency + communication
}

func (mcs *MultiChipSystem) EstimateEnergy(partitions) float64 {
    // Sum of compute + communication energy
}
```

#### Load Balancing and Wireless Overlay

```go
type LoadBalancer struct {
    system *MultiChipSystem
}

func (lb *LoadBalancer) BalanceWorkload(partitions) {
    // Sort chiplets by load
    // Transfer work from overloaded to underloaded
}

type WirelessOverlay struct {
    bandwidth    float64  // mmWave Gbps
    hopThreshold int      // Min hops to use wireless
}

func (wo *WirelessOverlay) ShouldUseWireless(src, dst) bool {
    return manhattanDist(src, dst) >= hopThreshold
}
```

### Usage Examples

```go
// Q-Learning on Cart-Pole
config := layers.DefaultRLConfig()
qn := layers.NewQNetwork(config)
buffer := layers.NewReplayBuffer(10000)
env := layers.NewCartPoleEnv()

for episode := 0; episode < 1000; episode++ {
    state := env.Reset()
    for {
        action := qn.SelectAction(state)
        nextState, reward, done := env.Step(action)
        buffer.Add(Experience{state, action, reward, nextState, done})
        batch := buffer.Sample(32)
        qn.Update(batch)
        if done { break }
    }
}

// Actor-Critic training
ac := layers.NewActorCritic(config)
for episode := range episodes {
    state := env.Reset()
    for {
        action := ac.SelectAction(state)
        nextState, reward, done := env.Step(action)
        ac.Update(state, action, reward, nextState, done)
    }
}

// Multi-chip system simulation
mcsConfig := layers.DefaultMultiChipConfig()  // 36 chiplets, 6×6 grid
mcs := layers.NewMultiChipSystem(mcsConfig)

// Partition layers across chiplets
partitions := []*LayerPartition{
    mcs.PartitionLayer(0, 1e9),   // 1B MACs
    mcs.PartitionLayer(1, 500e6), // 500M MACs
}

// Balance load
balancer := layers.NewLoadBalancer(mcs)
balancer.BalanceWorkload(partitions)

// Estimate performance
latency := mcs.EstimateLatency(partitions)
energy := mcs.EstimateEnergy(partitions)
report := layers.FormatMultiChipReport(mcs)

// Wireless overlay
wireless := layers.NewWirelessOverlay(mcsConfig, 96.0)  // 96 Gbps
speedup := wireless.EstimateSpeedup()  // ~1.10 (10%)
```
