## Section 255: Spiking Neural Networks with Ferroelectric Synapses

### All-Ferroelectric SNN Architecture (Advanced Science 2024)

Breakthrough all-ferroelectric SNN system:

```
Key Innovation:
- Double-gate MPB-TFT LIF neuron
- Morphotropic phase boundary material
- No capacitors or reset circuits needed
- 94.9% classification accuracy

Advantages:
- Area efficient (no external caps)
- Energy efficient
- Wafer-scale ALD fabrication
- Co-optimized material + device
```

### FeFET Synapse Properties

```
Biological Synaptic Functions:
1. EPSC (Excitatory Post-Synaptic Current)
2. PPF (Paired-Pulse Facilitation)
3. STDP (Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity)

Performance:
- Energy: ~2 fJ/spike
- Endurance: >10^5 cycles
- Cycle-to-cycle variation: ~1%
- Long-term potentiation/depression

Material: Al:HfO₂ thin films
- CMOS compatible
- High coercive field
- Improved retention
- Scalable
```

### Leaky Integrate-and-Fire Neuron

```go
type LIFNeuronConfig struct {
    Threshold       float64  // -55 mV (spike threshold)
    RestPotential   float64  // -70 mV
    ResetPotential  float64  // -75 mV
    LeakConstant    float64  // 20 ms (τ_m)
    RefractoryTime  float64  // 2 ms
    Capacitance     float64  // 100 pF
}

// Update dynamics
func (n *LIFNeuron) Update(dt, currentTime float64) bool {
    // Refractory period check
    if currentTime - lastSpike < refractoryTime {
        return false
    }

    // Leaky integration: dV/dt = -(V-V_rest)/τ + I/C
    leak := -(V - V_rest) / τ
    input := I / C

    V += (leak + input) * dt

    // Spike generation
    if V >= threshold {
        V = reset
        return true  // Spike!
    }
    return false
}
```

### CMOS LIF Implementations (2024)

```
Versatile CMOS Analog LIF (June 2024):
- 130nm CMOS ASIC
- Low-Dropout Regulator (LDO)
- Current attenuator
- Dual leakage for local learning
- Firing rate: 8 Hz to 25 kHz
- Memristor interface ready

Adiabatic LIF (December 2024):
- 180nm CMOS technology
- >90% energy exchange efficiency
- >99% at low frequencies
- Refractory period support
- Ultra-low energy neuromorphic

Adaptive LIF (2024):
- 180nm SCL CMOS
- No comparator needed
- 1.4 pJ/spike
- 8.8 MHz spike rate
- 198.5 μm² area
```

### Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity (STDP)

```go
type FerroelectricSynapse struct {
    Weight          float64  // Polarization: -1 to +1
    Conductance     float64  // nS

    // STDP parameters
    TauPlus         float64  // 20 ms (potentiation)
    TauMinus        float64  // 20 ms (depression)
    APlus           float64  // 0.1 (max LTP)
    AMinus          float64  // 0.12 (max LTD)
}

// Apply STDP rule
func (s *FerroelectricSynapse) ApplySTDP(preTime, postTime float64) {
    dt := postTime - preTime

    if dt > 0 {
        // Pre before post → LTP (potentiation)
        ΔW := A+ × exp(-dt/τ+)
        weight += ΔW
    } else {
        // Post before pre → LTD (depression)
        ΔW := -A- × exp(dt/τ-)
        weight += ΔW
    }

    weight = clamp(weight, -1, 1)
}
```

### TEXEL Neuromorphic Processor (2025)

```
Architecture:
- On-chip STDP/SRDP learning
- Memristor device interface
- Three-terminal FeFET support
- Full-CMOS and device-integrated modes

Capabilities:
- Hardware STDP implementation
- Spike-Rate-Dependent Plasticity
- Versatile device compatibility
- Large range of operation requirements
```

### SNN Network Implementation

```go
type SpikingNeuralNetwork struct {
    Neurons         [][]*LIFNeuron
    Synapses        [][]*FerroelectricSynapse
    InputSize       int
    OutputSize      int
    TimeStep        float64  // 0.1 ms
    LearningEnabled bool
}

// Present input as Poisson spike train
func (snn *SNN) Present(inputRates []float64, duration float64) {
    for step := 0; step < steps; step++ {
        for i, rate := range inputRates {
            prob := rate * dt / 1000  // Poisson probability
            if rand() < prob {
                neurons[0][i].InjectCurrent(500)  // pA
            }
        }
        snn.Step()
    }
}

// Classify by spike count
func (snn *SNN) Classify() int {
    counts := GetOutputSpikeCounts(100ms)
    return argmax(counts)
}
```

### Energy Comparison: SNN vs DNN

```
SNN Advantages:
- Sparse computation (event-driven)
- ~2 fJ/spike (FeFET)
- Only active synapses compute

DNN Reference:
- Dense computation (all MACs)
- ~0.5 pJ/MAC (digital)
- All weights processed

Comparison:
┌──────────────────┬─────────┬──────────┐
│ Metric           │ DNN     │ SNN      │
├──────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Energy/op        │ 0.5 pJ  │ 2 fJ     │
│ Sparsity         │ 0%      │ 90-99%   │
│ Effective energy │ 0.5 pJ  │ 0.02 pJ  │
│ Energy reduction │ 1×      │ 25×      │
└──────────────────┴─────────┴──────────┘

Key: SNN sparsity (1-10% activity) provides
     10-100× energy advantage over dense DNN
```

## Section 256: Transformer KV-Cache Optimization for CIM

### KV-Cache Bottleneck in LLM Inference

KV-cache stores key-value pairs for attention computation in autoregressive transformers:

```
KV-Cache Challenge:
- Memory grows linearly with sequence length
- 70B LLaMA: ~20GB KV-cache at 4k context
- Memory bandwidth becomes bottleneck
- Limits batch size and throughput

Traditional Solutions:
- Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)
- Multi-Query Attention (MQA)
- Sliding window attention
- Memory compression
```

### UniCAIM: FeFET-Based CAM/CIM Architecture (2025)

UniCAIM uses FeFET content-addressable memory (CAM) for efficient KV-cache lookup:

```
Architecture:
- FeFET-based CAM array for key matching
- CIM array for value computation
- Unified memory/compute fabric
- O(1) attention complexity

Key Innovation:
- Keys stored in CAM cells
- Parallel similarity search
- Analog distance computation
- 8.2× AEDP reduction

AEDP = Area × Energy × Delay Product
```

### FeFET CAM Operation

```
CAM Cell Structure:
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│    Match Line (ML)          │
├─────────────────────────────┤
│  ┌─────┐      ┌─────┐      │
│  │FeFET│──────│FeFET│      │
│  │  Q  │      │  Q̄  │      │
│  └──┬──┘      └──┬──┘      │
│     │            │         │
│  Search   Search (complement)│
└─────────────────────────────┘

Operation:
1. Store: Program FeFET Vth to encode key bits
2. Search: Apply search key to gates
3. Match: ML discharges if ANY bit mismatches
4. Result: Only matching rows keep ML high

Analog Extension:
- Multi-level Vth for similarity scoring
- Distance = sum of bit differences
- Enables nearest-neighbor search
```

### KV-Cache Pruning Strategies

```go
type KVCachePruner struct {
    Strategy      PruningStrategy
    TargetRatio   float64  // 0.5 = 50% pruning
    WindowSize    int      // Recent tokens to keep
}

// Static Pruning (compile-time)
type StaticPruning struct {
    HeadPruning    bool     // Remove attention heads
    LayerPruning   bool     // Skip layers
    ChannelPruning bool     // Reduce KV dimension
}

// Dynamic Pruning (runtime)
type DynamicPruning struct {
    ImportanceScoring  func(k, q []float64) float64
    EvictionPolicy     string  // "LRU", "importance", "random"
    RefreshInterval    int     // Re-score every N tokens
}
```

### SnapKV and MorphKV Compression

```
SnapKV (NeurIPS 2024):
- Observation: Attention patterns converge early
- Method: Cluster + select representative KV pairs
- Compression: Up to 10× with <1% accuracy loss
- Compatible with any attention architecture

MorphKV (2025):
- Dynamic compression ratio per layer
- Entropy-guided importance scoring
- Higher compression for redundant layers
- 2-3× additional compression over SnapKV

Combined Strategy:
1. SnapKV initial clustering
2. MorphKV adaptive compression
3. UniCAIM hardware acceleration
4. Overall: 20-30× memory reduction
```

### Hardware KV-Cache Implementation

```go
type HardwareKVCache struct {
    CAMArray      *FeFETCAMArray   // Key storage
    CIMArray      *CrossbarArray   // Value storage
    Controller    *CacheController
    Capacity      int              // Max entries
    Replacement   ReplacementPolicy
}

func (hkv *HardwareKVCache) AttentionLookup(query []float64) []float64 {
    // 1. Parallel key search in CAM
    matches := hkv.CAMArray.Search(query)

    // 2. Attention weight computation
    scores := hkv.CAMArray.GetSimilarityScores()

    // 3. Value retrieval from CIM
    values := hkv.CIMArray.Read(matches)

    // 4. Weighted sum in analog domain
    output := hkv.CIMArray.MVM(scores, values)

    return output
}
```

### Performance Comparison

```
Architecture Comparison:
┌────────────────────┬─────────┬─────────┬──────────┐
│ Metric             │ GPU     │ Digital │ UniCAIM  │
│                    │ (A100)  │ CIM     │ (FeFET)  │
├────────────────────┼─────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Attention latency  │ 1×      │ 0.3×    │ 0.12×    │
│ Energy per token   │ 1×      │ 0.4×    │ 0.08×    │
│ Memory bandwidth   │ 2TB/s   │ N/A     │ N/A      │
│ AEDP (relative)    │ 1×      │ 0.5×    │ 0.12×    │
└────────────────────┴─────────┴─────────┴──────────┘

UniCAIM achieves 8.2× AEDP reduction through:
- Eliminated data movement (in-memory)
- Parallel CAM search (O(1) vs O(n))
- Analog computation (no ADC bottleneck)
```

### Flash Joule Heating Overview

Flash Joule Heating (research sample) is a rapid synthesis technique developed by Dr. external research group's group at external research institution:

```
research sample Principle:
- High-current electrical pulse through carbon source
- Resistive heating to extreme temperatures
- Millisecond timescale synthesis
- Enables novel material phases

Key Parameters:
- Peak temperature: 3000-3500 K
- Pulse duration: 10-500 ms
- Heating rate: 10^4 - 10^5 K/s
- Cooling rate: 10^3 - 10^4 K/s
- Current density: 10^3 - 10^4 A/cm²
```

```
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

Quality Metrics:

- Yield: >90% conversion
- Purity: >99% carbon
```

### Flash-Within-Flash (FWF) Technology

```
FWF Concept (Nature Chemistry 2024):
- Two-stage rapid thermal treatment
- Different temperature profiles
- Enables compound synthesis
- 50% energy reduction vs conventional

FWF Temperature Profile:
    T(K)
    3000│    ┌──┐
        │   ╱    ╲         Flash 1
    2000│──╱      ╲
        │          ╲       ┌──┐
    1500│           ╲     ╱    ╲  Flash 2
        │            ╲───╱      ╲──
    1000│
        └────────────────────────────→ t(ms)
            10   20   30   40   50

Applications:
- In₂Se₃ synthesis (2D ferroelectric)
- HZO phase engineering
- Metal oxide nanoparticles
- Compound semiconductors
```

### research sample for HZO Synthesis

```go
type HZOSynthesisConfig struct {
    PrecursorRatio    float64  // Hf:Zr ratio (0.5 for 50:50)
    FlashTemperature  float64  // 2500-3000 K
    FlashDuration     float64  // 10-50 ms
    CoolingRate       float64  // K/s
    SubstrateTemp     float64  // Pre-heat temperature
}

func SynthesizeHZO(config HZOSynthesisConfig) *HZOProduct {
    // Rapid heating induces metastable orthorhombic phase
    phase := DeterminePhase(config)

    // Quench rate affects domain structure
    grainSize := CalculateGrainSize(config.CoolingRate)

    // Ferroelectric properties
    polarization := CalculatePolarization(phase, grainSize)

    return &HZOProduct{
        Phase:         phase,
        Polarization:  polarization,
        GrainSize:     grainSize,
        Thickness:     config.Thickness,
    }
}
```

```
Energy Comparison:
┌──────────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┐
│ Synthesis Method     │ Energy    │ Time      │
├──────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┤
│ Conventional ALD     │ 100×      │ Hours     │
│ Conventional CVD     │ 50×       │ 30 min    │
│ Flash Joule Heating  │ 1×        │ 10-50 ms  │

```

### research sample Temperature Dynamics

```
Heating Phase (0-10ms):
    T(t) = T₀ + ΔT × (1 - exp(-t/τ_heat))
    where τ_heat ~ 1-2 ms

Peak Temperature:
    T_peak = I² × R × t / (m × C_p)
    Typically 3000-3500 K

Cooling Phase (post-pulse):
    T(t) = T_peak × exp(-t/τ_cool)
    τ_cool ~ 10-50 ms (radiative)

Graphene Quality vs Temperature:
    T < 2500K: Amorphous carbon
    T = 2500-2800K: Mixed phases
    T = 2800-3200K: High-quality graphene
    T > 3200K: Sublimation begins
```

### research sample Apparatus Design

```
Laboratory research sample Setup:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                         │
│   ┌───┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌───┐    │
│   │ + ├─────┤ SAMPLE   ├─────┤ - │    │
│   └───┘     │ (carbon) │     └───┘    │
│    │        └──────────┘        │      │
│    │             │              │      │
│  ┌─┴─────────────┴──────────────┴─┐   │
│  │   Capacitor Bank (1000µF)      │   │
│  │   Voltage: 100-400V            │   │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                         │
│  Control: Pulse width, repetition,      │
│           sample mass, electrode gap    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Typical Parameters:
- Capacitance: 60-1000 µF
- Voltage: 100-400 V
- Pulse energy: 0.5-80 kJ
- Sample mass: 0.1-10 g
```

### HZO Flash Synthesis Protocol

```go
type FJHSimulator struct {
    Config      FJHConfig
    State       FJHState
    Product     *SynthesisProduct
}

func (fjh *FJHSimulator) Simulate(precursor Precursor) *SynthesisProduct {
    // Pre-flash conditioning
    fjh.State.Temperature = fjh.Config.PreheateTemp

    // Flash pulse
    for t := 0.0; t < fjh.Config.PulseDuration; t += dt {
        power := fjh.CalculatePower(t)
        dT := power * dt / (fjh.State.Mass * fjh.State.SpecificHeat)
        fjh.State.Temperature += dT - fjh.CalculateRadiativeLoss()
    }

    // Quench phase
    fjh.State.Temperature = fjh.QuenchWithProfile()

    // Determine product phase
    fjh.Product = fjh.DetermineProduct(precursor)

    return fjh.Product
}
```

## Section 258: Ferroelectric Reservoir Computing

### Reservoir Computing Fundamentals

Reservoir computing (RC) is a framework for temporal information processing derived from recurrent neural networks:

```
Echo State Network (ESN) Architecture:
┌─────────────┐
│   Input     │
│   u(t)      │
└──────┬──────┘
       │ W_in (fixed, random)
       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Reservoir (fixed)           │
│  ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐   │
│  │ x │─│ x │─│ x │─│ x │─│ x │   │
│  └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘   │
│    └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘      │
│         W_res (fixed, sparse)      │
└──────────────┬─────────────────────┘
               │ W_out (trainable)
               ▼
        ┌─────────────┐
        │   Output    │
        │   y(t)      │
        └─────────────┘

Key Properties:
- Reservoir weights fixed (no backpropagation)
- Only readout layer trained (simple regression)
- Fading memory: recent inputs stronger influence
- Echo state property: stable dynamics (ρ < 1)
```

### FeFET-Based Physical Reservoir

FeFETs provide natural reservoir dynamics through polarization memory:

```
FeFET Reservoir Advantages:
1. Inherent nonlinearity (polarization switching)
2. Fading memory (volatile state decay)
3. High dimensionality (many devices)
4. Low power (in-memory computation)

Dual-Memory FeFET (arXiv Nov 2025):
┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Non-Quasi-Static STM             │
│   - Overlap-driven charge trapping │
│   - Fast, volatile (~ms)           │
│   - Encodes recent input sequences │
├────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Ferroelectric LTM                │
│   - Polarization switching         │
│   - Slow, persistent (~hours)      │
│   - Stores learned patterns        │
└────────────────────────────────────┘

Combined Effect:
- Projects inputs to high-dimensional space
- Ideal for edge inference applications
```

### Leaky-FeFET Memory Capacity Improvement

```
Leaky-FeFET Neuron (ResearchGate Nov 2024):

Standard FeFET:
- STM capacity: 30 bits
- Parity Check: 70% accuracy

Leaky-FeFET:
- STM capacity: 53.6 bits (+78.6%)
- Parity Check: 91% accuracy (+62.9%)

Mechanism:
- Leaky integration introduces temporal filtering
- Slower decay preserves more history
- Better separation of temporal patterns

Speech Classification: 98.1% accuracy
(FeFET in parallel configuration)
```

### All-Ferroelectric Implementation

Nature Communications 2023 demonstrated complete ferroelectric RC:

```
All-Ferroelectric RC System:
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Reservoir: Volatile FDs              │
│  ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐        │
│  │ FD │ │ FD │ │ FD │ │ FD │ ...    │
│  └────┘ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘        │
│  Pt/BiFeO₃/SrRuO₃                    │
│  Eimp > 0 → Polarization back-switch │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Readout: Nonvolatile FDs            │
│  ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐              │
│  │ FD │ │ FD │ │ FD │ (trained)    │
│  └────┘ └────┘ └────┘              │
│  Eimp = 0 → Stable states            │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

Ferroelectric Diode Operation:
- Polarization modulates Schottky barrier
- Thick film (100-200nm) reduces depolarization
- Imprint field controls volatile/nonvolatile

Results:
- Hénon map prediction: NRMSE = 0.017
- Long-term stability in ambient air
- High endurance (>10^6 cycles)
- Low power consumption
```

### Reservoir Computing Tasks

```go
// Short-Term Memory (STM) Task
type STMTask struct {
    SequenceLength int      // Input sequence length
    DelaySteps     int      // Steps to recall past input
    MemoryCapacity float64  // Measured MC (bits)
}

// Parity Check (PC) Task
type ParityCheckTask struct {
    WindowSize     int      // Bits to XOR
    Accuracy       float64  // Classification accuracy
}

// Time-Series Prediction
type TimeSeriesTask struct {
    SeriesType     string   // "henon", "mackey_glass", "lorenz"
    PredictionHorizon int   // Steps ahead to predict
    NRMSE          float64  // Normalized RMSE
}
```

### HZO FeFET Reservoir Parameters

```
Ferroelectric CIM HZO Reservoir:
┌────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Parameter          │ Value          │
├────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ Hf:Zr ratio        │ 50:50          │
│ Film thickness     │ 10 nm          │
│ Phase              │ Orthorhombic   │
│ Coercive field     │ ~1 MV/cm       │
│ Retention (NV)     │ >10 years      │
│ Decay time (V)     │ 1-100 ms       │
│ Endurance          │ >10^10 cycles  │
│ Operating voltage  │ 2-3 V          │
└────────────────────┴────────────────┘

Grain size affects dynamics:
- Larger grains: slower switching, longer memory
- Smaller grains: faster response, better nonlinearity
- Optimal: 15-25 nm for reservoir computing
```

### References

- [All-Ferroelectric RC - Nature Communications 2023](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-39371-y)
- [Dual-Memory FeFET - arXiv Nov 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.07830)
- [Leaky-FeFET Reservoir - ResearchGate 2024](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/385696559)
- [FeFET RC Overview - MRS Bulletin 2025](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1557/s43577-025-00990-z)
- [HZO/Si FeFET RC - IEEE VLSI 2022](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9830412/)

## Section 259: In-Sensor Computing for Edge AI

### In-Sensor vs Traditional Processing

```
Traditional Vision Pipeline:
┌────────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌────────┐
│ Sensor │→→→│ ADC │→→→│ CPU │→→→│ Result │
└────────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └────────┘
     ↑           ↑         ↑
   Light    Conversion   Heavy
   capture   bottleneck   compute

In-Sensor Computing:
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  Sensor + Compute (fused)   │
│  ┌────────┐ ┌──────────┐   │
│  │ Photo- │→│ Analog   │   │→→→ Result
│  │ sensor │ │ CIM      │   │
│  └────────┘ └──────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────┘
         No ADC bottleneck

Benefits:
- 10-100× energy reduction
- 10-100× latency reduction
- Reduced data movement
- Privacy (local processing)
```

### M3D-SAIL Architecture

Monolithic 3D Sensor-AI-Logic integration (npj 2025):

```
M3D-SAIL Stack:
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Layer 3: IGZO Photosensor Array       │
│  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┬───┐               │
│  │ P │ P │ P │ P │ P │ ... (32×32)   │
│  └───┴───┴───┴───┴───┘               │
│  IGZO-FET photosensors               │
├───────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Layer 2: IGZO-FET + RRAM CIM         │
│  ┌───────────────────────┐           │
│  │  1T1R Array (1k-bit)  │           │
│  │  IGZO access transistor│           │
│  │  RRAM weight storage  │           │
│  └───────────────────────┘           │
├───────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Layer 1: Si CMOS Logic               │
│  Control, peripheral circuits        │
│  45nm technology node                │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘

Inter-Layer Vias (ILVs):
- Ultra-dense vertical connections
- >10^6 vias/mm²
- Low parasitic capacitance
```

### Performance Comparison

```
M3D-SAIL vs 2D Architecture:

┌────────────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
│ Metric         │ 2D       │ M3D-SAIL │
├────────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Energy         │ 1×       │ 0.032×   │
│ (31.5× lower)  │          │          │
├────────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Speed          │ 1×       │ 1.91×    │
│ (91% faster)   │          │          │
├────────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Footprint      │ 1×       │ 0.3×     │
│                │          │          │
├────────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Accuracy       │ 97%      │ 96.7%    │
│ (keyframes)    │          │          │
└────────────────┴──────────┴──────────┘

Energy Efficiency Scaling:
- 45nm IGZO: 138 TOPS/W (with peripherals)
- Array only: 686 TOPS/W
```

### IGZO Transistor Properties

```
IGZO-FET Characteristics:
- Mobility: 10-30 cm²/Vs
- Low leakage: <10^-18 A/μm
- Low temperature process: <400°C
- Compatible with back-end integration
- Almost nonvolatile operation

IGZO 2T0C DRAM (PMC 2025):
- 3-bit storage per cell
- >100 second retention
- 94.95% image recognition accuracy
- 8×8 3D stacked array demonstrated
```

### Spectral CNN Chip

Nature Communications 2024 demonstrated optical in-sensor computing:

```
Spectral CNN Architecture:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Matter Meta-Imaging Layer              │
│  ┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐       │
│  │ λ₁  │ λ₂  │ λ₃  │ ... │ λₙ  │       │
│  │filter│filter│filter│   │filter│       │
│  └──┬──┴──┬──┴──┬──┴─────┴──┬──┘       │
│     │     │     │           │          │
│     ▼     ▼     ▼           ▼          │
│  Spectral Inner Products (optical)      │
│  ═══════════════════════════════════   │
│  Parallel analog computation           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Performance:
- Pathological diagnosis: >96% accuracy
- Face anti-spoofing: ~100% accuracy
- Video-rate processing
- Ultra-high energy efficiency
- First integrated optical computing
  with natural (incoherent) light
```

### Retinomorphic Sensors

Bio-inspired in-sensor preprocessing:

```go
type RetinomorphicSensor struct {
    // Photoreceptor layer
    Cones [][]Photoreceptor  // Color sensing
    Rods  [][]Photoreceptor  // Low-light

    // Processing layers (in-sensor)
    BipolarCells   [][]float64  // Center-surround
    GanglionCells  [][]float64  // Event detection
}

Processing Pipeline:
1. Light adaptation (Weber-Fechner law)
2. Center-surround filtering (edge enhance)
3. Temporal derivative (motion detection)
4. Event output (sparse representation)

Benefits:
- 1000× data reduction
- Inherent preprocessing
- Always-on operation <1mW
```

### Neuromorphic Vision Sensors

```
Speck Chip (fully neuromorphic):
┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS)       │
│  - Event-driven output             │
│  - 120dB dynamic range             │
│  - µs temporal resolution          │
├────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Asynchronous SNN Processor        │
│  - Fully event-driven              │
│  - <1mW idle power                 │
│  - On-chip learning               │
└────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Advantage:
- No frame-based bottleneck
- Processes only changes
- Ideal for edge robotics
```

### In-Sensor CIM Implementations

```
Implementation Comparison:
┌──────────────────┬─────────┬───────────┐
│ Technology       │ TOPS/W  │ Accuracy  │
├──────────────────┼─────────┼───────────┤
│ ReRAM 1T1R (65nm)│ 78.4    │ 96%       │
│ ReRAM (22nm)     │ 121     │ 97%       │
│ IGZO-RRAM (M3D)  │ 138     │ 96.7%     │
│ Optical SCNN     │ >1000   │ 96-100%   │
│ FeFET CIM        │ ~200    │ 97%       │
└──────────────────┴─────────┴───────────┘

Sony 3D Vision Chip:
- 3-layer vertical integration
- 1000 fps processing speed
- Full vision pipeline on-chip
```

### Ferroelectric CIM In-Sensor Integration

```
HZO-Based In-Sensor CIM:
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 3: Photosensor Array            │
│  (standard CMOS or IGZO)               │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 2: HZO FeFET CIM Array          │
│  - Ferroelectric CIM HZO superlattice        │
│  - 6-bit weight precision              │
│  - 10^12 endurance                     │
│  - In-memory MAC operations            │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Layer 1: Control Logic (CMOS)         │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

Advantages vs RRAM:
- Better endurance (10^12 vs 10^6)
- Lower variation
- CMOS-compatible process
- Better retention
```

## Section 260: Optical Ferroelectric Photonic Computing

### Photonic Neural Network Fundamentals

Optical neural networks leverage photonic advantages for AI acceleration:

```
Photonic Computing Benefits:
- Sub-nanosecond latency
- THz bandwidth potential
- Low heat dissipation
- Intrinsic parallelism (WDM)
- Energy-efficient MVM

MVM Methods:
1. Plane Light Conversion (PLC)
2. Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI)
3. Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)
4. Micro-Ring Resonator (MRR)
```

### Mach-Zehnder Interferometer Architecture

MZI-based photonic computing implements unitary matrices:

```
MZI Unit Cell:
         ┌───────────┐
    ──►──┤  θ phase  ├──►──┬──►── Output 1
         └───────────┘     │
              BS           ×    BS
         ┌───────────┐     │
    ──►──┤  φ phase  ├──►──┴──►── Output 2
         └───────────┘

Clements Rectangular Mesh:
┌───┬───┬───┬───┐
│MZI│MZI│MZI│MZI│
├───┼───┼───┼───┤
│MZI│MZI│MZI│MZI│
├───┼───┼───┼───┤
│MZI│MZI│MZI│MZI│
└───┴───┴───┴───┘

For N×N unitary: N(N-1)/2 MZIs
SVD decomposition: U * Σ * V†
```

### Performance Metrics

```
State-of-the-Art Results:
┌─────────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Metric                  │ Value          │
├─────────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ Compute speed           │ 43.8 GOPS/ch   │
│ Energy efficiency       │ 0.0576 pJ/OP   │
│ Modulation rate         │ 40 Gbaud       │
│ Total throughput        │ 1.28 TOPS      │
│ (16-channel coherent)   │                │
├─────────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ MEMS-MZI speed          │ 100× faster    │
│ MEMS-MZI efficiency     │ 7500 IPS/W     │
│ MEMS-MZI precision      │ 8-bit          │
└─────────────────────────┴────────────────┘
```

### Ferroelectric-based Pockels Photonic Memory

Nature Communications 2025 breakthrough combining ferroelectric + LiNbO₃:

```
Device Architecture:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Top Gate (Metal)                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  HZO Ferroelectric (10nm)               │
│  - Switchable polarization              │
│  - Low coercive field                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  IGZO Channel                           │
│  - Heterojunction engineered            │
│  - High mobility (15 cm²/Vs)            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LiNbO₃ Waveguide                       │
│  - Pockels effect (r33 = 30.8 pm/V)     │
│  - Micro-ring resonator                 │
│  - Ultra-low loss (0.07 dB)             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Operating Principle:
1. HZO polarization → Electric field
2. Field → Pockels index change
3. Index change → Resonance shift
4. Resonance → Optical transmission
```

### Pockels Memory Performance

```
Key Metrics (Nature Communications 2025):
┌────────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Parameter              │ Value          │
├────────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ Multi-level states     │ 6 states       │
│ Switching energy       │ 65.1 fJ/state  │
│ (vs others: 100× lower)│                │
│ Data retention         │ 10 years       │
│ Endurance              │ >10⁷ cycles    │
│ Optical loss           │ 0.07 dB        │
│ Total switching energy │ 4.6 pJ         │
└────────────────────────┴────────────────┘

Comparison:
- PCM photonic: ~6500 fJ/state
- Chalcogenide: ~1000 fJ/state
- Pockels memory: 65.1 fJ/state (100×!)
```

### Thin-Film LiNbO₃ (TFLN) Computing

```go
type TFLNCompute struct {
    ModulationBW    float64  // >100 GHz
    ElectroOptic    float64  // 30.8 pm/V
    VpiL            float64  // 2.0 V·cm
    PropLoss        float64  // 0.027 dB/cm
    EnergyPerOP     float64  // 0.0576 pJ
    ComputeSpeed    float64  // 43.8 GOPS/ch
}

Advantages of TFLN:
- High electro-optic coefficient
- Low propagation loss
- CMOS-compatible integration
- Efficient modulation
- Wide bandwidth
```

### Integrated Photonic Computing Circuit

```
TFLN Photonic Computer (Nature Comms 2025):
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Input Encoding (Amplitude Modulation)   │
│  ┌────┬────┬────┬────┐                  │
│  │ λ₁ │ λ₂ │ λ₃ │ λ₄ │ (WDM channels)   │
│  └─┬──┴─┬──┴─┬──┴─┬──┘                  │
│    │    │    │    │                      │
│    ▼    ▼    ▼    ▼                      │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐                │
│  │    MZI Mesh         │                │
│  │    (Unitary)        │                │
│  └─────────────────────┘                │
│            │                            │
│    ┌───────▼───────┐                    │
│    │ Pockels Memory │                   │
│    │ (Weight Store) │                   │
│    └───────────────┘                    │
│            │                            │
│    Photodetector Array                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

Tasks Demonstrated:
- Binary classification: 97%+ accuracy
- Complex image inference
- Real-time operation
```

## Section 261: NeuroBench and CIM Benchmarking Standards

### NeuroBench Framework Overview

NeuroBench provides standardized benchmarks for neuromorphic computing:

```
Framework Structure:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           NeuroBench Framework          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Algorithm Track (HW-independent)       │
│  - Complexity metrics                   │
│  - Correctness metrics                  │
│  - Solution-agnostic evaluation         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  System Track (Deployed systems)        │
│  - Latency                              │
│  - Throughput                           │
│  - Energy efficiency                    │
│  - Real hardware measurements           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Analogous to MLPerf for ML benchmarking
```

### Algorithm Track Benchmarks

```
Benchmark Categories:
1. Few-Shot Continual Learning
2. Computer Vision
3. Motor Cortical Decoding (NHP)
4. Chaotic Forecasting

Metrics:
┌────────────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│ Correctness            │ Complexity      │
├────────────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│ Accuracy               │ Footprint (B)   │
│ Top-K Accuracy         │ Connection      │
│ mAP (detection)        │ Sparsity        │
│ MSE (regression)       │ Activation      │
│ R² Score               │ Sparsity        │
│                        │ Effective MACs  │
│                        │ Effective ACs   │
│                        │ Synaptic Ops    │
└────────────────────────┴─────────────────┘
```

### Speech Commands Benchmark Results

```
Google Speech Commands (35 keywords):
┌──────────────┬─────────┬──────────┬─────────┐
│ Metric       │ ANN     │ SNN      │ Units   │
├──────────────┼─────────┼──────────┼─────────┤
│ Accuracy     │ 0.865   │ 0.856    │         │
│ Effective    │ 1.7M    │ 3.3M     │ ops     │
│ Footprint    │ 500KB   │ 450KB    │ bytes   │
│ Activation   │ 0%      │ 96.7%    │ sparse  │
│ Sparsity     │         │          │         │
└──────────────┴─────────┴──────────┴─────────┘

Key Insight: SNN achieves 96.7% sparsity
with only 0.9% accuracy loss
```

```
NHP Motor Cortex Decoding:
- Task: Predict fingertip velocity from neural signals
- Input: 192 electrode channels
- Output: x, y velocity

Remarkable SNN Results:
┌────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Metric             │ Value          │
├────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ R² Score           │ 0.6604         │
│ Effective Syn Ops  │ 304 (!!)       │
│ Footprint          │ ~5KB           │
│ Sparsity           │ 95%            │
└────────────────────┴────────────────┘

Only 304 synaptic operations for
high-quality velocity prediction!
```

```
Neuromorphic Hardware Comparison:
┌─────────────────┬───────┬─────────┬──────────┐
│ Platform        │ Tech  │ TOPS    │ TOPS/W   │
│                 │ (nm)  │         │          │
├─────────────────┼───────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Intel Loihi 2   │ 7     │ 0.15    │ 150      │
│ SpiNNaker 2     │ 22    │ 0.1     │ 10       │
│ BrainChip Akida │ 28    │ 1.25    │ 4,200    │
│ RRAM CIM        │ 22    │ 10      │ 100,000* │
```

### MLPerf Power Benchmark

```
MLPerf Power Methodology:
- Comprehensive energy efficiency evaluation
- μWatts to MWatts range coverage
- 1,841 reproducible measurements
- 60 systems across all deployment scales

Key Milestones:
- v4 (2024): System-wide power/energy
- Training 5.0 (2025): Latest version

MLPerf Tiny Results:
┌────────────────────┬─────────┬────────┐
│ Task               │ Power   │ Acc    │
├────────────────────┼─────────┼────────┤
│ Keyword Spotting   │ 5.64 mW │ 90%    │
│ Visual Wake Words  │ 10 mW   │ 85%    │
│ Image Classification│ 15 mW  │ 87%    │
│ Anomaly Detection  │ 8 mW    │ 85%    │
└────────────────────┴─────────┴────────┘

Minimum: 5.64 mW for always-on systems
```

### CIM-Specific Benchmark Considerations

```
CIM Accuracy Degradation Factors:
1. Quantization error (weight bits)
2. Conductance noise
3. Device-to-device variation
4. Stuck-on/stuck-off defects
5. IR drop in crossbar
6. ADC quantization

Energy Breakdown:
┌────────────────┬────────────────────┐
│ Component      │ Typical Energy     │
├────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ Analog array   │ 0.1 pJ/MAC         │
│ ADC (8-bit)    │ 0.1-1 pJ/conv      │
│ DAC (8-bit)    │ 0.05 pJ/conv       │
│ Digital logic  │ 0.01 pJ/op         │
└────────────────┴────────────────────┘

ADC dominates energy at high precision
```

### CIM Benchmark Results

```
ResNet on CIFAR-10:
┌──────────────────┬─────────┬──────────┐
│ Architecture     │ Acc (%) │ TOPS/W   │
├──────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Digital baseline │ 92.5    │ 10       │
│ RRAM CIM (8-bit) │ 90.2    │ 78.4     │
│ RRAM CIM (4-bit) │ 88.1    │ 121      │
│ FeFET CIM        │ 91.8    │ 200      │
│ Binary CIM       │ 53.6    │ 500      │
└──────────────────┴─────────┴──────────┘

Trade-off: Higher precision → better
accuracy but lower energy efficiency
```

### Ferroelectric CIM Benchmark Integration

```
Ferroelectric CIM Optical CIM Targets:
┌─────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Metric              │ Target         │
├─────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ MNIST accuracy      │ >99%           │
│ CIFAR-10 accuracy   │ >90%           │
│ Energy efficiency   │ >50,000 TOPS/W │
│ Endurance           │ >10¹² cycles   │
│ Weight precision    │ 4-6 bit        │
│ Latency             │ <10 ns         │
└─────────────────────┴────────────────┘

Advantages:
- HZO endurance (10¹² vs 10⁶ RRAM)
- Optical speed (ps vs ns)
- Pockels memory efficiency (65 fJ)
```

## Section 262: Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction (FTJ) Memristors

### FTJ Fundamentals

Ferroelectric tunnel junctions enable non-volatile memory through polarization-dependent tunneling:

```
FTJ Structure:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Top Electrode (Metal)                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Ferroelectric Layer (2-10 nm)          │
│  - HZO, BTO, PTO, BiFeO₃               │
│  - Polarization switchable              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Interlayer (optional, 1-2 nm)          │
│  - TiO₂, Al₂O₃                         │
│  - Improves interface quality           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Bottom Electrode (Metal/Semiconductor) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Requirements:
1. FE layer ≤3-5 nm for tunneling
2. Asymmetric interfaces for TER
3. Stable ferroelectric phase
```

### Tunneling Electroresistance (TER)

```
TER Mechanism:
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Polarization UP (→)                   │
│  ┌─────┐                              │
│  │     │ High barrier                  │
│  │     │ Low current                   │
│  └─────┴──────────────                │
│                                        │
│  Polarization DOWN (←)                │
│  ┌─┐                                  │
│  │ │ Low barrier                       │
│  │ │ High current                      │
│  └─┴──────────────────                │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

TER = R_high / R_low = I_low / I_high

Barrier modulation sources:
1. Depolarization field
2. Interface charge screening
3. Strain effects
4. Band bending
```

### Transport Mechanisms

```
Direct Tunneling (low voltage):
  J ∝ exp(-2κd)
  κ = √(2m*φ)/ℏ

  - Dominates for thin barriers
  - Exponential thickness dependence
  - Low current density

Fowler-Nordheim (high voltage):
  ln(J/V²) ∝ -1/V

  - Dominates at high bias
  - Triangular barrier
  - Higher current density

Transition at V ~ φ (barrier height)
Switching between mechanisms enhances TER
```

### HZO FTJ Performance (2024-2025)

```
5nm HZO FTJ Results:
┌────────────────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Parameter              │ Value          │
├────────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ 2Pr (polarization)     │ 47.33 µC/cm²   │
│ TER ratio              │ 2974.44%       │
│ ON/OFF ratio           │ 580× (50ns)    │
│ Endurance              │ 2×10⁸ cycles   │
│ Retention              │ >10⁵ seconds   │
│ Current density        │ 75 A/cm² @0.1V │
│ Conductance states     │ 128            │
│ Cycle-to-cycle var     │ 2.75%          │
└────────────────────────┴────────────────┘

48×48 Crossbar Array:
- 2,304 cells
- Uniform 3-bit programming
- Half-bias operation verified
```

### Synaptic Properties

```go
type FTJSynapse struct {
    Device       *FTJDevice
    States       int      // 128 states demonstrated
    Variation    float64  // 2.75% cycle-to-cycle
    Symmetric    bool     // Highly symmetric LTP/LTD
}

Reservoir Computing Results:
- MNIST: 98.24% accuracy
- Fashion-MNIST: 87.01% accuracy
- FTJ used as reservoir layer

Speech Recognition (HZO FTJ):
- Classification task demonstrated
- Analog synaptic behavior
- On-chip learning potential
```

### Ultra-Thin FTJ Development

```
Thickness Scaling:
┌────────────┬──────────┬────────────────┐
│ Thickness  │ TER      │ Challenge      │
├────────────┼──────────┼────────────────┤
│ 10 nm      │ ~100     │ Low current    │
│ 5 nm       │ ~3000    │ Optimal        │
│ 2 nm       │ ~100     │ FE unstable    │
│ 1 nm HZO   │ ~200     │ Depolarization │
│ 1 nm BSO   │ 7×10⁵    │ Best reported  │
└────────────┴──────────┴────────────────┘

Key Insight: 5nm HZO provides best
balance of TER and stability
```

### FTJ vs Other NVM Technologies

```
Comparison:
┌────────────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┐
│ Property       │ FTJ    │ RRAM   │ PCM    │
├────────────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┤
│ Cell size      │ 4F²    │ 4F²    │ 8F²    │
│ Write energy   │ 1.8 pJ │ 10 pJ  │ 100 pJ │
│ Endurance      │ 10⁸    │ 10⁶    │ 10⁸    │
│ Retention      │ 10 yr  │ 10 yr  │ 10 yr  │
│ States         │ 128    │ 64     │ 16     │
│ Switching      │ Field  │ Filament│ Thermal│
│ Variation      │ 2.75%  │ 10%    │ 5%     │
└────────────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┘

FTJ Advantages:
- Non-filamentary (reliable)
- Low operation current
- CMOS compatible (HfO₂-based)
- Compact cell size
```

### References

- [HZO FTJ 5nm - Journal of Alloys 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0925838825034309)
- [FTJ Crossbar Array - Adv. Intelligent Systems 2025](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aisy.202500817)

## Section 263: Negative Differential Resistance (NDR) Neuromorphic Devices

### NDR Fundamentals

Negative differential resistance enables spiking neuron behavior:

```
N-Shaped I-V Characteristic:
    I
    │      Peak
    │     ╱╲
    │    ╱  ╲ ← NDR Region
    │   ╱    ╲    (dI/dV < 0)
    │  ╱      ╲___Valley
    │ ╱           ╲___
    │╱                 ╲___
    └──────────────────────── V
       Vp   Vv

Key Parameters:
- Peak voltage (Vp): ~0.3V
- Valley voltage (Vv): ~0.6V
- Peak/Valley ratio: 10-1000
- NDR region enables oscillation
```

### Ultra-Robust NDR Memristor (Nature Comms 2025)

```
Quantum Well Structure:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AlAs barrier                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  In₀.₈Ga₀.₂As quantum well             │
│  - Resonant tunneling                   │
│  - Sharp NDR characteristic             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  AlAs barrier                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Performance:
┌────────────────────┬────────────────────┐
│ Parameter          │ Value              │
├────────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ Endurance (RT)     │ >10¹¹ cycles       │
│ Endurance (400°C)  │ >10⁹ cycles        │
│ Variation          │ 0.264%             │
│ Lifetime (10Hz)    │ >310 years         │
│ Peak/Valley ratio  │ ~100               │
└────────────────────┴────────────────────┘

Key Advantage:
- No external capacitor needed
- Membrane integration built-in
- Ultra-low variation
```

### FitzHugh-Nagumo Neuron Implementation

```
FHN Model Equations:
  dV/dt = V - V³/3 - W + I_ext
  dW/dt = (V + a - b·W) / τ

  V: membrane potential (fast)
  W: recovery variable (slow)

NDR-Based Implementation:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                         │
│  V_in ──┬──[NDR]──┬── V_out            │
│         │         │                     │
│         │    ┌────┴────┐               │
│         │    │ Active  │               │
│         │    │Inductor │               │
│         │    │ (MOSFET)│               │
│         │    └────┬────┘               │
│         │         │                     │
│         └─────────┴─── GND             │
│                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

NDR provides:
- Membrane capacitance effect
- Threshold behavior
- Reset mechanism
```

### Neuron Functions Demonstrated

```
9 Biological Neuron Behaviors:
┌────────────────────────┬───────────────┐
│ Function               │ Description   │
├────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ Phasic spiking         │ Single spike  │
│ Anodal break excitation│ Post-inhibit  │
│ Spike accommodation    │ Adaptation    │
│ Subthreshold oscillation│ Below thresh │
│ Class 1 excitable      │ Rate coding   │
│ All-or-nothing firing  │ Binary spike  │
│ Tonic bursting         │ Spike trains  │
│ Refractory period      │ Dead time     │
│ Accommodation          │ Threshold adj │
└────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

All from single NDR memristor circuit!
```

### Oscillatory Neural Networks

```
Coupled NDR Oscillators:
┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐
│NDR 1│───│NDR 2│───│NDR 3│
└──┬──┘   └──┬──┘   └──┬──┘
   │         │         │
   └────┬────┴────┬────┘
        │  Coupling │
        └──────────┘

Kuramoto Order Parameter:
  R = |1/N · Σ exp(i·θⱼ)|

  R = 0: Desynchronized
  R = 1: Fully synchronized

Applications:
- Associative memory
- Pattern recognition
- Combinatorial optimization
- Ising machine implementation
```

### STDP with NDR Neurons

```
STDP Learning Rule:
  Δw = A⁺·exp(-Δt/τ⁺)  if Δt > 0 (LTP)
  Δw = -A⁻·exp(Δt/τ⁻)  if Δt < 0 (LTD)

  Δt = t_post - t_pre

Oscillatory FHN + STDP (2025):
- First demonstration of STDP training
  for oscillatory FHN networks
- Intrinsic dynamics → natural spikes
- Unlike LIF phenomenological models
- Emergent computation from coupling
```

### Light-Induced NDR Neurons

```
Photonic NDR Neurons (Scientific Reports 2025):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  III-V Semiconductor Micropillar        │
│  ┌───────────────────────┐             │
│  │   RTD Photodetector   │             │
│  │   (resonant tunneling)│             │
│  └───────────────────────┘             │
│            ↓                           │
│  NIR Light → NDR activation            │
│            ↓                           │
│  Large-amplitude oscillations          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Applications:
- Photonic spiking neurons
- In-sensor computing
- Insect brain circuits
- Sensory processing
```

### NDR Device Comparison

```
NDR Technologies:
┌─────────────────┬───────┬────────┬───────┐
│ Device          │ P/V   │ Endur  │ Temp  │
│                 │ ratio │ cycles │ (°C)  │
├─────────────────┼───────┼────────┼───────┤
│ AlAs/InGaAs QW  │ 100   │ 10¹¹   │ 400   │
│ Tunnel diode    │ 10    │ 10⁹    │ 150   │
│ NbO₂ Mott      │ 1000  │ 10⁶    │ 85    │
│ Organic NDR     │ 5     │ 10⁴    │ 100   │
│ Ferroelectric   │ 50    │ 10⁸    │ 200   │
└─────────────────┴───────┴────────┴───────┘

Quantum well RTD best for reliability
```

### Ferroelectric CIM NDR Integration

```
Ferroelectric NDR Concept:
- Antiferroelectric HZO → NDR behavior
- Liu et al.: 37 fJ/spike
- No additional capacitor needed
- Scalable without external elements

Hybrid FTJ-NDR Architecture:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NDR Neuron Layer                       │
│  (QW or ferroelectric)                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  FTJ Synapse Array                      │
│  (HZO-based)                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  CMOS Control Logic                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Benefits:
- All-ferroelectric possible
- Compact (no capacitors)
- Low power
- High endurance
```

## 264. Ferroelectric Domain Engineering and Switching Pathways (Iteration 84)

### Domain Switching Fundamentals in HfO₂

Ferroelectric domain switching in HfO₂-based materials is fundamentally different from traditional perovskite ferroelectrics due to the unique crystal structure of the metastable orthorhombic Pca21 phase.

### 47 Polarization Switching Pathways (PNAS 2025)

```
Group Theory Analysis of HfO₂ Switching:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Comprehensive Pathway Enumeration                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Total pathways identified: 47                              │
│  - 180° reversal pathways: 8 primary mechanisms             │
│  - 90° switching pathways: 16 variants                      │
│  - Multi-step pathways: 23 complex routes                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Energy Barriers (unconventional pathways):                 │
│  - Range: 0.32 - 0.57 eV                                    │
│  - Varies with suboxygen lattice shifts                     │
│  - Interface effects lower barriers significantly           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Pathway Types:
┌────────────────────┬─────────┬──────────────┐
│ Type               │ Barrier │ Mechanism    │
│                    │ (eV)    │              │
├────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────┤
│ 180° direct        │ 0.32    │ Suboxygen    │
│                    │         │ shift type 1 │
│ 90° rotation       │ 0.35    │ In-plane     │
│                    │         │ reorientation│
│ Multi-step         │ 0.40    │ Via inter-   │
│                    │         │ mediate phase│
│ Pure HfO₂ bulk     │ 1.34    │ Domain wall  │
│                    │         │ motion       │
└────────────────────┴─────────┴──────────────┘
```

### Interface Engineering for Reduced Switching Energy

```
HfO₂/CeO₂ Superlattice Interface (2024):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Energy Barrier Reduction via Interface Engineering         │
│                                                             │
│  Pure HfO₂: E_barrier = 1.34 eV                            │
│           ↓                                                 │
│  HfO₂/CeO₂ superlattice:                                   │
│           ↓                                                 │
│  E_barrier = 0.57 eV (57% reduction!)                      │
│                                                             │
│  Mechanism:                                                 │
│  - Coherent interface engineering                           │
│  - Strain-modified energy landscape                         │
│  - Scale-free ferroelectricity preservation                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Domain Nucleation and Growth

```
In-Situ Electron Holography Observation (2025):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  HZO/Al₂O₃ Tunnel Junction Switching                       │
│                                                             │
│  Phase 1: Domain Nucleation                                │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │  ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑                   │             │
│  │  Initial uniform polarization            │             │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                    ↓ V < Vc                                │
│  Phase 2: Nucleation Sites Appear                          │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │  ↑↑↑↑↓↑↑↑↑↑↑↓↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑                   │             │
│  │  Reverse domains nucleate                │             │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                    ↓ V → Vc                                │
│  Phase 3: Lateral Domain Growth                            │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │  ↑↑↓↓↓↓↓↑↑↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↑↑↑↑                   │             │
│  │  Domains expand via wall motion          │             │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                    ↓ V > Vc                                │
│  Phase 4: Complete Reversal                                │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │  ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓                   │             │
│  │  Fully switched state                    │             │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Enhanced Switching via Co-Doping (La³⁺-Ta⁵⁺)

```
Acceptor-Donor Co-Doping Strategy (Nature Comm. 2024):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Challenge: Defects stabilize polar phase BUT               │
│             pin domain walls → sluggish switching           │
│                                                             │
│  Solution: La³⁺-Ta⁵⁺ co-doping in HfO₂                     │
│                                                             │
│  La³⁺ (acceptor) + Ta⁵⁺ (donor) → charge compensation     │
│                                                             │
│  Results:                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ • Enhanced ferroelectricity             │               │
│  │ • FASTEST switching ever reported       │               │
│  │   for HfO₂ polar devices                │               │
│  │ • Reduced domain wall pinning           │               │
│  │ • Maintained phase stability            │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Multidomain Properties for Neuromorphic Devices

```
Preisach Model for HZO Multidomain Response:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Small Grain Size (10-20 nm) → Multidomain Nature          │
│                                                             │
│  Advantages for Scaling:                                    │
│  • Multiple switching units per device                     │
│  • Quasi-continuous conductance tuning                     │
│  • >200 resistance states demonstrated                     │
│  • Factor of 60 resistance modulation                      │
│                                                             │
│  Computational Framework:                                   │
│  PFM characterization → Preisach model → Device simulation │
│                                                             │
│  Multi-timescale Synaptic Behavior:                        │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│  │ Fast: Ferroelectric switching (~ns)       │             │
│  │ Slow: Ionic drift/diffusion (~ms-s)       │             │
│  │ Combined: Multi-timescale plasticity      │             │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 265. Wake-Up and Fatigue Mechanisms in HZO Ferroelectrics (Iteration 84)

### Oxygen Vacancy Role in Reliability

Oxygen vacancies (V_O) are the dominant defect species controlling wake-up, fatigue, and breakdown in HfO₂-based ferroelectrics. Understanding their behavior is critical for CIM reliability.

### Wake-Up Mechanism

```
Oxygen Vacancy Redistribution During Wake-Up:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  PRISTINE STATE (Cycle 0):                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ █████████████████████████████████████████│ Top IF      │
│  │ ○○○○○○●○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○●○○○○○○○○○○○○○│ Bulk        │
│  │ █████████████████████████████████████████│ Bottom IF   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│  █ = High vacancy concentration at interfaces              │
│  ● = Low vacancy concentration in bulk                     │
│                                                             │
│              ↓ Electrical cycling                          │
│                                                             │
│  WOKEN-UP STATE (After ~1000 cycles):                      │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ ███████████████████████████████████████○│ Top IF      │
│  │ ○○●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●○○│ Bulk        │
│  │ ○███████████████████████████████████████│ Bottom IF   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│  Vacancies diffuse INTO bulk → domain depinning            │
│  → Increased Pr, more uniform E-field                      │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Wake-Up Process Parameters:
┌────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
│ Parameter          │ Typical Value        │
├────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
│ Initial Pr         │ 10-15 μC/cm²        │
│ Max Pr (woken up)  │ 30-40 μC/cm²        │
│ Wake-up cycles     │ 100 - 10,000        │
│ Ec change          │ Decreases 20-40%    │
│ Built-in field     │ Becomes uniform     │
└────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘
```

### Fatigue Mechanisms

```
Two Types of Fatigue in HfO₂-Based Ferroelectrics:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  TYPE 1: DEGRADATION-TYPE FATIGUE                          │
│  ───────────────────────────────────                        │
│  P-V loop shrinkage with cycling                           │
│                                                             │
│  Mechanism: Oxygen vacancy redistribution                   │
│  - Vacancies accumulate at grain boundaries                │
│  - Phase transformation: Ortho → Tetra/Mono                │
│  - Gradual Pr decrease over 10⁷-10¹⁰ cycles               │
│                                                             │
│  Pr(N) = Pr_max × exp[-(N/τ)^β]                            │
│  where β ≈ 0.5 (stretched exponential)                     │
│                                                             │
│  ──────────────────────────────────────                    │
│                                                             │
│  TYPE 2: BREAKDOWN-TYPE FATIGUE                            │
│  ───────────────────────────────────                        │
│  Sudden failure without prior Pr degradation               │
│                                                             │
│  Mechanism: Conductive filament formation                   │
│  - Oxygen vacancies form percolation path                  │
│  - High field stress accelerates defect generation         │
│  - Trap density increases dramatically                     │
│  - Dielectric breakdown at ~10⁹-10¹¹ cycles               │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Phase Transformation During Fatigue (Zhang et al. 2024):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Orthorhombic (polar) → Non-polar phases                   │
│                                                             │
│  Oxygen vacancy redistribution causes:                      │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ O-phase (Pca21)    →  T-phase (P42/nmc) │               │
│  │ Ferroelectric         Antiferroelectric  │               │
│  │       ↓                                  │               │
│  │ Loss of remanent polarization            │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
│  Both theory (DFT) and experiment confirm this             │
│  mechanism is similar to PZT fatigue (domain pinning)      │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Electrical Pulse Engineering for Reliability Control

```
Novel Pulse Engineering Approach (Small Science 2024):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  WAKE-UP ACCELERATION:                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┐                        │
│  │   Short square pulses (~100ns) │                        │
│  │   ___     ___     ___          │                        │
│  │  |   |   |   |   |   |         │                        │
│  │──┘   └───┘   └───┘   └──       │                        │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘                        │
│  Result: 1.5× faster wake-up                               │
│                                                             │
│  FATIGUE RETARDATION:                                       │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┐                        │
│  │   Bipolar lower-amplitude      │                        │
│  │    ___     ___                 │                        │
│  │   |   |   |   |                │                        │
│  │───┘   └───┘   └───             │                        │
│  │      ╲___╱   ╲___╱             │                        │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘                        │
│  Result: 4× to 24× slower fatigue                          │
│                                                             │
│  LEAKAGE REDUCTION:                                         │
│  - Pulse train at 1 MHz                                    │
│  - 59% leakage reduction                                   │
│  - 20-80% polarization loss reduction                      │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Fatigue-Free Interface Design

```
CeO₂-x "Oxygen Sponge" Interface (Nature Comm. 2025):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Conventional MFM:           Oxygen Sponge Design:         │
│  ┌──────────────┐           ┌──────────────┐              │
│  │   TiN/Pt     │           │   TiN/Pt     │              │
│  ├──────────────┤           ├──────────────┤              │
│  │     HZO      │           │   CeO₂-x    │ ← O sponge   │
│  │              │           ├──────────────┤              │
│  │ Vo clusters  │           │     HZO      │              │
│  │   at IF      │           │   (clean)    │              │
│  ├──────────────┤           ├──────────────┤              │
│  │   TiN/Pt     │           │   CeO₂-x    │ ← O sponge   │
│  └──────────────┘           ├──────────────┤              │
│                             │   TiN/Pt     │              │
│  Fatigue ~10⁷               └──────────────┘              │
│                             Fatigue >10¹⁰                  │
│                                                             │
│  CeO₂-x Properties:                                        │
│  • Multivalent Ce³⁺/Ce⁴⁺ → oxygen activity                │
│  • Reversibly absorbs/releases oxygen                      │
│  • Prevents vacancy accumulation at interface              │
│  • Reduces coercive field (lower cycling stress)           │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Superlattice Approach (Nature Comm. 2025):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Epitaxial (HfO₂)n/(ZrO₂)n Superlattice                    │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                      │
│  │ HfO₂ │ ZrO₂ │ HfO₂ │ ZrO₂ │ ... │                      │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘                      │
│                                                             │
│  Performance:                                               │
│  ┌────────────────────┬──────────────┐                     │
│  │ Property           │ Value        │                     │
│  ├────────────────────┼──────────────┤                     │
│  │ Stable thickness   │ up to 100 nm │                     │
│  │ Endurance          │ >10⁹ cycles  │                     │
│  │ Coercive field     │ ~0.85 MV/cm  │                     │
│  │ Fatigue recovery   │ Superior     │                     │
│  └────────────────────┴──────────────┘                     │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Oxygen Vacancy Engineering

```
Controlled Vacancy Concentration (2024-2025):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Target: Low vacancy concentration with uniform distribution│
│                                                             │
│  Optimal Vo concentration: 1.9%                            │
│  (vs typical 2.5-5% in standard processes)                 │
│                                                             │
│  Benefits of Low Vo:                                        │
│  • Suppressed defect migration during cycling              │
│  • Milder imprint (Ec shift <0.6 MV/cm at 120°C)          │
│  • Enhanced endurance (>10⁹ cycles)                        │
│                                                             │
│  W Electrode Engineering:                                   │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐                │
│  │ Electrode │ Vo conc │ Wake-up │ Phase  │                │
│  ├───────────┼─────────┼─────────┼────────┤                │
│  │ TiN       │ 4.2%    │ Strong  │ Mixed  │                │
│  │ W         │ 2.5%    │ Weak    │ O-dom  │                │
│  │ Heterost. │ 2.49%   │ Minimal │ Stable │                │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘                │
│                                                             │
│  W promotes O-phase formation, reduces vacancies           │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Region-Selective Oxygen Vacancy Engineering (2025):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Low-Temperature (300°C) Process Enabled!                  │
│                                                             │
│  DFT Finding: Vo reduces T→O phase transition barrier      │
│  → Strategic Vo placement enables low-temp crystallization │
│                                                             │
│  Results at 300°C:                                         │
│  • Pr = 36.4 μC/cm² (excellent!)                          │
│  • Endurance > 10⁹ cycles                                  │
│  • BEOL compatible (critical for integration)              │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### FeFET-Specific Fatigue Considerations

```
FeFET vs FeCap Fatigue:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Observation: FeFET fatigue (~10⁵) << FeCap (~10⁸)         │
│                                                             │
│  Why? Different dominant mechanism:                         │
│                                                             │
│  FeCap Fatigue:              FeFET Fatigue:                │
│  • Ferroelectric film        • NOT ferroelectric film!     │
│    degradation               • Charge trapping in IL       │
│  • Vo redistribution         • Trap generation at IF       │
│  • Phase transformation      • High IF field stress        │
│                                                             │
│  Solutions for FeFET:                                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ 1. High-k interfacial layer             │               │
│  │ 2. Oxide semiconductor channel          │               │
│  │ 3. Interface passivation                │               │
│  │ 4. MFMIS gate stack                     │               │
│  │    (Metal-Ferro-Metal-Insulator-Semi)   │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Ferroelectric CIM Domain-Aware CIM Integration

```
Domain-Aware CIM Array Design:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Key Insight: Model domain switching at cell level         │
│               for accurate reliability prediction           │
│                                                             │
│  Implementation in domain_fatigue.go:                       │

│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │  DomainAwareCIMCell                      │               │
│  │  ├── Domains: *DomainStructure          │               │
│  │  │   └── Individual domain tracking     │               │
│  │  ├── Vacancies: *OxygenVacancyDistrib   │               │
│  │  │   └── Vo position & migration        │               │
│  │  ├── Cycles: int64                      │               │
│  │  └── State: WakeUpState                 │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
│  Benefits:                                                  │
│  • Accurate lifetime prediction                            │
│  • State-dependent accuracy modeling                       │
│  • Pulse engineering integration                           │
│  • Fatigue-free design validation                          │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 266. Ferroelectric Memcapacitor Arrays for Ultra-Low Power CIM (Iteration 85)

### Memcapacitor vs Memristor: A Paradigm Shift

Ferroelectric memcapacitors represent a fundamental shift from resistive to capacitive in-memory computing, eliminating key limitations of memristor-based systems.

### Key Advantages of Capacitive CIM

```
Resistive vs Capacitive Crossbar Comparison:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  RESISTIVE CROSSBAR (Memristor):                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ • Static current flow (always on)       │               │
│  │ • Sneak path currents                   │               │
│  │ • Severe IR drop with scaling           │               │
│  │ • Selector devices required             │               │
│  │ • High variability (5-15%)              │               │
│  │ • Write energy: ~pJ per cell            │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
│  CAPACITIVE CROSSBAR (Memcapacitor):                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ • ZERO static power (open circuit!)     │               │
│  │ • NO sneak paths                        │               │
│  │ • Minimal IR drop                       │               │
│  │ • Selector-free operation               │               │
│  │ • Low variability (1-3%)                │               │
│  │ • Write energy: ~fJ per cell            │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
│  Energy Comparison (22nm node):                            │
│  Capacitive is 20-200× more energy efficient!              │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HZO Memcapacitor Performance (Nano Energy 2025)

```
Device Characteristics:
┌────────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
│ Parameter              │ Value                │
├────────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
│ Memory window          │ ~7.8 fF/μm²         │
│ Endurance              │ >10⁹ cycles          │
│ Retention              │ >10 years            │
│ Dynamic energy         │ 31 fJ/inference      │
│ Static power           │ ~ZERO                │
│ MNIST accuracy         │ 96.68%               │
│ Training epochs        │ 200                  │
└────────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘

Work Function Engineering:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Key Innovation: Electrode work function tuning             │
│                                                             │
│  ┌──────────┐   Band engineering    ┌──────────┐           │
│  │ Electrode │ ←───────────────────→ │   HZO    │           │
│  │ (TiN/W)  │   Work function Δ     │ Ferro    │           │
│  └──────────┘                       └──────────┘           │
│                                                             │
│  Result: Enhanced ON/OFF ratio and memory window           │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Charge-Domain CIM Operation

```
Capacitive MVM Principle:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Input: V_j (voltage on column j)                          │
│  Weight: C_ij (capacitance of cell i,j)                    │
│  Output: Q_i = Σ_j C_ij × V_j (charge on row i)            │
│                                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │     V₁    V₂    V₃    ...   Vₙ         │               │
│  │     │     │     │           │           │               │
│  │  ┌──┼──┬──┼──┬──┼──┬─────┬──┼──┐        │               │
│  │  │ C₁₁│ C₁₂│ C₁₃│     │ C₁ₙ│────→ Q₁   │               │
│  │  ├──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼─────┼──┼──┤        │               │
│  │  │ C₂₁│ C₂₂│ C₂₃│     │ C₂ₙ│────→ Q₂   │               │
│  │  ├──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼─────┼──┼──┤        │               │
│  │  │    │    │    │     │    │            │               │
│  │  ├──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼─────┼──┼──┤        │               │
│  │  │ Cₘ₁│ Cₘ₂│ Cₘ₃│     │ Cₘₙ│────→ Qₘ   │               │
│  │  └──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴─────┴──┴──┘        │               │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                             │
│  Advantages:                                                │
│  • Read at DC 0V → read-disturbance-free                   │
│  • Only dynamic power (Q = CV, P = CV²f)                   │
│  • No DC sneak paths                                       │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Energy Efficiency Achievements

```
Energy Efficiency Comparison:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Technology           │ Efficiency      │ Improvement      │
│  ─────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────  │
│  GPU (baseline)       │ ~1 TOPS/W       │ 1×               │
│  RRAM crossbar        │ ~100 TOPS/W     │ 100×             │
│  PCM crossbar         │ ~50 TOPS/W      │ 50×              │
│  FeFET CIM            │ ~1000 TOPS/W    │ 1000×            │
│  Memcapacitor CIM     │ 29,600 TOPS/W   │ 29,600×          │
│                                                             │
│  Per-inference energy:                                      │
│  • Memcapacitor: 31 fJ/inference                           │
│  • SNN with memcap: 6.24 nJ/inference (MNIST)              │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Scaling Potential (45nm node):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Modeled performance at 45nm:                               │
│  • Energy efficiency: 29,600 TOPS/W                        │
│  • Precision: 6-8 bits                                     │
│  • Scalable to 45nm lateral dimensions                     │
│  • 3D stackable on CMOS (BEOL compatible)                  │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Low-Voltage Operation (1.2V)

```
IL Engineering for Low-Voltage Memcapacitor:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                             │
│  Standard HZO: 10nm thick → Vc ~ 2V                        │
│                                                             │
│  Low-voltage design:                                        │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│  │        Top electrode (TiN)           │                  │
│  │──────────────────────────────────────│                  │
│  │    Thin HZO layer (~5nm)             │                  │
│  │──────────────────────────────────────│                  │
│  │    Interfacial layer (IL) ~2nm       │ ← Key!          │
│  │──────────────────────────────────────│                  │
│  │        Bottom electrode              │                  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                             │
│  Result: Vc ~ 1.2V operation                               │
│  → Further energy reduction!                               │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
