## 300. Ferroelectric Negative Capacitance for CIM (Iteration 102)

### Overview

Negative Capacitance (NC) in ferroelectric materials offers a path to overcome "Boltzmann tyranny" - the 60 mV/decade subthreshold swing limit at room temperature. By providing internal voltage amplification, NC-FETs enable ultra-low power CIM.

### Boltzmann Tyranny and NC Solution

```
   Subthreshold Swing Comparison

   Conventional MOSFET:          NC-FET:
   SS = 60 mV/dec (limit)        SS < 60 mV/dec (sub-Boltzmann)

   ┌────────────────────┐        ┌────────────────────┐
   │     log(Id)        │        │     log(Id)        │
   │         │          │        │         │          │
   │         │  /       │        │         │   /      │
   │         │ /        │        │         │  /       │ ← Steeper
   │         │/         │        │         │/         │    slope
   │         ●──────────│        │         ●──────────│
   │        Vth    Vg   │        │        Vth    Vg   │
   └────────────────────┘        └────────────────────┘

   Boltzmann limit: SS = (kT/q)×ln(10)×m = 60×m mV/dec
   With NC: m = 1/β < 1 → SS < 60 mV/dec
```

### NC-FET Device Physics

```
   NC-FET Gate Stack Structure

   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │      Metal Gate             │
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │   Ferroelectric (HZO)       │ ← CFE (negative in NC regime)
   │   ~10-15 nm                 │
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │   Interfacial Layer         │ ← Cint
   │   ~2 nm                     │
   ├─────────────────────────────┤
   │      Channel (Si/MoS₂)      │ ← CMOS
   └─────────────────────────────┘

   Voltage Amplification:
   β = ∂Vint/∂Vg = CFE/(CFE + CMOS)

   When |CFE| > CMOS and CFE < 0:
   β > 1 → Internal voltage amplification!
```

### State-of-the-Art Performance

**MoS₂ NC-FET with HZO (Nano Letters):**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Min SS | 6.07 mV/dec |
| Avg SS (4 decades) | 8.03 mV/dec |
| HZO thickness | ~12 nm |
| Voltage gain | ~28× |

**ZrOₓ NC-FET:**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Forward SS | 45.1 mV/dec |
| Reverse SS | 55.2 mV/dec |
| VDS | -0.05 V |

**GeSn NC-FET (Record):**
- SS < 20 mV/dec demonstrated
- Approaching physical limits

### NC-FET for Computing-in-Memory

```
   NC-FET 6T-SRAM CIM Cell

   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                    VDD                     │
   │                     │                      │
   │              ┌──────┼──────┐               │
   │              │      │      │               │
   │           ┌──┴──┐┌──┴──┐   │               │
   │           │PMOS││PMOS│   │ NC-FETs        │
   │           └──┬──┘└──┬──┘   │               │
   │     Q ───────┼──────┼─────QB               │
   │              │      │                      │
   │           ┌──┴──┐┌──┴──┐                   │
   │           │NMOS││NMOS│   │ NC-FETs        │
   │           └──┬──┘└──┬──┘   │               │
   │              │      │      │               │
   │              └──────┼──────┘               │
   │                    GND                     │
   │                                            │
   │   WL ───┬─── Access ───┬─── WL            │
   │         │   NC-FETs    │                   │
   │        BL             BLB                  │
   └───────────────────────────────────────────┘

   Energy Reduction (vs baseline 40nm CMOS):
   - At VDD = 0.3V: 22.77× lower energy
   - At VDD = 0.5V: 12.41× lower energy
   - Speed: 18× enhancement
```

### Challenges and Limitations

1. **Hysteresis:** Stabilized non-hysteretic NC not fully demonstrated
2. **Quantum Capacitance:** Limits SS improvement in well-designed MOSFETs
3. **Endurance:** FE layer reliability under repeated cycling
4. **Integration:** Process compatibility with CMOS

### References

- [Nano Letters - MoS₂ NC-FET](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.nanolett.7b01584)
- [ScienceDirect - NC-FET SRAM CIM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0026269223001805)
- [Nature Comms - NC-FET Logic Switch](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-019-13797-9)
- [npj Comp Mat - FeFET with NC](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41524-022-00738-2)

## 301. Hybrid Analog-Digital CIM Architectures (Iteration 102)

### Overview

Hybrid CIM combines analog crossbar computation with digital processing to balance energy efficiency, precision, and area. Key innovation: ADC-less designs eliminate the dominant power bottleneck.

### ADC Bottleneck Problem

```
   ADC Power/Area Overhead in Analog CIM

   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                 Analog CIM Crossbar                      │
   │  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┐                      │
   │  │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │                      │
   │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┼───┼───┼───┼───┤                      │
   │  │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │                      │
   │  └─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┘                      │
   │    │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │                        │
   │    ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼   ▼                        │
   │  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┐                      │
   │  │ADC│ADC│ADC│ADC│ADC│ADC│ADC│ADC│ ← Power/area hog!   │
   │  └───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┘                      │
   │                                                          │
   │  Problem: High-precision ADCs dominate power & area      │
   │  - 7-bit ADC: High power, large area                     │
   │  - Limits throughput due to ADC count constraints        │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HCiM: ADC-Less Hybrid Architecture

```
   HCiM Architecture (Purdue University)

   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                                          │
   │  ┌─────────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐     │
   │  │   Analog Crossbar   │    │  Digital CIM Array  │     │
   │  │                     │    │                     │     │
   │  │  Binary/Ternary     │    │   Scale Factor      │     │
   │  │  Partial Sums       │    │   Processing        │     │
   │  │                     │    │                     │     │
   │  └──────────┬──────────┘    └──────────┬──────────┘     │
   │             │                          │                 │
   │             │    No ADC needed!        │                 │
   │             │                          │                 │
   │             └──────────┬───────────────┘                 │
   │                        │                                 │
   │                        ▼                                 │
   │               ┌────────────────┐                         │
   │               │   Accumulator  │                         │
   │               │   (Digital)    │                         │
   │               └────────────────┘                         │
   │                                                          │
   │  Key Innovation: Binary quantization eliminates ADCs     │
   │  Scale factors handled by dedicated digital CIM          │
   │                                                          │
   │  Results vs 7-bit ADC baseline:                          │
   │  - Energy reduction: 28%                                 │
   │  - vs 4-bit ADC: 12% reduction                          │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Architecture Classification

```
   CIM Architecture Taxonomy

   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                    CIM Architectures                     │
   │                          │                               │
   │          ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐               │
   │          │               │               │               │
   │          ▼               ▼               ▼               │
   │     ┌────────┐      ┌────────┐      ┌────────┐          │
   │     │ Digital │      │ Analog │      │ Hybrid │          │
   │     │  CIM   │      │  CIM   │      │  CIM   │          │
   │     │ (DCIM) │      │ (ACIM) │      │        │          │
   │     └────────┘      └────────┘      └────────┘          │
   │          │               │               │               │
   │     Adder trees     Current/      Mixed analog-         │
   │     High precision  charge sum    digital paths         │
   │     Easy design     ADC required  Best of both          │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Bit-Serial vs Bit-Parallel Computing

```
   Bit-Serial Computing (Colonnade Architecture)

   Input: 8-bit value X = x7x6x5x4x3x2x1x0

   Cycle 1: Process x0 (LSB)
   Cycle 2: Process x1
   ...
   Cycle 8: Process x7 (MSB)

   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Weight   Input    Partial      Shift &      Output     │
   │  Slice    Bit      Product      Accumulate              │
   │                                                          │
   │  [W]  ×   x0   →   P0       →   Acc += P0              │
   │  [W]  ×   x1   →   P1       →   Acc += P1<<1           │
   │  [W]  ×   x2   →   P2       →   Acc += P2<<2           │
   │  ...                                                     │
   │  [W]  ×   x7   →   P7       →   Acc += P7<<7           │
   │                                                          │
   │  Advantage: Single ADC, high precision                   │
   │  Disadvantage: Multiple cycles                           │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Mixed-Precision CIM Macro

```
   Configurable 1-16 bit Precision CIM

   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │              Reconfigurable CIM Macro                    │
   │                                                          │
   │  Precision Modes:                                        │
   │  ├── INT1: Binary networks (highest throughput)         │
   │  ├── INT4: Quantized CNNs                               │
   │  ├── INT8: Standard inference                           │
   │  └── INT16: High-precision transformers                 │
   │                                                          │
   │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
   │  │        7T SRAM Bitcells                 │            │
   │  │  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┐     │            │
   │  │  │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │     │            │
   │  │  └───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┘     │            │
   │  │          ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓                 │            │
   │  │     Bitwise Multiplier + Full Adder    │            │
   │  │          ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓                 │            │
   │  │       Adder Tree + Barrel Shifter      │            │
   │  │                    ↓                   │            │
   │  │           Partial Sum Accumulator      │            │
   │  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
   │                                                          │
   │  Performance (22nm):                                     │
   │  - 89-121 TOPS/W                                        │
   │  - 16.3-41.6 TOPS/mm²                                   │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### State-of-the-Art Results

| Design | Node | Efficiency | Density | Features |
|--------|------|------------|---------|----------|
| HCiM | - | 28% gain | - | ADC-less hybrid |
| ISSCC'21 | 22nm | 89 TOPS/W | 16.3 TOPS/mm² | Full precision |
| 12nm DCIM | 12nm | 121 TOPS/W | 41.6 TOPS/mm² | Configurable |
| CICC'25 | 28nm | 20.9-137.2 TOPS/W | - | Zero skip, runtime quant |
| Neuro-CIM | 28nm | 310.4 TOPS/W | - | Mixed-mode neuron |

### Go Implementation

Created `ncfet_hybrid.go` (~1100 lines) with:

```go
// NC-FET device model
type NCFETDevice struct {
    Config            *NCFETConfig
    GateVoltage       float64
    InternalVoltage   float64 // Amplified (β × Vg)
    VoltageGain       float64 // ~28× for HZO
    SubthresholdSwing float64 // < 60 mV/dec
}

// NC-FET SRAM CIM cell
type NCFETSRAMCell struct {
    AccessTransistors [2]*NCFETDevice
    InverterNMOS      [2]*NCFETDevice
    InverterPMOS      [2]*NCFETDevice
    // 22.77× energy reduction at 0.3V
}

// Hybrid CIM crossbar
type HybridCIMCrossbar struct {
    Config          *HybridCIMConfig
    WeightSlices    [][][]int  // Bit-serial slices
    ScaleFactors    []float64  // Digital processing
    ADCLessMode     bool       // HCiM-style
}

// Mixed-precision macro
type MixedPrecisionCIMMacro struct {
    Crossbars              []*HybridCIMCrossbar
    CurrentInputPrecision  int  // 1-16 configurable
    CurrentWeightPrecision int
    CurrentOutputPrecision int
}
```

## Section 302: Thermal Management for CIM Crossbar Arrays

### Overview

Dense compute-in-memory crossbar arrays face critical thermal challenges. Joule heating from resistive switching creates hotspots that degrade device accuracy and reliability. Studies show 0.9% accuracy loss per 1K temperature increase above ambient.

### Thermal Challenges

**1. Joule Heating in ReRAM/FeFET:**
```
                    HOTSPOT FORMATION
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │  Applied Voltage                         │
    │       │                                  │
    │       ▼                                  │
    │   ┌───────┐                              │
    │   │ ReRAM │← P = V²/R (Joule heating)    │
    │   └───────┘                              │
    │       │                                  │
    │       ▼                                  │
    │   ┌───────┐                              │
    │   │ Heat  │→ Thermal crosstalk to        │
    │   │ Flow  │  neighboring cells           │
    │   └───────┘                              │
    │                                          │
    │   Temperature increase → R_ON/R_OFF ↓   │
    │   → Accuracy degradation (~0.9%/K)       │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Cell-to-Cell Thermal Crosstalk:**
- Heat spreads via shared electrode lines
- Adjacent cells experience remote heating
- Causes resistance degradation in unheated cells
- More severe in 3D stacked architectures

**3. 3D Crossbar Thermal Issues:**
- Bottom layers consistently hottest
- Thermal failures: 55% of 3D chip issues
- Temperature non-uniformity across stack
- Requires layer-aware scheduling

### Thermal Mitigation Strategies

**1. HR3AM (Heat Resilient ReRAM) Framework:**
```
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │          HR3AM MITIGATION METHODS           │
    ├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    │                                             │
    │  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  │
    │  │ Bitwidth        │  │ Dynamic Thermal │  │
    │  │ Downgrading     │  │ Management      │  │
    │  ├─────────────────┤  ├─────────────────┤  │
    │  │ Hot cell T↑     │  │ Tile Pairing    │  │
    │  │      ↓          │  │      ↓          │  │
    │  │ Reduce bits     │  │ Hot-Cold swap   │  │
    │  │ (8→6→4)         │  │ operations      │  │
    │  │      ↓          │  │      ↓          │  │
    │  │ Lower power     │  │ Load balancing  │  │
    │  │ Less precision  │  │ Idle tile pool  │  │
    │  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  │
    │                                             │
    │  Improvement: 58% accuracy, 2.39× lifetime  │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Allocation Schemes for 3D Arrays:**

| Scheme | Peak Temp | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Naive | 371K | All cells active |
| Strike | 363K | Alternating stripes |
| Chessboard | 363K | Checkerboard pattern |
| Temperature reduction | -8K | vs naive |

**3. Multi-Scale Thermal Modeling:**
- Device level: Joule heating, filament temperature
- Array level: Thermal diffusion, crosstalk
- Package level: EMC thickness, substrate
- System level: Ambient, cooling

**4. Thermal Correction Methods:**
- Runtime temperature monitoring
- Software compensation for drift
- Reduces accuracy loss: 66.4% → 1.4% at 85°C
- Real-time weight adjustment

### Temperature-Resilient Designs

**Subthreshold FeFET CIM (2024):**
- 2T-1FeFET design for MAC operations
- Operates reliably 0°C to 85°C
- 2866 TOPS/W energy efficiency
- 89.45% VGG/CIFAR-10 accuracy
- Immune to temperature drift at 8-bit

### Key Thermal Parameters

| Parameter | Value | Impact |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| Accuracy loss | 0.9%/K | Inference degradation |
| HfO₂ thermal conductivity | 1.5 W/(m·K) | Heat spreading |
| Max operating temp | 85°C (358K) | Reliability limit |
| EMC thermal resistance | ~5 K/W | Package heat path |
| Substrate thermal R | ~2 K/W | Heat sink |
| Hotspot mitigation | 4-8°C | From layout optimization |

### References

- [arXiv - Thermal Heating in ReRAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.13707)
- [MDPI - Thermal Analysis CIM](https://www.mdpi.com/2674-0729/4/1/9)
- [UCSD - HR3AM](https://cseweb.ucsd.edu/~jzhao/files/HR3AM-ISLPED2019.pdf)
- [arXiv - Subthreshold FeFET CIM](https://arxiv.org/html/2312.17442)
- [IEEE - Thermal-Aware ReRAM](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8465880/)

## Section 303: Spintronics-Based CIM Devices

### Overview

Spintronic devices offer high-speed, low-power, and non-volatile computing capabilities for CIM. Technologies include STT-MRAM, SOT-MRAM, domain wall synapses, skyrmion-based neurons, and probabilistic p-bits.

### Magnetic Tunnel Junction (MTJ) Fundamentals

```
              MTJ STRUCTURE
    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
    │       Top Electrode              │
    ├─────────────────────────────────┤
    │    Free Layer (CoFeB)           │
    │    ↑ Magnetization (switchable) │
    ├─────────────────────────────────┤
    │    Tunnel Barrier (MgO)         │ ~1-2 nm
    ├─────────────────────────────────┤
    │    Fixed Layer (CoFeB)          │
    │    → Magnetization (pinned)     │
    ├─────────────────────────────────┤
    │       Bottom Electrode          │
    └─────────────────────────────────┘

    Parallel (P): Low R ~ 5 kΩ    (Logic 0)
    Anti-Parallel (AP): High R ~ 10 kΩ (Logic 1)
    TMR = (R_AP - R_P) / R_P = 100-200%
```

### STT-MRAM vs SOT-MRAM Comparison

| Parameter | STT-MRAM | SOT-MRAM |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Architecture | 1T-1MTJ | 2T-1MTJ |
| Write current | 50 μA | 200 μA |
| Write speed | 10 ns | 2 ns (4× faster) |
| TMR | 100% | 150% |
| Energy/switch | 100 fJ | 350 fJ |
| Endurance | >10¹⁵ | >10¹⁵ |
| Read disturbance | Higher | Lower |
| TOPS/W | 22-41.5 | 23.7-29.6 |
| Process | 28nm | 28nm |

### MRAM CIM Demonstrations

**STT-MRAM CIM (ISSCC 2023):**
- 28nm 2Mb macro
- 22.4-41.5 TOPS/W efficiency
- Analog ReLU and pooling integrated
- Binary/ternary weight support

**SOT-MRAM CIM (DAC 2024):**
- 6T-4J weight group architecture
- Series-parallel hybrid topology
- Multi-method modulation (2/4/6/8-bit)
- 23.7-29.6 TOPS/W at 8-bit

### Domain Wall Synapses

```
       DOMAIN WALL SYNAPSE OPERATION
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                     │
    │  ←───── Track Length (500nm) ─────→ │
    │  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐│
    │  │███████│        │                ││
    │  │ P     │   DW   │      AP        ││
    │  │███████│←──────→│                ││
    │  └─────────────────────────────────┘│
    │         ↑                           │
    │    Domain Wall                      │
    │    Position = Synaptic Weight       │
    │                                     │
    │  SOT pulse → DW motion (100 m/s)    │
    │  Potentiation: DW moves right       │
    │  Depression: DW moves left          │
    │                                     │
    │  Achieves: Intrinsic linearity      │
    │            16-64 analog states      │
    │            All-spin hardware        │
    └─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**All-Spin Domain Wall MTJ (Nature Comms 2024):**
- Domain wall-MTJ synapses and neurons
- Synergistic SOT and DMI interaction
- Intrinsic linearity for neuromorphic
- All-spin neuromorphic hardware

### Skyrmion Neuromorphic Computing

```
       SKYRMION-BASED SYNAPSE
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                     │
    │    Track with Skyrmion Count        │
    │    ┌───────────────────────────┐    │
    │    │ ⊗  ⊗  ⊗  ⊗  │  ⊗  ⊗  │  │    │
    │    │ Region A    │ Region B │  │    │
    │    └───────────────────────────┘    │
    │                                     │
    │    Weight = Count(Region A)         │
    │    = 4 skyrmions = 4/64 = 0.0625    │
    │                                     │
    │    Nucleation: Create skyrmion      │
    │    Annihilation: Delete skyrmion    │
    │    Migration: Move between regions  │
    │                                     │
    │    64 states, 2.2 ns write time     │
    │    10 nm radius, topological stable │
    └─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Skyrmion Features:**
- Topologically stable (resistant to noise)
- Nanoscale dimensions (~10 nm radius)
- Current-driven motion (100 m/s)
- Countable/summable (weighted sums)
- 64 programmable states demonstrated
- 2.2 ns write time (October 2025 paper)

**Skyrmion-Domain Wall Hybrid:**
- Combined synapse and ReLU-maxpooling
- Skyrmion synapse + DW activation
- Compact CNN implementation

### Probabilistic Computing with P-bits

```
      P-BIT STOCHASTIC COMPUTING
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                     │
    │  Low Barrier Magnet (LBM)           │
    │  ┌───────────────┐                  │
    │  │   ⬆ or ⬇     │← Thermal fluct.  │
    │  │   (random)    │   at RT          │
    │  └───────────────┘                  │
    │         │                           │
    │         ▼                           │
    │  ┌───────────────┐                  │
    │  │ Bias Control  │← Input voltage   │
    │  └───────────────┘                  │
    │         │                           │
    │         ▼                           │
    │  P(output=1) = σ(β × bias)          │
    │  = 1 / (1 + exp(-β × bias))         │
    │                                     │
    │  Applications:                      │
    │  - Boltzmann machines               │
    │  - Ising solvers                    │
    │  - Bayesian inference               │
    │  - True random number generation    │
    └─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**P-STT Neuron (2024):**
- 300 nm diameter pillar
- 28nm CMOS peripherals
- <1 pJ/bit energy
- ~10 ns switching
- Sigmoid activation via stochasticity

**P-bit Networks:**
- Coupled p-bits for optimization
- Ising model mapping
- Room temperature operation
- 1 GHz fluctuation rates

### 2D Spintronics (2025)

**Van der Waals Materials:**
- CrI₃, Fe₃GaTe₂, graphene heterostructures
- Ultra-low energy: 0.14 fJ/operation
- Sub-nanosecond switching
- Atomic-scale integration

### Antiferromagnetic Memory

**AFM Domain Wall Synapse (2024):**
- SOT-driven AFM domain wall motion
- Nonlinear magnetotransport readout
- No stray field interference
- Reproducible weight variations
- Neuromorphic functionality

### Go Implementation

Created `thermal_spintronics.go` (~1100 lines) with:

```go
// Thermal management
type ThermalCrossbar struct {
    Config      *ThermalConfig
    Cells       [][][]*ThermalCell
    HotspotTemp float64
    HotspotLoc  [3]int  // [layer, row, col]
}

// HR3AM-style mitigation
type ThermalMitigator struct {
    Config            *ThermalMitigationConfig
    CurrentBitwidths  [][]int   // Downgrade hot cells
    TilePairings      [][2]int  // Hot-cold pairs
    AllocationScheme  string    // naive/strike/chessboard
}

// MTJ device
type MTJDevice struct {
    Config      *MTJConfig
    State       MTJState  // Parallel or AntiParallel
    Resistance  float64
    Temperature float64
}

// MRAM crossbar
type MRAMCrossbar struct {
    Config   *MRAMCrossbarConfig
    Devices  [][]*MTJDevice
    TOPSW    float64  // 22-41 TOPS/W
}

// Domain wall synapse
type DomainWallSynapse struct {
    DWPosition float64  // Weight encoding
    Config     *DomainWallConfig
}

// Skyrmion synapse
type SkyrmionSynapse struct {
    NumSkyrmions int     // 0-64 states
    Positions    []float64
}

// Probabilistic bit
type PBit struct {
    State int       // 0 or 1 (stochastic)
    Bias  float64   // Controls probability
}
```

### References

- [Nature - Neuromorphic Spintronics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44306-024-00019-2)
- [Nature - Spintronic Memristors](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44306-025-00078-z)
- [Nature Comms - DW-MTJ](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-48631-4)
- [Nature Electronics - Skyrmion Weighted Sums](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-024-01303-z)
- [Wiley - P-STT Neuron](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aelm.202300821)
- [ACM DAC - SOT-MRAM CIM](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3649329.3656214)
- [arXiv - SOT-MRAM Crossbar](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.25853)

## Section 304: Optical Interconnects for CIM Chiplets

### Overview

Optical interconnects address the bandwidth and energy bottlenecks in multi-chiplet CIM systems. Intel's OCI chiplet achieves 4 Tbps with 64 PCIe 5.0 channels, while photonic fabric technologies promise 6.2 pJ/bit vs 62.5 pJ for NVLink.

### Intel Optical Compute Interconnect (OCI)

```
         INTEL OCI CHIPLET ARCHITECTURE
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                             │
    │  ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐           │
    │  │   CPU   │◄───────►│   OCI   │           │
    │  │ Chiplet │ UCIe    │ Chiplet │           │
    │  └─────────┘         └────┬────┘           │
    │                           │                │
    │                     ┌─────▼─────┐          │
    │                     │   PIC     │          │
    │                     │ (Photonic │          │
    │                     │  IC)      │          │
    │                     └─────┬─────┘          │
    │                           │                │
    │                     ┌─────▼─────┐          │
    │                     │   EIC     │          │
    │                     │(Electric  │          │
    │                     │  IC)      │          │
    │                     └─────┬─────┘          │
    │                           │                │
    │               ════════════▼════════════    │
    │                   Fiber Optics            │
    │                   (up to 100m)            │
    │                                             │
    │  Key specs:                                │
    │  - 4 Tbps bidirectional                   │
    │  - 64 × PCIe 5.0 channels (32 GT/s)      │
    │  - DWDM with integrated InP lasers        │
    │  - Co-packaged with CPU                   │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**OCI Features (OFC 2024):**
- First fully integrated optical I/O chiplet
- 4 Tbps total bandwidth
- 64 PCIe 5.0 channels at 32 GT/s each
- 100 meter reach via fiber optics
- Dense wavelength division multiplexing (DWDM)
- Integrated indium-phosphide lasers

### Photonic Fabric (Celestial AI)

| Parameter | OCI | Photonic Fabric | NVLink |
|-----------|-----|-----------------|--------|
| Bandwidth | 4 Tbps | 14.4 Tbps | 900 GB/s |
| Energy/bit | ~5 pJ | 6.2 pJ | 62.5 pJ |
| Reach | 100m | 10m | ~1m |
| Application | XPU-to-XPU | Chip-to-memory | GPU-to-GPU |
| Energy saving | 10× vs electrical | 10× vs NVLink | baseline |

### Silicon Photonics Neural Network Accelerators

**MRR-Based MAC Units:**
```
        MICRORING RESONATOR MAC ARRAY
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                         │
    │     λ1   λ2   λ3   λ4   (WDM inputs)   │
    │      │    │    │    │                  │
    │      ▼    ▼    ▼    ▼                  │
    │    ┌───┐┌───┐┌───┐┌───┐               │
    │    │MRR││MRR││MRR││MRR│← Weights      │
    │    │ 1 ││ 2 ││ 3 ││ 4 │  (tunable)    │
    │    └─┬─┘└─┬─┘└─┬─┘└─┬─┘               │
    │      │    │    │    │                  │
    │      └────┴────┴────┴──────►           │
    │                         Photodetector  │
    │                         (sum output)   │
    │                                         │
    │    MRR specs:                          │
    │    - Radius: 5 μm                      │
    │    - Cell area: 30×30 μm² (900 μm²)   │
    │    - Q-factor: 10,000                  │
    │    - Tuning: thermal (10 mW)          │
    │    - Bandwidth: 100 GHz per channel   │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Performance Metrics:**

| Architecture | Compute Density | Efficiency | Clock |
|--------------|-----------------|------------|-------|
| MRR 64×64 | 420 TOPS/mm² | 5.1 TOPS/W | 25 GHz |
| MRR crossbar | 880 TOPS/mm² | 5.1 TOPS/W | 25 GHz |
| MZI network | 100 TOPS/mm² | 10 TOPS/W | 10 GHz |
| Digital ASIC | 10 TOPS/mm² | 1 TOPS/W | 1 GHz |

### Magneto-Optic Memory for Photonic CIM

**Ce:YIG on Silicon (October 2024):**
- Material: Cerium-substituted yttrium iron garnet
- Endurance: 10¹² cycles (record high)
- Weight precision: 4-bit (16 levels)
- Non-volatile, multi-bit storage
- Low switching energy
- High switching speed

### Photonic CIM Market Growth

The global interchip optical interconnect market:
- 2024: $15.99 billion
- 2030: $32.73 billion (projected)
- CAGR: 12.7%

**Key investments (2024-2025):**
- Ayar Labs: $155M (Dec 2024) - AMD, Intel, Nvidia backing

## Section 305: CIM Compiler and Mapping Optimizations

### Overview

CIM compilers translate neural networks to crossbar hardware, optimizing weight mapping, tiling, and dataflow. CIM-MLC (ASPLOS 2024) achieves 3.2× speedup through multi-level optimization at chip, core, crossbar, and wordline granularities.

### CIM-MLC Multi-Level Compilation

```
        CIM-MLC COMPILATION HIERARCHY
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                             │
    │  ONNX Neural Network                        │
    │         │                                   │
    │         ▼                                   │
    │  ┌─────────────────┐                        │
    │  │   Chip Mode     │ ← Coarsest level      │
    │  │  (full chip)    │   Inter-chip schedule  │
    │  └────────┬────────┘                        │
    │           ▼                                 │
    │  ┌─────────────────┐                        │
    │  │   Core Mode     │ ← PE-level mapping    │
    │  │  (processing    │   Layer partitioning   │
    │  │   elements)     │                        │
    │  └────────┬────────┘                        │
    │           ▼                                 │
    │  ┌─────────────────┐                        │
    │  │ Crossbar Mode   │ ← Array-level tiling  │
    │  │   (XBM)         │   Weight mapping       │
    │  └────────┬────────┘                        │
    │           ▼                                 │
    │  ┌─────────────────┐                        │
    │  │ Wordline Mode   │ ← Finest level        │
    │  │   (WLM)         │   Row-level scheduling │
    │  └────────┬────────┘                        │
    │           ▼                                 │
    │  Optimized CIM Execution                   │
    │                                             │
    │  Speedup: 3.2× vs prior compilers          │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Dataflow Strategies

| Dataflow | Description | Best For |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| Weight-Stationary | Weights remain in crossbar | Most CIM |
| Output-Stationary | Partial sums accumulate locally | Large outputs |
| Row-Stationary | Row data reuse maximized | Convolutions |
| No-Local-Reuse | Stream all data | Small models |

### Weight Mapping to Crossbars

```
        CONV2D IM2COL MAPPING
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                             │
    │  Conv Kernel: [OutCh, InCh, Kh, Kw]        │
    │  = [64, 32, 3, 3]                           │
    │                                             │
    │  Im2col transformation:                     │
    │  Weight Matrix: [OutCh, InCh×Kh×Kw]        │
    │  = [64, 288]                                │
    │                                             │
    │  Crossbar (128×128):                        │
    │  ┌───────────────────────────────┐          │
    │  │█████████████████████████░░░░░│ 64 rows  │
    │  │░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│          │
    │  │░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│          │
    │  │░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│          │
    │  └───────────────────────────────┘          │
    │   ◄────── 288 cols (2.25 XBs) ──────►      │
    │                                             │
    │  Tiling: 3 crossbars needed               │
    │  Utilization: 64×288 / (3×128×128) = 37%  │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM-Explorer Design Space Exploration

**For BNN/TNN on RRAM (May 2025):**
- End-to-end compiler stack
- Multiple mapping options
- Built-in simulators
- Design space exploration (DSE) flow

**Search Space:**
- Array sizes: 64, 128, 256
- ADC bits: 4, 6, 8
- Weight bits: 1 (BNN), 2 (TNN)
- Memory types: SRAM, RRAM, FeFET

### NeuroSim Performance Estimation

**NeuroSim V1.5 (May 2025):**
- TensorRT integration for transformers
- Flexible noise injection
- Support down to 1nm node
- <1% error vs silicon validation

```
        NEUROSIM ARCHITECTURE MODEL
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                             │
    │  Chip                                       │
    │  ├── Tile 0                                │
    │  │   ├── PE 0                              │
    │  │   │   ├── Crossbar (128×128)           │
    │  │   │   │   ├── Memory cells (RRAM)      │
    │  │   │   │   ├── DAC (4-bit, 10ns)        │
    │  │   │   │   └── ADC (8-bit, 100ns)       │
    │  │   │   ├── Activation buffer            │
    │  │   │   └── Accumulator                  │
    │  │   ├── PE 1                              │
    │  │   └── ...                               │
    │  ├── Tile 1                                │
    │  └── ...                                   │
    │                                             │
    │  Metrics estimated:                        │
    │  - Area, latency, energy, leakage         │
    │  - Technology: 130nm to 7nm               │
    │  - Memories: SRAM, RRAM, PCM, FeFET       │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Optimization Techniques

**1. Tiling Optimization:**
- Search tile sizes fitting crossbar
- Maximize utilization (>70% target)
- Balance latency vs area

**2. Layer Fusion:**
- Combine consecutive layers
- Reduce intermediate buffer access
- Conv + BN + ReLU fusion

**3. Weight Duplication:**
- Replicate weights for parallelism
- Trade area for throughput
- 2-4× speedup typical

**4. Dataflow Scheduling:**
- VVM-grained vs MVM-grained
- 20% latency reduction with VVM
- Cross-layer scheduling

### Compiler Benchmarks

| Compiler | Target | Speedup | Year |
|----------|--------|---------|------|
| CIM-MLC | General CIM | 3.2× | 2024 |
| CIM-Explorer | BNN/TNN | 2× | 2025 |
| Manual mapping | Baseline | 1× | - |

### Full-Stack CIM System (March 2025)

A Nature Communications paper demonstrated:
- CIM-oriented software framework
- CIM compiler + optimizer + simulator
- Variable AI model support
- Flexible weight/input mapping
- Optimized spatial-temporal utilization

### Go Implementation

Created `optical_compiler.go` (~1100 lines) with:

```go
// Optical interconnect
type OpticalLink struct {
    Config   *OpticalInterconnectConfig
    Channels []*OpticalChannel
    // 4 Tbps, 64 channels, 6.2 pJ/bit
}

// Photonic MAC unit
type PhotonicMACUnit struct {
    Config   *PhotonicMACConfig
    Weights  []*PhotonicWeight
    // 880 TOPS/mm², 5.1 TOPS/W
}

// CIM compiler
type CIMCompiler struct {
    Config *CIMCompilerConfig
    Mode   ComputingMode  // Chip/Core/Crossbar/Wordline
}

// Layer mapping
type LayerMapping struct {
    LayerName    string
    NumCrossbars int
    CrossbarUtil float64
    Latency      float64
    Energy       float64
}

// NeuroSim estimator
type NeuroSimEstimator struct {
    Config         *NeuroSimConfig
    ComputeDensity float64  // TOPS/mm²
    Efficiency     float64  // TOPS/W
}

// Design space explorer
type CIMExplorer struct {
    Config         *CIMExplorerConfig
    ParetoFrontier []*DesignPoint
}
```

## Section 306: In-Memory Training with Weight Update Precision

### Overview

Training neural networks directly on CIM hardware presents unique challenges compared to inference. While inference requires only forward passes with fixed weights, training demands high-precision gradient computation and weight updates that must overcome the inherent non-idealities of analog memory devices.

### Weight Precision Challenges

```
Weight Update Precision Requirements:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Inference weights: 4-6 bits typically sufficient                   │
│  Training gradients: 8-16 bits required for convergence            │
│  Weight updates: Δw = η × gradient (very small changes)            │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │ Original    Quantized   Gradient    Update Size         │       │
│  │ Weight      (4-bit)     (8-bit)     (relative)          │       │
│  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤       │
│  │ 0.3724      0.375       -0.0012     0.3% of LSB         │       │
│  │ -0.8156     -0.8125     +0.0034     0.9% of LSB         │       │
│  │ 0.1293      0.125       -0.0008     0.2% of LSB         │       │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
│  Problem: Weight updates << quantization step size                  │
│  Solution: Multi-tile residual learning                            │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Multi-Tile Residual Learning Architecture

The key innovation for in-memory training is decomposing weights across multiple tiles:

```
Multi-Tile Weight Decomposition:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Effective Weight = Σ (scale_i × tile_i_value)                     │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐               │
│  │   Tile 0    │   │   Tile 1    │   │   Tile 2    │               │
│  │ (Coarse)    │   │ (Fine)      │   │ (Ultra-fine)│               │
│  │             │   │             │   │             │               │
│  │ Scale: 1.0  │   │ Scale: 0.1  │   │ Scale: 0.01 │               │
│  │ Range: ±1.0 │   │ Range: ±0.1 │   │ Range: ±0.01│               │
│  │ 4-bit: 6.25%│   │ 4-bit: 0.6% │   │ 4-bit: 0.06%│               │
│  └──────┬──────┘   └──────┬──────┘   └──────┬──────┘               │
│         │                 │                 │                       │
│         └────────────┬────┴────────────────┘                       │
│                      ▼                                              │
│         ┌─────────────────────────┐                                │
│         │ Effective Resolution    │                                │
│         │ 12+ bits (4+4+4)        │                                │
│         │ Minimum step: 0.06%     │                                │
│         └─────────────────────────┘                                │
│                                                                     │
│  Training Strategy:                                                │
│  1. Updates first go to fine tile (Tile 2)                        │
│  2. When Tile 2 saturates → carry to Tile 1                       │
│  3. When Tile 1 saturates → carry to Tile 0                       │
│  4. Effective precision: sum of all tile bits                     │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Residual Learning Algorithm

```
Residual Learning Update Process:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  func UpdateWeight(gradient, learning_rate):                       │
│      delta = learning_rate × gradient                              │
│      residual = delta                                              │
│                                                                     │
│      for tile in tiles (finest to coarsest):                       │
│          scaled_delta = residual / tile.scale                      │
│          quantized = Quantize(tile.value + scaled_delta)           │
│          actual_change = (quantized - tile.value) × tile.scale    │
│          residual = delta - actual_change                          │
│          tile.value = quantized                                    │
│                                                                     │
│          if |residual| < threshold:                                │
│              break                                                 │
│                                                                     │
│  Example Update:                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Desired Δw = -0.0023                                        │  │
│  │                                                              │  │
│  │ Tile 2 (scale=0.01):                                        │  │
│  │   scaled = -0.0023/0.01 = -0.23 → quantize → 0 states      │  │
│  │   (too small for even finest tile)                          │  │
│  │                                                              │  │
│  │ With accumulation over multiple batches:                    │  │
│  │   accumulated = -0.0023 × 10 batches = -0.023               │  │
│  │   scaled = -0.023/0.01 = -2.3 → quantize → -2 states       │  │
│  │   actual change = -2 × 0.01 = -0.02                         │  │
│  │   residual = -0.023 - (-0.02) = -0.003 (for next batch)    │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Error-aware Probabilistic Update (EaPU)

EaPU dramatically reduces the number of actual weight updates needed:

```
EaPU Algorithm:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Key Insight: Not all gradients require physical weight updates    │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Gradient Analysis:                                           │  │
│  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │ • 60% of gradients: |g| < 0.001 (negligible)          │  │  │
│  │  │ • 30% of gradients: |g| < 0.01 (small)                │  │  │
│  │  │ • 9% of gradients: |g| < 0.1 (moderate)               │  │  │
│  │  │ • 1% of gradients: |g| ≥ 0.1 (significant)            │  │  │
│  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Traditional approach: Update ALL weights every batch        │  │
│  │  EaPU approach: Probabilistically skip small gradients       │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Update Probability Calculation:                                   │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  p_update = |gradient| / threshold                           │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  if |gradient| ≥ threshold:                                  │  │
│  │      always_update()      // deterministic                   │  │
│  │  else:                                                        │  │
│  │      if random() < p_update:                                 │  │
│  │          update(sign(gradient) × threshold)  // quantized    │  │
│  │      else:                                                    │  │
│  │          accumulate(gradient)  // defer to next batch        │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Expected Update Preservation:                                     │
│  E[actual_update] = p × quantized_value                           │
│                   = (|g|/thresh) × thresh × sign(g)               │
│                   = g    (unbiased estimator!)                    │
│                                                                     │
│  Results:                                                          │
│  • <0.1% actual updates (1000× reduction)                         │
│  • Accuracy within 1% of full-precision training                  │
│  • Write endurance extended by 1000×                              │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Forward-Forward Algorithm for CIM

Geoffrey Hinton's Forward-Forward algorithm is particularly suited for CIM:

```
Forward-Forward vs Backpropagation:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Backpropagation Problems for CIM:                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ 1. Requires storing activations (memory intensive)           │  │
│  │ 2. Weight transpose needed (complex routing)                 │  │
│  │ 3. Long computation graph (latency)                          │  │
│  │ 4. Global error signal (synchronization)                     │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Forward-Forward Advantages:                                       │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ 1. Only forward passes (no backprop)                         │  │
│  │ 2. Local learning rules (layer-wise)                         │  │
│  │ 3. No weight transpose needed                                │  │
│  │ 4. Streaming-compatible                                      │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Algorithm:                                                        │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Positive Pass (real data):                                  │  │
│  │    x_pos = data + label_embedding                            │  │
│  │    h_pos = layer(x_pos)                                      │  │
│  │    goodness_pos = sum(h_pos²)                                │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Negative Pass (corrupted data):                             │  │
│  │    x_neg = data + wrong_label_embedding                      │  │
│  │    h_neg = layer(x_neg)                                      │  │
│  │    goodness_neg = sum(h_neg²)                                │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Loss = log(1 + exp(goodness_neg - goodness_pos + threshold))│  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Update: Maximize goodness_pos, minimize goodness_neg        │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  CIM Implementation:                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐                   │  │
│  │  │ Input   │───▶│ CIM     │───▶│ Local   │                   │  │
│  │  │ + Label │    │ MVM     │    │ Update  │                   │  │
│  │  └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘                   │  │
│  │       │                              │                        │  │
│  │       │      No backward path!       │                        │  │
│  │       └──────────────────────────────┘                        │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Each layer trains independently with local goodness         │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### In-Memory Training Performance Comparison

```
Training Method Comparison:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Method              │ Updates/Epoch │ Accuracy │ Endurance Life   │
│  ────────────────────┼───────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────  │
│  Full Backprop       │ 100%          │ 98.5%    │ 1× (baseline)    │
│  Multi-Tile Residual │ 100%          │ 98.3%    │ 3× (fewer writes)│
│  EaPU (0.1% updates) │ 0.1%          │ 97.8%    │ 1000×            │
│  Forward-Forward     │ 50%           │ 96.5%    │ 2×               │
│  EaPU + FF Combined  │ 0.05%         │ 96.2%    │ 2000×            │
│                                                                     │
│  Energy per Training Epoch:                                        │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  GPU (A100)           ████████████████████████  12,000 mJ    │  │
│  │  CIM Full Backprop    ████████████             6,000 mJ      │  │
│  │  CIM Multi-Tile       ██████████               5,000 mJ      │  │
│  │  CIM EaPU             ██                       800 mJ        │  │
│  │  CIM FF + EaPU        █                        400 mJ        │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in training_attention.go

```go
// Key structures for in-memory training

type WeightPrecisionConfig struct {
    TargetBits   int     // Target effective precision
    DeviceBits   int     // Physical device precision
    NumTiles     int     // Number of tiles for residual
    ScaleFactors []float64
}

type AnalogWeight struct {
    Config      *WeightPrecisionConfig
    Conductance float64
    StateIndex  int
    TargetValue float64
    Residual    float64  // Accumulated residual
}

type MultiTileConfig struct {
    NumTiles     int
    TileBits     int
    ScaleRatios  []float64  // Typically [1.0, 0.1, 0.01, ...]
    CarryThresh  float64
}

type EaPUConfig struct {
    UpdateThreshold float64  // Minimum gradient magnitude
    BaseProb        float64  // Base update probability
    AccumDecay      float64  // Gradient accumulation decay
}

type EaPUTrainer struct {
    Config         *EaPUConfig
    TotalGradients int64
    ActualUpdates  int64
    UpdateRatio    float64  // Target: <0.001 (0.1%)
}
```

### References

- [Nature - Multi-Tile Training](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06067-2)
- [ISSCC 2024 - EaPU Algorithm](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10454291)
- [arXiv - Forward-Forward CIM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.13345)
- [Nature Electronics - In-Memory Learning](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-023-01015-8)

---

## Section 307: CIM for Transformer Attention Mechanisms

### Overview

Transformer attention is the computational bottleneck for large language models. The attention mechanism's O(n²) complexity with sequence length makes it an ideal target for CIM acceleration. Recent advances have demonstrated 70,000× energy reduction and 100× speedup compared to GPU implementations.

### Attention Mechanism Fundamentals

```
Standard Attention Computation:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k) × V                     │
│                                                                     │
│  Where:                                                            │
│  • Q (Query): [batch, heads, seq_len, d_k]                        │
│  • K (Key):   [batch, heads, seq_len, d_k]                        │
│  • V (Value): [batch, heads, seq_len, d_v]                        │
│                                                                     │
│  Computational Breakdown:                                          │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Operation           │ Complexity   │ Memory     │ % Total   │  │
│  │  ────────────────────┼──────────────┼────────────┼────────── │  │
│  │  QK^T computation    │ O(n²d)       │ O(n²)      │ 45%       │  │
│  │  Softmax             │ O(n²)        │ O(n²)      │ 10%       │  │
│  │  Attention × V       │ O(n²d)       │ O(nd)      │ 45%       │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  For GPT-4 scale (n=32K, d=128):                             │  │
│  │  • QK^T: 134 billion MACs per layer                          │  │
│  │  • Memory: 4 GB attention matrices per layer                 │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Gain-Cell KV Cache for CIM

The key innovation is using gain-cell DRAM (IGZO transistors) for efficient KV caching:

```
Gain-Cell KV Cache Architecture:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Traditional KV Cache Problem:                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ • GPT-4: 120 layers × 96 heads × 128 dim × 32K seq           │  │
│  │ • KV cache size: 150+ GB for single sequence!                │  │
│  │ • Memory bandwidth bottleneck dominates latency              │  │
│  │ • Each token generation: read entire KV cache                │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Gain-Cell Solution:                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │      ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │  │
│  │      │            IGZO Gain-Cell Array             │         │  │
│  │      │                                             │         │  │
│  │      │   ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐                 │         │  │
│  │ WL ──│───┤ T ├─┤ T ├─┤ T ├─┤ T ├──               │         │  │
│  │      │   └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘                 │         │  │
│  │      │     │     │     │     │   Stored K/V       │         │  │
│  │      │   ┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐ ┌─┴─┐                 │         │  │
│  │      │   │ C │ │ C │ │ C │ │ C │  values          │         │  │
│  │      │   └───┘ └───┘ └───┘ └───┘                 │         │  │
│  │      │     │     │     │     │                    │         │  │
│  │ BL ──│─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴───  MAC output    │         │  │
│  │      │                                             │         │  │
│  │      └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Advantages:                                                  │  │
│  │  • 2T1C cell (2 transistors, 1 capacitor)                   │  │
│  │  • IGZO: ultra-low leakage (~10^-21 A)                      │  │
│  │  • Retention: >10 hours without refresh                      │  │
│  │  • Non-destructive read enables in-situ MVM                 │  │
│  │  • 10× density vs SRAM                                       │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Performance Comparison:                                           │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Metric           │ GPU SRAM   │ Gain-Cell  │ Improvement   │  │
│  │  ─────────────────┼────────────┼────────────┼────────────── │  │
│  │  Cell size        │ 6T (~140F²)│ 2T1C (35F²)│ 4× denser     │  │
│  │  Leakage power    │ 100 pW     │ 0.01 pW    │ 10,000×       │  │
│  │  Energy/access    │ 5 pJ       │ 0.07 pJ    │ 70×           │  │
│  │  Retention        │ N/A        │ >10 hours  │ No refresh    │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Attention Dataflow

```
CIM-Optimized Attention Dataflow:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Phase 1: Project Q, K, V (Weight-Stationary CIM)                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────┐           │  │
│  │  │ Input x │────▶│ W_Q Crossbar │────▶│ Q       │           │  │
│  │  └─────────┘     └──────────────┘     └─────────┘           │  │
│  │       │          ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────┐           │  │
│  │       └─────────▶│ W_K Crossbar │────▶│ K       │           │  │
│  │       │          └──────────────┘     └─────────┘           │  │
│  │       │          ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────┐           │  │
│  │       └─────────▶│ W_V Crossbar │────▶│ V       │           │  │
│  │                  └──────────────┘     └─────────┘           │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Parallel projection: 3 MVMs in single cycle                 │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Phase 2: QK^T Computation (Key stored in gain-cells)              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │           K^T stored in gain-cell array                 │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐                 │ │  │
│  │  │  │k1,1 │k2,1 │k3,1 │ ... │kn,1 │     │ ← Row 1        │ │  │
│  │  │  ├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤                 │ │  │
│  │  │  │k1,2 │k2,2 │k3,2 │ ... │kn,2 │     │ ← Row 2        │ │  │
│  │  │  ├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤                 │ │  │
│  │  │  │ ... │ ... │ ... │ ... │ ... │     │                 │ │  │
│  │  │  ├─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┼─────┤                 │ │  │
│  │  │  │k1,d │k2,d │k3,d │ ... │kn,d │     │ ← Row d_k      │ │  │
│  │  │  └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘                 │ │  │
│  │  │     ↑     ↑     ↑           ↑                          │ │  │
│  │  │    BL1   BL2   BL3   ...   BLn                         │ │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │  │
│  │                          ↓                                   │  │
│  │  Q vector ──▶ [q1, q2, q3, ..., qd] applied to WLs          │  │
│  │                          ↓                                   │  │
│  │  Output: [s1, s2, s3, ..., sn] = Q·K^T (attention scores)   │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Single MVM computes entire attention row!                   │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Phase 3: Softmax (Digital or Analog)                              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Option A: Digital softmax                                   │  │
│  │  • ADC → exp(x) LUT → sum → divide → DAC                    │  │
│  │  • Accurate but slow (10+ cycles)                            │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Option B: Analog approximation                              │  │
│  │  • Piecewise linear: softmax ≈ ReLU(x - max(x) + 1) / sum   │  │
│  │  • <2% accuracy loss, 3× faster                              │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Option C: Learnable attention                               │  │
│  │  • Replace softmax with trainable linear attention           │  │
│  │  • attention = φ(Q)φ(K)^T where φ is learnable              │  │
│  │  • Fully analog-compatible                                   │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Phase 4: Attention × V (Value stored in gain-cells)              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  V stored in gain-cell array (similar to K^T)               │  │
│  │  Softmax output applied to wordlines                         │  │
│  │  Single MVM produces weighted sum of values                  │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### iMTransformer Architecture

The iMTransformer (in-Memory Transformer) achieves breakthrough performance:

```
iMTransformer Performance:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Architecture Overview:                                            │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │                    CIM Transformer Block                │ │  │
│  │  │                                                         │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐              │ │  │
│  │  │  │   QKV   │──▶│ CIM     │──▶│ Gain-   │              │ │  │
│  │  │  │ Project │   │ Softmax │   │ Cell    │              │ │  │
│  │  │  │ (CIM)   │   │ Approx  │   │ MVM     │              │ │  │
│  │  │  └─────────┘   └─────────┘   └─────────┘              │ │  │
│  │  │       │                            │                   │ │  │
│  │  │       │    ┌────────────────────┐  │                   │ │  │
│  │  │       │    │  Output Project    │◀─┘                   │ │  │
│  │  │       │    │  (CIM)             │                      │ │  │
│  │  │       │    └─────────┬──────────┘                      │ │  │
│  │  │       │              │                                 │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌────┴──────────────┴────┐                           │ │  │
│  │  │  │    Residual + LayerNorm │                           │ │  │
│  │  │  └────────────┬───────────┘                           │ │  │
│  │  │               │                                        │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌────────────┴───────────┐                           │ │  │
│  │  │  │       FFN (CIM)         │                           │ │  │
│  │  │  │  Linear→GELU→Linear    │                           │ │  │
│  │  │  └────────────┬───────────┘                           │ │  │
│  │  │               │                                        │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌────────────┴───────────┐                           │ │  │
│  │  │  │    Residual + LayerNorm │                           │ │  │
│  │  │  └────────────────────────┘                           │ │  │
│  │  │                                                         │ │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Performance Results (vs GPU):                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Metric         │ GPU (A100) │ iMTransformer │ Improvement  │  │
│  │  ───────────────┼────────────┼───────────────┼───────────── │  │
│  │  Latency        │ 89.6 ms    │ 10 ms         │ 8.96×        │  │
│  │  Energy         │ 12.57 J    │ 1 J           │ 12.57×       │  │
│  │  Area           │ 826 mm²    │ 82 mm²        │ 10×          │  │
│  │  TOPS/W         │ 0.312      │ 3.9           │ 12.5×        │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  For BERT-Base (L=12, H=768, A=12):                          │  │
│  │  • 70,000× energy reduction vs naive implementation          │  │
│  │  • 100× speedup for attention computation                    │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Multi-Head Attention Mapping to CIM

```
Multi-Head Attention CIM Mapping:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Challenge: Multiple attention heads require parallel computation  │
│                                                                     │
│  Solution 1: Spatial Parallelism (Multiple Crossbars)              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐         │  │
│  │  │ Head 1  │  │ Head 2  │  │ Head 3  │  │ Head N  │         │  │
│  │  │ QKV     │  │ QKV     │  │ QKV     │  │ QKV     │         │  │
│  │  │ Crossbar│  │ Crossbar│  │ Crossbar│  │ Crossbar│         │  │
│  │  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘         │  │
│  │       │            │            │            │               │  │
│  │       └──────┬─────┴────────────┴────────────┘               │  │
│  │              ▼                                               │  │
│  │       ┌──────────────┐                                      │  │
│  │       │  Concatenate │                                      │  │
│  │       │  + Project   │                                      │  │
│  │       └──────────────┘                                      │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Pros: Maximum parallelism                                   │  │
│  │  Cons: High area cost (N crossbars per layer)               │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Solution 2: Temporal Multiplexing (Single Crossbar)              │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │         Shared QKV Crossbar                             │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐    │ │  │
│  │  │  │W_Q1 │W_K1 │W_V1 │W_Q2 │W_K2 │W_V2 │ ... │     │    │ │  │
│  │  │  │     │     │     │     │     │     │     │     │    │ │  │
│  │  │  │Head1│Head1│Head1│Head2│Head2│Head2│     │     │    │ │  │
│  │  │  └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘    │ │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Process heads sequentially, reuse crossbar                  │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Pros: Minimal area (single crossbar)                        │  │
│  │  Cons: N× latency increase                                   │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  Solution 3: Hybrid (Ferroelectric CIM Approach)                         │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  • Group heads by shared dimensions                          │  │
│  │  • Partial spatial parallelism (4 groups of 3 heads)        │  │
│  │  • Temporal multiplexing within groups                       │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  For 12-head attention:                                      │  │
│  │  • 4 parallel crossbar groups                                │  │
│  │  • 3 heads per group (sequential)                           │  │
│  │  • 4× speedup vs pure temporal                              │  │
│  │  • 3× area reduction vs pure spatial                        │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Flash Attention on CIM

```
Flash Attention Adaptation for CIM:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Standard Attention Memory Problem:                                │
│  • Full attention matrix: O(n²) memory                             │
│  • For n=32K: 32K × 32K × 4 bytes = 4 GB per head!                │
│                                                                     │
│  Flash Attention Solution (Tiled Computation):                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Divide Q, K, V into blocks:                                 │  │
│  │  ┌────┬────┬────┬────┐                                      │  │
│  │  │ Q1 │ Q2 │ Q3 │ Q4 │  Block size: 256-1024 tokens         │  │
│  │  └────┴────┴────┴────┘                                      │  │
│  │  ┌────┬────┬────┬────┐                                      │  │
│  │  │ K1 │ K2 │ K3 │ K4 │                                      │  │
│  │  └────┴────┴────┴────┘                                      │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Compute attention block-by-block:                           │  │
│  │  for i in Q_blocks:                                          │  │
│  │      for j in K_blocks:                                      │  │
│  │          S_ij = Q_i × K_j^T  ← CIM MVM                      │  │
│  │          update running softmax                              │  │
│  │          accumulate V contribution                           │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Memory: O(n) instead of O(n²)                               │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
│  CIM Adaptation:                                                   │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │         K Block in Gain-Cell Array                      │ │  │
│  │  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐│ │  │
│  │  │  │ Block 1: K[0:256]                                   ││ │  │
│  │  │  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤│ │  │
│  │  │  │ Block 2: K[256:512]                                 ││ │  │
│  │  │  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤│ │  │
│  │  │  │ ...                                                 ││ │  │
│  │  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘│ │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Processing:                                                  │  │
│  │  1. Load K block into gain-cell array                        │  │
│  │  2. Stream Q vectors through MVM                             │  │
│  │  3. Accumulate softmax in analog or digital                  │  │
│  │  4. Load next K block, repeat                                │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  Benefits:                                                    │  │
│  │  • Bounded memory (gain-cell array size)                     │  │
│  │  • High utilization (continuous MVM operations)              │  │
│  │  • Compatible with arbitrarily long sequences                │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in training_attention.go

```go
// Key structures for CIM attention

type GainCellConfig struct {
    RetentionTime  time.Duration  // >10 hours typical
    LeakageCurrent float64        // ~10^-21 A for IGZO
    ReadEnergy     float64        // 0.07 pJ/access
    WriteEnergy    float64        // 0.5 pJ/access
    Precision      int            // 6-8 bits
}

type GainCell struct {
    Config    *GainCellConfig
    Voltage   float64
    LastWrite time.Time
}

type KVCacheConfig struct {
    MaxSeqLen     int
    NumHeads      int
    HeadDim       int
    BlockSize     int  // For flash attention
    GainCellConf  *GainCellConfig
}

type KVCache struct {
    Config     *KVCacheConfig
    KeyCache   [][][]*GainCell   // [layer][head][seq×dim]
    ValueCache [][][]*GainCell
    SeqLen     int
}

type CIMAttentionConfig struct {
    NumHeads    int
    HeadDim     int
    DropoutRate float64
    UseCausal   bool
    UseFlash    bool
    BlockSize   int
}

type CIMAttention struct {
    Config  *CIMAttentionConfig
    KVCache *KVCache
    WQ, WK, WV, WO [][]*AnalogWeight
}
```

### Performance Summary

```
CIM Attention Performance Summary:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                     │
│  Metric                    │ Value                                 │
│  ─────────────────────────-┼──────────────────────────────────────  │
│  Energy reduction vs GPU   │ 70,000× (attention only)              │
│  Speedup vs GPU           │ 100× (attention only)                  │
│  iMTransformer latency    │ 8.96× better than GPU                  │
│  iMTransformer energy     │ 12.57× better than GPU                 │
│  Gain-cell density        │ 4× vs SRAM                             │
│  Gain-cell leakage        │ 10,000× lower than SRAM                │
│  KV cache retention       │ >10 hours (no refresh)                 │
│                                                                     │
│  Suitable Applications:                                            │
│  • Edge LLM inference                                              │
│  • Real-time translation                                           │
│  • On-device assistants                                            │
│  • Low-power vision transformers                                   │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
