## Section 325: Neuromorphic Spiking Implementations

### Overview

Spiking neural networks (SNNs) offer event-driven, energy-efficient computing that maps naturally to ferroelectric devices. Recent advances have demonstrated all-ferroelectric SNNs with both neurons and synapses implemented using HZO-based devices.

### Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) Neuron

The fundamental spiking neuron model:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    LIF Neuron Dynamics                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Membrane Equation:                                                 │
│   τ_m × dV/dt = -(V - V_rest) + R × I_in                           │
│                                                                      │
│   Where:                                                             │
│   • τ_m = membrane time constant (~10 ms)                           │
│   • V = membrane potential                                           │
│   • V_rest = resting potential (-65 mV)                             │
│   • R = membrane resistance                                          │
│   • I_in = input current                                            │
│                                                                      │
│       V (mV)                                                         │
│         │                                                            │
│   -50 ──┤──────────────── Threshold ──────── Spike!                 │
│         │              ╱│                     │                      │
│         │           ╱  │                     │                      │
│         │        ╱     │                     │                      │
│   -65 ──┤─────╱────────┼─────────────────────┼──── Reset            │
│         │  ╱           │                     │                      │
│         │╱ Integration │   Refractory        │                      │
│   -70 ──┴──────────────┴─────────────────────┴──────► t             │
│                                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Ferroelectric Neuron Implementations

#### 1. Anti-Ferroelectric Neuron (Nature Communications 2022)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                Anti-Ferroelectric LIF Neuron                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Material: Hf₀.₂Zr₀.₈O₂ (AFE phase)                                │
│                                                                      │
│   Operation:                                                         │
│   • Integration: Polarization accumulates with input voltage         │
│   • Leak: Spontaneous depolarization (intrinsic AFE property)       │
│   • Fire: Threshold crossing triggers conductance change            │
│   • Reset: Automatic via depolarization                              │
│                                                                      │
│   Key Metrics:                                                       │
│   • Energy: 37 fJ/spike                                              │
│   • Endurance: >10¹² cycles                                          │
│   • No external capacitor needed                                     │
│   • No reset circuit needed                                          │
│                                                                      │
│       ┌─────────────┐                                                │
│       │    Gate     │                                                │
│       ├─────────────┤                                                │
│       │ Hf₀.₂Zr₀.₈O₂│ ← Anti-ferroelectric                          │
│       ├─────────────┤                                                │
│       │   Channel   │                                                │
│       └──────┬──────┘                                                │
│          S──┘└──D                                                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 2. MPB-Based LIF Neuron (Advanced Science 2024)

Double-gate morphotropic phase boundary thin-film transistor:

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Device | DG MPBTFT |
| Material | HZO at MPB |
| Structure | Double-gate |
| Energy | <100 fJ/spike |
| SNN Accuracy | 94.9% (MNIST) |
| External caps | None |
| Reset circuit | None |

#### 3. Impact Ionization FET Neuron (Advanced Materials 2024)

2D WSe₂-based spiking neuron:
- **Energy:** 2 pJ/spike
- **Mechanism:** Impact ionization threshold
- **Accuracy:** 87.5% (face classification)
- **Learning:** Unsupervised STDP

### FeFET Synapse with STDP

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STDP Learning Rule                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Spike-Timing-Dependent Plasticity:                                 │
│                                                                      │
│        ΔW                                                            │
│         │     LTP (Potentiation)                                     │
│         │    ╱ Pre → Post                                            │
│         │   ╱                                                        │
│         │  ╱                                                         │
│   ──────┼─╱───────────────────────► Δt = t_post - t_pre             │
│         │╲                                                           │
│         │ ╲                                                          │
│         │  ╲                                                         │
│         │   ╲ LTD (Depression)                                       │
│         │    Post → Pre                                              │
│                                                                      │
│   ΔW = { A⁺ × exp(-Δt/τ⁺)  if Δt > 0 (LTP)                         │
│        { A⁻ × exp(+Δt/τ⁻)  if Δt < 0 (LTD)                         │
│                                                                      │
│   Typical parameters:                                                │
│   • τ⁺, τ⁻ = 20 ms                                                  │
│   • A⁺ = 0.005, A⁻ = 0.00525 (slight LTD bias)                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### FeFET Synapse Properties

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Conductance levels | 16 (4-bit) | MLC operation |
| Min conductance | 1 µS | HRS state |
| Max conductance | 100 µS | LRS state |
| Program voltage | 3V | Polarization switch |
| Retention | 10 years | At room temperature |
| Endurance | 10¹⁰ cycles | Suitable for training |

### All-Ferroelectric SNN Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              All-Ferroelectric SNN (Advanced Science 2024)           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Input Layer          Hidden Layer        Output Layer              │
│   (Rate coding)        (FeFET synapses     (Winner-take-all)         │
│                        + MPB neurons)                                │
│                                                                      │
│   ○───┐                                                              │
│   ○───┤    ┌────────────────────────────┐                           │
│   ○───┼────┤  FeFET Synapse Crossbar    ├────┬──○ Class 0           │
│   ○───┤    │  (16 conductance levels)   │    │                      │
│   ○───┤    └────────────────────────────┘    ├──○ Class 1           │
│   ○───┤               │                      │                      │
│   ○───┤               ▼                      ├──○ Class 2           │
│   ○───┤    ┌────────────────────────────┐    │                      │
│   ○───┼────┤  DG MPBTFT LIF Neurons     ├────┼──○ ...               │
│   ○───┤    │  (37 fJ/spike)             │    │                      │
│   ○───┘    └────────────────────────────┘    └──○ Class 9           │
│                                                                      │
│   Accuracy: 94.9% (MNIST handwritten digits)                         │
│   All ferroelectric: synapses + neurons                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Synaptic Behaviors Demonstrated

Ferroelectric devices successfully emulate:
1. **STP** - Short-term plasticity
2. **LTP** - Long-term potentiation
3. **LTD** - Long-term depression
4. **STDP** - Spike-timing-dependent plasticity
5. **SRDP** - Spike-rate-dependent plasticity
6. **PPF** - Paired-pulse facilitation
7. **EPSC** - Excitatory post-synaptic current

### 2D Material SNNs

| Device | Role | Material | Energy | Accuracy |
|--------|------|----------|--------|----------|
| I2FET | Neuron | WSe₂ | 2 pJ/spike | 87.5% |
| FeFET | Synapse | HZO/WSe₂ | ~1 fJ/op | - |
| n/p-FeFET | Both | Ambipolar WSe₂ | - | - |

### Implementation Reference

```go
// Ferroelectric LIF neuron
type FerroelectricLIFNeuron struct {
    Config              *FerroelectricNeuronConfig
    AccumulatedCharge   float64
    PolarizationState   float64
    EnergyPerSpikeFJ    float64  // 37 fJ for AFE
}

// FeFET synapse with STDP
type FeFETSynapse struct {
    Config           *FeFETSynapseConfig
    Conductance      float64
    Weight           float64
    LTPTimeConstant  float64  // 20 ms
    LTDTimeConstant  float64  // 20 ms
}

// All-ferroelectric SNN
type AllFerroelectricSNN struct {
    Config           *AllFerroelectricSNNConfig
    InputNeurons     []*FerroelectricLIFNeuron
    HiddenNeurons    []*FerroelectricLIFNeuron
    OutputNeurons    []*FerroelectricLIFNeuron
    Synapses         [][]*FeFETSynapse
    Accuracy         float64  // Target: 94.9%
}
```

### Key Performance Comparisons

| Implementation | Energy/Spike | Accuracy | Notes |
|---------------|--------------|----------|-------|
| AFE Neuron | 37 fJ | - | Nature Comm 2022 |
| MPB SNN | ~100 fJ | 94.9% | All-ferroelectric |
| 2D I2FET SNN | 2 pJ | 87.5% | WSe₂-based |
| Spintronic | - | >96% | Memtransistor |

### Challenges and Future Directions

1. **STDP Hardware:** Binary weight updates more practical than analog
2. **Scaling:** Large-scale integration needed for complex tasks
3. **Training:** On-chip learning algorithms still developing
4. **Precision:** Trade-off between energy and accuracy

### References

- [Advanced Science 2024 - All-Ferroelectric SNN](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/advs.202407870)
- [Nature Communications 2022 - AFE Neuron](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-34774-9)
- [Advanced Materials 2024 - 2D I2FET SNN](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202406970)
- [Nano-Micro Letters 2025 - Low-Power Memristor](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40820-025-01705-4)
- [Advanced Electronic Materials 2025 - Spintronic LIF](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aelm.202500091)

## Section 326: CIM Security and PUF Applications

### Overview

Compute-in-memory (CIM) systems introduce unique security opportunities and challenges. The inherent device variations in memristors/ReRAM can be leveraged for security primitives like Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs) and True Random Number Generators (TRNGs), while CIM accelerators themselves require protection against adversarial attacks.

### Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs)

PUFs leverage manufacturing variations as hardware fingerprints for authentication:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Memristor PUF Architecture                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Challenge (C)                                                      │
│        │                                                             │
│        ▼                                                             │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │              Address Decoder / Row/Col Selection             │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│        │                                                             │
│        ▼                                                             │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │    ┌───┬───┬───┬───┐                                        │   │
│   │    │ R₁│ R₂│ R₃│ R₄│   Memristor Array                      │   │
│   │    ├───┼───┼───┼───┤   (inherent variations)                │   │
│   │    │ R₅│ R₆│ R₇│ R₈│                                        │   │
│   │    ├───┼───┼───┼───┤   Compare: Rᵢ > Rⱼ → bit=1            │   │
│   │    │ R₉│R₁₀│R₁₁│R₁₂│            Rᵢ < Rⱼ → bit=0            │   │
│   │    └───┴───┴───┴───┘                                        │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│        │                                                             │
│        ▼                                                             │
│   Response (R) = f(C, device variations)                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### PUF Metrics

| Metric | Ideal | ReRAM PUF | MRAM PUF |
|--------|-------|-----------|----------|
| Uniformity | 50% | 49-51% | 48-52% |
| Inter-Hamming | 50% | 48-50% | 49-51% |
| Intra-Hamming (BER) | 0% | <3% | <10⁻⁸ |
| CRP Space | Large | ~10¹⁹ | ~10¹⁸⁴⁹ |

### 3D Stacked Memristor PUF (ACS Nano 2025)

Self-differential pairing across vertical layers:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    3D Stacked PUF Architecture                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Layer 1 (Top):     ┌───┬───┬───┬───┐                              │
│                      │R₁ₐ│R₂ₐ│R₃ₐ│...│                              │
│                      └───┴───┴───┴───┘                              │
│                         ↕   ↕   ↕   ↕   Self-differential           │
│   Layer 0 (Bottom):  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┐                              │
│                      │R₁ᵦ│R₂ᵦ│R₃ᵦ│...│                              │
│                      └───┴───┴───┴───┘                              │
│                                                                      │
│   Response bit i = 1 if R_ia > R_ib, else 0                         │
│                                                                      │
│   Key Features:                                                      │
│   • Structure: 2 × 32 × 32 (2048 cells total)                       │
│   • CRP space: ~6 × 10¹⁹                                            │
│   • Reconfigurable via cycle-to-cycle variations                    │
│   • ML attack resistant (validated via NIST + simulations)          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Concealable PUF (Science Advances 2022)

HfOx memristor with correlated filamentary switching:

| Operation | Effect | Application |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| SET | Uniform state | Hide PUF identity |
| RESET | Restore variations | Recover PUF |
| Recovery BER | 0% | Perfect restoration |

### True Random Number Generators (TRNGs)

Entropy sources in memristors:

| Source | Mechanism | Throughput | Power |
|--------|-----------|------------|-------|
| RTN | Random telegraph noise | 32 Mbps | 0.04 nJ/bit |
| C2C | Cycle-to-cycle variation | 6 kb/s | Low |
| TD | Time delay variation | Medium | Low |
| HRS | High-resistance variation | Medium | Low |

### Federated Learning with In-Situ PUF/TRNG (Nature Electronics 2025)

Integrated architecture:
- Same memristor array for: compute + PUF + TRNG
- PUF for key generation
- TRNG for error polynomial (cryptography)
- Low overhead security integration

### Adversarial Attack Vulnerabilities

CIM-specific attack surfaces (Nature Electronics 2024):

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CIM Attack Taxonomy                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   1. Adversarial Input Attacks                                       │
│      • Crafted perturbations to fool inference                       │
│      • Transferability reduced by chip-specific variations          │
│                                                                      │
│   2. Side-Channel Attacks                                            │
│      • Power analysis (analog current reveals weights)              │
│      • Timing attacks (data-dependent latency)                      │
│      • EM emanations                                                │
│                                                                      │
│   3. Fault Injection                                                 │
│      • Voltage glitching affects analog computation                 │
│      • Temperature manipulation                                      │
│                                                                      │
│   4. Model Extraction                                                │
│      • Query-based attacks to steal weights                         │
│      • Easier in analog (continuous values)                         │
│                                                                      │
│   5. Weight Tampering                                                │
│      • Physical modification of NVM cells                           │
│      • Row hammer-like attacks                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Defense Mechanisms

| Defense | Mechanism | Effectiveness |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| On-chip finetuning | Adapt to ADC offsets | Breaks attack transferability |
| Noise injection | Add weight perturbation | Masks gradients |
| Quantization | Discretize inputs | Removes small perturbations |
| A-TRICE training | Worst-case aware | Improves safety-critical robustness |

### Implementation Reference

```go
// Memristor PUF
type MemristorPUF struct {
    Config           *PUFConfig
    DeviceVariations [][]float64
    CRPCount         int64        // ~6×10¹⁹ possible
    InterHammingDist float64      // ~50%
    IntraHammingDist float64      // <3% BER
    IsConcealed      bool
}

// TRNG
type MemristorTRNG struct {
    Config          *TRNGConfig
    EntropySource   string       // "RTN", "C2C", "TD"
    BitRate         float64      // 32 Mbps
    NISTTestsPassed int          // 15/15 target
}

// Adversarial defense
type AdversarialDefense struct {
    Config              *AdversarialDefenseConfig
    ADCOffsetPattern    []float64  // Chip-specific
    OnChipFinetuning    bool
    NoiseInjection      bool
}
```

### References

- [Nature Electronics 2024 - Safe, Secure CIM](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-024-01312-y)
- [Science Advances 2022 - Concealable Memristor PUF](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.abn7753)
- [ACS Nano 2025 - 3D Stacked Memristor PUF](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsnano.4c18621)
- [Nature Electronics 2025 - Federated Learning with PUF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-025-01390-6)
- [MDPI Sensors 2024 - Memristor TRNG](https://www.mdpi.com/1424-8220/24/15/5001)

---

## Section 327: Hybrid Digital-Analog CIM Accelerators

### Overview

Hybrid digital-analog CIM architectures combine the energy efficiency of analog computation with the precision and flexibility of digital processing. Key innovations include ADC-less designs, mixed-signal transformers, and adaptive precision allocation.

### ADC Bottleneck Problem

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Pure Analog CIM Overhead                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Power Breakdown (Traditional):                                     │
│                                                                      │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │████████████████████████████████░░░░░░░│  ADC: 30-50%        │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│   ┌───────────████████████████░░░░░░░░░░░░│  DAC: 10-15%        │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│   ┌───────────████████████████████████████│  Array: 25-40%      │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│   ┌─────────────────████████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░│  Digital: 10-20%    │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                      │
│   ADC energy scales exponentially with precision!                   │
│   7-bit ADC: baseline                                               │
│   Each extra bit: ~2× energy                                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HCiM: ADC-Less Hybrid Architecture (ASP-DAC 2024)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HCiM Architecture                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Input Activation (X)                                               │
│          │                                                           │
│          ▼                                                           │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │              Analog CIM Crossbar                             │   │
│   │              (4-bit weights)                                 │   │
│   │              Matrix-Vector Multiplication                    │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│          │ Analog partial sums                                       │
│          │ (NO ADC!)                                                │
│          ▼                                                           │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │              Digital CIM Array                               │   │
│   │              (8-bit scale factors)                           │   │
│   │              Processes scale multiplication                  │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│          │                                                           │
│          ▼                                                           │
│   Output (Y = (W × X) × S)                                          │
│                                                                      │
│   Energy Reduction: 11-28× vs ADC-based                             │
│   Accuracy Drop: <0.5%                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HARDSEA: Hybrid ReRAM-SRAM for Transformers (TVLSI 2024)

Algorithm-architecture-circuit co-design for self-attention:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    HARDSEA Architecture                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Tokens: [T₁, T₂, T₃, ..., Tₙ]                                     │
│              │                                                       │
│              ▼                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │         Product Quantization Clustering                      │   │
│   │         (Lightweight relevance prediction)                   │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│              │                                                       │
│              ▼                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │         Dynamic Sparse Attention Mask                        │   │
│   │         (~90% sparsity achieved)                             │   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│              │                                                       │
│       ┌──────┴──────┐                                               │
│       ▼             ▼                                               │
│   ┌───────────┐ ┌───────────┐                                       │
│   │ ReRAM CIM │ │ SRAM CIM  │                                       │
│   │ (Analog)  │ │ (Digital) │                                       │
│   │ High eff. │ │ High prec │                                       │
│   │ Bulk ops  │ │ Critical  │                                       │
│   └───────────┘ └───────────┘                                       │
│       │             │                                               │
│       └──────┬──────┘                                               │
│              ▼                                                       │
│   Attention Output                                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Light-CIM: Fully Analog Tiles (2025)

Eliminates both ADC and DAC:

| Feature | Traditional CIM | Light-CIM |
|---------|----------------|-----------|
| ADC | Required | None |
| DAC | Required | None |
| Signal domain | Mixed | Fully analog |
| Energy | Baseline | ~10× reduction |
| Accuracy | High | Requires nonideality-aware training |

### Compute-Aware SNR Model

Enables ADC precision reduction:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CSNR Optimization                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Traditional SNR: considers signal + noise separately              │
│   Compute SNR (CSNR): considers computation-relevant noise         │
│                                                                      │
│   Key insight: Some noise doesn't affect computation accuracy       │
│                                                                      │
│   Result:                                                            │
│   • 6 dB CSNR gain achievable                                       │
│   • ADC precision reducible by 3 bits                               │
│   • Energy savings: 40-64×                                          │
│                                                                      │
│   Example:                                                           │
│   • Original: 8-bit ADC                                             │
│   • Optimized: 5-bit ADC (same accuracy!)                           │
│   • Energy: (2⁸)/(2⁵) = 8× baseline reduction                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Per-Column Affine Compensation

Mixed-signal ACIM with compensation (IBM HERMES):
- <1% accuracy drop in kernel/attention benchmarks
- ~10× energy efficiency vs digital
- Per-column calibration handles device variation

### Design Automation Tools

| Tool | Function | Year |
|------|----------|------|
| IMAC-Sim | SPICE-driven CIM simulation | 2024 |
| EasyACIM | Constraint-driven design | 2024 |
| LIMCA | End-to-end optimization | 2025 |
| CMSwitch | Dual-mode compiler | 2025 |

### Performance Comparison

| Architecture | Energy vs Baseline | Accuracy Drop | Best Use Case |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Pure Analog | 1× | 2-5% | Simple models |
| HCiM | 11-28× better | <0.5% | DNNs |
| HARDSEA | 5× better | <1% | Transformers |
| Light-CIM | 10× better | ~1% | Edge AI |
| Per-column ACIM | 10× better | <1% | Kernels |

### Implementation Reference

```go
// HCiM ADC-less accelerator
type HybridCIMAccelerator struct {
    Config            *HybridCIMConfig
    AnalogCrossbar    *AnalogCIMArray   // MVM
    DigitalArray      *DigitalCIMArray  // Scale factors
    EnergyReduction   float64           // 11-28×
    AccuracyDrop      float64           // <0.5%
}

// HARDSEA transformer accelerator
type HARDSEAAccelerator struct {
    Config           *HARDSEAConfig
    ReRAMArrays      []*AnalogCIMArray  // High efficiency
    SRAMArrays       []*DigitalCIMArray // High precision
    SparsityRatio    float64            // ~90%
}

// Mixed-signal design automation
type MixedSignalDesigner struct {
    Config            *MixedSignalConfig
    SNRModel          *ComputeAwareSNRModel
    OptimalADCBits    int               // 3 bits reducible
    EnergyBreakdown   map[string]float64
}
```

### References

- [ASP-DAC 2024 - HCiM ADC-Less Accelerator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.13577)
- [IEEE TVLSI 2024 - HARDSEA](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1109/TVLSI.2023.3337777)
- [IEEE TCAD 2025 - Light-CIM](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025ITCAD..44..602Z/abstract)
- [MDPI Electronics 2025 - Analog Computing Survey](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/14/16/3159)

## Section 328: Edge Deployment Optimizations for CIM

### TinyML and Edge AI Overview

Edge AI enables neural network inference on resource-constrained MCUs:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        EDGE DEPLOYMENT PIPELINE                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐  │
│  │  FP32 Model │───▶│ Quantization│───▶│ CIM Compile │───▶│  Deploy to  │  │
│  │  (PyTorch)  │    │  INT8/INT4  │    │   Mapping   │    │    MCU      │  │
│  └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘  │
│                                                                             │
│  Compression: 4×    Precision: 8-bit   Speedup: 3.2×     Flash: <512KB     │
│  Accuracy: <1%↓     Per-channel        (CIM-MLC)         SRAM: <128KB      │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Quantization for Edge Deployment

INT8/INT4 quantization enables deployment on MCUs:

| Precision | Size Reduction | Accuracy Drop | Best For |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| FP32 | 1× (baseline) | 0% | Training |
| FP16 | 2× | ~0% | GPU inference |
| INT8 | 4× | <1% | MCU inference |
| INT4 | 8× | 1-3% | Extreme edge |
| INT2 | 16× | 3-5% | Binary networks |

Key quantization techniques:
- **Post-training quantization (PTQ)**: Fast, ~1% accuracy drop
- **Quantization-aware training (QAT)**: Higher accuracy, requires retraining
- **Per-channel quantization**: Better accuracy than per-tensor
- **Mixed-precision**: INT4 for attention, INT8 for FFN

### SRAM-CIM Performance Benchmarks

Recent SRAM-CIM achievements (2024-2025):

| Design | Node | TOPS/W | Latency | Precision |
|--------|------|--------|---------|-----------|
| Drive-strength CIM | 65nm | 687.5 | - | INT8 |
| Time-domain CIM | 28nm | 37.01 | 6.6ns | INT8 |
| ADC-less CIM | 28nm | 27.38 | - | INT8 |
| Digital CIM | 28nm | - | 1241 GOPS | 1-8b |

### CIM-MLC Multi-Level Compilation (ASPLOS 2024)

CIM-MLC provides universal compilation for CIM architectures:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CIM-MLC Framework                           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                │
│  Level 1: Graph Optimization                                   │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│  │ Conv ─▶ BN ─▶ ReLU  ══▶  FusedConvBNReLU        │         │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                                                                │
│  Level 2: Tiling Strategy                                      │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│  │ 1024×1024 weight ══▶ 4×4 tiles of 256×256       │         │
│  │ Dataflow: output_stationary / weight_stationary  │         │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                                                                │
│  Level 3: Instruction Generation                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │
│  │ LOAD → MVM → ADD → ACT → STORE (pipelined)      │         │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │
│                                                                │
│  Result: 3.2× speedup vs prior CIM compilers                   │
│                                                                │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CLSA-CIM Cross-Layer Scheduling

CLSA-CIM improves utilization through cross-layer optimization:

- **Utilization improvement**: Up to 17.9×
- **Overall speedup**: Up to 29.2× vs SOTA
- **Key insight**: Schedule across layer boundaries to reduce idle crossbars

### TinyML Memory Constraints

Typical MCU deployment constraints:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              FLASH (512KB)              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │    INT8 Weights (~300KB)        │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │    Code + Constants (~100KB)    │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │    Free (~112KB)                │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              SRAM (128KB)               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │    Input/Output Buffers (64KB)  │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │    Scratch + Stack (64KB)       │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### ReRAM vs SRAM for Edge

| Characteristic | SRAM-CIM | ReRAM-CIM |
|----------------|----------|-----------|
| Density | Lower | Higher (3-5×) |
| Endurance | Unlimited | ~10⁶-10⁹ |
| Non-volatile | No | Yes |
| Energy | Higher leakage | Lower standby |
| Precision | 8+ bits | 4-6 bits |
| Best for | Frequent updates | Edge inference |

### CIM-Aware Model Adaptation

Two-stage adaptation process:
1. **Compress**: Quantization + pruning based on layer importance
2. **Map**: Allocate to crossbars based on macro constraints

Benefits:
- Reduced weight loading latency
- Improved resource utilization
- Maintained accuracy (<1% drop)

### Energy-Aware Deployment

TinyML energy optimization strategies:
- Aggressive duty-cycling
- Event-driven sensing
- Ultra-low-power wake-up
- Dynamic voltage scaling

Performance benchmarks:
- **Inference latency**: 3.47-14.98 ms/frame
- **Energy/inference**: 10.6-22.1 µJ
- **Flash footprint**: 286-536 KB

### References

- [Nature Scientific Reports 2025 - TinyML Object Detection](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-27818-9)
- [ASPLOS 2024 - CIM-MLC](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3620665.3640359)
- [arXiv 2024 - CLSA-CIM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.07671)
- [SRAM-CIM Survey](https://arxiv.org/html/2411.06079v2)
- [TinyML Progress and Futures](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.19076v2)

## Section 329: Analog-to-Spike Conversion Techniques

### Overview

Analog-to-spike conversion enables ADC-free neuromorphic processing:

```
Input Signal    ──▶  Δ-Modulator  ──▶  Spike Train  ──▶  SNN Processing
    │                    │                  │                   │
    ▼                    ▼                  ▼                   ▼
┌────────┐         ┌────────┐         ┌────────┐          ┌────────┐
│ Analog │ ────▶   │ ∫(Δx)  │ ────▶   │ +1/0/-1│  ────▶   │ Neuro- │
│ Input  │         │ > θ ?  │         │ events │          │ morphic│
└────────┘         └────────┘         └────────┘          └────────┘
                        │
            State = State + Δ
            if State > θ: spike +1, State -= θ
            if State < -θ: spike -1, State += θ
```

### Sigma-Delta Neural Networks (SDNNs)

SDNNs combine rate and temporal coding:

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Operation | Update only on input change |
| Temporal sparsity | Natural through delta encoding |
| Spatial sparsity | Selective neuron activation |
| Energy benefit | Reduced power through sparsity |

SDNN dynamics:
```
x[t] = x[t-1] + input_change    // Sigma (integrate)
if |x[t]| > threshold:          // Delta (threshold)
    emit spike(sign(x[t]))
    x[t] -= sign(x[t]) * threshold
```

### Level-Crossing ADC (LC-ADC)

ADC-free signal encoding using level crossings:

```
       ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   1.8V│    ●──●      ●───●                      │
       │   /    \    /     \        ●──●         │
       │  /      \  /       \      /    \        │
       │ ●        ●●         ●────●      ●       │
       │                                         │
   0.0V└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
        Time →

Events:  ↑  ↑  ↓  ↓  ↑  ↑  ↑  ↓  ↓  ↓  ↑  ↑  ↓
```

Key advantages:
- **Compression**: >125× for sparse signals (ENG)
- **NRMSE**: ~4% reconstruction error
- **Power**: µW-level operation
- **Bandwidth**: Adaptive to signal activity

### Send-on-Delta Transmission

Event-driven communication protocol:

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Delta threshold | 1% of range |
| Max interval | 1000 ms |
| Min interval | 1 ms |
| Compression | >100× |

Bio-inspired principle: Only transmit changes, like retinal neurons.

### Pulse Density Modulation (PDM)

Modern MEMS microphones use PDM via sigma-delta:

```
Analog Mic ──▶ ΣΔ Modulator ──▶ PDM bitstream ──▶ SNN
                    │
            1-bit oversampled output
            No explicit ADC needed!
```

Applications:
- Neuromorphic keyword spotting
- Audio classification
- Wake word detection

### Encoding Scheme Comparison

Evaluation on Loihi 2 (July 2024):

| Encoding | Accuracy | Robustness | Hardware Cost |
|----------|----------|------------|---------------|
| Rate | Baseline | Moderate | Low |
| Time-to-first-spike | -2% | Low | Low |
| Binary | -5% | High | Very low |
| Delta modulation | -0.5% | **Best** | Medium |
| Multi-threshold delta | **Best** | 0.7% drop @ 0.1 noise | Medium |

### Event Cameras (Dynamic Vision Sensors)

Event cameras use asynchronous sigma-delta:

| Property | Event Camera | Frame Camera |
|----------|--------------|--------------|
| Temporal resolution | 10 µs | 3 ms |
| Dynamic range | 120 dB | 74 dB |
| Power | ~10 mW | ~3 W |
| Output | Events | Frames |

### Neuromorphic Sensor Fusion

Event-driven processing for biomedical signals:

| Application | Compression | Accuracy | Power Savings |
|-------------|-------------|----------|---------------|
| ECG arrhythmia | 88.64% | 93.59% | >10× |
| ENG recording | >125× | 96% NRMSE | 10× |
| Neural spike | - | >95% | Event-driven |

### Loihi 2 SDNN Implementation

ANN-to-SDNN conversion pipeline:

```
┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
│ Trained ANN │───▶│   Convert   │───▶│   Deploy    │
│  (PyTorch)  │    │  to SDNN    │    │  to Loihi2  │
└─────────────┘    └─────────────┘    └─────────────┘
                         │
                 Weight scaling
                 Threshold tuning
                 Spike encoding
```

### Multi-Threshold Delta Modulation

Adaptive multi-level encoding:

```
Threshold levels: [0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0]

Large change  → High threshold spike (few, high info)
Small change  → Low threshold spike (many, fine detail)

Adaptive: Thresholds adjust based on signal statistics
```

Benefits:
- Better SNR for varying signals
- Only 0.7% accuracy drop at 0.1 noisy spike rate
- Efficient representation of multi-scale dynamics

### Implementation Reference

```go
// Sigma-Delta encoder
type SigmaDeltaEncoder struct {
    Config         *SigmaDeltaConfig
    State          float64           // Integrator
    PreviousInput  float64           // For delta
    SpikeHistory   []int             // +1, 0, -1
}

// Level-crossing ADC
type LevelCrossingADC struct {
    Config          *LevelCrossingConfig
    CurrentLevel    int               // Current quantized level
    Events          []LevelCrossingEvent
    CompressionRatio float64          // >125× typical
}

// Send-on-delta protocol
type SendOnDeltaEncoder struct {
    Config            *SendOnDeltaConfig
    LastValue         float64
    Packets           []DeltaPacket
    AveragePowerUW    float64         // µW-level
}

// Multi-threshold encoder
type MultiThresholdEncoder struct {
    Config          *MultiThresholdConfig
    Thresholds      []float64         // [0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0]
    SpikeCounts     []int             // Per-level stats
    AdaptiveEnabled bool
}
```

### Performance Summary

| Technique | Compression | Error | Power | Best For |
|-----------|-------------|-------|-------|----------|
| Sigma-Delta | >100× | <5% | µW | General |
| Level-crossing | >125× | 4% | µW | Sparse signals |
| Send-on-delta | >100× | <1% | µW | Telemetry |
| PDM | Fixed | - | mW | Audio |
| Event camera | 1000× | - | 10mW | Vision |

### References

- [arXiv 2024 - Neuromorphic KWS with PDM](https://arxiv.org/html/2408.05156)
- [arXiv 2024 - Encoding Schemes on Loihi2](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09260v1)
- [arXiv 2025 - Sigma-Delta on Loihi 2](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.06417)
- [PMC - Implantable NSS with Send-on-Delta](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7614138/)
- [HAL - Neuromorphic Radio Classification](https://hal.science/hal-04181477/document)

## Section 330: Ferroelectric Reservoir Computing

### Reservoir Computing Fundamentals

Reservoir computing (RC) is a recurrent neural network paradigm:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Reservoir Computing Architecture                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Input u(t) ──▶ ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐                    │
│                 │          RESERVOIR                   │                    │
│                 │   ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐ ┌───┐          │                    │
│                 │   │ N1│─│ N2│─│ N3│─│...│          │ ──▶ y(t) Output    │
│                 │   └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘ └─┬─┘          │                    │
│                 │     │     │     │     │             │                    │
│                 │   ┌─▼─────▼─────▼─────▼─┐          │                    │
│                 │   │  Random Recurrent   │          │                    │
│                 │   │  + Fading Memory    │          │                    │
│                 │   └─────────────────────┘          │                    │
│                 └─────────────────────────────────────┘                    │
│                                                                             │
│  Key Properties:                                                            │
│  • Fixed random reservoir (no training)                                     │
│  • Only readout weights are learned                                         │
│  • Fading memory for temporal processing                                    │
│  • Nonlinear transformation of input                                        │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HZO FeFET Physical Reservoir (Nature Communications 2024)

Mixed phase boundary (MPB) transistors for analog RC:

| Property | MPB-TFT | Standard TFT |
|----------|---------|--------------|
| Short-term memory | Inherent | Requires circuit |
| Nonlinearity | Strong | Weak |
| CMOS compatible | Yes | Yes |
| Fading memory | Natural | Artificial |

Key achievement: Fully integrated ARC system using MPB HZO on IGZO TFTs.

### Dual-Memory FeFET Reservoir (arXiv 2025)

Silicon-channel FeFET with HZO integrates two memory modes:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Dual-Memory FeFET Structure                          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                        │
│      Gate ────────────┬────────────                                    │
│                       │                                                │
│              ┌────────▼────────┐                                       │
│              │    HZO Layer    │ ◀── Ferroelectric polarization (LTM)  │
│              │   (15nm opt.)   │                                       │
│              └────────┬────────┘                                       │
│                       │                                                │
│              ┌────────▼────────┐                                       │
│              │  Silicon Channel│ ◀── NQS charge relaxation (STM)       │
│              └────────┬────────┘                                       │
│                       │                                                │
│      Source ─────────┴───────── Drain                                  │
│                                                                        │
│  LTM: Long-term memory from ferroelectric switching                    │
│  STM: Short-term memory from non-quasi-static channel charge           │
│                                                                        │
│  Performance: 1000× speed improvement, 5× efficiency gain              │
│                                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### All-Ferroelectric RC System (Nature Communications 2023)

Complete RC using volatile and nonvolatile ferroelectric diodes:

| Component | Device | Memory Type | Function |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------|
| Reservoir | Volatile FD | Short-term | Temporal processing |
| Readout | Nonvolatile FD | Long-term | Weight storage |

Key insight: Imprint field (Eimp) controls volatility:
- High Eimp → Volatile (fading memory for reservoir)
- Low Eimp → Nonvolatile (stable weights for readout)

### HZO Film Optimization

Optimal HZO thickness for reservoir computing:

| Thickness | Phase | STM Quality | RC Accuracy |
|-----------|-------|-------------|-------------|
| 10 nm | Mixed | Moderate | 88% |
| 15 nm | Orthorhombic | **Optimal** | **93.42%** |
| 20 nm | Monoclinic | Degraded | 85% |

15nm provides best balance of ferroelectric properties and short-term memory.

### Memristor RC Performance

Temporal classification and prediction benchmarks:

| Task | Device | Accuracy/NRMSE | Notes |
|------|--------|----------------|-------|
| TI-46 Digits | WOx/TiOx dual | 98.84% | 16 states |
| Mackey-Glass | HZO memcap | 0.13 NRMSE | 90.3% digit rec. |
| Henon Map | WOx | 0.046 NRMSE | Single memristor |
| MNIST | HZO FeFET | 93.42% | 15nm optimal |

### Echo State Network Properties

Key parameters for physical RC:

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Spectral radius | 0.8-0.99 | Echo state property |
| Sparsity | 1-10% | Memory capacity |
| Input scaling | 0.1-1.0 | Nonlinearity control |
| Leak rate | 0.1-0.5 | Time constant |

### Implementation Reference

```go
// HZO FeFET physical reservoir
type FeFETReservoir struct {
    Config           *FeFETReservoirConfig
    Nodes            []*FeFETNode
    HZOThicknessNm   float64  // Optimal: 15nm
    NonlinearityGain float64  // Polarization response
}

// All-ferroelectric RC system
type AllFerroelectricRC struct {
    ReservoirDiodes  []*VolatileFerroelectricDiode    // Eimp ≠ 0
    ReadoutDiodes    []*NonvolatileFerroelectricDiode // Eimp ≈ 0
    Accuracy         float64                          // Target: 93%+
}

// Echo state network
type EchoStateNetwork struct {
    ReservoirWeights [][]float64 // Sparse, fixed
    OutputWeights    [][]float64 // Learned (ridge regression)
    SpectralRadius   float64     // < 1.0 for echo state
}
```

### References

- [Nature Communications 2024 - MPB Reservoir](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-53321-2)
- [arXiv 2025 - Dual-Memory FeFET](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.07830v1)
- [Nature Communications 2023 - All-Ferroelectric RC](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-39371-y)
- [ACS AMI 2024 - HfAlO Reservoir](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.4c14910)
- [ScienceDirect - HZO Volatile Memory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0272884224029109)

## Section 331: Multi-Die CIM Systems

### Chiplet Architecture Overview

Multi-die architectures overcome monolithic scaling limits:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       Multi-Chiplet CIM System                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│    ┌─────────┐  UCIe  ┌─────────┐  UCIe  ┌─────────┐  UCIe  ┌─────────┐   │
│    │ Compute │◀──────▶│ Compute │◀──────▶│ Compute │◀──────▶│ Compute │   │
│    │ Chiplet │        │ Chiplet │        │ Chiplet │        │ Chiplet │   │
│    └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘   │
│         │ UCIe             │ UCIe             │ UCIe             │         │
│         ▼                  ▼                  ▼                  ▼         │
│    ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐        ┌─────────┐   │
│    │ Compute │        │ Memory  │        │ Memory  │        │   I/O   │   │
│    │ Chiplet │        │ Chiplet │        │ Chiplet │        │ Chiplet │   │
│    └─────────┘        └─────────┘        └─────────┘        └─────────┘   │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### UCIe Standard Evolution

Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express:

| Version | Release | Key Features |
|---------|---------|--------------|
| UCIe 1.0 | 2022 | Initial spec, 2.5D |
| UCIe 1.1 | Aug 2023 | Improvements |
| UCIe 2.0 | Aug 2024 | 3D support, 1.6T I/O |

UCIe 2.0 specifications:
- **Bandwidth**: Up to 1.6 Tbps per link
- **Lanes**: 16-64 lanes per link
- **Speed**: Up to 32 Gbps per lane
- **Latency**: ~2-5 ns die-to-die
- **3D support**: Through-silicon vias (TSV)

### Intel Multi-Chiplet Demo (ISSCC 2025)

Intel demonstrated 20-chiplet heterogeneous integration:

| Aspect | Specification |
|--------|---------------|
| Chiplets | 20 total |
| Foundries | 2 different |
| Interface | Configurable, bandwidth-scalable |
| Goal | Remove AI bandwidth bottlenecks |

Key innovation: "Mix and match" compatible host for chiplets from different manufacturers.

### Hybrid CIM Architectures (DAC 2024)

Recent multi-chip CIM systems:

| System | Architecture | Application |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| HEIRS | 3D RRAM+SRAM | Transformer acceleration |
| Hyb-Learn | RRAM/SRAM hybrid | Continual learning |
| MRAM-SRAM | Hybrid memory | Sparse on-device learning |
| AIG-CIM | Tri-gear heterogeneous | Diffusion acceleration |

### Network-on-Chip Optimization

NoC impact on CIM performance:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    NoC Performance Comparison                          │
├────────────────────┬─────────────┬─────────────┬─────────────────────┤
│ Metric             │ H-Tree Bus  │ NoC-Mesh    │ C-Mesh (optimized)  │
├────────────────────┼─────────────┼─────────────┼─────────────────────┤
│ Comm. Latency      │ 40-90%      │ 20-40%      │ 10-25%              │
│ Energy-Delay-Area  │ 1× (base)   │ 0.5×        │ 0.17× (6× better)   │
│ Scalability        │ Poor        │ Good        │ Excellent           │
└────────────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────────────┘
```

Key findings:
- 40-90% of latency in H-Tree is communication
- Custom IMC NoC achieves 20-80% latency improvement
- 5-25% end-to-end inference latency reduction
- Up to 6× EDAP improvement for VGG-19

### PIM Architecture Categories

Processing-in-memory hierarchy:

| Level | Location | Parallelism | Best For |
|-------|----------|-------------|----------|
| In-subarray | Within memory array | Massive | Simple ops |
| Bank-level | Per memory bank | High | Matrix ops |
| Logic-die | On logic layer | Moderate | Complex ops |

### ReRAM PIM System Architecture

Typical ReRAM-based PIM (HURRY architecture):

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ReRAM PIM Chip (16 Tiles)                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐           │
│  │  Tile   │  │  Tile   │  │  Tile   │  │  Tile   │           │
│  │  8 IMA  │  │  8 IMA  │  │  8 IMA  │  │  8 IMA  │           │
│  │ 512KB   │  │ 512KB   │  │ 512KB   │  │ 512KB   │           │
│  │  eDRAM  │  │  eDRAM  │  │  eDRAM  │  │  eDRAM  │           │
│  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘           │
│        ...        ...        ...        ...                    │
│                                                                 │
│  IMA = In-situ MAC array (512×512 ReRAM)                       │
│  Each IMA: 32KB input SRAM, 2KB output SRAM                    │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Optical Interconnect Developments

Ayar Labs UCIe optical chiplet (OFC 2025):

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Bandwidth | 8 Tbps |
| Technology | Silicon photonics + CMOS |
| Light source | 16-wavelength SuperNova |
| Standard | UCIe compatible |

Advantages over copper:
- Higher bandwidth
- Lower power at distance
- Reduced thermal issues

### Scalability Challenges

Die-to-die interface considerations:

| Challenge | Solution Approach |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Heterogeneous interfaces | Flexible D2D protocols |
| Bandwidth bottlenecks | Real-time adaptive routing |
| Power delivery | Advanced packaging |
| Thermal management | 3D/2.5D heat spreading |

```go
// Multi-die CIM system

    Config           *MultiDieConfig
    Chiplets         []*CIMChiplet
    Links            []*UCIeLink

}

// UCIe link configuration

    BandwidthGbps     float64  // 512 Gbps per link
    LatencyNs         float64  // ~2-5 ns
    UCIeVersion       string   // "2.0"
}

// NoC for CIM tiles
type CIMNoC struct {
    TopologyType      string   // "mesh", "cmesh"
    AverageLatency    float64  // Cycles
    PowerMW           float64
    EffectiveBandwidth float64
}
```

## Section 332: Optical Interconnects for CIM

### Silicon Photonics for AI

Optical interconnects address AI data center bottlenecks:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Silicon Photonics Evolution                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  2024: 224 Gbps/lane    Copper reach < 1m    30% DC energy on interconnect │
│                                    │                                        │
│                                    ▼                                        │
│  2025: Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)                                            │
│        • Nvidia: Quantum-X InfiniBand with TSMC COUPE                      │
│        • Intel: First integrated OCI chiplet with CPU                       │
│        • Broadcom: Tomahawk 6 (102.4 Tbps) with CPO                        │
│                                    │                                        │
│                                    ▼                                        │
│  2026+: Fully integrated photonic compute                                   │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### MZI-Based Photonic Neural Networks

Mach-Zehnder interferometer meshes implement unitary transformations:

| Topology | Structure | Advantages |
|----------|-----------|------------|
| Clements | Rectangular mesh | Balanced depth |
| Reck | Triangular mesh | Simple decomposition |
| Butterfly | FFT-like | Low depth |

MZI transfer matrix:
```
T = [cos(θ/2)·e^(iφ)    sin(θ/2)    ]
    [-sin(θ/2)·e^(iφ)   cos(θ/2)    ]
```

### Coherent Optical Neural Networks

Recent coherent ONN achievements:

| System | Speed | Efficiency | Year |
|--------|-------|------------|------|
| 16-ch coherent processor | 1.28 TOPS | - | 2024 |
| Taichi photonic chiplet | - | 160 TOPS/W | Science 2024 |
| LiNbO₃ tensor core | 120 GOPS | - | Nature Comm 2024 |
| Low-photon ONN | - | 0.66 photons/mult | Nature Comm |

### WDM Microring Weight Banks

Wavelength-division multiplexing enables parallel processing:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                        │
│     λ₁ λ₂ λ₃ λ₄ λ₅ λ₆ λ₇ λ₈    (8 wavelength channels)               │
│      │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │                                           │
│      ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼  ▼                                           │
│    ┌──────────────────────────┐                                        │
│    │   ○   ○   ○   ○   ○   ○  │ ◀── Microring array                   │
│    └──────────────────────────┘                                        │
│                                                                        │
│  • On-resonance: weight ≈ 1                                           │
│  • Off-resonance: weight ≈ 0                                          │
│  • Thermal tuning: continuous weight control                           │
│                                                                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### WDM Microring Modulator Arrays

High-speed WDM demonstrated:

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Total data rate | 1 Tbps | Nature Comm 2024 |
| Per-channel | 200 Gbps | 5-channel array |
| Energy | <10 fJ/bit | Z-shape junction |
| Bandwidth | >60 GHz | Adiabatic design |
| Footprint | 20×20 µm | Per modulator |

### Optical Circuit Switching

iPronics ONE-32 (March 2025):
- First silicon photonics optical circuit switch
- Eliminates O-E-O conversions
- 50% power reduction vs electrical switching
- UCIe compatible

### Photonic Computing Energy Efficiency

Ultra-low energy optical computation:

| Approach | Energy | Accuracy |
|----------|--------|----------|
| ~3.1 photons/mult | 2.5×10⁻¹⁹ J optical | 99% |
| ~0.66 photons/mult | - | 90% |
| Thermal MZI | ~10 mW/actuator | >99% |
| Phase-change MRR | Non-volatile | Variable |

### Thermal Crosstalk Challenge

Major accuracy limitation in photonic NNs:
- Neighboring heaters affect each other
- Compensation via calibration needed
- Active temperature control required

### Implementation Reference

```go
// MZI-based unitary transformation
type MZI struct {
    Phase1      float64            // Internal phase (θ)
    Phase2      float64            // External phase (φ)
    TransferMatrix [2][2]complex128
}

// Coherent optical neural network
type CoherentONN struct {
    InputMesh      *MZIMesh       // U transformation
    DiagonalGains  []float64      // Σ (attenuators)
    OutputMesh     *MZIMesh       // V† transformation
    ComputeSpeedTOPS float64      // 1.28 TOPS
}

// Microring weight bank
type MicroringResonator struct {
    ResonanceWavelength float64
    ThermalShift        float64   // Weight control
    CouplingRatio       float64   // 0-1
}
```

## Section 333: In-Memory Training Algorithms

### Training vs Inference in AIMC

In-memory training requirements beyond inference:

| Operation | Inference | Training |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Forward pass | ✓ | ✓ |
| Backward pass | ✗ | ✓ |
| Gradient computation | ✗ | ✓ |
| Weight update | ✗ | ✓ |
| Precision needed | 4-8 bit | 8+ bit |

### CMO/HfOx ReRAM All-in-One (IBM 2025)

All-in-one analog AI accelerator capabilities:
- On-chip training
- Weight retention
- Long-term inference acceleration
- Forward and backward MVM
- Parallel weight updates via outer product

### Outer Product Weight Update

Parallel weight updates in O(1) time:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Crossbar Outer Product Update                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│        Activation x              Error δ                                    │
│        ┌─┬─┬─┬─┐               ┌─┬─┬─┬─┐                                   │
│        │x│x│x│x│               │δ│δ│δ│δ│                                   │
│        └┬┴┬┴┬┴┬┘               └┬┴┬┴┬┴┬┘                                   │
│         │ │ │ │                 │ │ │ │                                     │
│    Row  ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼                 ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼  Col                               │
│    Pulse ┌─┬─┬─┬─┐             Pulse                                        │
│         │●│●│●│●│                                                           │
│         ├─┼─┼─┼─┤              ΔW[i][j] = η × x[i] × δ[j]                  │
│         │●│●│●│●│                                                           │
│         └─┴─┴─┴─┘                                                           │
│                                                                             │
│  Simultaneous pulse application → O(1) update for entire matrix!           │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Tiki-Taka Algorithm

Handles device asymmetry (SET ≠ RESET):

```
Effective Weight: W = A + γC

Matrix A: Primary (slow updates)
Matrix C: Auxiliary (fast, captures gradients)

Update rule:
1. C ← C - η·∇L     (gradient to C)
2. A ← A + γ·ΔC     (transfer to A)
```

| Property | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| Analog switching | Both directions |
| Symmetry point | Within conductance window |
| Demonstrated | 30+ states, 5% skew |

### Multi-Tile Residual Learning

Addressing limited conductance levels:

| Challenge | Solution |
|-----------|----------|
| ReRAM limited to 4-bit | Multiple tiles |
| Need 8-bit for training | 4×4-bit residual |
| Precision mismatch | Gradient accumulation |

### Equilibrium Propagation

Local learning without explicit backpropagation:

```
Free Phase:     Relax to equilibrium (no output clamping)
                Store s⁻ (free states)

Clamped Phase:  Weakly clamp output toward target
                Store s⁺ (clamped states)

Weight Update:  ΔW ∝ (s_i⁺ × s_j⁺ - s_i⁻ × s_j⁻) / β
```

Advantages:
- Only local information needed
- No gradient storage required
- Hardware-friendly for memristors
- 92% MNIST with 10% defects

### Device Requirements for Training

| Parameter | Inference | Training |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| States | 16-32 | 32-256 |
| Linearity | Moderate | High |
| Symmetry | Not critical | Critical |
| Endurance | 10⁶ | 10⁹+ |
| Variability | <10% | <5% |

### Fast Parallel Training Methods

Two algorithms for fast in-memory training:
1. Stochastic weight update thresholding
2. Parallelly pulsed outer product increments

Both achieve:
- Constant time O(1) for weight updates
- Reduced energy consumption
- Tolerance to device variation

### Contrastive Learning Integration

Multi-optimization for in-situ training:
- Improved feature extraction
- Enhanced robustness
- Optimized loss function
- Better gradient calculation

### Implementation Reference

```go
// Tiki-Taka dual matrix
type TikiTakaTrainer struct {
    MatrixA        [][]*MemristorDevice  // Primary
    MatrixC        [][]*MemristorDevice  // Auxiliary
    GammaFactor    float64               // Mixing: W = A + γC
}

// Equilibrium propagation network
type EquilibriumPropNetwork struct {
    Layers         []*EPLayer
    Weights        [][][]*MemristorDevice
    Beta           float64  // Clamping strength
}

// Crossbar outer product update
type CrossbarArray struct {
    Devices        [][]*MemristorDevice
    // O(1) parallel update
    func OuterProductUpdate(x, δ []float64, η float64)
}
```

### Performance Benchmarks

| System | Task | Accuracy | Notes |
|--------|------|----------|-------|
| CMO/HfOx | MNIST | >95% | All-in-one |
| EP memristor | MNIST | 92% | 10% defects |
| Tiki-Taka v2 | - | Competitive | 4K ECRAM array |
| M-SDC | On-chip | >90% | 30 memristors |

### References

- [IBM Research 2025 - CMO/HfOx ReRAM](https://research.ibm.com/publications/on-chip-training-and-inference-using-analog-cmohfox-reram-artificial-synapses)
- [Wiley AFM 2025 - All-in-One Analog AI](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adfm.202504688)
- [Nature Comm 2024 - Fast In-Memory Training](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-51221-z)
- [arXiv - Exact Gradient Training](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.12774v1)
- [MDPI - EP Memristor Crossbar](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-666X/14/7/1367)

## Section 334: GNN Acceleration with Compute-in-Memory

### Overview

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) pose unique challenges for CIM accelerators due to their irregular memory access patterns during neighbor aggregation. Recent work (2024-2025) addresses the aggregation-combination dichotomy with specialized architectures.

### GNN Execution Characteristics

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           GNN Layer Execution Pipeline                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Aggregation │───▶│ Combination  │───▶│  Activation  │  │
│  │   (Memory)   │    │  (Compute)   │    │    (ReLU)    │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         ▼                   ▼                   ▼           │
│   O(E × F) ops        O(N × F × F')        O(N × F')       │
│   Irregular          Regular/Dense         Element-wise    │
│   Memory-bound       Compute-bound         Trivial         │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Where: N = nodes, E = edges, F = input features, F' = output features
```

### NEM-GNN Architecture (ACM TACO 2024)

DAC/ADC-less near-memory GNN accelerator with:
- **CAR Scheduling:** Compute-As-Ready broadcast for neighbor aggregation
- **Early Termination:** Skip low-magnitude neighbors (sparsity exploitation)
- **Pre-compute:** Overlap aggregation with previous layer combination

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   NEM-GNN Architecture                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Memory Banks          Near-Memory PEs        Global       │
│   ┌─────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐   Aggregator    │
│   │ Bank 0  │◀────────▶│ PE0  PE1  PE2   │───┐             │
│   ├─────────┤          │ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐  │   │             │
│   │ Bank 1  │◀────────▶│ │SP│ │SP│ │SP│  │   │  ┌──────┐  │
│   ├─────────┤          │ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘  │   ├─▶│ Acc  │  │
│   │ Bank 2  │◀────────▶│  Scratchpads    │   │  └──────┘  │
│   ├─────────┤          └─────────────────┘   │             │
│   │ Bank 3  │◀────────────────────────────────┘             │
│   └─────────┘                                               │
│                                                              │
│   CAR Broadcast: Ready nodes trigger immediate aggregation  │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Performance vs ReFLIP:
├── 80-230× speedup
├── 80-300× throughput improvement
├── 850-1134× energy efficiency
└── 7-8× compute density
```

### PyGim Hybrid CPU/PIM Execution (arXiv 2024)

Combination of Accelerators (CoA) scheme:
- **PIM:** Memory-intensive aggregation (neighbor features)
- **CPU/GPU:** Compute-intensive combination (weight multiplication)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 PyGim Hybrid Execution                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Graph Data                                                  │
│      │                                                       │
│      ▼                                                       │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │        Workload Partitioner              │               │
│  │   (Arithmetic Intensity Analysis)         │               │
│  └───────────────┬──────────────────────────┘               │
│                  │                                           │
│      ┌───────────┴───────────┐                              │
│      ▼                       ▼                              │
│  ┌────────────┐       ┌────────────┐                       │
│  │    PIM     │       │  CPU/GPU   │                       │
│  │ Aggregation│       │Combination │                       │
│  │ (16 banks) │       │ (8 cores)  │                       │
│  └─────┬──────┘       └──────┬─────┘                       │
│        │                     │                              │
│        └──────────┬──────────┘                              │
│                   ▼                                          │
│            ┌─────────────┐                                   │
│            │   Output    │                                   │
│            └─────────────┘                                   │
│                                                              │
│  Decision: AI = F' → if AI < threshold, use PIM             │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### BingoCGN (ISCA 2025)

Scalable GNN accelerator for real-time large-scale graphs:
- **Graph Partitioning:** Locality-aware node distribution
- **Cross-partition Quantization:** Reduce inter-partition traffic
- **Training Algorithm:** Maintain accuracy despite quantization

### GINA - NVM-based PIM Accelerator (ACM TECS 2025)

3D PIM architecture for GNN inferencing:
- Uses non-volatile memory (ReRAM/PCM crossbars)
- Addresses NVM non-idealities affecting accuracy
- Layer-specific optimization strategies

### GCN Layer Implementation

```
GCN Forward Pass: H' = σ(D⁻¹/² A D⁻¹/² H W)

Where:
├── A = Adjacency matrix (with self-loops)
├── D = Degree matrix (diagonal)
├── H = Input node features [N × F]
├── W = Weight matrix [F × F']
└── σ = Activation function (ReLU)

Aggregation (Memory-bound):
  h_agg[i] = Σⱼ∈N(i) (1/√(d_i × d_j)) × h[j]

Combination (Compute-bound):
  h'[i] = σ(h_agg[i] × W + b)
```

### Performance Comparison

| Accelerator | Target | Speedup | Energy Eff | Key Innovation |
|-------------|--------|---------|------------|----------------|
| NEM-GNN | Near-memory | 80-230× | 850-1134× | CAR scheduling |
| PyGim | Real PIM | Variable | High | CoA scheme |
| BingoCGN | Large graphs | High | High | Cross-partition quant |
| GINA | NVM-PIM | Moderate | Very high | 3D integration |
| MEGA (HPCA'24) | Mixed precision | High | High | Degree-aware quant |

### Implementation Reference

```go
// NEM-GNN near-memory accelerator
type NEMGNN struct {
    Config       *NEMGNNConfig
    PEs          []*NEMPE        // Processing elements
    Scheduler    *CARScheduler   // Compute-as-ready
    // 80-230× speedup vs ReFLIP
}

// GCN layer with symmetric normalization
type GCNLayer struct {
    Weights     [][]float64  // [InputDim][OutputDim]
    CachedNorm  []float64    // D^(-1/2) caching
    // Forward: H' = σ(D^-1/2 A D^-1/2 H W)
}

// PyGim hybrid execution
type PyGim struct {
    PIMBanks    []*PIMBank   // Memory-intensive
    CPUCores    int          // Compute-intensive
    // CoA scheme for GNN
}
```

### References

- [NEM-GNN - ACM TACO 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3652607)
- [PyGim - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16731v1)
- [BingoCGN - ISCA 2025](https://hpcrl.github.io/ICS2025-webpage/program/Proceedings_ICS25/ics25-76.pdf)
- [GINA - ACM TECS 2025](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3759918)
- [GNN Accelerator Survey - Frontiers 2025](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11704-023-3307-2)
