## Section 335: Emerging Memory Technologies for CIM (MRAM, PCM)

### Overview

Beyond ferroelectric memory, other emerging non-volatile memories offer complementary advantages for compute-in-memory. This section covers STT-MRAM, SOT-MRAM, and PCM - their physics, CIM applications, and comparison with FeFET.

### STT-MRAM (Spin-Transfer Torque MRAM)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                STT-MRAM Cell Structure                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│         Bit Line (BL)                                        │
│              │                                               │
│              ▼                                               │
│         ┌─────────┐                                          │
│         │  Free   │◀── Magnetization switchable              │
│         │  Layer  │    by spin-polarized current             │
│         ├─────────┤                                          │
│         │  MgO    │◀── Tunnel barrier (~1nm)                 │
│         │ Barrier │                                          │
│         ├─────────┤                                          │
│         │ Fixed   │◀── Reference magnetization               │
│         │ Layer   │                                          │
│         └────┬────┘                                          │
│              │                                               │
│         Source Line (SL)                                     │
│                                                              │
│  States:                                                     │
│  ├── Parallel (P): Low resistance (R_P)                     │
│  └── Anti-Parallel (AP): High resistance (R_AP)             │
│                                                              │
│  TMR = (R_AP - R_P) / R_P × 100% ≈ 200%                     │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

TSMC 16nm STT-MRAM (2024):
├── Read time: 7.5 ns
├── Write time: 20 ns
├── Endurance: 10¹² cycles
├── Retention: 10 years @ 85°C
└── Cell area: ~30 F²
```

### SOT-MRAM (Spin-Orbit Torque MRAM)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                SOT-MRAM Cell Structure                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Read Path (Vertical)     Write Path (Horizontal)           │
│         │                         │                          │
│         ▼                         ▼                          │
│    ┌─────────┐              Write Current                    │
│    │  Free   │◀────────────────────────────                  │
│    │  Layer  │                    │                          │
│    ├─────────┤                    │                          │
│    │   MgO   │                    │                          │
│    ├─────────┤                    │                          │
│    │  Fixed  │                    │                          │
│    └────┬────┘                    │                          │
│         │                         │                          │
│    ═════╪═════════════════════════╪═════                     │
│    │    Heavy Metal (W, Ta, Pt)   │                          │
│    ═════════════════════════════════════                     │
│                                                              │
│  Spin Hall Effect:                                           │
│  ├── Current in heavy metal → Spin current (perpendicular)  │
│  └── Spin current → Torque on free layer                    │
│                                                              │
│  Advantages over STT:                                        │
│  ├── Separate read/write paths                              │
│  ├── No current through tunnel barrier during write         │
│  ├── 4× faster switching (~10 ns)                           │
│  └── 1% power consumption (ITRI/TSMC IEDM 2023)             │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Imec SOT-MRAM (2024):
├── Switching energy: <100 fJ
├── Endurance: >10¹⁵ cycles
├── 63% energy reduction vs conventional
└── Scaling to sub-10nm
```

### Phase Change Memory (PCM)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   PCM Cell Operation                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│              Top Electrode                                   │
│                   │                                          │
│         ┌─────────┴─────────┐                               │
│         │                   │                               │
│         │   Phase Change    │                               │
│         │    Material       │                               │
│         │   (GST: Ge₂Sb₂Te₅)│                               │
│         │                   │                               │
│         └─────────┬─────────┘                               │
│                   │                                          │
│              Heater                                          │
│                   │                                          │
│           Bottom Electrode                                   │
│                                                              │
│  States:                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐                     │
│  │ Crystalline │        │  Amorphous  │                     │
│  │   (SET)     │        │   (RESET)   │                     │
│  │  Low R: 10kΩ│        │ High R: 10MΩ│                     │
│  │  Ordered    │        │  Disordered │                     │
│  └─────────────┘        └─────────────┘                     │
│        ▲                       ▲                            │
│        │                       │                            │
│    Long pulse              Short pulse                       │
│    (crystallize)           (melt-quench)                    │
│                                                              │
│  Multi-Level Cell (MLC):                                    │
│  └── Partial crystallization → 16+ levels (4 bits)         │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

STMicroelectronics 18nm PCM (2024):
├── GST on FD-SOI
├── Operating temp: up to 165°C (automotive)
├── Endurance: 10⁸ cycles
└── Production: 2024-2025
```

### Resistance Drift in PCM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PCM Resistance Drift Model                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   R(t) = R₀ × (t/t₀)^ν                                      │
│                                                              │
│   Where:                                                     │
│   ├── R₀ = Initial resistance                               │
│   ├── t₀ = Reference time (typically 1s)                    │
│   ├── ν  = Drift coefficient (0.02-0.1)                     │
│   └── t  = Time since programming                           │
│                                                              │
│   Drift vs Level:                                            │
│   10MΩ ────●                                                │
│            │ ╲                                               │
│            │   ╲    High states drift more                  │
│            │     ╲                                           │
│   1MΩ  ────┼───────●                                        │
│            │         ╲                                       │
│            │           ╲                                     │
│   100kΩ ───┼─────────────●──────                            │
│            │               Low states stable                 │
│   10kΩ  ───┼───────────────────●────                        │
│            └───────────────────────────▶                    │
│            1s     100s    10ks   1Ms   Time                 │
│                                                              │
│   Mitigation strategies:                                     │
│   ├── Drift-aware encoding (lower states)                   │
│   ├── Periodic refresh                                       │
│   └── Read-before-write verification                        │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Memory Technology Comparison

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          Emerging Memory Technology Comparison               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│ Metric        │ SRAM  │STT-MRAM│SOT-MRAM│  PCM  │ FeFET  │ │
│───────────────┼───────┼────────┼────────┼───────┼────────│ │
│ Read (ns)     │  0.5  │   7.5  │   5.0  │  50   │  20    │ │
│ Write (ns)    │  0.5  │  20.0  │  10.0  │ 100   │  50    │ │
│ Endurance     │ 10¹⁶  │  10¹²  │  10¹⁵  │ 10⁸   │ 10¹⁰   │ │
│ Retention     │  Vol  │ 10 yr  │ 10 yr  │ 10 yr │ 10 yr  │ │
│ E/bit (fJ)    │   1   │  100   │   50   │  10   │   1    │ │
│ Area (F²)     │ 100   │   30   │   50   │   8   │   6    │ │
│ CIM (TOPS/W)  │  10   │   50   │  100   │ 200   │ 500    │ │
│───────────────┴───────┴────────┴────────┴───────┴────────│ │
│                                                              │
│ Use Cases:                                                   │
│ ├── SRAM: Cache, buffers (speed)                            │
│ ├── STT-MRAM: IoT, embedded (endurance + NV)                │
│ ├── SOT-MRAM: Cache replacement (speed + NV)                │
│ ├── PCM: Storage class memory (density)                     │
│ └── FeFET: Neural networks (efficiency)                     │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Architecture with Emerging Memories

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Hybrid Memory CIM Architecture                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│   │              Weight Storage (PCM)               │       │
│   │  High density, 4-bit MLC, drift-tolerant coding │       │
│   └───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘       │
│                           │ Load weights                     │
│                           ▼                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│   │          Weight Buffer (STT-MRAM)               │       │
│   │     Fast access, high endurance, non-volatile   │       │
│   └───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘       │
│                           │                                  │
│   ┌───────────┬───────────┼───────────┬───────────┐        │
│   ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼        │
│ ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     │
│ │ CIM │   │ CIM │     │ CIM │     │ CIM │     │ CIM │     │
│ │Array│   │Array│     │Array│     │Array│     │Array│     │
│ │FeFET│   │FeFET│     │FeFET│     │FeFET│     │FeFET│     │
│ └──┬──┘   └──┬──┘     └──┬──┘     └──┬──┘     └──┬──┘     │
│    │         │           │           │           │         │
│    └─────────┴───────────┼───────────┴───────────┘         │
│                          ▼                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│   │        Activation Buffer (SOT-MRAM)             │       │
│   │     Fast read/write, ultra-high endurance       │       │
│   └───────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘       │
│                           │                                  │
│                           ▼                                  │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│   │              Output (SRAM)                      │       │
│   │            Volatile, fastest access             │       │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Industry Status (2024-2025)

**TSMC Production Status:**
- STT-MRAM: Production on 16nm FinFET (2024)
- RRAM: Production
- PCRAM: Exploratory
- SOT-MRAM: Exploratory (IEDM 2023 with ITRI)

**STMicroelectronics:**
- PCM (GST) on 18nm FD-SOI
- Automotive grade (165°C)
- Production 2024-2025

**Market Projections:**
- PCM market: $564.7M (2024) → $6.18B (2034)
- CAGR: 27.2%
- Key drivers: Edge AI, automotive, IoT

### Implementation Reference

```go
// STT-MRAM compute-in-memory
type STTMRAM struct {
    Cells       [][]*STTMRAMCell
    TMRRatio    float64  // ~200%
    WriteTime   float64  // 20 ns
    Endurance   int64    // 10^12
}

// SOT-MRAM with separate read/write
type SOTMRAM struct {
    Cells          [][]*SOTMRAMCell
    SwitchingEnergy float64  // <100 fJ
    WriteTime      float64   // 10 ns (4× faster than STT)
    Endurance      int64     // 10^15
}

// PCM with drift model
type PCM struct {
    Cells            [][]*PCMCell
    NumLevels        int     // 16 levels (4-bit)
    DriftCoefficient float64 // 0.05
    // R(t) = R0 × (t/t0)^ν
}

// Hybrid memory system
type HybridMemoryCIM struct {
    STTMRAM   *STTMRAM   // Weight buffer
    SOTMRAM   *SOTMRAM   // Activations
    PCM       *PCM       // Model storage
    FeFET     *Crossbar  // CIM compute
}
```

### Performance Benchmarks

| Memory | Task | Metric | Value | Reference |
|--------|------|--------|-------|-----------|
| STT-MRAM | MVM | Endurance | 10¹² | TSMC 2024 |
| SOT-MRAM | Cache | Energy | <100 fJ | Imec 2024 |
| SOT-MRAM | Speed | Write | 10 ns | ITRI 2023 |
| PCM | MLC | Levels | 16+ | STMicro 2024 |
| PCM | Drift | ν coeff | 0.05 | Literature |
| Hybrid | AI SoC | Efficiency | High | Multi-vendor |

## Section 336: CIM for Recommendation Systems

### Overview

Deep Learning Recommendation Models (DLRMs) are memory-bound workloads where embedding table lookups dominate execution time. CIM architectures address this by processing embeddings where they are stored, eliminating costly data movement.

### DLRM Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Deep Learning Recommendation Model              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Dense Features          Sparse Features (Categorical)       │
│  [x₁,x₂,...,x₁₃]         [idx₁] [idx₂] ... [idx₂₆]         │
│         │                    │      │          │            │
│         ▼                    ▼      ▼          ▼            │
│  ┌────────────┐         ┌──────┐┌──────┐  ┌──────┐         │
│  │ Bottom MLP │         │Embed ││Embed │  │Embed │         │
│  │ 13→512→256 │         │Table₁││Table₂│  │Table₂₆│        │
│  │    →128    │         └──┬───┘└──┬───┘  └──┬───┘         │
│  └─────┬──────┘            │       │         │              │
│        │                   └───────┼─────────┘              │
│        │                           │                        │
│        └───────────┬───────────────┘                        │
│                    ▼                                        │
│           ┌────────────────┐                                │
│           │   Feature      │                                │
│           │  Interaction   │  (dot product or concat)       │
│           └───────┬────────┘                                │
│                   ▼                                         │
│            ┌────────────┐                                   │
│            │  Top MLP   │                                   │
│            │ 512→256→1  │                                   │
│            └─────┬──────┘                                   │
│                  ▼                                          │
│             Prediction                                      │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Memory Bottleneck:
├── Embedding tables: 100GB - 1TB total
├── Sparse access pattern (power-law distribution)
├── Communication overhead: >50% at scale (16+ nodes)
└── Memory bandwidth bound, not compute bound
```

### ARCHER - ReRAM-based PIM (Frontiers 2024)

Processing-in-Memory architecture for fully on-chip recommendation:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ARCHER Architecture                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Full Embedding        Decomposed Embedding                 │
│   W [N × D]             L [N × R] × R_mat [R × D]           │
│                                                              │
│   ┌───────────┐         ┌─────┐   ┌─────────┐              │
│   │           │         │     │   │         │              │
│   │  10M×128  │  ───▶   │10M×32│ × │  32×128 │              │
│   │           │         │     │   │         │              │
│   └───────────┘         └─────┘   └─────────┘              │
│   1.28 GB               0.32 GB + 0.004 GB = 0.324 GB      │
│                         4× compression                      │
│                                                              │
│   On-Chip ReRAM Crossbar Array:                             │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│   │  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┐    ┌───┬───┬───┬───┐│               │
│   │  │ L₀│ L₁│ L₂│...│    │ R₀│ R₁│ R₂│...││               │
│   │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┤    ├───┼───┼───┼───┤│               │
│   │  │ L₃│ L₄│ L₅│...│    │ R₃│ R₄│ R₅│...││               │
│   │  └───┴───┴───┴───┘    └───┴───┴───┴───┘│               │
│   │   Left Matrix          Right Matrix    │               │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                              │
│   Lookup: e[idx] = L[idx] × R (MVM on crossbar)             │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Benefits:
├── Fully on-chip execution (no off-chip access)
├── ReRAM MVM for decompression
├── High compute efficiency compensates decompression overhead
└── ~0.1 pJ/MAC energy
```

### Meta MTIA - Table Branch Embedding (Hot Chips 2024)

Meta's custom accelerator for recommendation inference:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Meta MTIA Architecture                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Specifications (Next-Gen):                                 │
│   ├── Process: TSMC 5nm                                     │
│   ├── TDP: 90W                                              │
│   ├── Memory: 128GB LPDDR5                                  │
│   └── Cores: 64 custom RISC-V                               │
│                                                              │
│   Table Branch Embedding (TBE):                             │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│   │                                         │               │
│   │   Hot Embeddings        Cold Embeddings │               │
│   │   (Frequently accessed) (Rarely accessed)│               │
│   │                                         │               │
│   │   ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────────┐│               │
│   │   │  Cache   │         │   Main       ││               │
│   │   │  (Fast)  │         │   Memory     ││               │
│   │   └────┬─────┘         └───────┬──────┘│               │
│   │        │                       │       │               │
│   │        └───────────┬───────────┘       │               │
│   │                    ▼                   │               │
│   │            ┌─────────────┐             │               │
│   │            │ TBE Merger  │             │               │
│   │            └─────────────┘             │               │
│   │                                         │               │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                              │
│   Performance:                                               │
│   ├── 2-3× runtime improvement                              │
│   └── Optimized for power-law access patterns               │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### RecMG - ML-Guided Tiered Memory (HPCA 2025)

Machine learning-guided embedding placement for tiered memory:

```
Tiered Memory Architecture:
├── Tier 1: HBM (fast, expensive, limited)
├── Tier 2: DDR5 (moderate speed, larger)
└── Tier 3: CXL/NVMe (slow, very large)

RecMG Approach:
├── ML model predicts access patterns
├── Dynamic caching of hot embeddings
├── Prefetching based on predicted access
└── Terabyte-scale embedding support
```

### NVIDIA EMBark (RecSys 2024)

3D flexible embedding sharding for distributed training:

```
EMBark Components:
├── Embedding clusters (grouped tables)
├── Flexible 3D sharding (table × row × column)
├── Communication compression
└── Sharding planner (automated optimization)

Results on DGX H100:
├── 1.5× average speedup
├── Up to 1.77× faster than baseline
└── Reduces communication overhead at scale
```

### Implementation Reference

```go
// DLRM embedding table
type EmbeddingTable struct {
    Weights     [][]float32  // [NumEmbeddings][EmbeddingDim]
    AccessCount []int64      // Frequency tracking
    // Power-law access pattern
}

// ARCHER ReRAM-based PIM
type ARCHER struct {
    Crossbars         []*ReRAMCrossbar
    DecomposedWeights *DecomposedEmbedding
    // W ≈ L × R decomposition
    // 4× compression ratio
}

// Meta MTIA with TBE
type MTIA struct {
    TBEUnits       []*TBEUnit
    EmbeddingCache map[int][]float32
    // 2-3× runtime improvement
}
```

### Performance Comparison

| System | Approach | Speedup | Key Innovation |
|--------|----------|---------|----------------|
| ARCHER | ReRAM PIM | High | Low-rank decomposition |
| Meta MTIA | Custom ASIC | 2-3× | Table Branch Embedding |
| RecMG | ML-guided | Variable | Tiered memory placement |
| EMBark | Distributed | 1.5-1.77× | 3D flexible sharding |

### References

- [ARCHER - Frontiers 2024](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11704-023-3397-x)
- [Meta MTIA - Hot Chips 2024](https://www.servethehome.com/meta-ai-acceleration-in-the-next-gen-meta-mtia-for-recommendation-inference-risc-v/)
- [RecMG - HPCA 2025](https://www.pasalabs.org/papers/2025/HPCA25_DLRM.pdf)
- [EMBark - RecSys 2024](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/boost-large-scale-recommendation-system-training-embedding-using-embark/)
- [Monolith - arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.07663)

---

## Section 337: Neuromorphic Vision Systems and Event Cameras

### Overview

Event cameras (Dynamic Vision Sensors, DVS) are bio-inspired sensors that asynchronously report per-pixel brightness changes, enabling low-latency, low-power vision with extreme temporal resolution. Combined with CIM-based spiking neural networks, they form complete neuromorphic vision systems.

### Event Camera Operating Principle

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Event Camera vs Frame Camera                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Frame-Based Camera:                                        │
│   ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐                  │
│   │Frame│ │Frame│ │Frame│ │Frame│ │Frame│   30-60 FPS      │
│   │  1  │ │  2  │ │  3  │ │  4  │ │  5  │                  │
│   └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘                  │
│   ──────────────────────────────────────▶ Time              │
│   All pixels sampled synchronously                          │
│                                                              │
│   Event Camera (DVS):                                        │
│   ●    ●●   ●  ●●●    ● ●   ●●●  ●   ●●   Events only      │
│   ──────────────────────────────────────▶ Time              │
│   Per-pixel asynchronous, 1µs resolution                    │
│                                                              │
│   Event Format: (x, y, t, p)                                │
│   ├── x, y: Pixel coordinates                               │
│   ├── t: Timestamp (µs resolution)                          │
│   └── p: Polarity (+1 ON, -1 OFF)                          │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Event Generation:
├── Pixel detects log(I) change > threshold
├── Generates ON event if brightness increases
├── Generates OFF event if brightness decreases
└── Silent otherwise (no data for static scenes)
```

### DVS Specifications

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Event Camera Specifications (2024-2025)            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Sensor        │Resolution│Temp Res│Dyn Range│Latency│Power│
│  ──────────────┼──────────┼────────┼─────────┼───────┼─────│
│  Prophesee Gen4│1280×720  │ 1 µs   │ 120 dB  │ 20 µs │100mW│
│  Samsung DVS   │ 640×480  │ 1 µs   │ 120 dB  │ 10 µs │ 50mW│
│  Sony EVS      │1280×960  │ 0.2 µs │ 124 dB  │ <1 µs │ 30mW│
│  DAVIS346      │ 346×260  │ 1 µs   │ 120 dB  │ 12 µs │ 10mW│
│                                                              │
│  Key Advantages:                                             │
│  ├── 1 µs temporal resolution (vs 33ms for 30fps)           │
│  ├── 120+ dB dynamic range (vs 60dB for standard camera)    │
│  ├── 100× lower data rate than frame cameras                │
│  ├── No motion blur                                         │
│  └── Sub-millisecond latency                                │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Neuromorphic Vision Pipeline

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Complete Neuromorphic Vision Pipeline             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Scene                                                      │
│     │                                                        │
│     ▼                                                        │
│   ┌─────────────┐                                           │
│   │ Event Camera│  Asynchronous events (x,y,t,p)            │
│   │    (DVS)    │                                           │
│   └──────┬──────┘                                           │
│          │                                                   │
│          ▼                                                   │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│   │         Event Preprocessing             │               │
│   │  ├── Noise filtering                    │               │
│   │  ├── Event surface/voxel grid           │               │
│   │  └── Temporal windowing                 │               │
│   └──────────────────┬──────────────────────┘               │
│                      │                                       │
│          ┌───────────┴───────────┐                          │
│          ▼                       ▼                          │
│   ┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐                  │
│   │   SNN on    │         │  EvGNN on   │                  │
│   │  CIM Array  │         │ Graph Accel │                  │
│   └──────┬──────┘         └──────┬──────┘                  │
│          │                       │                          │
│          └───────────┬───────────┘                          │
│                      ▼                                       │
│              ┌─────────────┐                                │
│              │   Output    │  Object detection, tracking,   │
│              │  (Spikes)   │  SLAM, gesture recognition     │
│              └─────────────┘                                │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### SPIDR - Digital CIM for Event-Based Perception (arXiv 2024)

Reconfigurable digital compute-in-memory SNN accelerator:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   SPIDR Architecture                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Event Input                                                │
│       │                                                      │
│       ▼                                                      │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│   │           Event Router                  │               │
│   │   (Maps events to PE by spatial hash)   │               │
│   └─────────────┬───────────────────────────┘               │
│                 │                                            │
│     ┌───────────┼───────────┬───────────┐                   │
│     ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼                   │
│   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐                │
│   │ PE₀ │   │ PE₁ │     │ PE₂ │     │PE₆₃│                │
│   │┌───┐│   │┌───┐│     │┌───┐│     │┌───┐│                │
│   ││CIM││   ││CIM││     ││CIM││     ││CIM││                │
│   ││MAC││   ││MAC││     ││MAC││     ││MAC││                │
│   │├───┤│   │├───┤│     │├───┤│     │├───┤│                │
│   ││LIF││   ││LIF││     ││LIF││     ││LIF││                │
│   │└───┘│   │└───┘│     │└───┘│     │└───┘│                │
│   └──┬──┘   └──┬──┘     └──┬──┘     └──┬──┘                │
│      │         │           │           │                    │
│      └─────────┴───────────┴───────────┘                    │
│                      │                                       │
│                      ▼                                       │
│              Output Spike Bus                                │
│                                                              │
│   Features:                                                  │
│   ├── Digital SRAM-based CIM                                │
│   ├── LIF neuron integration                                │
│   ├── Event-driven processing                               │
│   └── Reconfigurable topology                               │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Neuro-CIM - Mixed-Mode Neuromorphic Processor (VLSI 2022)

310.4 TOPS/W efficiency through activity-aware computing:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Neuro-CIM Architecture                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│   Key Innovation: Low WL/BL Activity                        │
│                                                              │
│   Traditional CIM:         Neuro-CIM:                       │
│   All rows active          Only active rows computed        │
│   ┌─────────────┐          ┌─────────────┐                  │
│   │■ ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ │          │■ □ □ ■ □ □ │                  │
│   │■ ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ │          │□ ■ □ □ □ ■ │                  │
│   │■ ■ ■ ■ ■ ■ │          │□ □ ■ □ ■ □ │                  │
│   └─────────────┘          └─────────────┘                  │
│   100% activity            ~10% activity                    │
│   High energy              10× lower energy                 │
│                                                              │
│   Digital-Analog Mixed-Mode:                                │
│   ├── Digital accumulation (precise)                        │
│   ├── Analog membrane potential (efficient)                 │
│   └── Mixed-mode neuron firing                              │
│                                                              │
│   Results:                                                   │
│   ├── 310.4 TOPS/W peak efficiency                          │
│   ├── Low activity = low power                              │
│   └── Ideal for sparse event-based input                    │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Event-Based Applications

```
Application Areas:
├── SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)
│   └── Low latency + HDR enables fast autonomous navigation
├── Object Detection and Tracking
│   └── Sub-ms response for high-speed objects
├── Gesture Recognition
│   └── Low power for always-on edge devices
├── Automotive
│   └── 120dB dynamic range handles tunnels/sunlight
└── Robotics
    └── Event-driven perception for reactive control

Recent Work:
├── eTraM: Event-based Traffic Monitoring (CVPR 2024 Highlight)
├── EvGNN: Graph-based event processing (IEEE 2024)
├── S4D on Loihi 2: State-space models neuromorphic (2024)
└── ECCV 2024 Workshop on Neuromorphic Vision
```

### Implementation Reference

```go
// Dynamic Vision Sensor
type DVS struct {
    PixelStates     [][]float64  // Log intensity per pixel
    ContrastThreshold float64    // ~15% change
    TemporalResUS    float64     // 1 µs
    DynamicRangeDB   float64     // 120 dB
}

// SPIDR digital CIM accelerator
type SPIDR struct {
    PEs          []*SPIDRPE
    EventQueue   chan Event
    // LIF neurons with CIM integration
}

// Neuro-CIM mixed-mode processor
type NeuroCIM struct {
    Arrays       []*CIMArray
    Neurons      []*LIFNeuron
    // 310.4 TOPS/W target
    // Low WL/BL activity optimization
}
```

### Performance Metrics

| System | Task | Metric | Value |
|--------|------|--------|-------|
| Prophesee Gen4 | Vision | Temporal res | 1 µs |
| DVS | General | Dynamic range | 120 dB |
| DVS | General | Data reduction | ~100× |
| Neuro-CIM | SNN | Efficiency | 310.4 TOPS/W |
| SPIDR | Event proc | Throughput | High |
| EvGNN | Graph | Latency | 10× reduction |

## Section 338: CIM Security and Privacy

### Overview

Compute-in-memory accelerators face unique security challenges due to their analog nature, persistent weight storage, and physical accessibility. This section covers threats specific to CIM and defense mechanisms.

### CIM Threat Landscape

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CIM Security Threat Model                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                CIM Accelerator                       │    │
│  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐            │    │
│  │  │ Weights │  │ Crossbar│  │  ADC/   │            │    │
│  │  │ (Model) │  │  Array  │  │  DAC    │            │    │
│  │  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘  └────┬────┘            │    │
│  └───────┼────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────┘    │
│          │            │            │                       │
│          ▼            ▼            ▼                       │
│  ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐           │
│  │Model Theft  │ │Side-Chan │ │ Adversarial  │           │
│  │(IP Piracy)  │ │ Attacks  │ │   Inputs     │           │
│  └─────────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────────┘           │
│                                                              │
│  Threat Categories:                                         │
│  ├── Model extraction via power/timing analysis            │
│  ├── Adversarial perturbations to cause misclassification  │
│  ├── Hardware trojans in memory devices                    │
│  ├── Weight manipulation through fault injection           │
│  └── Privacy leakage from inference patterns               │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Side-Channel Attacks on CIM (Nature Electronics 2024)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Side-Channel Attack Vectors                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Power Analysis:                                            │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│  │  Power                               │                  │
│  │   ▲                                  │                  │
│  │   │    ╱╲    ╱╲     Weight-dependent │                  │
│  │   │   ╱  ╲  ╱  ╲    leakage          │                  │
│  │   │  ╱    ╲╱    ╲                    │                  │
│  │   │ ╱            ╲                   │                  │
│  │   └──────────────────▶ Time          │                  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                              │
│  CIM-Specific Leakage:                                      │
│  ├── Analog current proportional to weight × input         │
│  ├── ADC sampling creates timing signatures                │
│  ├── Column current reveals weight distribution            │
│  └── Write operations expose programming patterns          │
│                                                              │
│  Attack: Correlation Power Analysis (CPA)                   │
│  ├── Collect N power traces during inference               │
│  ├── Hypothesize intermediate values                       │
│  ├── Correlate hypothesis with power consumption           │
│  └── Extract weights with >95% accuracy (research shows)   │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF) for CIM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PUF-Based CIM Authentication                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  PUF leverages manufacturing variations:                    │
│                                                              │
│  MRAM-PUF (Scientific Reports 2024):                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │  MTJ 1      MTJ 2      MTJ 3    ...    │               │
│  │  ┌───┐      ┌───┐      ┌───┐           │               │
│  │  │ ↑ │      │ ↓ │      │ ↑ │           │               │
│  │  └───┘      └───┘      └───┘           │               │
│  │  TMR:200%   TMR:198%   TMR:202%        │               │
│  │                                        │               │
│  │  Stochastic switching creates unique response           │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                              │
│  ReRAM-PUF:                                                 │
│  ├── Filament formation randomness                         │
│  ├── SET/RESET voltage variations                          │
│  └── Resistance distribution                               │
│                                                              │
│  FeFET-PUF:                                                 │
│  ├── Domain wall pinning randomness                        │
│  ├── Coercive field variations                             │
│  └── Polarization switching stochasticity                  │
│                                                              │
│  PUF Metrics:                                               │
│  ├── Uniqueness: ~50% inter-device Hamming distance        │
│  ├── Reliability: >95% reproducibility                     │
│  ├── Uniformity: ~50% 1s and 0s                           │
│  └── ML resistance: adversarial training improves security │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Adversarial Attack Defense

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Adversarial Defense for CIM                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  Adversarial Example:                                       │
│  ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐              │
│  │ Original│ + ε │Perturb  │ =   │Adversarial              │
│  │  Input  │     │  δ      │     │  Input  │              │
│  │  "7"    │     │(noise)  │     │  "2" ?  │              │
│  └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └─────────┘              │
│   Class: 7       ||δ||∞ < ε      Class: 2 (wrong!)        │
│                                                              │
│  CIM-Specific Defenses:                                     │
│  ├── Input quantization (DAC resolution limits precision)  │
│  ├── Weight noise injection (analog variability as defense)│
│  ├── Statistical detection (anomaly in input distribution) │
│  └── Gradient masking (nonlinear activation)               │
│                                                              │
│  Detection Methods:                                         │
│  ├── Z-score based: flag inputs > 2σ from mean            │
│  ├── Neural guard: separate network checks validity        │
│  └── Input transformation: consistency check               │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Side-Channel Defense Countermeasures

```
Defense Strategies:
├── Random delay insertion (timing attack resistance)
├── Power balancing (constant power consumption)
├── Noise injection (mask power signature)
├── Shuffled execution (randomize row access order)
├── Masking (split computation with random values)
└── Differential power analysis resistance circuits

CIM-Specific Countermeasures:
├── Dummy operations to balance power
├── Randomized crossbar activation patterns
├── ADC timing jitter
└── Encrypted weight storage
```

### Implementation Reference

```go
// Side-channel attack simulation
type SideChannelAttacker struct {
    Traces       []*PowerTrace
    // CPA extracts weights from power traces
}

// MRAM-based PUF
type PUF struct {
    SecretState  []float64  // Manufacturing variations
    // Resistant to ML modeling attacks
    // Scientific Reports 2024
}

// Adversarial detector
type AdversarialDetector struct {
    InputStatistics  *InputStats
    DetectionThreshold float64  // 2σ
    DefenseEnabled   bool
}

// Secure CIM accelerator
type SecureCIM struct {
    PUF                *PUF
    AdversarialDetector *AdversarialDetector
    SideChannelDefense *SideChannelDefense
}
```

### References

- [Nature Electronics 2024 - Safe, Secure CIM](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-024-01312-y)
- [Scientific Reports 2024 - MRAM PUF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-71730-7)
- [Preprints 2025 - Hardware Security Survey](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202501.1559)
- [Cybersecurity 2024 - DNN Modeling Attacks](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s42400-024-00308-7)

---

## Section 339: Ferroelectric Tunnel Junctions (FTJ) for Neuromorphic Computing

### Overview

Ferroelectric Tunnel Junctions (FTJ) based on HfO₂/ZrO₂ (HZO) are emerging as leading candidates for neuromorphic computing due to their CMOS compatibility, low energy, high endurance, and multi-level conductance states.

### FTJ Operating Principle

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│              Top Electrode (Pt)                              │
│              ════════════════                                │
│                    │                                         │
│              ┌─────┴─────┐                                  │
│              │           │                                  │
│              │    HZO    │◀── Ferroelectric (5nm)           │
│              │   (FE)    │    Hf₀.₅Zr₀.₅O₂                  │
│              │           │                                  │
│              ├───────────┤                                  │
│              │   TiO₂    │◀── Interlayer (2nm)              │
│              │   (IL)    │    Interface improvement         │
│              └─────┬─────┘                                  │
│                    │                                         │
│              ════════════════                                │
│              Bottom Electrode (TiN)                          │
│                                                              │
│  Tunnel Electroresistance (TER):                            │
│  ├── Polarization UP: High barrier → Low conductance        │
│  ├── Polarization DOWN: Low barrier → High conductance      │
│  └── TER ratio = G_ON / G_OFF = 580× (ACS 2025)            │
│                                                              │
│  Tunneling Current: I ∝ exp(-2κd)                           │
│  where κ depends on barrier height (polarization-dependent) │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

}

    Conductance        float64  // Tunneling current
    // I ∝ exp(-2κd), κ depends on P
}

// FTJ synapse with STDP

    FTJArray         *FTJ
    STDPEnabled      bool
    STDPWindow       float64  // 20 ms
    // Symmetric potentiation/depression
}

## Section 340: Cryogenic CIM Operation

### Overview

Cryogenic compute-in-memory explores operating memristive devices at extremely low temperatures (4K, 1.4K, even mK) for:
- Quantum-classical interfaces
- Superconducting computing integration
- Reduced thermal noise and improved precision
- Novel switching mechanisms (quantum tunneling)

### Key Research Findings

**Nature Materials 2024 - Magnetic Topological Memristors:**
- Magnetic topological memristors for cryogenic in-memory computing
- Exploits domain wall and skyrmion dynamics at low temperatures
- High-precision states enabled by reduced thermal fluctuations

**C2RAM - Cryogenic Capacitorless RAM:**
- Designed for quantum computing interfaces
- Compatible with superconducting logic (RSFQ, AQFP)
- Sub-10K operation with flux-based state storage

**HfO₂ RRAM at 1.4K:**
- Demonstrated 4 resistance levels at 1.4K (Physical Review Applied)
- Thermal activation suppressed → field-driven switching dominates
- Quantum tunneling through filament gap becomes significant
- Reduced cycle-to-cycle variation due to frozen phonons

**Superconducting Memristors (CA-SQUID):**
- Controllable-asymmetric SQUID configuration
- Flux quanta as analog weight storage
- Perfect retention (quantized flux states)
- Sub-fJ energy per operation

### Temperature-Dependent Physics

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               CRYOGENIC MEMRISTOR SWITCHING MECHANISMS              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  300K (Room Temperature)        4K (Liquid Helium)                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────────┐              │
│  │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  │       │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │              │
│  │  Thermal Activation │       │  Thermal Suppressed │              │
│  │  Dominant (Arrhenius)│       │  (Rate ≈ 0)         │              │
│  └─────────────────────┘       └─────────────────────┘              │
│                                                                     │
│  Arrhenius: k = f₀ × exp(-Eₐ/kT)                                    │
│                                                                     │
│  At T = 4K:                                                         │
│    kT = 0.34 meV (vs 26 meV at 300K)                                │
│    Thermal rate drops by exp(-Eₐ × 76) ≈ 10⁻³³ for Eₐ = 1 eV       │
│                                                                     │
│  Alternative Mechanisms at Cryo:                                    │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ 1. QUANTUM TUNNELING                                    │        │
│  │    T = exp(-2κd), κ = √(2m*φ)/ℏ                         │        │
│  │    Becomes dominant when thermal activation frozen       │        │
│  │                                                          │        │
│  │ 2. FIELD-DRIVEN SWITCHING                               │        │
│  │    Electric field lowers barrier: φ_eff = φ₀ - eEd      │        │
│  │    Higher fields needed at cryo                         │        │
│  │                                                          │        │
│  │ 3. CARRIER FREEZE-OUT                                   │        │
│  │    Resistance increases as carriers localize            │        │
│  │    Below ~50K for most semiconductors                   │        │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Superconducting Memristor Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               CA-SQUID SUPERCONDUCTING MEMRISTOR                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│                    Control Line (Write)                             │
│                          │                                          │
│                    ┌─────▼─────┐                                    │
│         ┌──────────┤  Φ_ctrl   ├──────────┐                         │
│         │          └───────────┘          │                         │
│         │                                 │                         │
│    ┌────┴────┐                       ┌────┴────┐                    │
│    │   JJ₁   │       L_loop          │   JJ₂   │                    │
│    │  Ic₁    │◄─────────────────────►│  Ic₂    │                    │
│    └────┬────┘                       └────┬────┘                    │
│         │                                 │                         │
│         └───────────────┬─────────────────┘                         │
│                         │                                           │
│                    ┌────▼────┐                                      │
│                    │ Output  │  Ic(Φ) = Ic₀|cos(πΦ/Φ₀)|             │
│                    │ (Read)  │                                      │
│                    └─────────┘                                      │
│                                                                     │
│  Flux States:     Weight Encoding:                                  │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │ Φ₀ = h/2e   │  │ State 0:  Φ = 0      → Ic = Ic₀ │               │
│  │ ≈ 2.07 mV·ps│  │ State 1:  Φ = Φ₀/4  → Ic = 0.7Ic₀│              │
│  │             │  │ State 2:  Φ = Φ₀/2  → Ic = 0    │               │
│  │ 16 states   │  │ State 3:  Φ = 3Φ₀/4 → Ic = 0.7Ic₀│              │
│  │ programmable│  │ ...                              │              │
│  └─────────────┘  └─────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                     │
│  Advantages:                                                        │
│  • Zero static power (superconducting)                              │
│  • Quantized states (perfect retention)                             │
│  • Sub-fJ switching energy                                          │
│  • Compatible with RSFQ/AQFP logic                                  │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Magnetic Topological Memristor

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               MAGNETIC TOPOLOGICAL MEMRISTOR                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Domain Wall Racetrack:                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ ↑↑↑↑↑│↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓│↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑│↓↓↓↓↓│↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑│           │        │
│  │      DW1         DW2           DW3    DW4                │        │
│  │ ◄──────────────────────────────────────────────────────► │        │
│  │              Domain wall position encodes weight          │        │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                     │
│  Skyrmion State Encoding:                                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │                                                          │        │
│  │    State 0      State 1       State 2       State 3      │        │
│  │    (no Sk)      (1 Sk)        (2 Sk)        (3 Sk)       │        │
│  │                                                          │        │
│  │     ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑⟲↑↑        ↑⟲↑⟲↑        ⟲↑⟲↑⟲       │        │
│  │     ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑↑↑↑        │        │
│  │     ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑↑↑↑        ↑↑↑↑↑        │        │
│  │                                                          │        │
│  │  R = R₀         R = R₀+ΔR    R = R₀+2ΔR   R = R₀+3ΔR    │        │
│  │  (Topological Hall effect adds resistance per skyrmion)  │        │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                     │
│  Materials (at Cryogenic Temps):                                    │
│  • Cr₂Te₃: Tc ≈ 180K, strong perpendicular anisotropy               │
│  • MnBi₂Te₄: Topological antiferromagnet, TN ≈ 25K                  │
│  • Fe₃GeTe₂: Tc ≈ 220K, tunable by gating                           │
│                                                                     │
│  Switching Mechanisms:                                              │
│  • Spin-orbit torque (SOT): τ = ℏ/(2e) × θ_SH × J × m×ŝ             │
│  • Spin-transfer torque (STT): Current-driven DW motion             │
│  • Electric field control: Anisotropy modulation via VCMA           │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in cryo_reram_cim.go

```go
// Cryogenic RRAM with temperature-dependent physics
type CryoRRAM struct {
    Config *CryoRRAMConfig
    Cells  [][]*CryoRRAMCell
}

// Temperature-dependent switching rate
func (c *CryoRRAM) ArrheniusRate(temperature float64) float64 {
    kB := 8.617e-5  // eV/K
    rate := c.Config.AttemptFrequency *
            math.Exp(-c.Config.ActivationEnergy/(kB*temperature))
    return rate
}

// Quantum tunneling at cryogenic temperatures
func (c *CryoRRAM) QuantumTunnelingProbability(cell *CryoRRAMCell) float64 {
    // WKB approximation: T = exp(-2κd)
    // κ = √(2m*φ)/ℏ
    m_e := 9.109e-31    // Electron mass
    hbar := 1.055e-34   // Reduced Planck constant
    phi := c.Config.TunnelBarrier * 1.602e-19  // Barrier in J
    d := cell.FilamentGap * 1e-9               // Gap in m

    kappa := math.Sqrt(2 * m_e * phi) / hbar
    return math.Exp(-2 * kappa * d)
}

// CA-SQUID superconducting memristor
type CASQUID struct {
    Config *CASQUIDConfig
    Cells  [][]*CASQUIDCell
}

// Flux-modulated critical current
// Ic(Φ) = Ic₀|cos(πΦ/Φ₀)|
func (s *CASQUID) ProgramFluxState(row, col int, state int) {
    cell := s.Cells[row][col]
    fluxNormalized := float64(state) / float64(s.Config.NumFluxStates)
    cell.CriticalCurrent = s.Config.Ic0 *
        math.Abs(math.Cos(math.Pi * fluxNormalized * s.Config.IcModulation))
}
```

### Performance at Different Temperatures

| Temperature | Technology | States | Switching | Key Feature |
|-------------|-----------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| 300K | HfO₂ RRAM | 4-8 | Thermal | Standard CIM |
| 77K | HfO₂ RRAM | 8-16 | Mixed | Reduced noise |
| 4K | HfO₂ RRAM | 4 | Field/Quantum | Quantum interface |
| 4K | Magnetic topo | 8+ | SOT | Domain/skyrmion |
| <10K | CA-SQUID | 16+ | Flux | Superconducting |
| mK | CA-SQUID | 64+ | Flux | Quantum coherent |

- [IEEE TED 2024 - C2RAM for Quantum Computing](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10477890)

## Section 341: ReRAM Variability and Yield

### Overview

ReRAM (Resistive RAM) variability is a critical challenge for CIM accuracy and yield. Understanding and mitigating variability enables reliable deployment of analog compute-in-memory systems.

### Types of Variability

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ReRAM VARIABILITY TAXONOMY                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │              DEVICE-TO-DEVICE (D2D) VARIATION              │     │
│  │                    (Fixed per device)                      │     │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ Source: Manufacturing process variations                   │     │
│  │   • Oxide thickness fluctuation (±1 nm)                    │     │
│  │   • Metal electrode roughness                              │     │
│  │   • Defect density variation                               │     │
│  │   • Doping non-uniformity                                  │     │
│  │                                                            │     │
│  │ Impact:                                                    │     │
│  │   • HRS distribution: σ/μ ≈ 15-30%                         │     │
│  │   • LRS distribution: σ/μ ≈ 10-20%                         │     │
│  │   • Vset distribution: σ ≈ 0.2-0.5V                        │     │
│  │   • Vreset distribution: σ ≈ 0.1-0.3V                      │     │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │             CYCLE-TO-CYCLE (C2C) VARIATION                 │     │
│  │                (Changes each write cycle)                  │     │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ Source: Stochastic filament formation/rupture              │     │
│  │   • Random oxygen vacancy migration                        │     │
│  │   • Filament path variations                               │     │
│  │   • Local temperature fluctuations                         │     │
│  │                                                            │     │
│  │ Impact:                                                    │     │
│  │   • HRS C2C: σ/μ ≈ 5-15%                                   │     │
│  │   • LRS C2C: σ/μ ≈ 3-10%                                   │     │
│  │   • Limits achievable precision to ~4-6 bits               │     │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │              TEMPORAL VARIATIONS                           │     │
│  │                 (Changes over time)                        │     │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │ RTN (Random Telegraph Noise):                              │     │
│  │   • Amplitude: 1-5% of resistance                          │     │
│  │   • Frequency: 0.1-100 Hz typical                          │     │
│  │   • Cause: Trap capture/emission                           │     │
│  │                                                            │     │
│  │ Read Disturb:                                              │     │
│  │   • Probability: 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ per read                     │     │
│  │   • Causes gradual resistance drift                        │     │
│  │                                                            │     │
│  │ Endurance Degradation:                                     │     │
│  │   • Onset: ~10⁶ cycles (varies by technology)              │     │
│  │   • HRS/LRS window closure over cycles                     │     │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Variation Impact on DNN Accuracy

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DNN ACCURACY vs. RERAM VARIABILITY                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Accuracy (%)                                                       │
│  100 ┤                                                              │
│      │●─────●─────●─────●                                           │
│   95 ┤                  ╲                                           │
│      │                   ●─────●                                    │
│   90 ┤                          ╲                                   │
│      │                           ●                                  │
│   85 ┤                            ╲                                 │
│      │                             ●                                │
│   80 ┤                              ╲                               │
│      │                               ●                              │
│   75 ┤                                ╲                             │
│      │                                 ●                            │
│   70 ┼──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬            │
│      0      5      10     15     20     25     30    35             │
│              σ/μ (Coefficient of Variation %)                       │
│                                                                     │
│  ○ Baseline (no compensation)                                       │
│  ● With CTSF compensation (+5-10% tolerance)                        │
│  ■ With Bayesian training (+10-15% tolerance)                       │
│                                                                     │
│  Network Sensitivity:                                               │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ Network Type     │ Var. Tolerance │ Compensation Gain │    │     │
│  ├──────────────────┼────────────────┼───────────────────┤    │     │
│  │ MLP (MNIST)      │ ~20% σ/μ       │ +8% accuracy      │    │     │
│  │ CNN (CIFAR-10)   │ ~15% σ/μ       │ +12% accuracy     │    │     │
│  │ ResNet (ImageNet)│ ~10% σ/μ       │ +15% accuracy     │    │     │
│  │ Transformer      │ ~8% σ/μ        │ +10% accuracy     │    │     │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Variation-Aware Training Techniques

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VARIATION-AWARE TRAINING FRAMEWORK                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Standard Training:                                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │   W_ideal ──►[Quantize]──►[Deploy]──► Poor accuracy      │       │
│  │                          (variations cause degradation)  │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
│  CTSF (Cycle-Time Scaling Factor):                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │   Forward:  W_train = α × W + noise(σ_D2D, σ_C2C)        │       │
│  │   Backward: ∇W = β × ∇W_original                          │       │
│  │                                                           │       │
│  │   α = scaling factor (typically 0.8-1.2)                  │       │
│  │   β = gradient scaling (typically 1.0-1.5)                │       │
│  │   noise = simulated hardware variation                    │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
│  VACTSF (Variation-Aware CTSF):                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │   Extends CTSF with:                                      │       │
│  │   • Per-device D2D noise injection                        │       │
│  │   • Statistical sampling during training                  │       │
│  │   • Robustness regularization term                        │       │
│  │                                                           │       │
│  │   Loss = L_task + λ × Var[L(W + ε)]                       │       │
│  │          ↑           ↑                                    │       │
│  │       task loss   robustness term                         │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
│  Bayesian Neural Networks:                                          │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │   W ~ N(μ_W, σ_W²)   ← Learn distribution, not point      │       │
│  │                                                           │       │
│  │   Training: Minimize ELBO                                 │       │
│  │   ELBO = E_q[log p(D|W)] - KL(q(W) || p(W))               │       │
│  │                                                           │       │
│  │   σ_W naturally absorbs hardware variation                │       │
│  │   Weights with high σ_W → robust to variation             │       │
│  │   Weights with low σ_W → need protection                  │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in cryo_reram_cim.go

```go
// Comprehensive variability model
type VariabilityConfig struct {
    // Device-to-device (fixed per device)
    D2D_HRS_Sigma    float64  // 15% typical
    D2D_LRS_Sigma    float64  // 10% typical
    D2D_Vset_Sigma   float64  // 0.3V typical
    D2D_Vreset_Sigma float64  // 0.2V typical

    // Cycle-to-cycle (per operation)
    C2C_HRS_Sigma    float64  // 5% typical
    C2C_LRS_Sigma    float64  // 3% typical

    // Temporal
    RTN_Amplitude    float64  // 2% typical
    RTN_Frequency    float64  // 10 Hz typical
    ReadDisturbProb  float64  // 10⁻⁶ typical
}

// Variable RRAM cell with full variation model
type VariableRRAMCell struct {
    NominalHRS, NominalLRS float64

    // D2D offsets (sampled once at init)
    D2D_HRS_Offset    float64
    D2D_LRS_Offset    float64
    D2D_Vset_Offset   float64
    D2D_Vreset_Offset float64

    CurrentResistance float64
    CycleCount        int
    RTN_State         bool
}

// SET with C2C variation
func (v *VariableRRAM) SET(row, col int) {
    cell := v.Cells[row][col]
    c2c := rand.NormFloat64() * v.Config.C2C_LRS_Sigma
    effectiveLRS := cell.NominalLRS * (1 + cell.D2D_LRS_Offset + c2c)
    cell.CurrentResistance = effectiveLRS
}

// Variation-aware training
type VACTSFConfig struct {
    Alpha           float64  // Weight scaling
    Beta            float64  // Gradient scaling
    NoiseScale      float64  // Training noise
    UseBayesian     bool     // Enable Bayesian
    TargetD2D_Sigma float64  // Target robustness
    TargetC2C_Sigma float64
}
```

### Yield Analysis

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    RERAM ARRAY YIELD MODEL                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Defect Types:                                                      │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │ • Stuck-at-HRS: Device cannot SET (p ≈ 0.1-1%)           │       │
│  │ • Stuck-at-LRS: Device cannot RESET (p ≈ 0.1-1%)         │       │
│  │ • Low ON/OFF: Ratio < threshold (p ≈ 1-5%)               │       │
│  │ • High variation: σ > threshold (p ≈ 2-10%)              │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
│  Array Yield vs. Size:                                              │
│                                                                     │
│  Yield (%)                                                          │
│  100 ┤●                                                             │
│      │ ●                                                            │
│   80 ┤  ●                                                           │
│      │   ●                                                          │
│   60 ┤    ●                                                         │
│      │     ●                                                        │
│   40 ┤      ●                                                       │
│      │       ●                                                      │
│   20 ┤        ●                                                     │
│      │         ●────●                                               │
│    0 ┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────        │
│      16   32   64  128  256  512  1K   Array Size                   │
│                                                                     │
│  Yield = (1 - p_defect)^(N × N)                                     │
│  For p = 0.1%, 256×256 array: Yield ≈ 52%                           │
│  For p = 0.01%, 256×256 array: Yield ≈ 94%                          │
│                                                                     │
│  Mitigation Strategies:                                             │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│  │ 1. Redundancy: Add spare rows/columns (5-10% overhead)   │       │
│  │ 2. Remapping: Route around defects at runtime            │       │
│  │ 3. Defect-aware training: Train with known defect map    │       │
│  │ 4. Soft errors: Use variation-tolerant algorithms        │       │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Technology Comparison

| Technology | D2D σ/μ | C2C σ/μ | Endurance | ON/OFF | Yield |
|------------|---------|---------|-----------|--------|-------|
| HfO₂ RRAM | 15-30% | 5-10% | 10⁶-10¹² | 10-100× | 95-99% |
| TaOx RRAM | 10-20% | 3-8% | 10⁹-10¹² | 100-1000× | 97-99% |
| FeFET | 8-15% | 2-5% | 10¹⁰-10¹² | 10⁴× | 98-99% |
| FTJ | 5-10% | 2-3% | 10⁸ | 580× | 95-98% |
| PCM | 5-15% | 3-8% | 10⁶-10⁹ | 100× | 95-98% |
| STT-MRAM | 3-8% | 1-3% | 10¹² | 2-5× | 99%+ |
