## 352. Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion with Compute-in-Memory

### Overview

Multi-modal sensor fusion combines data from diverse sensors (camera, LiDAR, radar, IMU, tactile) to achieve robust perception. CIM architectures offer significant advantages for sensor fusion through parallel processing of multiple modalities with low latency and energy consumption.

### Fusion Strategies for CIM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FUSION STRATEGY COMPARISON                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  EARLY FUSION:              MID FUSION:           LATE FUSION:      │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────┐                    ┌─────┐               ┌─────┐           │
│  │Cam  │─┐                  │Cam  │──►[CNN]──┐    │Cam  │──►[Model]─┐│
│  └─────┘ │                  └─────┘          │    └─────┘           ││
│          │ Concat           ┌─────┐          │    ┌─────┐           ││
│  ┌─────┐ │──►[Model]──►Out  │LiDAR│──►[PN]───┼──►Out  │LiDAR│──►[Model]─┼──►Vote
│  │LiDAR│─┤                  └─────┘    │     │    └─────┘           ││
│  └─────┘ │                  ┌─────┐    │Attn │    ┌─────┐           ││
│          │                  │Radar│──►[FFT]─┘    │Radar│──►[Model]─┘│
│  ┌─────┐ │                  └─────┘               └─────┘           │
│  │Radar│─┘                                                          │
│  └─────┘                                                            │
│                                                                     │
│  Pros:                      Pros:                 Pros:             │
│  • Simple                   • Selective fusion    • Modular         │
│  • Full info preserved      • Cross-modal attn    • Fault tolerant  │
│                                                                     │
│  Cons:                      Cons:                 Cons:             │
│  • Sensor alignment         • Complex             • Info loss       │
│  • Dimension explosion      • Training difficulty • Late integration│
│                                                                     │
│  CIM Suitability: ★★★       CIM Suitability: ★★★★★  CIM Suit: ★★★★  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Architecture for Multi-Modal Fusion

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

│  │  │ Camera │  │ LiDAR  │  │ Radar  │  │  IMU   │              │   │
│  │  │224×224 │  │64K pts │  │128×128 │  │ 6-DOF  │              │   │
│  │  │  ×3    │  │        │  │        │  │        │              │   │
│  │  └───┬────┘  └───┬────┘  └───┬────┘  └───┬────┘              │   │

│         ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼                       │

│                   └────────────────┘                                 │
```

### Neuromorphic Sensor Fusion (Loihi-2)

Research from 2024 demonstrated sensor fusion on Intel's Loihi-2:

```

│                  LOIHI-2 SENSOR FUSION RESULTS                      │

│                                                                     │

│  ┌──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┬─────────────────┐   │
│  │ Platform     │ Energy (mJ)  │ Latency (ms) │ Efficiency      │   │
│  ├──────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┤   │
│  │ CPU          │ 150          │ 45           │ 1× (baseline)   │   │
│  │ GPU          │ 50           │ 12           │ 3× energy       │   │
│  │ Loihi-2      │ 1.5          │ 8            │ 100× energy     │   │
│  │              │              │              │ 30× vs GPU      │   │
│  └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┴─────────────────┘   │
│                                                                     │

│  achieve 100× energy efficiency for sensor fusion tasks            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### In-Sensor Computing Integration

```

│                                                                     │

│                                                                     │

│  │                  │               │                  │            │
│  │                  │    Weight     │                  │            │
│  │                  └───────────────┘                  │            │
│  │                                                     │            │
│  │   In-pixel computation:                             │            │
│  │   • Feature extraction at sensor                   │            │
│  │   • 1000× data reduction before transmission       │            │
│  │   • <1 pJ/pixel processing                         │            │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                     │

│  • 5-bit in-memory sensing + computing                              │
│  • Broadband (UV to visible) imaging                               │
│  • Motion extraction + grayscale fusion                            │
│  • Reservoir computing for temporal processing                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in multimodal_gnn_cim.go

```go
// Multi-modal fusion configuration
type MultiModalFusionConfig struct {
    Sensors           []*SensorConfig  // Camera, LiDAR, Radar, IMU
    FusionStrategy    FusionStrategy   // Early, Mid, Late, Hybrid
    FusedFeatureDim   int              // Output dimension
    AttentionHeads    int              // Cross-modal attention
    TemporalWindow    int              // Frames for temporal fusion
    EnableQualityGate bool             // Dynamic quality weighting
}

// Quality-gated cross-modal attention
func (cma *CrossModalAttentionCIM) Forward(
    features map[ModalityType][]float64,
    qualities map[ModalityType]float64,
) []float64 {
    // Weight attention by sensor quality
    for modality, feat := range features {
        q := qualities[modality]
        // Scale contribution by quality score
        scaledFeat := scaleVector(feat, q)
        // ... attention computation on CIM
    }
}
```

- [Deep Multimodal Fusion - ACM Computing Surveys 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3649447)
- [Loihi-2 Sensor Fusion - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.16096)
- [Birdlike Neuromorphic Sensor - Nature Communications 2024](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52563-4)
- [Edge Intelligence In-Sensor - npj Unconventional Computing 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00040-6)
- [imec Sensor Fusion](https://www.imec-int.com/en/79GHz-140GHz-radar-systems/sensor-data-fusion)

## 353. Graph Neural Networks on CIM Accelerators

### Overview

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) process irregular graph-structured data for tasks like node classification, link prediction, and graph classification. ReRAM-based CIM accelerators offer massive speedups (228×) and energy reduction (305×) compared to GPUs due to efficient handling of sparse matrix operations.

### GNN Computation Pattern

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    GNN LAYER COMPUTATION                            │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  For each node v:                                                   │
│                                                                     │
│  1. AGGREGATE: Collect messages from neighbors                      │
│     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│     │                                                         │     │
│     │      N(v) = {u₁, u₂, u₃, u₄}  (neighbors of v)          │     │
│     │                                                         │     │
│     │           u₁ ──┐                                        │     │
│     │           u₂ ──┼──► AGG(h_u : u ∈ N(v)) ──► m_v         │     │
│     │           u₃ ──┤                                        │     │
│     │           u₄ ──┘                                        │     │
│     │                                                         │     │
│     │  AGG options: SUM, MEAN, MAX, ATTENTION                 │     │
│     └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
│  2. UPDATE: Combine with self and transform                         │
│     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│     │                                                         │     │
│     │   h_v^(l+1) = σ(W · COMBINE(h_v^(l), m_v))              │     │
│     │                                                         │     │
│     │   Where W is learned weight matrix (stored in CIM)      │     │
│     └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
│  Key Operations for CIM:                                            │
│  • Sparse-dense matrix multiply (SpMM) - Aggregation               │
│  • Dense matrix multiply (GEMM) - Transformation                   │
│  • Both naturally map to crossbar MVM                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### ReRAM-Based GNN Accelerator (ReGNN)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ReGNN ARCHITECTURE                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    ReGNN CHIP                                │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │    │
│  │   │              ReRAM CROSSBAR TILES                     │   │    │
│  │   │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐         │   │    │
│  │   │  │ W_agg  │ │ W_agg  │ │ W_upd  │ │ W_upd  │         │   │    │
│  │   │  │ Tile 0 │ │ Tile 1 │ │ Tile 0 │ │ Tile 1 │  ...    │   │    │
│  │   │  │128×128 │ │128×128 │ │128×128 │ │128×128 │         │   │    │
│  │   │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘         │   │    │
│  │   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │    │
│  │                          │                                   │    │
│  │   ┌──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┐   │    │
│  │   │              AGGREGATION ENGINES                     │   │    │
│  │   │                                                      │   │    │
│  │   │   High-degree nodes ──► Engine 0 (parallel MVM)      │   │    │
│  │   │   Mid-degree nodes  ──► Engine 1 (batched)           │   │    │
│  │   │   Low-degree nodes  ──► Engine 2 (sequential)        │   │    │
│  │   │                                                      │   │    │
│  │   │   Degree-aware scheduling for load balancing         │   │    │
│  │   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │   Performance vs GPU:                                        │    │
│  │   • Speedup: 228×                                            │    │
│  │   • Energy reduction: 305×                                   │    │
│  │   • Area efficiency: 8.4×                                    │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HePGA: Heterogeneous PIM GNN Accelerator

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              HePGA HETEROGENEOUS ARCHITECTURE                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  3D-Stacked Heterogeneous PIM:                                      │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │  Layer 3: SRAM Buffer (High-speed scratch)                 │     │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │  Layer 2: FeFET Arrays (Low-power aggregation)             │     │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │  Layer 1: ReRAM Arrays (High-precision transform)          │     │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤     │
│  │  Layer 0: PCM Storage (Weight archival)                    │     │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                     │
│  Device Selection Strategy:                                         │
│  ┌────────────────┬──────────┬───────────┬─────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Device         │ Use Case │ Precision │ Power               │    │
│  ├────────────────┼──────────┼───────────┼─────────────────────┤    │
│  │ ReRAM          │ High-deg │ 6-8 bit   │ Medium              │    │
│  │ FeFET          │ Low-deg  │ 4-6 bit   │ Low                 │    │
│  │ SRAM           │ Gradient │ 16+ bit   │ High                │    │
│  │ PCM            │ Storage  │ MLC       │ Very Low            │    │
│  └────────────────┴──────────┴───────────┴─────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Results:                                                           │
│  • 3.8× energy efficiency (TOPS/W) vs homogeneous                  │
│  • 6.8× compute efficiency (TOPS/mm²) vs homogeneous               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Data Pruning for GNN Training on ReRAM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DATA PRUNING FRAMEWORK FOR GNN TRAINING                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Challenge: Large graphs → excessive ReRAM writes → endurance limit │
│                                                                     │
│  Solution: Binary Graph Classifier (BGC) for early pruning          │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    PRUNING PIPELINE                          │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │   Input Graph ──► BGC ──► Importance ──► Pruned Graph        │    │
│  │       G              Score              G' (smaller)         │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │   BGC classifies subgraphs:                                  │    │
│  │   • "Important" (keep): Contains class-relevant structure    │    │
│  │   • "Redundant" (prune): Minimal impact on accuracy          │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │   Pruning criteria:                                          │    │
│  │   • Low-degree nodes with similar neighbors                  │    │
│  │   • Distant nodes from labeled nodes                         │    │
│  │   • Structurally redundant motifs                            │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Results:                                                           │
│  • 1.6× GNN training acceleration                                  │
│  • 100× reduction in training cost                                 │
│  • Improved ReRAM reliability (fewer writes)                       │
│  • Minimal accuracy loss (<1%)                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### GNN Layer Types on CIM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    GNN VARIANTS ON CIM                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  1. GCN (Graph Convolutional Network):                              │
│     h_v = σ(Σ (1/√(d_u·d_v)) · W · h_u)                             │
│     └── Symmetric normalization, direct MVM                         │
│                                                                     │
│  2. GAT (Graph Attention Network):                                  │
│     α_ij = softmax(LeakyReLU(a^T[Wh_i || Wh_j]))                    │
│     h_v = σ(Σ α_ij · W · h_j)                                       │
│     └── Attention weights computed on separate CIM array            │
│                                                                     │
│  3. GraphSAGE:                                                      │
│     h_v = σ(W · [h_v || AGG({h_u : u ∈ N(v)})])                     │
│     └── Sampling + aggregation, good for large graphs               │
│                                                                     │
│  4. GIN (Graph Isomorphism Network):                                │
│     h_v = MLP((1+ε)·h_v + Σ h_u)                                    │
│     └── Sum aggregation, MLP as CIM array                           │
│                                                                     │
│  CIM Mapping Efficiency:                                            │
│  ┌──────────┬────────────┬───────────┬──────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Layer    │ Operations │ Sparsity  │ CIM Efficiency           │   │
│  ├──────────┼────────────┼───────────┼──────────────────────────┤   │
│  │ GCN      │ SpMM+GEMM  │ Medium    │ ★★★★☆                    │   │
│  │ GAT      │ SpMM+Attn  │ Medium    │ ★★★★★ (attention-native) │   │
│  │ SAGE     │ Sample+MVM │ High      │ ★★★☆☆ (sampling overhead)│   │
│  │ GIN      │ Sum+MLP    │ Low       │ ★★★★☆                    │   │
│  └──────────┴────────────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in multimodal_gnn_cim.go

```go
// GNN configuration for CIM
type GNNCIMConfig struct {
    LayerType        GNNLayerType    // GCN, GAT, SAGE, GIN
    AggregationType  AggregationType // Sum, Mean, Max, Attention
    NumLayers        int
    HiddenDim        int
    NumHeads         int             // For GAT
    EnablePruning    bool            // Graph/model pruning
    PruningThreshold float64
}

// GCN layer with CIM MVM
func (gnn *GNNCIM) gcnLayer(layer int, h [][]float64, graph *Graph) [][]float64 {
    for node := 0; node < numNodes; node++ {
        // Normalized aggregation
        degree := graph.NodeDegrees[node]
        normFactor := 1.0 / math.Sqrt(float64(degree + 1))

        // Aggregate from neighbors
        aggregated := make([]float64, len(h[0]))
        for _, neighbor := range graph.AdjList[node] {
            neighborNorm := 1.0 / math.Sqrt(float64(graph.NodeDegrees[neighbor] + 1))
            edgeNorm := normFactor * neighborNorm
            // Accumulate with normalization
        }

        // Transform via CIM MVM
        output[node] = gnn.cimMVM(aggregated, gnn.TransformWeights[layer])
    }
}

// HePGA heterogeneous scheduling
func (h *HePGA) ScheduleGNNLayer(layer int, graph *Graph) {
    // High-degree nodes → ReRAM (precision)
    // Low-degree nodes → FeFET (power)
    for i := 0; i < graph.NumNodes; i++ {
        if graph.NodeDegrees[i] > threshold {
            h.scheduleOnReRAM(i, layer)
        } else {
            h.scheduleOnFeFET(i, layer)
        }
    }
}
```

## 354. Emerging Memory Technologies for CIM: PCM, MRAM, and FTJ

### Overview

Multiple non-volatile memory (NVM) technologies compete for compute-in-memory applications. Each offers distinct trade-offs in write energy, endurance, precision, and drift characteristics. Ferroelectric tunnel junctions (FTJs) are emerging as a particularly promising candidate due to their low write energy (10-500 fJ), high endurance, and multi-level capability.

### Technology Comparison

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              NVM TECHNOLOGY COMPARISON FOR CIM                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Technology │ Write   │ Speed  │ Endurance │ On/Off │ MLC   │    │
│  │            │ Energy  │  (ns)  │  (cycles) │ Ratio  │ Bits  │    │
│  ├────────────┼─────────┼────────┼───────────┼────────┼───────┤    │
│  │ ReRAM      │  100 fJ │   10   │   10⁶     │  100   │   4   │    │
│  │ PCM        │ 1000 fJ │   50   │   10⁹     │ 1000   │   4   │    │
│  │ STT-MRAM   │  500 fJ │    5   │   10¹⁵    │    3   │   1   │    │
│  │ FTJ (HZO)  │   50 fJ │   10   │   10¹⁰    │  100   │   6   │    │
│  │ FeFET      │   30 fJ │   20   │   10⁸     │ 10⁴    │   5   │    │
│  │ 2D-FTJ     │   20 fJ │    5   │   10¹²    │  10⁶   │   7   │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Key Advantages:                                                    │
│  • ReRAM: Mature, dense, good for inference                        │
│  • PCM: High endurance, large on/off, but drift issues             │
│  • MRAM: Unlimited endurance, fast, but low on/off (binary only)   │
│  • FTJ: Low energy, high MLC, non-filamentary (uniform)            │
│  • FeFET: Large memory window, CMOS compatible                     │
│  • 2D-FTJ: Extreme on/off ratio, lowest energy                     │
│                                                                     │
│  Key Challenges:                                                    │
│  • PCM: Resistance drift (5%/decade) - needs compensation          │
│  • MRAM: Small TMR limits precision to binary weights              │
│  • ReRAM: Filamentary switching causes variability                 │
│  • FTJ: Emerging technology, array yield still improving           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction (FTJ) Details

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FTJ DEVICE STRUCTURE                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│              Top Electrode (W, Pt)                                  │
│            ═══════════════════════                                  │
│                      │                                              │
│            ┌─────────┴─────────┐                                    │
│            │ Ferroelectric     │ ◄── HfₓZr₁₋ₓO₂ (HZO)              │
│            │ Tunnel Barrier    │     ~3-6 nm                        │
│            │      ↕↕↕↕↕↕      │ ◄── Polarization states           │
│            └─────────┬─────────┘                                    │
│                      │                                              │
│            ═══════════════════════                                  │
│              Bottom Electrode (TiN)                                 │
│                                                                     │
│  Operating Principle:                                               │
│  • Polarization direction modulates tunnel barrier height          │
│  • P↑: Low resistance (high tunnel probability)                    │
│  • P↓: High resistance (low tunnel probability)                    │
│  • Analog states via partial polarization switching                │
│                                                                     │
│  W/HfₓZr₁₋ₓO₂/TiN FTJ Performance (2024):                          │
│  • 60 programmable conductance states                              │
│  • Dynamic range: 10× (on/off)                                     │
│  • Current density: >3 A/m² at V_read = 0.3V                       │
│  • I-V nonlinearity: >1100 (for sneak current suppression)         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### FTJ Crossbar Array Demonstration

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    48×48 FTJ CROSSBAR ARRAY                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Array Specifications:                                              │
│  • Size: 48 × 48 = 2,304 cells                                     │
│  • Half-bias operation for cell selection                          │
│  • Uniform 3-bit (8-level) programming                             │
│  • Neighbor cell interference: None observed                       │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│  │  WL₁  ━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━ ...       │           │
│  │         │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │               │           │
│  │  WL₂  ━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━           │           │
│  │         │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │               │           │
│  │  WL₃  ━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━●━━━           │           │
│  │         │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │               │           │
│  │        BL₁ BL₂ BL₃ BL₄ BL₅ BL₆ BL₇ BL₈             │           │
│  │                                                      │           │
│  │  ● = FTJ cell (non-filamentary, uniform)            │           │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                     │
│  Half-Bias Selection:                                               │
│  • Selected cell: V_WL = V_full, V_BL = 0                          │
│  • Unselected same row: V_WL = V_full, V_BL = V/2                  │
│  • Unselected same col: V_WL = V/2, V_BL = 0                       │
│  • Unselected other: V_WL = V/2, V_BL = V/2                        │
│                                                                     │
│  Nonlinearity >1100 ensures:                                        │
│  I_sneak/I_selected < 0.1% → accurate MVM                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### PCM Drift Compensation

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    PCM DRIFT PHENOMENON                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Drift Model: R(t) = R₀ × (t/t₀)^ν                                 │
│  where ν ≈ 0.05-0.1 (drift coefficient)                            │
│                                                                     │
│  Resistance                                                         │
│  (log)                                                              │
│    │     State 7  ──────────────────────── High drift              │
│    │     State 6  ──────────────────────                           │
│    │     State 5  ──────────────────                               │
│    │     State 4  ───────────────                                  │
│    │     State 3  ────────────                                     │
│    │     State 2  ────────                                         │
│    │     State 1  ──────                                           │
│    │     State 0  ────── Low drift (crystalline)                   │
│    └────────────────────────────────────────────► Time (log)       │
│                                                                     │
│  Compensation Strategies:                                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ 1. Reference Cell Method                                    │    │
│  │    • Dedicated cells programmed to known states             │    │
│  │    • Track drift over time                                  │    │
│  │    • Apply correction factor to all reads                   │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │ 2. Periodic Refresh                                         │    │
│  │    • Reprogram cells before drift exceeds threshold         │    │
│  │    • Trade-off: endurance vs accuracy                       │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │ 3. Drift-Aware Training                                     │    │
│  │    • Train with simulated drift noise                       │    │
│  │    • Networks learn drift-tolerant representations          │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │ 4. Global Scaling                                           │    │
│  │    • Batch normalization absorbs drift into scale/shift    │    │
│  │    • Only retrain BN parameters periodically               │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in emerging_analog_cim.go

```go
// Memory technology specifications
type MemoryTechSpec struct {
    Technology      MemoryTechnology
    WriteEnergyFJ   float64  // FTJ: 50 fJ vs PCM: 1000 fJ
    SwitchingTimeNs float64  // MRAM: 5 ns fastest
    EnduranceCycles float64  // MRAM: 10¹⁵, FTJ: 10¹⁰
    OnOffRatio      float64  // 2D-FTJ: 10⁶ highest
    ConductLevels   int      // FTJ: 64 levels (6-bit)
    DriftRate       float64  // PCM: 5%/decade (worst)
}

// FTJ crossbar with half-bias selection
type FTJCrossbar struct {
    Config       *FTJCrossbarConfig
    Cells        [][]*MemoryCell
    Nonlinearity float64  // >1100 for sneak suppression
}

// PCM with drift compensation
type PCMCrossbar struct {
    Config         *PCMCrossbarConfig
    ReferenceCell  *MemoryCell  // For drift tracking
    DriftEstimate  float64
}

func (cb *PCMCrossbar) compensateDrift(cond float64) float64 {
    // Read reference cell and estimate drift
    refCond := cb.ReferenceCell.Read()
    expectedCond := 0.5 * math.Pow(1+decades, -driftExponent)
    driftFactor := refCond / expectedCond
    return cond / driftFactor
}
```

### References

- [FTJ Crossbar Annealing - Adv Intell Sys 2025](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aisy.202500817)
- [FTJ for CIM Accelerators - Adv Intell Sys 2024](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aisy.202300554)
- [FTJ as Game Changer - APL Machine Learning 2025](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/aml/article/3/2/020902/3344306)
- [FTJ Universal Memory - Frontiers in Materials 2023](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/materials/articles/10.3389/fmats.2023.1148979/full)
- [High-Speed FTJ - ACS Applied Materials 2022](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.2c04441)

---

## 355. Analog Gradient Descent Hardware for In-Memory Training

### Overview

Training neural networks on analog CIM hardware is fundamentally more challenging than inference due to the need for weight updates, gradient computation, and backpropagation. Recent breakthroughs (2024-2025) demonstrate practical hardware implementations with 40-140× energy efficiency improvements over GPUs.

### Challenge: Training vs Inference

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                INFERENCE vs TRAINING REQUIREMENTS                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  INFERENCE:                        TRAINING:                        │
│  ┌────────────────────┐            ┌────────────────────┐           │
│  │ Forward Pass Only  │            │ Forward + Backward │           │
│  │                    │            │ + Weight Update    │           │
│  │ y = Wx             │            │ ∂L/∂W = xᵀ × ∂L/∂y│           │
│  │                    │            │ W ← W - η∂L/∂W     │           │
│  └────────────────────┘            └────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                     │
│  Requirements:                     Requirements:                    │
│  • Read weights                    • Read weights                   │
│  • MVM operation                   • MVM forward                    │
│                                    • MVM backward (transpose)       │
│                                    • Outer product update           │
│                                    • Write weights (many times!)    │
│                                                                     │
│  Writes: 0                         Writes: O(epochs × batches × N²) │
│  Energy: ~100 fJ/MAC               Energy: ~10 pJ/MAC               │
│                                                                     │
│  Key Insight: Training requires 100-1000× more writes than         │
│  inference → Endurance becomes critical constraint                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Progressive Gradient Descent (Science Advances 2024)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PROGRESSIVE GRADIENT DESCENT                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Traditional Backprop:             Progressive SGD:                 │
│                                                                     │
│  Forward: L1 → L2 → L3 → Loss      Forward: L1 → L2 → L3 → Loss    │
│  Store all activations             Update L3 immediately            │
│  Backward: L3 ← L2 ← L1            Forward: L1 → L2 → L3 → Loss    │
│  Update all layers                 Update L2                        │
│                                    Forward: L1 → L2 → L3 → Loss    │
│                                    Update L1                        │
│                                                                     │
│  Memory: O(N layers × M acts)      Memory: O(1 layer)              │
│  Parallelism: Limited              Parallelism: Per-layer          │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │              PROGRESSIVE UPDATE CIRCUIT                      │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │  Input ──► [CIM Array] ──► Activation ──► [Loss]            │    │
│  │               │                  │            │               │    │
│  │               │                  └────────────┘               │    │
│  │               │                        │                      │    │
│  │               ▼                        ▼                      │    │
│  │         ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                   │    │
│  │         │   In-Situ Weight Update         │                   │    │
│  │         │   ΔW = -η × input × error       │                   │    │
│  │         │   Applied via parallel pulses   │                   │    │
│  │         └─────────────────────────────────┘                   │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Advantages:                                                        │
│  • No activation storage required                                  │
│  • Each layer updated independently                                │
│  • Naturally suited to analog hardware                             │
│  • Reduces memory bottleneck                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Parallel Outer Product Weight Update

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PARALLEL OUTER PRODUCT UPDATE                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Weight Update: ΔW = -η × a × δᵀ                                   │
│  (a = activation, δ = error, η = learning rate)                    │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                                                              │   │
│  │     a₁ ─────────────────────────────────────────────         │   │
│  │          │       │       │       │       │                   │   │
│  │     a₂ ─────────────────────────────────────────────         │   │
│  │          │       │       │       │       │                   │   │
│  │     a₃ ─────────────────────────────────────────────         │   │
│  │          │       │       │       │       │                   │   │
│  │         δ₁      δ₂      δ₃      δ₄      δ₅                   │   │
│  │                                                              │   │
│  │  Each intersection: ΔWᵢⱼ = -η × aᵢ × δⱼ                      │   │
│  │  All 25 updates happen in PARALLEL via voltage pulses        │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                     │
│  Pulse-Based Programming:                                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Row Voltage:   V_row = f(aᵢ) × V_pulse                      │    │
│  │ Column Voltage: V_col = f(δⱼ) × V_pulse                     │    │
│  │ Cell Update:   ΔG ∝ (V_row - V_col) × pulse_width           │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │ Stochastic rounding for sub-LSB gradients:                  │    │
│  │ If |ΔW| < LSB:                                               │    │
│  │   P(update) = |ΔW|/LSB                                       │    │
│  │   Update by ±LSB with probability P                         │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Energy: Single outer product = ~1 pJ (vs ~1 nJ digital)           │
│  Parallelism: N² updates simultaneous                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Fast and Robust Analog Training (Nature Communications 2024)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              FAST ANALOG IN-MEMORY TRAINING                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Two Learning Algorithms Proposed:                                  │
│                                                                     │
│  1. TIKI-TAKA (Asymmetric Update):                                 │
│     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│     │ Forward:  A = Array, use as-is                           │    │
│     │ Backward: A' = transpose(Array) via separate access      │    │
│     │ Update:   ΔA using SET/RESET pulses                      │    │
│     │                                                          │    │
│     │ Handles asymmetric conductance updates:                  │    │
│     │ • SET (increase G) faster than RESET                     │    │
│     │ • Different pulse requirements                           │    │
│     │ • Learns to compensate for asymmetry                     │    │
│     └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  2. Chopped-TTv2 (Noise Injection):                                │
│     ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│     │ Inject controlled noise during training:                 │    │
│     │ • Breaks symmetry for gradient estimation               │    │
│     │ • Regularization effect improves generalization         │    │
│     │ • Hardware noise becomes feature, not bug               │    │
│     └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Results:                                                           │
│  • 40-140× energy efficiency vs GPUs                               │
│  • <1% accuracy loss vs digital baseline                           │
│  • Works with limited conductance states (8-16 levels)             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Multi-Tile Residual Learning

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTI-TILE RESIDUAL LEARNING                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Problem: Limited conductance states (e.g., 4-bit) reduce precision│
│  Solution: Multiple tiles storing residual values                   │
│                                                                     │
│  Weight Decomposition:                                              │
│  W = W_main + α×W_res1 + α²×W_res2 + ...                           │
│  (α = scaling factor, e.g., 0.1)                                   │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  MAIN TILE              RESIDUAL TILE 1    RESIDUAL TILE 2  │    │
│  │  ┌───────────┐          ┌───────────┐      ┌───────────┐    │    │
│  │  │           │          │           │      │           │    │    │
│  │  │   W_main  │    +     │ W_res1×α  │  +   │ W_res2×α² │    │    │
│  │  │  (4-bit)  │          │  (4-bit)  │      │  (4-bit)  │    │    │
│  │  │           │          │           │      │           │    │    │
│  │  └───────────┘          └───────────┘      └───────────┘    │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │  Effective precision: 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 bits equivalent        │    │
│  │  Storage overhead: 3×                                        │    │
│  │  Energy overhead: ~3× (parallel reads)                       │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Programming Algorithm:                                             │
│  1. Quantize W to main tile: W_main = Q(W)                         │
│  2. Compute residual: R1 = (W - W_main) / α                        │
│  3. Quantize residual: W_res1 = Q(R1)                              │
│  4. Repeat for additional tiles                                    │
│                                                                     │
│  This approach enables training on devices with severe limitations │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in emerging_analog_cim.go

```go
// Progressive SGD for hardware backprop
type ProgressiveSGD struct {
    Config      *ProgressiveSGDConfig
    Layers      []*AnalogLayer
    Activations [][]float64  // Only current layer needed
}

// Process each layer immediately after forward
func (psgd *ProgressiveSGD) ProgressiveBackward(target []float64) {
    for l := numLayers - 1; l >= 0; l-- {
        // Update weights using outer product
        layer.SGD.OuterProductUpdate(
            psgd.Activations[l],
            psgd.Errors[l],
        )
        // Compute error for previous layer
        psgd.Errors[l-1] = psgd.backpropError(l, psgd.Errors[l])
    }
}

// Parallel outer product update
func (sgd *AnalogSGD) OuterProductUpdate(activation, error []float64) {
    for i := range activation {
        for j := range error {
            grad := -sgd.Config.LearningRate * activation[i] * error[j]
            pulse := sgd.quantizeGradient(grad)
            sgd.WeightArray.Cells[i][j].Write(currentCond + pulse)
        }
    }
    // Note: In hardware, all updates happen in parallel!
}

// Multi-tile residual for high precision
type MultiTileArray struct {
    MainTile     *FTJCrossbar
    ResidualTile []*FTJCrossbar  // Additional precision tiles
}

func (mta *MultiTileArray) MVM(input []float64) []float64 {
    output := mta.MainTile.MVM(input)
    for t, tile := range mta.ResidualTile {
        scale := math.Pow(0.1, float64(t+1))
        residualOut := tile.MVM(input)
        for j := range output {
            output[j] += residualOut[j] * scale
        }
    }
    return output
}
```

### References

- [Progressive Gradient Descent - Science Advances 2024](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.ado8999)
- [Fast Analog Training - Nature Communications 2024](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-51221-z)
- [Pipeline Analog SGD Convergence - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.15155)
- [Multi-tile Residual Learning - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.02516v1)
- [All-in-One Analog AI - Adv Functional Materials 2025](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adfm.202504688)

## 356. Federated Learning on CIM Edge Devices

### Overview

By 2025, over 90% of data is generated outside traditional data centers, with edge devices becoming dominant data producers. Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, addressing privacy concerns and communication bottlenecks. CIM edge devices offer unique advantages for FL through energy-efficient local computation and PUF-based security.

### Federated Learning Architecture for CIM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              FEDERATED CIM ARCHITECTURE                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│                    ┌─────────────────────┐                          │
│                    │   CENTRAL SERVER    │                          │
│                    │  (Aggregation Only) │                          │
│                    │                     │                          │
│                    │  Global Model: W    │                          │
│                    └──────────┬──────────┘                          │
│                               │                                     │
│           ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐                 │
│           │                   │                   │                 │
│           ▼                   ▼                   ▼                 │
│  ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐        │
│  │  CIM Edge #1    │ │  CIM Edge #2    │ │  CIM Edge #N    │        │
│  │                 │ │                 │ │                 │        │
│  │ ┌─────────────┐ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ │        │
│  │ │ Local Data  │ │ │ │ Local Data  │ │ │ │ Local Data  │ │        │
│  │ │ (Private)   │ │ │ │ (Private)   │ │ │ │ (Private)   │ │        │
│  │ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ └──────┬──────┘ │        │
│  │        │        │ │        │        │ │        │        │        │
│  │        ▼        │ │        ▼        │ │        ▼        │        │
│  │ ┌─────────────┐ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ │        │
│  │ │ CIM Array   │ │ │ │ CIM Array   │ │ │ │ CIM Array   │ │        │
│  │ │ Local Train │ │ │ │ Local Train │ │ │ │ Local Train │ │        │
│  │ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ └──────┬──────┘ │ │ └──────┬──────┘ │        │
│  │        │        │ │        │        │ │        │        │        │
│  │        ▼        │ │        ▼        │ │        ▼        │        │
│  │   Δw₁ (update)  │ │   Δw₂ (update)  │ │   Δwₙ (update)  │        │
│  └────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘        │
│           │                   │                   │                 │
│           └───────────────────┴───────────────────┘                 │
│                               │                                     │
│                    ┌──────────▼──────────┐                          │
│                    │   FedAvg:           │                          │
│                    │   W = Σ(nᵢ/n)×Δwᵢ   │                          │
│                    └─────────────────────┘                          │
│                                                                     │
│  Data never leaves device → Privacy preserved                       │
│  Only gradients/updates transmitted → Bandwidth efficient           │
│  CIM enables energy-efficient local training                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Privacy Mechanisms for CIM-FL

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    PRIVACY PROTECTION LAYERS                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  1. DIFFERENTIAL PRIVACY (DP)                                       │
│     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  Client gradient: g                                         │ │
│     │                    │                                        │ │
│     │                    ▼                                        │ │
│     │            ┌─────────────┐                                  │ │
│     │            │ Clip: ||g|| ≤ C                                │ │
│     │            └──────┬──────┘                                  │ │
│     │                   │                                         │ │
│     │                   ▼                                         │ │
│     │            ┌─────────────┐                                  │ │
│     │            │ Add noise:  │                                  │ │
│     │            │ g' = g + N(0, σ²C²)                            │ │
│     │            └──────┬──────┘                                  │ │
│     │                   │                                         │ │
│     │                   ▼                                         │ │
│     │            Send g' to server                                │ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  Privacy guarantee: (ε, δ)-DP per round                    │ │
│     │  Typical: ε=1-10, δ=10⁻⁵                                    │ │
│     └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                     │
│  2. PUF-BASED PROTECTION (CIM-specific)                             │
│     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  CIM Array inherent variation → Physical Unclonable Function│ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  Applications:                                              │ │
│     │  • Device authentication                                    │ │
│     │  • Model binding (model only works on specific device)      │ │
│     │  • Input/weight/structure protection                        │ │
│     │  • Secure key generation for encrypted gradients            │ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  PUF-CIM achieves zero bit-error-rate with SRAM CIM        │ │
│     └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                     │
│  3. GRADIENT COMPRESSION                                            │
│     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  Top-K Sparsification: Send only top 1-10% of gradients    │ │
│     │  Random Sparsification: Randomly sample gradients           │ │
│     │  Quantization: Reduce gradient precision (1-8 bits)        │ │
│     │                                                             │ │
│     │  Benefits:                                                  │ │
│     │  • Reduces communication by 10-100×                         │ │
│     │  • Implicit privacy (less information leaked)               │ │
│     │  • Error feedback preserves convergence                     │ │
│     └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Advantages for Federated Learning

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CIM BENEFITS FOR EDGE FEDERATED LEARNING               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Energy Efficiency:                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Operation     │ GPU (pJ)  │ CIM (pJ)  │ Reduction          │    │
│  ├───────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────────────┤    │
│  │ MAC           │ 100       │ 0.1       │ 1000×              │    │
│  │ Data movement │ 500       │ 0         │ ∞ (in-place)       │    │
│  │ Local epoch   │ 100 mJ    │ 0.1 mJ    │ 1000×              │    │
│  └───────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Privacy Benefits:                                                  │
│  • Data stays on device (inherent to FL)                           │
│  • CIM noise provides natural masking                              │
│  • PUF enables device-specific model binding                       │
│  • Low power enables always-on local training                      │
│                                                                     │
│  Scalability:                                                       │
│  • Monolithic 3D integration (sensor + CIM + logic)                │
│  • Supports 100s of edge clients                                   │
│  • 10-100× bandwidth reduction with compression                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in federated_recsys_cim.go

```go
// Federated learning configuration for CIM
type FederatedConfig struct {
    NumClients          int
    LocalEpochs         int
    AggregationMethod   AggregationMethod  // FedAvg, FedProx, etc.
    PrivacyMechanism    PrivacyMechanism   // DP, SMPC, PUF
    DifferentialPrivacy *DPConfig          // ε-DP parameters
    GradientCompression *CompressionConfig // Top-K, quantize
}

// CIM client with local training
type FederatedCIMClient struct {
    ID           int
    LocalModel   *CIMModel
    LocalData    *LocalDataset  // Private, never shared
    CIMArray     *EdgeCIMArray
    HasPUF       bool           // For device authentication
}

// Differential privacy for gradients
func (s *FederatedCIMServer) applyDifferentialPrivacy(update *ClientUpdate) {
    // Clip gradients
    norm := computeGradientNorm(update.Gradients)
    if norm > dp.ClipNorm {
        scale := dp.ClipNorm / norm
        // Scale all gradients
    }
    // Add Gaussian noise
    sigma := dp.ClipNorm * dp.NoiseMult
    for l := range update.Gradients {
        for i, j := range update.Gradients[l] {
            update.Gradients[l][i][j] += rand.NormFloat64() * sigma
        }
    }
}
```

### References

- [Federated Edge AI Survey - Preprints 2025](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202512.0118)
- [PUF-CIM Protection - Nature Communications 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-56412-w)
- [Edge Intelligence In-Sensor - npj Unconventional Computing 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00040-6)
- [Federated Edge Computing - IJCTT 2025](https://www.ijcttjournal.org/2025/Volume-73%20Issue-1/IJCTT-V73I1P110.pdf)

## 357. CIM for Recommendation Systems

### Overview

Deep learning-based recommendation systems consume major resources in modern AI data centers, with embedding layers identified as the primary bottleneck due to large memory capacity requirements and high bandwidth demands. CIM and near-bank processing-in-memory (RecPIM) architectures address this through in-place computation on embedding tables.

### Recommendation System Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DLRM-STYLE RECOMMENDATION SYSTEM                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                      INPUT FEATURES                          │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │  Dense Features        Sparse/Categorical Features          │    │
│  │  (13 numerical)        (26 embedding tables)                │    │
│  │       │                      │                               │    │
│  │       ▼                      ▼                               │    │
│  │  ┌─────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────┐           │    │
│  │  │ Bottom  │          │   EMBEDDING LOOKUPS     │ ◄─ BOTTLENECK
│  │  │  MLP    │          │   User: 1M entries      │           │    │
│  │  │         │          │   Item: 100K entries    │           │    │
│  │  │ 512→256 │          │   ...26 tables total    │           │    │
│  │  │ →128→64 │          │   64-128 dim each       │           │    │
│  │  └────┬────┘          └──────────┬──────────────┘           │    │
│  │       │                          │                           │    │
│  │       │    ┌─────────────────────┘                           │    │
│  │       │    │                                                 │    │
│  │       ▼    ▼                                                 │    │
│  │  ┌────────────────────┐                                      │    │
│  │  │  FEATURE CROSSING  │  ◄── Pairwise interactions          │    │
│  │  │  (dot products)    │                                      │    │
│  │  └─────────┬──────────┘                                      │    │
│  │            │                                                 │    │
│  │            ▼                                                 │    │
│  │  ┌────────────────────┐                                      │    │
│  │  │     TOP MLP        │                                      │    │
│  │  │   512→256→1        │                                      │    │
│  │  └─────────┬──────────┘                                      │    │
│  │            │                                                 │    │
│  │            ▼                                                 │    │
│  │      CTR Prediction                                          │    │
│  │       (0-1 score)                                            │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Memory Bottleneck:                                                 │
│  • Embedding tables: 10-100 GB total                               │
│  • Random access pattern (sparse lookups)                          │
│  • High bandwidth demand: 100+ GB/s                                │
│  • Low compute-to-memory ratio                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### RecPIM: Near-Bank Processing-in-Memory

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              RecPIM NEAR-BANK ARCHITECTURE                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Traditional Memory:           RecPIM Near-Bank:                    │
│                                                                     │
│  CPU ◄──────► DRAM             CPU ◄──────► Banks+PIM              │
│       Long data path                  Compute at memory             │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    DRAM MODULE                               │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐            │    │
│  │  │  Bank 0    │  │  Bank 1    │  │  Bank N    │            │    │
│  │  │ ┌────────┐ │  │ ┌────────┐ │  │ ┌────────┐ │            │    │
│  │  │ │Embedding│ │  │ │Embedding│ │  │ │Embedding│ │            │    │
│  │  │ │ Table   │ │  │ │ Table   │ │  │ │ Table   │ │            │    │
│  │  │ │Partition│ │  │ │Partition│ │  │ │Partition│ │            │    │
│  │  │ └────────┘ │  │ └────────┘ │  │ └────────┘ │            │    │
│  │  │     │      │  │     │      │  │     │      │            │    │
│  │  │     ▼      │  │     ▼      │  │     ▼      │            │    │
│  │  │ ┌────────┐ │  │ ┌────────┐ │  │ ┌────────┐ │            │    │
│  │  │ │PIM Unit│ │  │ │PIM Unit│ │  │ │PIM Unit│ │            │    │
│  │  │ │ • ALU  │ │  │ │ • ALU  │ │  │ │ • ALU  │ │            │    │
│  │  │ │ • Acc  │ │  │ │ • Acc  │ │  │ │ • Acc  │ │            │    │
│  │  │ └───┬────┘ │  │ └───┬────┘ │  │ └───┬────┘ │            │    │
│  │  └─────┼──────┘  └─────┼──────┘  └─────┼──────┘            │    │
│  │        │               │               │                    │    │
│  │        └───────────────┴───────────────┘                    │    │
│  │                        │                                    │    │
│  │                   Reduced Output                            │    │
│  │                   (aggregated embeddings)                   │    │
│  └────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                           │                                         │
│                           ▼                                         │
│                     To CPU/MLP                                      │
│                                                                     │
│  Benefits:                                                          │
│  • Compute happens where data lives                                │
│  • Parallel lookups across banks                                   │
│  • Reduced data movement (only aggregated results sent)            │
│  • Bandwidth savings: 10-100×                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM for Embedding Operations

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CIM EMBEDDING ACCELERATION                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Embedding Lookup as MVM:                                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  One-hot input: x = [0, 0, 1, 0, 0, ...]  (sparse)         │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  Embedding table (stored in CIM):                          │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐               │    │
│  │  │  e₀ = [0.1, 0.2, 0.3, ...]              │               │    │
│  │  │  e₁ = [0.4, 0.5, 0.6, ...]              │               │    │
│  │  │  e₂ = [0.7, 0.8, 0.9, ...] ◄── Selected │               │    │
│  │  │  e₃ = [1.0, 1.1, 1.2, ...]              │               │    │
│  │  │  ...                                     │               │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘               │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  Output: y = E × x = e₂ (selected embedding)               │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  CIM enables single-cycle lookup!                          │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Feature Interaction on CIM:                                        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  Pairwise dot products: eᵢ · eⱼ for all pairs              │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  Naïve: O(n² × d) operations                               │    │
│  │  CIM:   Parallel MVM computes all interactions             │    │
│  │                                                             │    │
│  │  Store embeddings as rows, input as one-hot                │    │
│  │  Output column = all dot products with selected embedding  │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Performance Comparison:                                            │
│  ┌─────────────────┬──────────┬──────────┬─────────────────────┐    │
│  │ Metric          │ GPU      │ CIM      │ Improvement         │    │
│  ├─────────────────┼──────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────┤    │
│  │ Throughput      │ 10K QPS  │ 100K QPS │ 10×                 │    │
│  │ Latency         │ 10 ms    │ 1 ms     │ 10×                 │    │
│  │ Energy/query    │ 100 mJ   │ 1 mJ     │ 100×                │    │
│  │ Memory BW used  │ 100 GB/s │ 10 GB/s  │ 10× reduction       │    │
│  └─────────────────┴──────────┴──────────┴─────────────────────┘    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Hot-Cold Embedding Optimization

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              HOT-COLD EMBEDDING TIERING                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│  Observation: Embedding access follows power-law distribution       │
│  • Top 1% of entries: 50%+ of accesses (HOT)                       │
│  • Bottom 90%: <10% of accesses (COLD)                              │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │              TIERED EMBEDDING STORAGE                        │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │    HOT TIER (CIM/SRAM)         COLD TIER (DRAM/NVM)         │    │
│  │    ┌───────────────────┐       ┌───────────────────┐        │    │
│  │    │ • Top 1% entries  │       │ • Remaining 99%   │        │    │
│  │    │ • Fast CIM lookup │       │ • Standard DRAM   │        │    │
│  │    │ • In-place compute│       │ • Prefetch on     │        │    │
│  │    │ • <1ns latency    │       │   miss            │        │    │
│  │    └───────────────────┘       └───────────────────┘        │    │
│  │                                                              │    │
│  │    Access: Check hot tier first → Fallback to cold          │    │
│  │    Update: Track access frequency, promote/demote entries   │    │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                     │
│  Benefits:                                                          │
│  • 50%+ accesses served from fast CIM                              │
│  • Cold tier uses high-density NVM (ReRAM, PCM)                    │
│  • Dynamic adaptation to access patterns                           │
│  • Overall: 5-10× latency reduction                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation in federated_recsys_cim.go

```go
// Recommendation system configuration for CIM
type RecSysCIMConfig struct {
    NumUsers          int
    NumItems          int
    EmbeddingDim      int
    NumEmbeddingTables int  // Typical: 26 for DLRM
    MLPLayers         []int
    NearBankPIM       bool  // RecPIM-style architecture
    FeatureCrossing   bool  // Enable dot product interactions
}

// Embedding table with CIM storage
type EmbeddingTable struct {
    Name       string
    NumEntries int
    Dimension  int
    Weights    [][]float64
    CIMTiles   []*CIMTile  // Near-bank PIM tiles
    HotEntries map[int]bool // Frequently accessed
}

// RecPIM near-bank architecture
type RecPIM struct {
    Config  *RecPIMConfig
    Banks   []*MemoryBank  // Each bank has PIM units
}

func (r *RecPIM) BatchLookup(requests []EmbeddingRequest) [][]float64 {
    // Group requests by bank for parallel processing
    bankRequests := groupByBank(requests)

    // Process in parallel across banks
    for bankID, reqs := range bankRequests {
        go func(bid int, requests []int) {
            bank := r.Banks[bid]
            for _, req := range requests {
                // Lookup happens at memory, no data movement
                embedding := bank.LocalLookup(req)
                // PIM unit can aggregate/reduce locally
            }
        }(bankID, reqs)
    }
}
```

### References

- [RecPIM Near-Bank - IEEE TCAD 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1109/TCAD.2024.3386117)
- [Memory Is All You Need - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.08413v1)
- [RRAM CIM Nature - Nature 2022](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-022-04992-8)
