## Section 358: Neuromorphic Vision Sensors with CIM

### Overview

Event-based cameras (Dynamic Vision Sensors, DVS) produce asynchronous, sparse event streams with microsecond latency and extreme dynamic range (120-140 dB). Combining DVS with compute-in-memory enables ultra-low-power vision processing at the sensor edge.

### Event Camera Fundamentals

**Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) Operation:**
```
Traditional Camera:              DVS:
┌─────────────────┐              ┌─────────────────┐
│  Frame 1        │              │ Events (async)  │
│  ████████████   │              │    ⚡ ⚡         │
│  ████████████   │              │  ⚡    ⚡ ⚡     │
│  ████████████   │              │    ⚡      ⚡   │
├─────────────────┤              │ ⚡  ⚡ ⚡       │
│  Frame 2        │              │      ⚡ ⚡  ⚡   │
│  ████████████   │              └─────────────────┘
│  ████████████   │
└─────────────────┘
   30-60 fps                        μs latency
   Fixed exposure                   140 dB DR
   Motion blur                      No blur
```

**Event Representation:**
- (x, y): pixel coordinates
- t: timestamp (microsecond resolution)
- p: polarity (ON: +1 brightness increase, OFF: -1 decrease)

### Key DVS Specifications

| Parameter | DAVIS346 | Prophesee EVK4 | Samsung DVS |
|-----------|----------|----------------|-------------|
| Resolution | 346×260 | 1280×720 | 640×480 |
| Latency | 3 μs | <5 μs | <10 μs |
| Dynamic range | 130 dB | 120 dB | 120 dB |
| Power | 5-14 mW | 30 mW | 20 mW |
| Bandwidth | 12 Meps | 60 Meps | 30 Meps |
| Contrast threshold | 15% | 15% | 20% |

### In-Sensor Computing Architecture

**Pixel-Level Processing:**
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                DVS Pixel Array                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐                │
│  │ PE  │ │ PE  │ │ PE  │ │ PE  │  ... 346×260   │
│  │+CIM │ │+CIM │ │+CIM │ │+CIM │                │
│  └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘ └──┬──┘                │
│     │       │       │       │                    │
│  ┌──┴───────┴───────┴───────┴──┐                │
│  │      Local SRAM Buffer       │                │
│  └──────────────┬───────────────┘                │
│                 │                                │
│  ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐                │
│  │  Small CIM Crossbar (64×64)  │  ← First conv │
│  └──────────────┬───────────────┘                │
│                 │                                │
│  ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐                │
│  │    Feature Output Buffer     │                │
│  └──────────────────────────────┘                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Speck Neuromorphic Chip (SynSense)

**Architecture:**
- DVS sensor + SNN accelerator on single chip
- 8 cores × 1024 neurons = 8192 total neurons
- Fully asynchronous event-driven operation
- <1 mW idle power, 10 mW active

**Key Features:**
- Always-on operation without frame capture
- Computation triggered only by events
- Sub-millisecond inference latency
- Battery-powered edge deployment

### SpiDR CIM-SNN Accelerator

**Reconfigurable Digital CIM for DVS:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              SpiDR Architecture                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                  │
│  ┌───────────┐   ┌───────────┐   ┌───────────┐ │
│  │ Weight    │   │ Membrane  │   │ Spike     │ │
│  │ Memory    │   │ Potential │   │ Generator │ │
│  │ (SRAM)    │   │ Memory    │   │           │ │
│  └─────┬─────┘   └─────┬─────┘   └─────┬─────┘ │
│        │               │               │        │
│        └───────────────┼───────────────┘        │
│                        │                        │
│        ┌───────────────▼───────────────┐       │
│        │    In-Memory MAC + LIF        │       │
│        │    (Fused Operation)          │       │
│        └───────────────┬───────────────┘       │
│                        │                        │
│        ┌───────────────▼───────────────┐       │
│        │    Spike Routing Network      │       │
│        └───────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Memristor-CMOS Hybrid Vision

**Performance Results (MDPI 2024):**
| Dataset | Accuracy | Power Savings |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| POKER-DVS | 99.5% (vs 100%) | 79% |
| MNIST-DVS | 99.25% (vs 100%) | 75% |
| N-MNIST | 98.8% | 70% |

### Event Processing Pipeline

```
DVS Events → [Time Window] → [Spatial Binning] → CIM SNN
     │              │               │              │
     │              ▼               ▼              ▼
     │        10-50 ms         Event Frame    Spike Train
     │                          (sparse)       Inference
     │
     └── Rate: 0.1-10 Meps (sparse, 1-5% active pixels)
```

### Applications

1. **Driver Monitoring:** Event cameras + SNNs for distraction detection
2. **SLAM:** Visual odometry with 1000× lower power than frame-based
3. **Gesture Recognition:** Real-time hand tracking
4. **High-Speed Robotics:** Microsecond latency for collision avoidance
5. **Surveillance:** Always-on with <1 mW idle power

### Key Metrics

| Metric | Value | Context |
|--------|-------|---------|
| DVS latency | 1-10 μs | Per-pixel response |
| Dynamic range | 120-140 dB | vs 60 dB frame camera |
| Speck idle power | <1 mW | Always-on operation |
| SNN inference power | 10 mW | Active processing |
| Memristor power savings | 75-79% | vs digital baseline |
| Event sparsity | 95-99% | Typical scenes |

---

## Section 359: CIM Compiler and Mapping Optimization

### Overview

CIM compilers map neural network computations to crossbar arrays, optimizing for latency, throughput, and energy. Key challenges include tiling large layers, handling non-idealities, and balancing precision vs. resource usage.

### Compilation Stack Levels

**CIM-MLC Multi-Level Compilation (ASPLOS 2024):**
```
Level 1: Network Graph
    ├── Layer fusion opportunities
    ├── Dependency analysis
    └── Pipeline scheduling

Level 2: Core Mapping
    ├── Load balancing across cores
    ├── Communication minimization
    └── Memory hierarchy placement

Level 3: Crossbar Mapping
    ├── Weight matrix → tile partitioning
    ├── Im2col transformation for conv
    └── Tile replication for throughput

Level 4: Wordline Scheduling
    ├── Row activation batching (9 rows typical)
    ├── ADC/DAC timing coordination
    └── Partial sum accumulation
```

### Crossbar Tiling Strategies

**Problem:** Large weight matrices exceed single crossbar capacity (typically 256×256).

**Solutions:**
```
Row-Major Tiling:                 Blocked Tiling:
┌─────┬─────┬─────┐              ┌─────────┬─────────┐
│ T0  │ T1  │ T2  │              │ ┌───┬───┐│ ┌───┬───┐│
├─────┼─────┼─────┤              │ │T0 │T1 ││ │T4 │T5 ││
│ T3  │ T4  │ T5  │              │ ├───┼───┤│ ├───┼───┤│
├─────┼─────┼─────┤              │ │T2 │T3 ││ │T6 │T7 ││
│ T6  │ T7  │ T8  │              │ └───┴───┘│ └───┴───┘│
└─────┴─────┴─────┘              └─────────┴─────────┘
 Sequential access                Better locality
```

### Layer Replication with Mixed Precision (LRMP)

**Motivation:** Non-uniform layer processing times create pipeline bottlenecks.

**LRMP Algorithm:**
1. Profile layer latencies at max precision
2. Identify bottleneck layer (max latency)
3. Replicate bottleneck tiles for throughput
4. Reduce precision of non-critical layers
5. Iterate until tile budget exhausted

**Precision-Replication Trade-off:**
```
Layer    | Latency | Tiles | Precision | Replication
---------|---------|-------|-----------|------------
conv1    | 100 cy  | 4     | 8-bit     | 1×
conv2    | 400 cy  | 16    | 8-bit     | 4× (bottleneck)
fc1      | 50 cy   | 8     | 4-bit     | 1×
fc2      | 10 cy   | 2     | 4-bit     | 1×
```

### Dataflow Optimization

**Weight-Stationary (default for CIM):**
- Weights programmed once, reused for all inputs
- Inputs streamed through crossbar
- Best when weight >> activation data

**Output-Stationary:**
- Output accumulator stays fixed
- Useful for large batch sizes
- Reduces partial sum communication

**Row-Stationary:**
- Maximizes data reuse in both dimensions
- Complex control but optimal for CNNs

### Row Activation Batching

**Problem:** Activating all rows simultaneously causes IR drop and non-idealities.

**Solution:** Batch activation of 9 rows at a time (empirical optimum).

```
Crossbar (256×256):
┌────────────────────────────────┐
│ Rows 0-8   ████████ Active     │
│ Rows 9-17  ░░░░░░░░ Waiting    │
│ Rows 18-26 ░░░░░░░░ Waiting    │
│ ...                            │
│ Rows 252-255 ░░░░░░ Waiting    │
└────────────────────────────────┘

Cycles: ceil(256/9) = 29 activation batches per MVM
```

### CMSwitch: Dual-Mode CIM Compiler (ASPLOS 2025)

**Innovation:** Dynamic switching between compute and memory modes.

**Compute Mode:**
- Full crossbar used for MVM
- Maximum parallelism

**Memory Mode:**
- Crossbar used as weight cache
- Digital compute for irregular operations
- Handles attention, softmax, etc.

**Decision Criteria:**
```
if (operation == dense_MVM && weight_fits_crossbar):
    use_compute_mode()
elif (operation == irregular || activation_dominant):
    use_memory_mode()
else:
    hybrid_schedule()
```

### Mapping Convolutions (Im2col)

**Im2col Transformation:**
```
Input Feature Map:          Im2col Matrix:
┌───────────────┐           ┌─────────────┐
│ a b c d e     │           │ a b e f i j │ ← Kernel 1 receptive field
│ e f g h i     │    →      │ b c f g j k │ ← Kernel 2 receptive field
│ i j k l m     │           │ c d g h k l │ ← ...
│ m n o p q     │           │ ...         │
└───────────────┘           └─────────────┘

Convolution → Matrix Multiplication on CIM crossbar
```

### Performance Results

**CIM-MLC (ASPLOS 2024):**
| Network | Speedup vs Naive | Energy Reduction |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| ResNet-18 | 3.2× | 2.8× |
| VGG-16 | 4.1× | 3.5× |
| MobileNet | 2.4× | 2.1× |
| BERT-base | 2.8× | 2.4× |

**LRMP Optimization:**
| Metric | Before LRMP | After LRMP |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Latency | 1.0× (baseline) | 0.4× |
| Tile usage | 100% | 85% |
| Accuracy | 95.2% | 94.8% |

### Typical CIM System Specifications

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Tile size | 256×256 | Standard |
| Total tiles | 5688 | Large system |
| Clock frequency | 192 MHz | Analog limited |
| Rows per activation | 9 | Non-ideality mitigation |
| ADC bits | 4 | Flash ADC |
| DAC bits | 1 | Binary input |
| Vector modules | 40 | Digital compute |
| SRAM per module | 128 KB | Weight cache |

### Code Implementation

```go
// CIM Compiler (vision_compiler_cim.go)
type CIMCompiler struct {
    Config        *CIMCompilerConfig
    Crossbar      *CrossbarSpec
    LayerMappings map[string][]*TileMapping
    TileUsage     int
}

func (c *CIMCompiler) MapLayer(layer *LayerSpec) ([]*TileMapping, error) {
    switch layer.Type {
    case LayerLinear:
        return c.mapLinearLayer(layer)
    case LayerConv2D:
        return c.mapConv2DLayer(layer)  // Uses im2col
    case LayerAttention:
        return c.mapAttentionLayer(layer) // Q,K,V,O projections
    }
}

// LRMP Optimizer
type LRMPOptimizer struct {
    Config     *LRMPConfig
    Compiler   *CIMCompiler
    LayerStats map[string]*LayerPerfStats
}

func (o *LRMPOptimizer) OptimizeNetwork(layers []*LayerSpec) (*OptimizationResult, error) {
    // Phase 1: Compute baseline latencies
    // Phase 2: Identify bottleneck
    // Phase 3: Allocate precision and replication
    // Phase 4: Return optimized mapping
}
```

## Section 360: Heterogeneous CIM Systems (Mixed NVM)

### Overview

Heterogeneous CIM combines multiple NVM technologies (FeFET, RRAM, PCM, SRAM) in a single system to leverage the unique strengths of each device type. 3D integration enables tier-based allocation where different operations map to optimal devices.

### Device Technology Comparison

| Parameter | FeFET | RRAM | PCM | STT-MRAM | SRAM |
|-----------|-------|------|-----|----------|------|
| Read latency | 10 ns | 10 ns | 50 ns | 10 ns | 1 ns |
| Write latency | 10 ns | 10 ns | 100 ns | 10 ns | 1 ns |
| Read energy | 1 fJ | 10 fJ | 100 fJ | 10 fJ | 1 fJ |
| Write energy | 10 fJ | 100 fJ | 10 pJ | 100 fJ | 1 fJ |
| Endurance | 10¹² | 10⁶ | 10⁸ | 10¹⁵ | ∞ |
| Area factor | 0.2× | 0.1× | 0.15× | 0.3× | 1× |
| On/off ratio | 10⁴ | 10² | 10³ | 3 | 10⁶ |
| MLC levels | 64 | 16 | 32 | 2 | 2 |
| CIM suitability | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |

### HEIRS Architecture (DAC 2024)

**Hybrid RRAM + SRAM 3D Architecture:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              SRAM-CIM Tier (Top)                │
│  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐              │
│  │SRAM │ │SRAM │ │SRAM │ │SRAM │  High-speed  │
│  │ CIM │ │ CIM │ │ CIM │ │ CIM │  Dynamic ops │
│  └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                   TSV Layer                      │
│              (100 GB/s bandwidth)               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              RRAM-CIM Tier (Bottom)             │
│  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐              │
│  │RRAM │ │RRAM │ │RRAM │ │RRAM │  High-density│
│  │ CIM │ │ CIM │ │ CIM │ │ CIM │  Static wts  │
│  └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘              │
│        (×4 more arrays due to density)          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Results:**
- 7.83× energy efficiency improvement vs RRAM-only
- 2.29× improvement vs SRAM-only
- 85.5% latency reduction
- 7.2× storage density improvement

### HePGA: Heterogeneous PIM for GNN (2025)

**3D Integration with Multiple Device Types:**
```
Tier 3: SRAM       ← Aggregation (frequent write)
        ↑ TSV
Tier 2: FeFET      ← Feature transformation (high precision)
        ↑ TSV
Tier 1: RRAM       ← Weight storage (high density)
        ↑ TSV
Tier 0: Controller ← Scheduling & data movement
```

**Performance:**
- 3.8× TOPS/W vs existing PIM architectures
- 6.8× TOPS/mm² compute efficiency
- Optimal mapping of GNN kernels to device types

### HyDe: Hybrid Device Search Framework

**Two-Phase Optimization:**
1. **Device Search:** Evaluate PCM/FeFET/SRAM combinations
2. **Architecture Search:** Optimize array allocation per layer

**Decision Criteria:**
```
For each layer:
  if (write_frequency == HIGH):
    prefer SRAM (high endurance)
  elif (precision_required == HIGH):
    prefer FeFET (64 MLC levels)
  elif (density_required == HIGH):
    prefer RRAM (0.1× area)
  else:
    balance based on access pattern
```

### Device Selection Guidelines

| Workload | Recommended Device | Rationale |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| Static weights | FeFET, RRAM | Low write, high density |
| Attention scores | SRAM | Frequent rewrite |
| KV-cache | SRAM/FeFET hybrid | Mixed access pattern |
| Embedding tables | RRAM | Large size, read-heavy |
| FFN weights | FeFET | High precision needed |
| Binary networks | MRAM | Fast binary ops |

### Key Metrics

| Architecture | Efficiency | Speedup | Density |
|--------------|------------|---------|---------|
| HEIRS (RRAM+SRAM) | 7.83× | 6× | 7.2× |
| HePGA (3-tier) | 3.8× TOPS/W | - | 6.8× TOPS/mm² |
| H3DAtten | 2.5× | 3× | 4× |
| AESHA | 2.1× | 2.5× | 3× |

---

## Section 361: CIM for LLM Inference (Attention, KV-Cache)

### Overview

Large Language Model inference on CIM faces unique challenges: quadratic attention complexity, massive KV-cache memory, and dynamic computation patterns. Specialized architectures combine NVM projection engines with CMOS attention engines.

### X-Former Architecture

**Hybrid NVM + CMOS Design:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    X-Former Architecture                 │
├──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┤
│   Projection Engine  │      Attention Engine            │
│      (NVM Tiles)     │       (CMOS Tiles)               │
├──────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│  ┌────────────────┐  │  ┌────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ FeFET Crossbar │  │  │    SRAM + Digital MAC      │ │
│  │                │  │  │                            │ │
│  │  Q Projection  │  │  │  Q × K^T (Dynamic)         │ │
│  │  K Projection  │  │  │  Softmax                   │ │
│  │  V Projection  │  │  │  Score × V                 │ │
│  │  O Projection  │  │  │  Score Buffer (Reuse)      │ │
│  │                │  │  │                            │ │
│  │  (Static wts,  │  │  │  (Dynamic scores,          │ │
│  │   no rewrite)  │  │  │   high endurance needed)   │ │
│  └────────────────┘  │  └────────────────────────────┘ │
├──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┤
│              Bus Interconnect (256 GB/s)                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Performance vs GPU (GTX 1060):**
- 69.8× latency improvement
- 13× energy reduction
- 24.1× vs NVM-only accelerators

### KV-Cache Challenge

**Memory Growth Problem:**
```
KV Cache Size = 2 × num_layers × num_heads × seq_len × head_dim × precision

Example (LLaMA-7B, 2K context):
  = 2 × 32 × 32 × 2048 × 128 × 2 bytes
  = 1.07 GB per sequence!

For 32K context: 17 GB!
```

**CIM Solutions:**
1. **Offload to NVM:** Store cold KV pairs in high-density RRAM
2. **KV Pruning:** Reduce cache size via attention sparsity
3. **CAM Search:** Content-addressable lookup for relevant keys

### UniCAIM: Unified CAM/CIM Architecture

**Static-Dynamic KV Pruning:**
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  UniCAIM Architecture                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                          │
│  Step 1: Static Pruning (Fixed Pattern)                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ [SINK][SINK][SINK][SINK]....[WINDOW][WINDOW]... │   │
│  │   ↑ Keep first 4 tokens    ↑ Keep last N tokens │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                          │
│  Step 2: Dynamic Pruning (Top-K Selection)              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │   CAM Array: Parallel similarity search          │   │
│  │   → Select top-K highest attention scores        │   │
│  │   → ~75% tokens pruned on average               │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                          │
│  Step 3: Full Attention on Selected Tokens              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │   CIM Array: Dense MVM on ~25% tokens           │   │
│  │   → High precision, low latency                 │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Pruning Comparison:**

| Method | Type | Tokens Kept | Accuracy |
|--------|------|-------------|----------|
| Full Attention | None | 100% | 100% (baseline) |
| StreamingLLM | Static only | Sink + Window | 95-98% |
| Top-K Selection | Dynamic only | Top-K per query | 96-99% |
| UniCAIM | Static + Dynamic | ~25% | 97-99% |

### KV-Cache Optimization Techniques (2024-2025)

| Technique | Description | Memory Reduction |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| SnapKV | Fine-tuning-free, keep important positions | 8.2× |
| MorphKV | Adaptive fixed-size cache | 4× |
| RetrievalAttention | ANN search, 1-3% data access | 30-100× |
| Entropy-guided | Budget per attention head entropy | 3-5× |
| Quantized KV | 1-bit per channel | 16× |

### Hybrid Transformer Accelerator Design

**Layer-to-Device Mapping:**
```
Transformer Block:
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Q, K, V Projections ──→ FeFET CIM (Static)       │
│          ↓                                         │
│  Attention Scores ────→ SRAM CIM (Dynamic)        │
│          ↓                                         │
│  KV-Cache ────────────→ RRAM (High density)       │
│          ↓                                         │
│  Output Projection ───→ FeFET CIM (Static)        │
│          ↓                                         │
│  FFN Layer 1 ─────────→ FeFET CIM (Static)        │
│          ↓                                         │
│  FFN Layer 2 ─────────→ FeFET CIM (Static)        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Summary

```go
// Heterogeneous device specs (hetero_llm_cim.go)
type NVMDeviceSpec struct {
    Type           NVMDeviceType
    ReadLatencyNS  float64
    WriteLatencyNS float64
    Endurance      float64
    AnalogLevels   int
    CIMSuitability float64
}

// X-Former style accelerator
type XFormerAccelerator struct {
    ProjectionEngine *ProjectionEngine  // NVM tiles
    AttentionEngine  *AttentionEngine   // CMOS tiles
    Interconnect     *BusInterconnect
}

// UniCAIM with KV pruning
type UniCAIMAccelerator struct {
    CAMArrays  []*CAMArray    // Similarity search
    CIMArrays  []*CIMArrayLLM // Dense compute
    KVCache    *KVCache
    PruneStats *PruneStats
}
```

### Key Performance Numbers

| Metric | X-Former | UniCAIM | HEIRS |
|--------|----------|---------|-------|
| Latency vs GPU | 69.8× | 50× | 40× |
| Energy reduction | 13× | 10× | 7.83× |
| KV-cache reduction | - | 4× | - |
| Attention pruning | - | 75% | - |
| Memory efficiency | 3× | 8× | 7.2× |

### References

- [X-Former - arXiv 2023](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.07470)
- [UniCAIM - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.07479)
- [HEIRS - DAC 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3649329.3657327)
- [HePGA - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.16011)
- [KV Cache Survey - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2412.19442v3)
- [Memory Is All You Need - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.08413v1)

## Section 362: Radiation-Hardened CIM for Space Applications

### Overview

Space environments expose electronics to harsh radiation including total ionizing dose (TID), single event effects (SEE), and displacement damage. Ferroelectric memories (FRAM, FeFET) offer inherent radiation hardness, making them ideal for space-grade CIM systems.

### Space Radiation Environment

| Environment | TID Rate | Heavy Ion LET | Temperature |
|-------------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| LEO | ~1 krad/yr | 40 MeV·cm²/mg | -40°C |
| GEO | ~10 krad/yr | 60 MeV·cm²/mg | -20°C |
| Deep Space | ~100 krad/yr | 100 MeV·cm²/mg | -100°C |
| Solar Probe | ~50 krad/yr | 80 MeV·cm²/mg | +150°C |

### Radiation Effects on Memory

**Single Event Effects (SEE):**
```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Single Event Effects                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                     │
│  Heavy Ion → Strikes Device → Ionization Trail     │
│                    │                                │
│         ┌─────────┴─────────┐                      │
│         │                   │                       │
│    Recoverable         Permanent                   │
│         │                   │                       │
│    ┌────┴────┐         ┌───┴───┐                  │
│    │         │         │       │                   │
│   SEU      SET       SEL     SEGR                 │
│  (Upset)  (Trans)  (Latch)  (Gate                 │
│                              Rupture)              │
│                                                     │
│  FRAM: SEU Immune, No SEL (no latch structure)    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Total Ionizing Dose (TID):**
- Accumulated radiation damage over mission lifetime
- Causes threshold shifts, leakage increase
- FRAM tolerates >150 krad(Si)
- HfO₂ FeFET: >1 Mrad(Si) demonstrated

### FRAM Radiation Specifications (Infineon QML-V)

| Parameter | Specification |
|-----------|--------------|
| TID tolerance | >150 krad(Si) |
| SEL threshold | >114 MeV·cm²/mg @115°C |
| SEU | Immune |
| SEFI rate | <1.34×10⁻⁴ err/dev.day |
| Data retention | >100 years |
| Endurance | 10¹⁴ cycles |

### HfO₂ FeFET Radiation Hardness

**Proton Radiation Testing (2024):**
- Zr-doped HfO₂ (HZO) tested at 5 MeV protons
- 1×10¹² ions/cm² does not significantly affect hysteresis
- Orthorhombic Pbc21 phase stable under radiation
- Higher Zr content (50%) shows better resistance

**Gamma-Ray TID Testing:**
- Tested up to 1 Mrad(Si)
- P-V and C-V show decrease due to pinned domains
- Weaker built-in field after radiation
- Still functional at 1 Mrad - remarkable tolerance

### Why Ferroelectric is Radiation-Hard

1. **No latch structure:** Eliminates SEL susceptibility
2. **Ionic polarization:** Radiation doesn't affect domain state
3. **Thick oxide:** HfO₂ is inherently radiation-tolerant
4. **Fast recovery:** Domains self-restore after transients
5. **Non-volatile:** No refresh vulnerable to interruption

### Radiation-Hardened CIM Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Radiation-Hardened CIM System               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                      │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  │
│  │  FeFET CIM   │  │  FeFET CIM   │  │ FeFET CIM│  │
│  │  Array 0     │  │  Array 1     │  │ Array 2  │  │
│  │  (TMR Copy)  │  │  (TMR Copy)  │  │(TMR Copy)│  │
│  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └────┬─────┘  │
│         │                 │               │         │
│         └────────┬────────┴───────┬───────┘         │
│                  │                │                  │
│         ┌────────▼────────┐  ┌────▼────┐           │
│         │  Voting Logic   │  │   ECC   │           │
│         │  (Majority)     │  │ Decoder │           │
│         └────────┬────────┘  └────┬────┘           │
│                  │                │                  │
│         ┌────────▼────────────────▼────────┐       │
│         │      Hardened Controller         │       │
│         │   (Triple-redundant registers)   │       │
│         └──────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Protection Techniques

| Technique | BER Improvement | Area Overhead | Power Overhead |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|
| TMR | 10¹²× | 200% | 200% |
| EDAC (SEC-DED) | 10⁴× | 29% | 26% |
| Scrubbing | 10²× | 5% | 10% |
| Hardened cells | 10³× | 30% | 20% |

---

## Section 363: Error Correction for Analog CIM

### Overview

Analog CIM faces unique error sources: device variation, noise, non-linearities, and quantization. Multiple mitigation strategies exist including ECC, bit slicing, and Residue Number Systems (RNS).

### Error Sources in Analog CIM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Analog CIM Error Sources                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                      │
│  Device Level:                                       │
│  ├── Programming noise (write variation)            │
│  ├── Cycle-to-cycle read noise                      │
│  ├── Retention drift                                │
│  ├── Endurance degradation                          │
│  └── Stuck-at faults (on/off)                       │
│                                                      │
│  Circuit Level:                                      │
│  ├── ADC quantization error                         │
│  ├── DAC quantization error                         │
│  ├── IR-drop (parasitic resistance)                 │
│  ├── Sneak paths (crossbar leakage)                 │
│  └── Thermal noise                                  │
│                                                      │
│  System Level:                                       │
│  ├── Temperature drift                              │
│  ├── Aging effects                                  │
│  └── Process variation                              │
│                                                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Error Correction Code Techniques

**Hamming SEC-DED:**
- Single Error Correction, Double Error Detection
- ~29% area overhead, ~26% power overhead
- 16,000× BER reduction demonstrated

**Checksum Codes (2024):**
- Lightweight detection for IMC
- 50% area savings vs TMR
- Higher fault detection with correction
- Suitable for safety-critical neural networks

**Non-Binary LDPC for PIM (2025):**
- Supports multi-level cell memory
- Works with differential weight mapping
- Enables ternary element error correction
- General PIM MAC arithmetic support

### Bit Slicing for Precision

**Concept:** Decompose high-precision weights into multiple low-precision slices.

```
16-bit Weight Decomposition:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Original 16-bit weight: 0xABCD                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                   │
│  Slice 3 (MSB): 0xA × 2¹²  →  Crossbar 3        │
│  Slice 2:       0xB × 2⁸   →  Crossbar 2        │
│  Slice 1:       0xC × 2⁴   →  Crossbar 1        │
│  Slice 0 (LSB): 0xD × 2⁰   →  Crossbar 0        │
│                                                   │
│  Final output = Σ (slice_output × 2^(slice×4))  │
│                                                   │
│  Benefits:                                        │
│  • Each crossbar operates at 4-bit precision     │
│  • Device variation affects slices independently │
│  • MSB slices can use higher-precision arrays    │
│  • Graceful degradation if slice fails           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Residue Number System (RNS)

**Concept:** Represent numbers using residues modulo co-prime integers.

```
RNS Representation (moduli: 5, 7, 9, 11, 13):

Value 12345:
  12345 mod 5  = 0
  12345 mod 7  = 4
  12345 mod 9  = 6
  12345 mod 11 = 3
  12345 mod 13 = 11

RNS: (0, 4, 6, 3, 11)

Dynamic Range = 5 × 7 × 9 × 11 × 13 = 45,045
Equivalent precision: ~15.5 bits

Benefits:
• Each channel operates with small modulus (low precision)
• Errors in one channel don't propagate
• Parallel computation across channels
• RRNS enables error detection/correction
```

**Performance (Mirage, 2024):**
- 23.8× faster training vs systolic arrays
- 32.1× lower EDP in iso-energy scenario
- 42.8× lower power in iso-area scenario
- 99% FP32 accuracy with 6-bit arithmetic

### Compilation Framework for Error Correction (2024)

**SRAM-CIM Compilation with Error Correction:**
1. **CCVLE:** Calibration of Computation Voltage Linear Error
2. **MAQE:** Mitigation of Analog-to-Digital Quantization Error

**Optimization Flow:**
```
Neural Network Model
        ↓
[Weight Analysis] → Sensitivity profiling
        ↓
[Bit Slicing Allocation] → MSB gets more protection
        ↓
[ECC Assignment] → Per-layer ECC selection
        ↓
[Mapping to Crossbars] → Error-aware placement
        ↓
[Runtime Scrubbing] → Periodic correction
        ↓
Deployed CIM Accelerator
```

### Straight-Through Estimation for Robust Training (2025)

**Problem:** Simplified noise models during training don't match real hardware.

**Solution:** Extended STE framework models:
- ADC/DAC quantization
- Device non-linearity
- Programming noise
- Cycle-to-cycle variance
- Thermal drift
- Retention degradation
- IR-drop effects

### Error Mitigation Comparison

| Technique | BER Reduction | Area | Power | Latency |
|-----------|---------------|------|-------|---------|
| None | 1× | 0% | 0% | 0% |
| TMR | 10¹²× | 200% | 200% | 10% |
| Hamming ECC | 10⁴× | 29% | 26% | 5% |
| Checksum | 10²× | 15% | 10% | 3% |
| Bit Slicing (4×4) | 10²× | 300% | 250% | 0% |
| RNS (5 mod) | 10⁴× | 400% | 300% | 20% |
| Fault-aware training | 10× | 0% | 0% | 0% |

### Implementation Summary

```go
// Radiation-hardened crossbar (radiation_ecc_cim.go)
type RadHardCrossbar struct {
    Config       *RadHardCIMConfig
    Weights      [][]float64
    WeightsTMR   [3][][]float64  // Triple copies
    ParityBits   [][]int         // ECC parity
}

// Bit slicing for noise tolerance
type BitSlicedCrossbar struct {
    Config  *BitSlicingConfig
    Slices  []*CrossbarSlice  // Multiple low-precision slices
    Shifts  []int             // Bit positions
}

// RNS-based CIM
type RNSCrossbar struct {
    Config    *RNSConfig
    Channels  []*RNSChannel  // Parallel modular channels
    CRTCoeffs []int          // Chinese Remainder Theorem
}

// Hamming ECC
func (c *CIMErrorCorrector) EncodeHamming(data []int) []int
func (c *CIMErrorCorrector) DecodeHamming(encoded []int) ([]int, bool)
```

- [High Precision AIMC - npj 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00044-2)
- [CIM ECC Successive Correction - DAC 2022](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3489517.3530526)
- [NB-LDPC for PIM - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.11487)
- [FRAM Radiation - TI Whitepaper](https://www.ti.com/lit/wp/sboa154/sboa154.pdf)
- [HfO2 Proton Radiation - ScienceDirect 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0925838824019595)
- [Mirage RNS - ISCA 2024](https://bu-icsg.github.io/publications/2024/rns_training_isca2024.pdf)

## 364. CIM Scheduling and Resource Management (Iteration 134)

### The Scheduling Challenge for CIM

CIM architectures adopt a tiled structure with tiles interconnected via Network-on-Chip (NoC). Key challenge: **neural network weights are statically assigned to PEs and remain there during inference**, making traditional scheduling approaches suboptimal.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CIM Tiled Architecture                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│   ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐                   │
│   │Tile │══│Tile │══│Tile │══│Tile │   ← Row 0         │
│   │ 0   │  │ 1   │  │ 2   │  │ 3   │                   │
│   └──╦══┘  └──╦══┘  └──╦══┘  └──╦══┘                   │
│      ║       ║       ║       ║     NoC Mesh            │
│   ┌──╩══┐  ┌──╩══┐  ┌──╩══┐  ┌──╩══┐                   │
│   │Tile │══│Tile │══│Tile │══│Tile │   ← Row 1         │
│   │ 4   │  │ 5   │  │ 6   │  │ 7   │                   │
│   └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘                   │
│                                                         │
│   Each Tile: 256×256 crossbar, 64 PEs                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Layer-by-Layer vs Cross-Layer Scheduling

**Problem with Layer-by-Layer:**
- Only one layer's PEs active at any time
- Low PE utilization (often <5%)
- Memory bandwidth bottleneck

**CLSA-CIM Cross-Layer Approach (DATE 2024):**
- Partial results passed to next layer before full computation
- Exploits both intra- and cross-layer parallelism
- PE utilization: 4.1% → 28.4% (with weight duplication)
- Speedup: up to 21.9×

```
Layer-by-Layer:
┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐
│Layer 1│→│Layer 2│→│Layer 3│→│Layer 4│
└───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘
   ████      ░░░░      ░░░░      ░░░░      Time →
   ░░░░      ████      ░░░░      ░░░░
   ░░░░      ░░░░      ████      ░░░░
   ░░░░      ░░░░      ░░░░      ████

Cross-Layer (Pipelined):
┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐
│Layer 1│→│Layer 2│→│Layer 3│→│Layer 4│
└───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘
   ████      ░░░░      ░░░░      ░░░░      Time →
   ██░░      ████      ░░░░      ░░░░      ← Overlap!
   ░░░░      ██░░      ████      ░░░░
   ░░░░      ░░░░      ██░░      ████
```

### CLSA-CIM Performance Results

**Benchmarks (DATE 2024):**

| Model | PEs | Baseline Util | CLSA Util | Speedup |
|-------|-----|---------------|-----------|---------|
| VGG16 | 117 | 0.8% | 28.4%* | 21.9× |
| VGG19 | 145 | 0.7% | 26.1%* | 19.2× |
| ResNet50 | 52 | 1.9% | 15.3%* | 8.1× |
| ResNet101 | 103 | 1.0% | 14.8%* | 7.9× |
| TinyYOLOv3 | 34 | 2.9% | 12.1%* | 4.2× |

*With weight duplication (wdup+32 additional PEs)

### Dual-Mode Switchable Arrays (CMSwitch 2025)

Arrays can operate as either compute engines or memory buffers:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        Dual-Mode CIM Array             │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                        │
│   Mode 0: Compute (In-situ MAC)        │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ WL₀ ─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─ → BL  │     │
│   │ WL₁ ─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─●─ → BL  │     │
│   │      ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓       │     │
│   │      Σ MAC operations        │     │
│   └─────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                        │
│   Mode 1: Memory (Scratchpad)          │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────┐     │
│   │ Address → Data Buffer       │     │
│   │ ┌─────────────────────────┐ │     │
│   │ │ Activation storage      │ │     │
│   │ │ Intermediate results    │ │     │
│   │ └─────────────────────────┘ │     │
│   └─────────────────────────────┘     │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**CMSwitch Results:**
- Mixed-integer + dynamic programming optimization
- 1.3× mean acceleration for large DNNs
- Dynamic compute/memory balance per workload segment

### Workload Tiling and Mapping

**Key Optimization Dimensions:**
1. Tile size selection
2. Dataflow type (output/weight/input stationary)
3. Reuse factor optimization
4. Data movement minimization

**Tiling Strategy Comparison:**

| Dataflow | Weight Reuse | Input Reuse | Output Reuse | Best For |
|----------|--------------|-------------|--------------|----------|
| Output Stationary | Low | Low | High | Large batches |
| Weight Stationary | High | Low | Low | Small batches |
| Input Stationary | Low | High | Low | Wide layers |
| Row Stationary | Medium | Medium | Medium | Balanced |

**WWW Framework Results (arXiv 2024):**
- "What, When, Where to Compute-in-Memory"
- Up to 3.4× energy efficiency vs baseline
- Up to 15.6× throughput improvement

### CIM-MLC Multi-Level Compilation (ASPLOS 2024)

Hierarchical compilation stack:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            High-Level Network           │
│           (TensorFlow, PyTorch)         │
└───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                    ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Chip-Level Mapping              │
│    - Model partitioning                 │
│    - Inter-chip communication           │
└───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                    ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Core-Level Mapping              │
│    - Layer-to-core assignment           │
│    - Dataflow selection                 │
└───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                    ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│       Crossbar-Level Mapping            │
│    - Weight tiling                      │
│    - MVM scheduling                     │
└───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                    ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Row-Level Mapping               │
│    - Wordline activation                │
│    - Row batching                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**CIM-MLC Results:**
- 2.4-4.1× speedup over naive mapping
- Device-agnostic and device-aware modes
- Operator duplication and pipelining support

### Implementation Summary

```go
// Cross-layer scheduler (scheduling_diffusion_cim.go)
type CLSACIMScheduler struct {
    Config          *CLSAConfig
    Tiles           []*TileConfig
    NoC             *NoCConfig
    LayerMappings   []*LayerMapping
    Schedule        []*ScheduleEntry
    PEUtilization   float64
}

// Scheduling heuristics
const (
    HeuristicLayerByLayer  // Traditional
    HeuristicCrossLayer    // CLSA-CIM partial results
    HeuristicPipelined     // Full pipeline overlap
    HeuristicDynamic       // Runtime optimization
)

// Dual-mode resource management
type DualModeManager struct {
    Arrays          []*DualModeArray
    CurrentWorkload *WorkloadProfile
}

// Tiling optimization
type TilingOptimizer struct {
    Config       *TilingConfig
    BestTiling   *TilingStrategy
    SearchSpace  []*TilingStrategy
}
```

### References

- [CLSA-CIM - DATE 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.07671)
- [CIM-MLC - ASPLOS 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.15896)
- [CMSwitch - DAC 2025](https://past.date-conference.com/proceedings-archive/2024/DATA/406_pdf_upload.pdf)
- [WWW Framework - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.15896)

## 365. CIM for Diffusion Model Acceleration (Iteration 134)

### Diffusion Model Hardware Challenges

Diffusion models face unique deployment challenges:
1. **Iterative Denoising:** 20-50 sequential forward passes
2. **Large U-Net:** 865M parameters, 1.6T operations (Stable Diffusion)
3. **Memory Bandwidth:** Attention activations dominate
4. **Mixed Precision:** Sensitive layers need higher precision

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Stable Diffusion Pipeline                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Text ─→ [Text Encoder] ─→ Conditioning                 │
│                              ↓                          │
│  Noise ─→ [U-Net × 50 steps] ←─┘                        │
│              ↓                                          │
│  Latent ─→ [VAE Decoder] ─→ Image                       │
│                                                         │
│  U-Net per step: ~1.6T FLOPs                            │
│  Total: 50 × 1.6T = 80T FLOPs                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### AIG-CIM: Tri-Gear Heterogeneous CIM (DAC 2024)

First heterogeneous CIM accelerator for diffusion models:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AIG-CIM Architecture                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│   ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │
│   │   INT Gear   │  │   FP Gear    │  │ Sparse Gear  │ │
│   │   (INT8)     │  │   (BF16)     │  │  (Pruned)    │ │
│   │              │  │              │  │              │ │
│   │ ●Booth-8 mul │  │ ●Exponent    │  │ ●Redundancy  │ │
│   │ ●Conv layers │  │  accel       │  │  search      │ │
│   │              │  │ ●Attention   │  │ ●Sparse conv │ │
│   └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘ │
│          │                 │                 │         │
│          └────────┬────────┴────────┬────────┘         │
│                   │   Scheduler     │                   │
│                   │ (Layer → Gear)  │                   │
│                   └─────────────────┘                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**AIG-CIM Innovations:**
1. **Booth-8 Multiplier:** Efficient bit-parallel multiplication
2. **Exponent Acceleration:** Balanced INT/FP latency
3. **In-Memory Redundancy Search:** FP sparsity exploitation

**Performance (22nm TSMC):**
- 21.3× latency reduction vs RTX 3090
- 231.2× throughput improvement
- 1000× energy efficiency improvement

### Exploiting Denoising Similarity

Adjacent denoising steps produce similar latent representations:

```
Step t:   ████████████████████████████████
Step t+1: ████████████████████████████░░░░  ~95% similar
Step t+2: ████████████████████████░░░░░░░░  ~90% similar

Strategy: Skip redundant computation when similarity > threshold
```

**Similarity-Based Optimization:**
- Cosine similarity between adjacent latents
- Threshold: 0.7-0.9 depending on quality requirements
- Compute skip: up to 50% reduction
- Quality degradation: <1% FID increase

### DDSM: Denoising Diffusion Step-aware Models (ICLR 2024)

Variable network capacity per denoising step:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        Step-Aware Capacity Allocation                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Capacity                                               │
│  1.0 ┤                                    ▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄   │
│      │                              ▄▄▄▄▄▄            │
│  0.8 ┤                        ▄▄▄▄▄▄                   │
│      │                  ▄▄▄▄▄▄                         │
│  0.6 ┤            ▄▄▄▄▄▄                               │
│      │      ▄▄▄▄▄▄                                     │
│  0.4 ┤▄▄▄▄▄▄                                           │
│      ├──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┤│
│        0    10     20     30     40     50   Step     │
│                                                         │
│  Early steps (high noise): Lower capacity (redundant)  │
│  Late steps (fine detail): Full capacity (important)   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**DDSM Results:**
- CIFAR-10: 49% FLOPs reduction
- CelebA-HQ: 61% FLOPs reduction
- LSUN-bedroom: 59% FLOPs reduction
- AFHQ: 71% FLOPs reduction
- ImageNet: 76% FLOPs reduction

### Hybrid Analog/Digital CIM (ISSCC 2024)

Lightning-like hybrid structure from Southeast University:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Hybrid ACIM/DCIM Architecture                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│   ┌─────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────┐           │
│   │   Analog CIM    │    │   Digital CIM   │           │
│   │                 │    │                 │           │
│   │ + High energy   │    │ + High accuracy │           │
│   │   efficiency    │    │ + Deterministic │           │
│   │ - Noise prone   │    │ - Higher power  │           │
│   │                 │    │                 │           │
│   └────────┬────────┘    └────────┬────────┘           │
│            │                      │                     │
│            └──────────┬───────────┘                     │
│                       │                                 │
│              Dynamic Selection                          │
│            based on layer sensitivity                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Allocation Strategy:**
- Attention layers → Digital (precision-critical)
- Conv layers (early) → Analog (energy-efficient)
- Conv layers (late) → Mixed (quality-sensitive)

### Stable Diffusion U-Net CIM Mapping

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              U-Net Architecture Mapping                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Encoder                    Decoder                     │
│  ┌─────┐                    ┌─────┐                     │
│  │Conv │←─ Analog CIM ─────→│Conv │                     │
│  │Down │   (6-8 bit)        │Up   │                     │
│  └──┬──┘                    └──┬──┘                     │
│     │                          │                        │
│  ┌──┴──┐                    ┌──┴──┐                     │
│  │Attn │←─ Digital CIM ────→│Attn │                     │
│  │Block│   (16 bit FP)      │Block│                     │
│  └──┬──┘                    └──┬──┘                     │
│     │                          │                        │
│     └──────────┬───────────────┘                        │
│                │                                        │
│         ┌──────┴──────┐                                 │
│         │  Middle     │                                 │
│         │  Block      │← Sparse CIM                     │
│         │ (Attention) │   (pruned weights)              │
│         └─────────────┘                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Quantization for Diffusion CIM

**Dynamic Activation Quantization (DAQ):**
- Timestep-dependent activation drift
- Dynamic rescaling per denoising step
- Stable low-bit inference

**Layer-Specific Precision:**

| Layer Type | Recommended Bits | Reason |
|------------|------------------|--------|
| ResBlocks (early) | 6-bit | High redundancy |
| ResBlocks (late) | 8-bit | Detail preservation |
| Self-Attention | 8-16 bit | Softmax sensitivity |
| Cross-Attention | 8 bit | Conditioning accuracy |
| GroupNorm | 16 bit | Statistics precision |
| Conv final | 8 bit | Output quality |

### Performance Comparison

| Accelerator | Technology | Latency | Throughput | Energy Eff. |
|-------------|------------|---------|------------|-------------|
| RTX 3090 | 8nm | 1× | 1× | 1× |
| AIG-CIM | 22nm | 0.047× | 231× | ~1000× |
| Tsinghua HetCIM | 28nm | 0.02× | 50× | 400× |
| IMAGINE | 22nm FD-SOI | 0.03× | 150× | 800× |

### Implementation Summary

```go
// Diffusion CIM accelerator (scheduling_diffusion_cim.go)
type DiffusionCIMAccelerator struct {
    Config           *DiffusionCIMConfig
    UNetTiles        []*CIMTileBank
    AttentionTiles   []*CIMTileBank
    DenoiseScheduler *DenoiseScheduler
}

// Tri-gear heterogeneous CIM
type TriGearCIM struct {
    IntGear     *GearConfig  // INT8 with Booth multiplier
    FPGear      *GearConfig  // BF16 for attention
    SparseGear  *GearConfig  // Sparse/pruned weights
}

// DDSM step-aware scheduling
type DDSMScheduler struct {
    StepCapacities []float64  // Per-step network capacity
    StepImportance []float64  // Computed importance scores
    PrunedArchs    []*PrunedArchitecture
}

// Denoising with similarity exploitation
func (d *DiffusionCIMAccelerator) RunDenoising() *DiffusionStats {
    // Compute similarity, skip redundant computation
    // Return: latency, energy, throughput, skipped compute
}
```

## 366. Cryogenic Compute-in-Memory Operation (Iteration 135)

### Why Cryogenic CIM?

Cryogenic operation (4K-77K) offers compelling advantages for compute-in-memory:
1. **Reduced thermal noise:** kT noise scales linearly with temperature
2. **Improved device characteristics:** Higher mobility, lower leakage
3. **Quantum computing interface:** Direct integration with qubits
4. **Enhanced retention:** Near-infinite data retention at 4K

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Temperature Effects on CIM                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Parameter        300K    77K     4K      Improvement  │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│  Thermal noise    1.0×    0.26×   0.013×   77× @ 4K    │
│  Leakage current  1.0×    10⁻⁵×   10⁻¹⁰×  Negligible  │
│  Carrier mobility 1.0×    ~3×     ~10×     10× @ 4K    │
│  Retention time   10 yr   >100 yr >1000 yr Infinite    │
│  Vth shift        0 mV    +88 mV  +118 mV  Compensate  │
│                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HZO Ferroelectric at Cryogenic Temperatures (2024)

**Key Findings from Bohuslavskyi et al. (Adv. Electronic Materials 2024):**

| Parameter | 300K | 77K | 4K |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| Remanent Polarization (Pr) | 30 µC/cm² | 50 µC/cm² | 75 µC/cm² |
| Memory Window | 4V | 6V | 8V |
| Endurance | >10⁹ cycles | >10⁹ cycles | >10⁹ cycles |
| Analog States | ~10 (poor linearity) | ~20 (good) | ~20 (near-ideal) |
| Linearity Factor | 0.7 | 0.9 | 0.95+ |

**Critical Insight:** Below 100K, HZO devices support higher amplitude pulses (±7V vs ±5V), yielding record-high Pr of 75 µC/cm² with nearly ideal analog weight linearity.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        HZO Analog Weight Linearity vs Temperature        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Polarization                                           │
│  +Pr ┤               Ideal          ●●●●●●●●● 4K       │
│      │             ●●                                   │
│      │           ●●    ○○○○○○○○○ 77K                   │
│      │         ●●   ○○                                  │
│   0  ┤       ●●   ○○     □□□□□□□□□ 300K               │
│      │     ●●   ○○    □□                               │
│      │   ●●   ○○   □□                                  │
│      │ ●●   ○○  □□                                     │
│  -Pr ┤●●  ○○ □□                                        │
│      ├───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┤        │
│       1   2   3   4   5   ...  16  17  18  19  20      │
│                    Pulse Number                         │
│                                                         │
│  ● 4K: Near-ideal linearity (deviation <5%)            │
│  ○ 77K: Good linearity (deviation <10%)                │
│  □ 300K: Non-linear (deviation >20%)                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Cryogenic Memory Technologies Comparison

| Technology | 77K Operation | 4K Operation | Quantum Compatible |
|------------|---------------|--------------|-------------------|
| HZO FeFET | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Enhanced | ✅ Yes |
| RRAM/ReRAM | ⚠️ Higher V_set | ⚠️ Higher power | ⚠️ Limited |
| STT-MRAM | ✅ 2.3× read margin | ✅ 69% energy saving | ✅ Yes |
| DRAM | ✅ Near-NVM retention | ✅ Non-volatile-like | ⚠️ Limited |
| FeSQUID TCAM | N/A | ✅ 1.36 aJ/search | ✅ Native |

### Ferroelectric SQUID CAM (Nature 2025)

For quantum computing interfaces, ferroelectric SQUIDs offer exceptional efficiency:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              FeSQUID TCAM Architecture                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│   Josephson Junction Array                              │
│   ┌───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┐                   │
│   │FE │FE │FE │FE │FE │FE │FE │FE │ ← Ferroelectric   │
│   │JJ │JJ │JJ │JJ │JJ │JJ │JJ │JJ │   Josephson       │
│   └─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┴─┬─┘   Junctions      │
│     │   │   │   │   │   │   │   │                      │
│   Match Match Match Match Match Match Match Match       │
│     ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓   ↓                      │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐                  │
│   │      Match Line (ML)            │                  │
│   └─────────────────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                         │
│   Energy: 1.36 aJ (binary), 26.5 aJ (ternary)          │
│   Speed: Single-cycle associative search               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### RRAM/ReRAM at Cryogenic Temperatures

**Challenges:**
- SET/RESET voltages increase at low temperature
- Oxygen ion mobility decreases (higher barrier)
- Power consumption increases despite lower leakage

**Benefits:**
- ROFF/RON ratio improves (10x at 77K typical)
- Retention dramatically enhanced
- Endurance maintained (>5×10⁷ cycles at 10K-490K)

**ITO/HfOx/ITO Results:**
- Working range: 10K to 490K
- Large ROFF/RON maintained across range
- >5×10⁷ endurance cycles demonstrated

### Cryogenic CIM for Quantum Computing

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│       Quantum-Classical CIM Interface                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Room Temperature (300K)                                │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  Control Electronics, I/O, Power       │            │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                    ↕ Data bus                          │
│  77K Stage (Liquid N₂)                                 │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  CIM Arrays (HZO/STT-MRAM)             │            │
│  │  - Weight storage                      │            │
│  │  - Neural network inference            │            │
│  │  - Error correction                    │            │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                    ↕ Reduced bandwidth                 │
│  4K Stage (Liquid He)                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  CIM Interface + Classical Control     │            │
│  │  - Qubit readout processing            │            │
│  │  - Feedback control                    │            │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
│                    ↕ Minimal I/O                       │
│  <100mK Stage (Dilution Refrigerator)                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│  │  Quantum Processor (Qubits)            │            │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Challenge:** Data transfer between 4K and 300K consumes most of system power. Cryogenic CIM reduces this by performing computation at low temperature.

### Implementation Summary

```go
// Cryogenic temperature effects (cryo_olfactory_cim.go)
type CryoTempEffects struct {
    Temperature      float64 // Kelvin
    CarrierMobility  float64 // Relative improvement
    ThresholdVoltage float64 // Vth shift (mV)
    LeakageCurrent   float64 // Relative to 300K
    RetentionTime    float64 // Relative improvement
    ThermalNoise     float64 // kT factor
}

// Cryogenic HZO device
type CryoHZODevice struct {
    Config          *CryoHZOConfig
    Pr              float64   // Up to 75 µC/cm² at 4K
    MemoryWindow    float64   // 6-8V at cryogenic
    LinearityFactor float64   // 0.95+ at 4K
    AnalogStates    []float64 // 20 nearly-ideal states
}

// Cryogenic CIM array
type CryoCIMArray struct {
    Config      *CryoCIMConfig
    HZODevices  [][]*CryoHZODevice
    CryoEffects *CryoTempEffects
    Stats       *CryoCIMStats  // Energy, SNR, ENOB
}
```

### References

- [HZO Cryogenic - Adv. Electronic Materials 2024](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aelm.202300879)
- [Cryogenic Ferroelectric TCAM - IEEE 2025](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10909519/)
- [FeSQUID CAM - npj 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00039-z)
- [Cryogenic Memory Technologies - arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.09436)

## 367. Neuromorphic Olfactory and Gustatory Systems (Iteration 135)

### Electronic Nose (E-Nose) Architecture

Electronic noses mimic biological olfaction using gas sensor arrays and pattern recognition:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Biological vs Electronic Olfaction             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Biological                    Electronic               │
│  ──────────                    ──────────               │
│  Olfactory receptors (400)  →  Gas sensor array (8-32) │
│  Olfactory bulb             →  Signal conditioning      │
│  Piriform cortex            →  Pattern recognition      │
│  Memory (hippocampus)       →  Crossbar memory          │
│                                                         │
│  Key Metrics:                                           │
│  - Response time: 100-400ms (biological) vs <50ms (e)  │
│  - Sensitivity: ppb-ppm range for both                 │
│  - Classification: context-dependent vs trained        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Gas Sensor Types for E-Nose

| Sensor Type | Principle | Response Time | Sensitivity | Power |
|-------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|-------|
| MOX (Metal Oxide) | Resistance change | 1-30s | ppb-ppm | 100mW |
| Conducting Polymer | Resistance change | 1-10s | ppm | 1mW |
| QCM | Mass loading | ms | ng | 10mW |
| SAW | Wave velocity | µs | pg | 50mW |
| Electrochemical | Current | 10-30s | ppb | 1mW |

### Neuromorphic E-Nose with Spiking Neural Networks

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        Spike-Based Olfactory Processing                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│  Gas Sensors           Spike Encoding      Crossbar    │
│  ┌─────────┐          ┌───────────┐      ┌─────────┐  │
│  │ MOX  ○──┼──────────┼→ ║║║ ║║ ║──────→│●●●●●●●●│  │
│  │ MOX  ○──┼──────────┼→ ║║║║║║ ║──────→│●●●●●●●●│  │
│  │ CP   ○──┼──────────┼→ ║ ║║ ║║║──────→│●●●●●●●●│  │
│  │ CP   ○──┼──────────┼→ ║║ ║ ║║ ──────→│●●●●●●●●│  │
│  │ QCM  ○──┼──────────┼→ ║║║║ ║║║──────→│●●●●●●●●│  │
│  │ EC   ○──┼──────────┼→ ║ ║║║ ║ ──────→│●●●●●●●●│  │
│  └─────────┘          └───────────┘      └────┬────┘  │
│                                               │        │
│  Concentration → LIF Neuron → Spike Rate → Weights    │
│                                               ↓        │
│                                        Classification  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Intel Loihi Results:**
- Trained on single sample per odor (vs 3000 for deep learning)
- Detects ammonia, acetone, methane in complex mixtures
- 97% accuracy at <1mW, 0.4ms latency

### High-Speed Miniaturized E-Nose (Science Advances 2024)

Dennler et al. achieved biological-timescale odor sensing:
- Matches animal olfaction timescales
- Enables mobile robotics applications
- Miniaturized form factor

### AI-Powered E-Nose (2025)

Next-generation electronic nose capabilities:
- 95%+ accuracy on 9 fragrances
- Concentration estimation
- Applications: healthcare, cosmetics, environmental

### Memristor-Based Artificial Olfactory System

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

│   │  ●──○──●──○──●──○──●──○  Gasitor │                │

### Electronic Tongue (E-Tongue)

Gustatory neurons detect sour (H⁺) and salty (Na⁺) using ion-sensitive FETs:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        Artificial Gustatory Neuron (Nano Letters 2022)   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│   Extended Gate ISFET Structure                         │
│                                                         │
│   Taste Solution                                        │
│        ↓                                                │
│   ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐              │
│   │ Ion-Sens.  │     │                  │              │
│   │ Membrane   │─────│  MOSFET          │→ Spikes     │
│   │ (Al₂O₃ or  │     │  (Integrator)    │              │
│   │ Ionophore) │     │                  │              │
│   └────────────┘     └──────────────────┘              │
│                                                         │
│   Sensing Materials:                                    │
│   - Al₂O₃: pH sensing (sour, ~58 mV/pH)                │
│   - Na Ionophore X: Na⁺ sensing (salty, ~55 mV/dec)    │
│                                                         │
│   Output: Spike frequency ∝ Ion concentration           │
│   Classification: SNN distinguishes vinegar vs brine    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Five Basic Tastes + Umami

| Taste | Ion/Molecule | Receptor Mechanism | E-Tongue Sensing |
|-------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Sour | H⁺ | Ion channel (ENaC) | pH electrode |
| Salty | Na⁺, K⁺ | Ion channel | ISE membrane |
| Sweet | Sugars | T1R2/T1R3 GPCR | Enzyme electrode |
| Bitter | Alkaloids | T2R GPCR | Lipid membrane |
| Umami | Glutamate | T1R1/T1R3 GPCR | Glutamate oxidase |

### Multi-Sensory Fusion (Flavor)

True flavor perception combines olfaction + gustation:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Multi-Sensory Fusion Architecture              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                         │
│   E-Nose                        E-Tongue                │
│   ┌─────────┐                  ┌─────────┐             │
│   │ Gas     │                  │ Taste   │             │
│   │ Sensors │                  │ Sensors │             │
│   └────┬────┘                  └────┬────┘             │
│        ↓                            ↓                   │
│   ┌─────────┐                  ┌─────────┐             │
│   │ Spike   │                  │ Spike   │             │
│   │ Encoder │                  │ Encoder │             │
│   └────┬────┘                  └────┬────┘             │
│        │                            │                   │
│        └──────────┬─────────────────┘                   │
│                   ↓                                     │
│           ┌─────────────┐                              │
│           │   Fusion    │  Methods:                    │
│           │   Layer     │  - Early concatenation       │
│           │             │  - Late decision             │
│           │             │  - Attention-weighted        │
│           └──────┬──────┘                              │
│                  ↓                                      │
│           ┌─────────────┐                              │
│           │  Crossbar   │                              │
│           │  Classifier │                              │
│           └──────┬──────┘                              │
│                  ↓                                      │
│           Flavor Classification                         │
│           (coffee, wine, tea, etc.)                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CIM Implementation for Sensory Processing

**Advantages of CIM for E-Nose/E-Tongue:**
1. Low power: <1mW total system
2. Edge deployment: No cloud connectivity needed
3. Fast inference: <1ms classification
4. Adaptive: In-memory learning possible

### Implementation Summary

```go
// Electronic nose (cryo_olfactory_cim.go)
type ElectronicNose struct {
    Config        *OlfactoryConfig
    Sensors       []*GasSensor      // MOX, CP, QCM, EC
    Neurons       []*OlfactoryNeuron // LIF spiking
    CrossbarWeights [][]float64     // Pattern memory
    PatternMemory map[string][]float64
}

// Electronic tongue
type ElectronicTongue struct {
    Config    *GustatoryConfig
    Receptors []*TasteReceptor  // ISFET-based
    Neurons   []*GustatoryNeuron
    TastePatterns map[string][]float64
}

// Multi-sensory fusion
type MultiSensorySystem struct {
    ENose      *ElectronicNose
    ETongue    *ElectronicTongue
    FusedPatterns map[string][]float64
    Crossbar   [][]float64  // CIM classifier
}
```

## 368. CIM for Reinforcement Learning

### Overview

Reinforcement learning (RL) presents unique opportunities for compute-in-memory acceleration due to its iterative nature and heavy reliance on matrix-vector operations in value function approximation and policy networks. The temporal difference (TD) learning rule maps naturally to memristive synapses, enabling online weight updates without explicit gradient computation.

### Actor-Critic Architecture with Memristors

Recent work from Nature Machine Intelligence 2025 demonstrates memristors as "multipurpose elements" in actor-critic RL:

```
Actor-Critic TD Learning in CIM
================================

                    Environment
                         │
                         ▼
                   ┌───────────┐
                   │   State   │
                   │    sₜ     │
                   └─────┬─────┘
                         │
           ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
           │                           │
           ▼                           ▼
    ┌─────────────┐             ┌─────────────┐
    │    Actor    │             │   Critic    │
    │  π(a|s;θ)   │             │   V(s;w)    │
    │             │             │             │
    │ ┌─────────┐ │             │ ┌─────────┐ │
    │ │Memristor│ │             │ │Memristor│ │
    │ │Crossbar │ │             │ │Crossbar │ │
    │ │   θ     │ │             │ │    w    │ │
    │ └─────────┘ │             │ └─────────┘ │
    └──────┬──────┘             └──────┬──────┘
           │                           │
           ▼                           ▼
       Action aₜ                   V(sₜ)
           │                           │
           └───────────┬───────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
              ┌────────────────┐
              │   TD Error     │
              │ δ = r + γV(s') │
              │     - V(s)     │
              └────────┬───────┘
                       │
            ┌──────────┴──────────┐
            ▼                     ▼
     Δθ = α·δ·∇logπ        Δw = β·δ·∇V

     (In-memory updates via conductance modulation)
```

### Reward-Modulated STDP (R-STDP)

R-STDP extends STDP with a third factor (reward signal), enabling biologically plausible RL:

```
Three-Factor Learning Rule
===========================

Standard STDP:           ΔW = f(Δt) where Δt = tpost - tpre

R-STDP (Three-Factor):   ΔW = R · e · f(Δt)
                              │   │
                              │   └─ Eligibility trace (decaying memory)
                              └───── Reward signal (global modulator)

Eligibility Trace Dynamics:
  de/dt = -e/τₑ + δ(tpre)·δ(tpost)

  where: τₑ ≈ 100-1000 ms (bridges temporal gap)

Hardware Implementation (Memristor):
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                  │
│   Pre ───┬──[Memristor W]──┬─── Post            │
│          │                 │                     │
│          ▼                 ▼                     │
│    ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐                │
│    │Eligibility│     │  Spike   │                │
│    │ Capacitor │     │ Detector │                │
│    │    Ce     │     │   STDP   │                │
│    └─────┬────┘     └────┬─────┘                │
│          │               │                       │
│          └───────┬───────┘                       │
│                  ▼                               │
│           ┌───────────┐                          │
│           │ Reward ×  │                          │
│           │ Modulator │ ◄─── Global R signal    │
│           └─────┬─────┘                          │
│                 ▼                                │
│           Conductance                            │
│            Update                                │
│                                                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Typical Parameters:
  τₑ = 100-1000 ms
  Learning rate α = 0.01
  Discount factor γ = 0.99
```

### Q-Learning Hardware Accelerator

Dedicated Q-learning accelerators achieve remarkable throughput:

```
Q-Learning CIM Accelerator
===========================

Performance (8×4 crossbar):
  - Throughput: 222 MSPS (mega samples per second)
  - Power: 37 mW
  - Energy: 0.17 nJ/sample

Architecture:
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
                    │         Q-Table Crossbar        │
                    │                                 │
   State ──────────►│  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┐            │
   Encoding         │  │Q₀₀│Q₀₁│Q₀₂│Q₀₃│ ◄── Action │
   (One-hot or      │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┤      0     │
    Binary)         │  │Q₁₀│Q₁₁│Q₁₂│Q₁₃│ ◄── Action │
                    │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┤      1     │
                    │  │Q₂₀│Q₂₁│Q₂₂│Q₂₃│ ◄── Action │
                    │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┤      2     │
                    │  │Q₃₀│Q₃₁│Q₃₂│Q₃₃│ ◄── Action │
                    │  └───┴───┴───┴───┘      3     │
                    └──────────┬──────────────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
                    ┌──────────────────┐
                    │   Max Circuit    │
                    │   (Analog WTA)   │
                    └────────┬─────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
                      Best Action aₜ

Q-Update (Bellman):
  Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α[r + γ·max_a'Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]

In-Memory Update:
  ΔG = α · [r + γ·max(I_out) - I_current]
```

### Deep RL with CIM

For function approximation with deep networks:

```
DQN/PPO with CIM Acceleration
==============================

          ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
          │            Policy/Value Network            │
          │                                            │
   sₜ ───►│  ┌────────┐   ┌────────┐   ┌────────┐    │
          │  │CIM FC  │──►│  ReLU  │──►│CIM FC  │───►│──► Q(s,a)/π(a|s)
          │  │Layer 1 │   │        │   │Layer 2 │    │
          │  │(64×64) │   │        │   │(64×|A|)│    │
          │  └────────┘   └────────┘   └────────┘    │
          │                                            │
          │  Hardware specs (per layer):               │
          │  - Energy: 0.5-2 pJ/MAC                   │
          │  - Latency: 10-50 ns                      │
          │  - On-chip SRAM for replay buffer         │
          └────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Experience Replay with CIM:
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │          Replay Buffer (SRAM)          │
  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │ (s₀,a₀,r₀,s'₀) │ ... │ (sₙ,...)  │ │
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                    │                   │
  │     Random Sample ─┘                   │
  │          │                             │
  │          ▼                             │
  │   CIM Forward + Backward               │
  └────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Summary

```go
// RL CIM structures (rl_3dchiplet_cim.go)
type ActorCriticCIM struct {
    Config         *RLConfig
    ActorWeights   [][]*MemristorSynapse  // Policy network
    CriticWeights  [][]*MemristorSynapse  // Value network
    TDError        float64
    ValueEstimate  float64
    Stats          *RLStats
}

type RSTDPSynapse struct {
    Weight           float64
    EligibilityTrace float64
    Config           *RSTDPConfig  // τe, α, window
}

type QLearningAccelerator struct {
    Config      *QLearningConfig
    QTable      [][]float64         // |S|×|A|
    Crossbar    [][]*MemristorSynapse
    WTACircuit  *WinnerTakeAll      // Analog max
    Stats       *QLearningStats     // 222 MSPS, 37mW
}

// Example benchmarks
func BenchmarkRLCIM() {
    // Actor-critic: CartPole in ~50 episodes
    // Q-learning: GridWorld 10×10 in <100 episodes
    // Throughput: 222M state-action evaluations/sec
}
```

### References

- [Actor-Critic Memristor - Nature Machine Intelligence 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-025-01023-7)
- [R-STDP Survey - Frontiers 2024](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnins.2018.00435/full)
- [Q-Learning Accelerator - IEEE JSSC 2024](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10542651)
- [DRL Hardware Survey - ACM Computing Surveys 2025](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3639476)
