## 387. In-Situ Training and Adaptation (Iteration 145)

### Overview

In-situ training enables on-chip weight updates without requiring external processing. This is critical for edge adaptation, continual learning, and autonomous systems. Recent advances in memristor-based training have achieved near-ideal weight transfer with >60% accuracy improvement over traditional backpropagation.

### All-in-One Analog AI Accelerator (2025)

IBM/Université Paris-Saclay demonstrated the first unified platform for on-chip training + inference:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ALL-IN-ONE ANALOG AI ACCELERATOR                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Technology: CMO/HfOₓ ReRAM in 130nm BEOL                                  │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│  │                    Training Pipeline                              │      │
│  │                                                                   │      │
│  │  Input ──► Forward Pass ──► Loss ──► Backward Pass ──► Update    │      │
│  │              │                            │               │       │      │
│  │              ▼                            ▼               ▼       │      │
│  │         [ReRAM Array]              [Gradient Comp]   [Open-Loop]  │      │
│  │              │                            │           Pulse Update│      │
│  │              ▼                            │               │       │      │
│  │         Output                           Error──────────►│       │      │
│  │                                                          │       │      │
│  │                                    O(1) Time Complexity──┘       │      │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                                             │
│  Key Performance Metrics:                                                   │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ Multi-bit capability:      >32 stable states (5+ bits)        │        │
│  │ Programming noise:         10 nS (record-low)                  │        │
│  │ Weight transfer quality:   10× better than other memristors   │        │
│  │ RMS error @ 1s:            0.06                                │        │
│  │ RMS error @ 10 years:      0.2                                 │        │
│  │ Array size demonstrated:   64×64                               │        │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Error-Aware Probabilistic Update (EaPU)

Traditional backpropagation conflicts with stochastic memristor updates. EaPU bridges this gap:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                ERROR-AWARE PROBABILISTIC UPDATE (EaPU)                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Problem: Device write noise σ conflicts with deterministic BP              │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  Gradient ────► Probability ────► Update Decision                  │    │
│  │     g              P(g,σ)            Yes/No                        │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  P(update) = σ(|g|/σ - θ)   where σ = sigmoid                     │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐         │    │
│  │  │    1.0 ─┐                                            │         │    │
│  │  │        │     ╱──────────────                         │         │    │
│  │  │        │    ╱                                        │         │    │
│  │  │  P(u) │   ╱                                          │         │    │
│  │  │        │  ╱                                          │         │    │
│  │  │        │ ╱                                           │         │    │
│  │  │    0.0 ─────────────────────────────────────────     │         │    │
│  │  │         0      σ     2σ     3σ                       │         │    │
│  │  │                    |g| / σ                           │         │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘         │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  If |g| << σ: Low probability → Skip update (save energy)         │    │
│  │  If |g| >> σ: High probability → Update (meaningful change)       │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                             │
│  Results (180nm memristor system):                                          │
│  - >60% accuracy improvement vs. standard BP                                │
│  - ~50.54× lower training energy                                            │
│  - <0.1% of weight updates vs. full BP                                      │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Multi-Tile Residual Learning

For devices with limited conductance states (~4 bits), multi-tile residual learning achieves high precision:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    MULTI-TILE RESIDUAL LEARNING                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Target: 8-bit precision   Device: 4-bit only                              │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    Weight Decomposition                            │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  W_target = W_tile1 + W_tile2 + W_tile3 + residual                │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  Scale:     1.0        0.5        0.25                            │    │
│  │             MSB       Middle      LSB                              │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  ┌─────────────┬─────────────┬─────────────┬─────────────┐        │    │
│  │  │   Tile 1    │   Tile 2    │   Tile 3    │   Residual  │        │    │
│  │  │   4-bit     │   4-bit     │   4-bit     │   (error)   │        │    │
│  │  │   ×1.0      │   ×0.5      │   ×0.25     │             │        │    │
│  │  └─────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────┘        │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  Training:                                                         │    │
│  │  1. Update Tile 1 with gradient g                                  │    │
│  │  2. Compute residual r₁ = g - ΔW₁                                 │    │
│  │  3. Update Tile 2 with residual r₁                                │    │
│  │  4. Compute residual r₂ = r₁ - ΔW₂                                │    │
│  │  5. Continue until all tiles updated                               │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                             │
│  Advantages:                                                                │
│  - No auxiliary CMOS components required                                    │
│  - Fully asymmetric updates supported                                       │
│  - Simple open-loop transfer between tiles                                  │
│  - Effective precision: NumTiles × BitsPerTile                             │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Forward-Forward Algorithm (BP-Free Training)

The Forward-Forward algorithm eliminates backpropagation entirely:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FORWARD-FORWARD ALGORITHM                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Key Idea: Train each layer locally using "goodness" metric                 │
│                                                                             │
│  Goodness G = Σ(activation²)                                               │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    Training Process                                │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  POSITIVE DATA (real):                                             │    │
│  │  ┌──────┐    ┌──────┐    ┌──────┐                                 │    │
│  │  │Input │──►│Layer1│──►│Layer2│──► ...                            │    │
│  │  │+label│    │      │    │      │                                  │    │
│  │  └──────┘    └──────┘    └──────┘                                 │    │
│  │              G₁ > θ?    G₂ > θ?     ← Want: YES (high goodness)   │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  NEGATIVE DATA (corrupted label):                                  │    │
│  │  ┌──────┐    ┌──────┐    ┌──────┐                                 │    │
│  │  │Input │──►│Layer1│──►│Layer2│──► ...                            │    │
│  │  │-label│    │      │    │      │                                  │    │
│  │  └──────┘    └──────┘    └──────┘                                 │    │
│  │              G₁ < θ?    G₂ < θ?     ← Want: NO (low goodness)     │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  Local Update Rule:                                                │    │
│  │  - Positive: Δw ∝ +2·activation·input (increase goodness)         │    │
│  │  - Negative: Δw ∝ -2·activation·input (decrease goodness)         │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                             │
│  Hardware Advantages:                                                       │
│  - No gradient backpropagation needed                                       │
│  - Each layer trains independently                                          │
│  - Simpler than BP: no chain rule, no stored activations                   │
│  - Naturally suited for analog/memristor implementation                     │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Open-Loop Weight Update Scheme

Open-loop updates eliminate feedback sensing during programming:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    OPEN-LOOP WEIGHT UPDATE                                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Traditional (Closed-Loop):           Open-Loop:                            │
│  ┌────────────────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ Write → Read → Compare →   │      │ Write → Done               │        │
│  │ Adjust → Write → Read → ...│      │ (no verify)                │        │
│  └────────────────────────────┘      └────────────────────────────┘        │
│  ~10× slower, higher energy          Fast, low energy                       │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                    Programming Model                               │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  SET (Potentiation):                                              │    │
│  │  ΔG = α · V · (1 - (G-Gmin)/(Gmax-Gmin))^n                       │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  RESET (Depression):                                              │    │
│  │  ΔG = -β · V · ((G-Gmin)/(Gmax-Gmin))^n                          │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  │  Where:                                                           │    │
│  │    α, β = device-specific slopes (typically α ≠ β, asymmetric)   │    │
│  │    V = pulse amplitude                                            │    │
│  │    n = nonlinearity exponent (~2 for most devices)               │    │
│  │    Gmin, Gmax = conductance range                                 │    │
│  │                                                                    │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                             │
│  CMO/HfOₓ Results:                                                         │
│  - Successful analog switching with open-loop identical pulses              │
│  - Device model accounts for:                                               │
│    * Non-linear switching                                                   │
│    * Asymmetric SET/RESET                                                   │
│    * Inter-device variability                                               │
│    * Intra-device cycle-to-cycle variation                                  │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation

```go
// Error-aware probabilistic update trainer
type EaPUTrainer struct {
    Config      *EaPUConfig
    Weights     [][]float64
    Statistics  *EaPUStats
}

// Compute update probability based on gradient and device noise
func (et *EaPUTrainer) ComputeUpdateProbability(gradient float64) float64 {
    gradMag := math.Abs(gradient)
    snr := gradMag / et.Config.WriteNoiseSigma

    // Sigmoid probability: high gradient → high update probability
    prob := 1.0 / (1.0 + math.Exp(-snr + 2.0))

    // Apply threshold for very small gradients
    if gradMag < et.Config.UpdateThreshold {
        prob *= et.Config.ProbabilityDecay
    }

    return prob
}

// Probabilistic weight update
func (et *EaPUTrainer) UpdateWeights(gradients [][]float64) {
    for i := range gradients {
        for j := range gradients[i] {
            prob := et.ComputeUpdateProbability(gradients[i][j])

            if rand.Float64() < prob {
                // Apply update with device noise
                noise := rand.NormFloat64() * et.Config.WriteNoiseSigma
                et.Weights[i][j] -= et.Config.LearningRate*gradients[i][j] + noise
                et.Statistics.TotalUpdates++
            } else {
                et.Statistics.SkippedUpdates++
            }
        }
    }
}

// Multi-tile residual learning
type MultiTileTrainer struct {
    NumTiles     int
    BitsPerTile  int
    TileWeights  [][][]float64
    TileScales   []float64  // [1.0, 0.5, 0.25, ...]
    Residuals    [][]float64
}

func (mtt *MultiTileTrainer) DecomposeWeights(target [][]float64) {
    // Initialize residual with target
    copy(mtt.Residuals, target)

    for t := 0; t < mtt.NumTiles; t++ {
        levels := float64(1 << mtt.BitsPerTile)

        for i := range mtt.Residuals {
            for j := range mtt.Residuals[i] {
                // Quantize residual to tile precision
                normalized := mtt.Residuals[i][j] / mtt.TileScales[t]
                quantized := math.Round(normalized * (levels-1) / 2)

                // Store in tile
                mtt.TileWeights[t][i][j] = quantized * mtt.TileScales[t] * 2 / (levels-1)

                // Update residual for next tile
                mtt.Residuals[i][j] -= mtt.TileWeights[t][i][j]
            }
        }
    }
}

// Forward-Forward trainer (BP-free)
type ForwardForwardTrainer struct {
    Config     *FFConfig
    Weights    [][][]float64
    Threshold  float64  // Goodness threshold
}

func (fft *ForwardForwardTrainer) TrainLayer(layerIdx int, posInput, negInput []float64) {
    // Compute goodness for positive sample (want high)
    posOutput := fft.Forward(layerIdx, posInput)
    posGoodness := fft.ComputeGoodness(posOutput)

    // Compute goodness for negative sample (want low)
    negOutput := fft.Forward(layerIdx, negInput)
    negGoodness := fft.ComputeGoodness(negOutput)

    // Local update: no backprop needed!
    for i := range fft.Weights[layerIdx] {
        for j := range fft.Weights[layerIdx][i] {
            // Increase goodness for positive
            if posGoodness < fft.Threshold {
                fft.Weights[layerIdx][i][j] += 2 * posOutput[i] * posInput[j]
            }
            // Decrease goodness for negative
            if negGoodness > fft.Threshold {
                fft.Weights[layerIdx][i][j] -= 2 * negOutput[i] * negInput[j]
            }
        }
    }
}

func (fft *ForwardForwardTrainer) ComputeGoodness(activations []float64) float64 {
    goodness := 0.0
    for _, a := range activations {
        goodness += a * a
    }
    return goodness
}
```

### Comparison of In-Situ Training Methods

| Method | Precision | Energy | Complexity | Accuracy |
|--------|-----------|--------|------------|----------|
| Standard BP | High | High | High | Baseline |
| EaPU | Medium | 50× lower | Medium | +60% |
| Multi-Tile Residual | High (n×4) | Low | Medium | ~BP |
| Forward-Forward | Medium | Very Low | Low | -2-5% |
| Open-Loop | Medium | Lowest | Lowest | -5-10% |

## 388. Emerging Memory Technologies: FTJ and NDR (Iteration 146)

### Overview

Ferroelectric Tunnel Junctions (FTJs) and Negative Differential Resistance (NDR) devices represent the next generation of neuromorphic memory technologies. FTJs offer non-filamentary switching with multi-level conductance for synaptic applications, while NDR devices enable compact neuron implementations without external capacitors.

### Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction (FTJ) Memristors

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FTJ DEVICE STRUCTURE AND OPERATION                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │                        Pt (Top Electrode)                          │     │
│  │═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════│     │
│  │                                                                    │     │
│  │    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │     │
│  │    │             Hf₀.₅Zr₀.₅O₂ (Ferroelectric)               │    │     │
│  │    │                    4.5 nm                                │    │     │
│  │    │    ↑↑↑↑↑↑↑↑  or  ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓                              │    │     │
│  │    │   (P+ state)     (P- state)                              │    │     │
│  │    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │     │
│  │                                                                    │     │
│  │    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │     │
│  │    │               TiO₂ (Interlayer)                         │    │     │
│  │    │                    2 nm                                  │    │     │
│  │    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │     │
│  │                                                                    │     │
│  │═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════│     │
│  │                        TiN (Bottom Electrode)                      │     │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
│                                                                             │
│  Tunneling Electroresistance (TER):                                         │
│  - P+ state: High barrier → Low conductance (HRS)                          │
│  - P- state: Low barrier → High conductance (LRS)                          │
│  - TER ratio = G_LRS / G_HRS                                               │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HfO2/ZrO2-Based FTJ Performance (2025)

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| TER Ratio | 580-834 | Record: epitaxial HZO |
| ON/OFF Ratio | 5.8×10² | Pt/HZO/TiO2/TiN |
| Switching Speed | 20-50 ns | High-speed operation |
| Endurance | 2×10⁸ cycles | With TiO2 interlayer |
| Retention | >10⁵ s | Room temperature |
| Conductance States | 128 | Symmetric programming |
| Cycle Variation | 2.75% | Low C2C variation |
| Speech Recognition | 97.6% | CNN accuracy |

### FTJ as Artificial Synapse

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FTJ ARTIFICIAL SYNAPSE CHARACTERISTICS                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Multi-Level Conductance Programming:                                       │
│                                                                             │
│  Conductance                                                                │
│       │                                                                     │
│  G_max├─────────────────────────────────────────────● 128                  │
│       │                                         ●                           │
│       │                                     ●                               │
│       │                                 ●                                   │
│       │                             ●                                       │
│       │                         ●       ← 128 symmetric states             │
│       │                     ●                                               │
│       │                 ●                                                   │
│       │             ●                                                       │
│       │         ●                                                           │
│  G_min├─────●                                                    1          │
│       └─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────► State              │
│             0    16    32    48    64    80    96   112  128               │
│                                                                             │
│  Key Advantages:                                                            │
│  - Non-filamentary switching → Better uniformity                           │
│  - Area-scalable → 4F² cell size                                           │
│  - Low write energy → Sub-pJ switching                                      │
│  - CMOS compatible → HfO2 already in CMOS                                  │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### FTJ Crossbar Array (2025)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    48×48 FTJ CROSSBAR DEMONSTRATION                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Array Specifications:                                                       │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │ Array size:         48 × 48 = 2,304 cells                     │        │
│  │ Programming:        3-bit (8 levels) per cell                  │        │
│  │ Half-bias:          V/2 scheme for unselected cells           │        │
│  │ Interference:       None observed (unselected stable)          │        │
│  │ Uniformity:         Clearly separated states across array      │        │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                             │
│  Half-Bias Operation:                                                       │
│       WL1  WL2  WL3  ...  WL48                                             │
│        │    │    │         │                                                │
│   BL1──●────●────●────...──●    ● = FTJ device                             │
│        │    │    │         │                                                │
│   BL2──●────●────●────...──●    Selected: V_write on WL, GND on BL         │
│        │    │    │         │    Unselected: V/2 on WL, V/2 on BL           │
│   ...  │    │    │         │                                                │
│        │    │    │         │    Result: Only selected cell sees full V     │
│   BL48─●────●────●────...──●    Half-selected cells see V/2 (no switch)    │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### NDR (Negative Differential Resistance) Memristors

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    NDR MEMRISTOR I-V CHARACTERISTIC                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Current (I)                                                                │
│       │                                                                     │
│       │        ●  Peak (Ip, Vp)                                            │
│       │       ╱ ╲                                                           │
│       │      ╱   ╲                                                          │
│       │     ╱     ╲  ← NDR Region (negative slope)                         │
│       │    ╱       ╲                                                        │
│       │   ╱         ●  Valley (Iv, Vv)                                     │
│       │  ╱           ╲                                                      │
│       │ ╱             ╲                                                     │
│       │╱               ╲________                                            │
│       └─────────────────────────────────► Voltage (V)                       │
│              Vp      Vv                                                     │
│                                                                             │
│  Device: AlAs/In₀.₈Ga₀.₂As/AlAs Quantum Well                               │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│  │ Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio (PVCR):  10                    │           │
│  │ Endurance:                            10¹¹ cycles @ RT      │           │
│  │ High-temp endurance:                  10⁹ cycles @ 400°C    │           │
│  │ Device variation:                     0.264%                 │           │
│  │ Lifetime @ 10 Hz:                     >310 years             │           │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### NDR-Based Neuron Circuits

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FITZHUGH-NAGUMO NEURON WITH NDR                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐      │
│  │                   NDR-Based FHN Circuit                           │      │
│  │                                                                   │      │
│  │   V_in ──┬──────────────────────────────────────────┬── V_out    │      │
│  │          │                                          │             │      │
│  │          │    ┌─────────┐                          │             │      │
│  │          ├────│   NDR   │────┤                     │             │      │
│  │          │    │ Device  │                          │             │      │
│  │          │    └─────────┘                          │             │      │
│  │          │                                          │             │      │
│  │          │    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐           │             │      │
│  │          └────│   L     │────│    R    │───────────┘             │      │
│  │               │ (MOSFET)│    │Recovery │                         │      │
│  │               └─────────┘    └─────────┘                         │      │
│  │                                                                   │      │
│  │   NDR provides:  Integration function of membrane                │      │
│  │   L (inductor): Active inductive element via MOSFET              │      │
│  │   Result:       No external capacitor needed!                    │      │
│  │                                                                   │      │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘      │
│                                                                             │
│  Nine Neuron Functions Demonstrated:                                        │
│  1. Tonic spiking          6. Subthreshold oscillation                     │
│  2. Phasic spiking         7. Resonator                                    │
│  3. Tonic bursting         8. Integrator                                   │
│  4. Phasic bursting        9. Bistability                                  │
│  5. Mixed mode                                                              │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### FTJ for Leaky-Integrate-and-Fire Neurons

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FTJ-BASED LIF NEURON                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Concept: Use FTJ accumulative switching + depolarization                   │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│  │                                                                 │        │
│  │   Input Pulses ──► FTJ ──► Polarization Accumulation           │        │
│  │                      │                                          │        │
│  │                      ▼                                          │        │
│  │            ┌─────────────────┐                                  │        │
│  │            │  Polarization   │                                  │        │
│  │            │  accumulates    │                                  │        │
│  │            │  with pulses    │                                  │        │
│  │            └────────┬────────┘                                  │        │
│  │                     │                                           │        │
│  │                     ▼                                           │        │
│  │            [Threshold Check]                                    │        │
│  │                     │                                           │        │
│  │           ┌────────┴────────┐                                   │        │
│  │           │                 │                                   │        │
│  │      P < Pth           P > Pth                                  │        │
│  │           │                 │                                   │        │
│  │           ▼                 ▼                                   │        │
│  │    [Natural Leak]    [Fire Spike]                               │        │
│  │    (depolarization)  [Reset P]                                  │        │
│  │                                                                 │        │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                                             │
│  Advantages:                                                                │
│  - Single FTJ implements full LIF neuron                                   │
│  - No external capacitor needed                                             │
│  - 37 fJ/spike demonstrated (antiferroelectric)                            │
│  - Dense integration of neurons + synapses on single chip                  │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation

```go
// FTJ memristor device
type FTJMemristor struct {
    Config            *FTJConfig
    Polarization      float64   // Current polarization (-1 to +1)
    Conductance       float64   // Current conductance (S)
    ProgrammedStates  []float64 // Available conductance levels
    CurrentState      int       // Current state index
}

// Program to specific state
func (ftj *FTJMemristor) ProgramState(targetState int) error {
    targetConductance := ftj.ProgrammedStates[targetState]

    // Apply programming with variation
    variation := rand.NormFloat64() * ftj.Config.CycleVariation / 100
    ftj.Conductance = targetConductance * (1 + variation)
    ftj.CurrentState = targetState

    // Update polarization
    ftj.Polarization = 2*float64(targetState)/float64(levels-1) - 1

    return nil
}

// NDR memristor for neurons
type NDRMemristor struct {
    Config       *NDRConfig
    Voltage      float64
    Current      float64
    State        string // "peak", "valley", "ndr"
}

func (ndr *NDRMemristor) ApplyVoltage(voltage float64) float64 {
    vp := ndr.Config.PeakVoltage
    vv := ndr.Config.ValleyVoltage
    ip := ndr.Config.PeakCurrent
    iv := ndr.Config.ValleyCurrent

    if voltage < vp {
        // Pre-peak: positive slope
        return ip * voltage / vp
    } else if voltage < vv {
        // NDR region: negative slope
        ratio := (voltage - vp) / (vv - vp)
        return ip - (ip-iv)*ratio
    } else {
        // Post-valley: positive slope
        return iv + (voltage-vv)*iv/vv
    }
}
```

## 389. Neuromorphic Integration for CIM (Iteration 146)

### Overview

Neuromorphic computing integrates biological neural principles with compute-in-memory architectures. The convergence of IMC and neuromorphic systems enables energy-efficient, scalable AI for edge applications. Recent advances include memristive SNNs, on-chip STDP learning, and wafer-scale crossbar fabrication.

### Memristive Spiking Neural Network Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    MEMRISTIVE SNN ARCHITECTURE                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                      SNN Processing Core                             │   │
│  │                                                                      │   │
│  │  Input Layer        Crossbar Synapses        Hidden Layer           │   │
│  │  (784 neurons)                               (800 neurons)          │   │
│  │                                                                      │   │
│  │    ○ ○ ○ ○         ┌─────────────┐            ○ ○ ○ ○               │   │
│  │    │ │ │ │    ────►│ Memristor   │────►       │ │ │ │               │   │
│  │    │ │ │ │         │ Crossbar    │            │ │ │ │               │   │
│  │    ○ ○ ○ ○         │ 784×800     │            ○ ○ ○ ○               │   │
│  │    │ │ │ │         │             │            │ │ │ │               │   │
│  │    │ │ │ │    ────►│  [G_ij]     │────►       │ │ │ │               │   │
│  │    ○ ○ ○ ○         │             │            ○ ○ ○ ○               │   │
│  │      ...           └─────────────┘              ...                  │   │
│  │                                                                      │   │
│  │  Encoding:         Weight Storage:             LIF Neurons:          │   │
│  │  Rate/Temporal     Analog G states             Leaky I&F             │   │
│  │                                                                      │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                             │
│  Crossbar Details:                                                          │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Configuration:     1T1R or passive                                  │   │

│  │ Weight precision:  1-6 bits analog                                   │   │

└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) Neuron Model

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    LIF NEURON DYNAMICS                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Membrane Equation:                                                         │
│  τ_m · dV/dt = -(V - V_rest) + R_m · I_input                               │
│                                                                             │
│  Where:                                                                     │
│  - τ_m = C_m / g_leak (membrane time constant)                             │
│  - V_rest = resting potential (-70 mV)                                     │
│  - R_m = membrane resistance                                               │
│  - I_input = synaptic current                                              │
│                                                                             │
│  Membrane Potential                                                         │
│       │                                                                     │
│  V_th ├─────────────────────────●───────────────────────────────           │
│       │                        ╱│╲                                          │
│       │                       ╱ │ ╲     ← Spike!                           │
│       │                      ╱  │  ╲                                        │
│       │                     ╱   │   ╲                                       │
│       │                    ╱    │    ╲                                      │
│       │             ●─────╱     │     ●────────                             │
│       │            ╱            │           ╲                               │
│       │           ╱             │            ╲                              │
│  V_rest├─────────●──────────────┼─────────────●────────────────            │
│       │        Input           Reset        Refractory                      │
│       │        spikes          to V_rest    period                          │
│       └─────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────► Time          │
│                                                                             │
│  Memristor Implementation:                                                  │
│  - Volatile memristor mimics leaky membrane                                │
│  - Conductivity decay = membrane leak                                       │
│  - Threshold switching = spike generation                                   │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### STDP (Spike-Timing Dependent Plasticity)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STDP LEARNING RULE                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Weight Change (ΔW)                                                         │
│       │                                                                     │
│       │    LTP (Potentiation)                                              │
│ +A⁺   │    ●                                                                │
│       │     ●                                                               │
│       │      ●                                                              │
│       │       ●                                                             │
│       │        ●                                                            │
│   0───┼─────────●──────────●─────────── Δt = t_post - t_pre                │
│       │                     ●                                               │
│       │                      ●                                              │
│       │                       ●                                             │
│       │                        ●                                            │
│ -A⁻   │                         ●  LTD (Depression)                        │
│       │                                                                     │
│       └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────►              │
│              -50ms         0         +50ms                                  │
│                                                                             │
│  Mathematical Form:                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│  │ If Δt > 0 (post after pre):                                 │           │
│  │   ΔW = A⁺ · exp(-Δt / τ⁺)  → LTP (strengthen)              │           │
│  │                                                              │           │
│  │ If Δt < 0 (pre after post):                                 │           │
│  │   ΔW = -A⁻ · exp(Δt / τ⁻)  → LTD (weaken)                  │           │
│  │                                                              │           │
│  │ Typical parameters:                                          │           │
│  │   A⁺ = 0.01, A⁻ = 0.012                                     │           │
│  │   τ⁺ = τ⁻ = 20 ms                                           │           │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                             │
│  Hardware Implementation:                                                   │
│  - Pre-spike: Increase conductance if post-trace high                      │
│  - Post-spike: Decrease conductance if pre-trace high                      │
│  - Trace decay: Exponential with τ time constant                           │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

```
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Threshold-Switching Memristors for SNNs

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    THRESHOLD-SWITCHING MEMRISTOR NEURONS                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Spiking Behaviors Emulated:                                                │
│                                                                             │
│  1. Oscillatory Spiking        2. LIF Dynamics                             │
│     ┌──┐  ┌──┐  ┌──┐              │    ●                                   │
│     │  │  │  │  │  │              │   /│                                    │
│  ───┘  └──┘  └──┘  └──           │  / │                                    │
│     Regular spikes               │ /  └──●                                  │
│                                  └───────────                               │
│  3. Stochastic Dynamics        4. Hodgkin-Huxley                           │
│     ┌┐ ┌──┐  ┌┐                   │  ●                                     │
│     ││ │  │  ││                   │ ╱│╲                                     │
│  ───┘└─┘  └──┘└──                │╱ │ ╲_●                                  │
│     Random intervals              └───────────                              │
│                                  Action potential                           │
│                                                                             │
│  Key Advantages:                                                            │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐           │
│  │ Intrinsic spiking dynamics (no external circuit needed)     │           │
│  │ Sub-pJ energy consumption per spike                         │           │
│  │ Nanoscale footprint for dense integration                   │           │
│  │ STDP naturally implemented via device physics               │           │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation

```go
// LIF neuron implementation
type LIFNeuron struct {
    Config            *LIFNeuronConfig
    MembranePotential float64 // Current membrane potential (mV)
    LastSpikeTime     float64 // Time of last spike (ms)
    InRefractory      bool
    TimeConstantMs    float64 // τ = C/g
}

func (lif *LIFNeuron) Step(inputCurrent float64, currentTimeMs, dtMs float64) bool {
    // Check refractory period
    if currentTimeMs - lif.LastSpikeTime < lif.Config.RefractoryPeriodMs {
        return false
    }

    // LIF dynamics
    resistance := 1.0 / lif.Config.LeakConductanceNs * 1000 // MOhm
    dV := (lif.Config.RestingPotentialMv - lif.MembranePotential +
           inputCurrent*resistance) / lif.TimeConstantMs

    lif.MembranePotential += dV * dtMs

    // Check for spike
    if lif.MembranePotential >= lif.Config.ThresholdMv {
        lif.MembranePotential = lif.Config.ResetPotentialMv
        lif.LastSpikeTime = currentTimeMs
        return true // Spike!
    }

    return false
}

// STDP synapse implementation
type STDPSynapse struct {
    Config    *STDPConfig
    Weight    float64
    PreTrace  float64
    PostTrace float64
}

func (syn *STDPSynapse) PreSpike(timeMs float64) {
    syn.PreTrace = 1.0

    // LTD: check for recent post-spike
    // ΔW = -A⁻ · exp(-Δt/τ⁻)
}

func (syn *STDPSynapse) PostSpike(timeMs float64) {
    syn.PostTrace = 1.0

    // LTP: check for recent pre-spike
    // ΔW = +A⁺ · exp(-Δt/τ⁺)
}

// SNN crossbar with STDP
type SNNCrossbar struct {
    Config      *SNNCrossbarConfig
    InputLayer  []*LIFNeuron
    HiddenLayer []*LIFNeuron
    OutputLayer []*LIFNeuron
    Synapses    [][]*STDPSynapse
    EnableSTDP  bool
}

func (snn *SNNCrossbar) ForwardPass(inputSpikes []bool, timeMs, dtMs float64) []bool {
    // Propagate input spikes through crossbar
    // Update neuron states
    // Apply STDP if enabled
    // Return output spikes
}
```

## Iteration 146 Summary

### Research Completed
1. ✅ FTJ (Ferroelectric Tunnel Junction) memristors for CIM
2. ✅ HZO-based FTJ: TER ratio 580-834, 128 conductance states
3. ✅ FTJ crossbar arrays: 48×48 demonstrated with 3-bit programming
4. ✅ FTJ for speech recognition: 97.6% accuracy
5. ✅ NDR (Negative Differential Resistance) memristors
6. ✅ NDR-based Fitzhugh-Nagumo neurons (9 neuron functions)
7. ✅ Ultra-robust NDR: 10¹¹ cycles, 0.264% variation
8. ✅ Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neuron implementation
9. ✅ STDP learning rule for on-chip plasticity
10. ✅ TEXEL neuromorphic processor with BiCaLL learning
11. ✅ Wafer-scale memristive crossbar (>95% yield)
12. ✅ Threshold-switching memristors for SNNs

### Code Artifacts
- `demo2-inference/pkg/layers/emerging_neuromorphic_cim.go` (~1100 lines)
  - FTJMemristor with multi-level conductance
  - FTJCrossbar array with half-bias operation
  - NDRMemristor with I-V characteristics
  - FHNNeuron (Fitzhugh-Nagumo with NDR)
  - LIFNeuron (Leaky Integrate-and-Fire)
  - STDPSynapse with trace-based learning
  - SNNCrossbar with STDP-enabled training
  - NeuromorphicCIMSystem (integrated)

| FTJ speech recognition | 97.6% accuracy |
| NDR endurance | 10¹¹ cycles @ RT |
| NDR high-temp endurance | 10⁹ cycles @ 400°C |
| NDR device variation | 0.264% |
| NDR lifetime @ 10 Hz | >310 years |
| SNN energy per SOP | 0.35 pJ |
| SNN inference speed | 2 μs/image |
| Wafer-scale yield | >95% |
| Antiferroelectric neuron | 37 fJ/spike |

### Key References Added
- [FTJ Crossbar Array - Adv. Intelligent Systems 2025](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aisy.202500817)
- [HZO FTJ Speech Recognition - ACS AMI 2025](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsami.5c01547)
- [Ultra Robust NDR Memristor - Nature Comms 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-55293-9)
- [TEXEL Processor - Nature Comms 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-61576-6)
- [Memristors for IMC and SNNs - Adv. Intelligent Systems 2025](https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aisy.202500806)
- [Wafer-Scale Crossbars - Nature Comms 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-63831-2)

## 390. Edge Deployment Case Studies for CIM

### Overview

Edge AI deployment with Compute-in-Memory represents one of the most promising
application domains for ferroelectric and memristive CIM technologies. The
combination of ultra-low power operation, high parallelism, and in-situ
computation makes CIM ideal for resource-constrained edge devices.

### Edge AI Market Context

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Edge AI Market Projection 2025-2030                  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  Market Size ($ Billion)                                                │
│  300 ┤                                                           ○      │
│      │                                                      ○           │
│  250 ┤                                                 ○                │
│      │                                            ○                     │
│  200 ┤                                       ○                          │
│      │                                  ○                               │
│  150 ┤                             ○                                    │
│      │                        ○                                         │
│  100 ┤                   ○                                              │
│      │              ○                                                   │
│   50 ┤         ○                                                        │
│      │    ○                                                             │
│    0 ┼────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬────┬───────────────│
│         2021 2022 2023 2024 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030              │
│                                                                         │
│  2025 Market: $26.1 billion                                             │

│                                                                         │

└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Case Study 1: IoT Sensor Nodes with In-Sensor CIM

**Application:** Smart Agriculture - Crop Health Monitoring

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              In-Sensor CIM for Agricultural IoT                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                        │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐             │
│  │  Image   │───▶│  In-Sensor CIM   │───▶│   Decision   │             │
│  │  Sensor  │    │    Processing    │    │    Output    │             │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └──────────────┘             │
│       │                   │                     │                      │
│       ▼                   ▼                     ▼                      │
│  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐             │
│  │ 96×96×3  │    │  FeFET Crossbar  │    │  Alert/Log   │             │
│  │  pixels  │    │    256×256       │    │   Transmit   │             │
│  └──────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └──────────────┘             │
│                                                                        │
│  Power Budget: <1 mW                                                   │
│  Battery Life: 5+ years (coin cell)                                    │
│  Accuracy: 85% crop disease detection                                  │
│  Latency: <100 ms per image                                            │
│  Duty Cycle: 1% (wake-on-trigger)                                      │
│                                                                        │
│  Key Advantage: No ADC required for near-sensor compute               │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Specifications:**
- Power: 0.5-1.0 mW active, <1 μW sleep
- Memory: 64 KB SRAM + 256 KB NVM (weights)
- Processing: 0.1-1.0 GOPS
- Communication: LoRa/NB-IoT (<1 kbps)
- Battery: 500 mWh (CR2032 coin cell)

### Edge Device Power Comparison

| Device Type | Power Budget | CIM Advantage | Battery Life |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|--------------|
| IoT Sensor | 1 mW | 100× vs MCU | 5+ years |
| Wearable | 10 mW | 50× vs DSP | 2-7 days |
| Smart Home | 100 mW | 20× vs CPU | Wall powered |
| Industrial | 500 mW | 30× vs GPU | Powered |
| Automotive | 1000 mW | 10× vs GPU | Vehicle power |
| Biomedical | 0.5 mW | 1000× vs GPU | 10 years |

### Implementation Code Reference

```go
// From edge_codesign_cim.go

// Edge deployment configuration
type EdgeDeploymentConfig struct {
    DeviceType        EdgeDeviceType
    PowerBudgetMW     float64
    LatencyBudgetMs   float64
    MemoryBudgetKB    float64
    AccuracyThreshold float64
    BatteryCapacityMWh float64
    DutyCyclePercent  float64
    PrivacyRequired   bool
    RealTimeRequired  bool
    EnvironmentalTemp float64
}

// IoT sensor with in-sensor CIM
type IoTSensorNode struct {
    Config           *EdgeDeploymentConfig
    CIMArray         *InSensorCIMArray
    SensorType       string
    SamplingRateHz   float64
    PreprocessStages []PreprocessStage
    InferenceModel   *EdgeNeuralNetwork
    BatteryLevel     float64
}

// Wearable health device
type WearableHealthDevice struct {
    Config           *EdgeDeploymentConfig
    CIMProcessor     *WearableCIMProcessor  // 50 TOPS/W
    Sensors          []WearableSensor
    HealthModels     []*HealthInferenceModel
    PrivacyModule    *OnDevicePrivacy
}

// Automotive edge system
type AutomotiveEdgeSystem struct {
    Config           *EdgeDeploymentConfig
    CIMAccelerator   *AutomotiveCIMAccelerator  // 100 TOPS, ASIL-D
    VisionPipeline   *VisionProcessingPipeline
    SensorFusion     *MultiSensorFusion
    SafetyMonitor    *FunctionalSafetyMonitor
}
```

## 391. Hardware-Software Co-Design Frameworks for CIM

### Overview

Hardware-software co-design is critical for maximizing CIM performance. Modern
frameworks must bridge the gap between high-level neural network descriptions
and low-level crossbar array programming while handling analog non-idealities.

### CIM Compilation Stack

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CIM Compilation Stack                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                    High-Level Framework                          │   │
│  │            (PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX)                           │   │
│  └────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                         │
│                               ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                    CIM-MLC Frontend                              │   │
│  │         (Multi-Level Compilation - HPCA 2025)                    │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  • Graph IR construction                                         │   │
│  │  • Operator fusion (Conv+BN+ReLU)                                │   │
│  │  • Quantization-aware transformations                            │   │
│  │  • Sparsity pattern analysis                                     │   │
│  └────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                         │
│                               ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                    CMSwitch Optimizer                            │   │
│  │           (Dual-Mode - ASPLOS 2025)                              │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐                    │   │
│  │  │  Compute Mode   │ ◀─▶ │  Memory Mode    │                    │   │
│  │  │  (High compute  │     │  (High memory   │                    │   │
│  │  │   intensity)    │     │   bandwidth)    │                    │   │
│  │  └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘                    │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  Per-layer mode selection based on arithmetic intensity          │   │
│  └────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                         │
│                               ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                    PIMCOMP Backend                               │   │
│  │         (End-to-End DNN Compiler - DAC 2024)                     │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  • Layer-to-tile mapping                                         │   │
│  │  • Weight slicing for crossbar capacity                          │   │
│  │  • Dataflow scheduling (WS/OS/RS)                                │   │
│  │  • Buffer allocation                                             │   │
│  │  • Interconnect routing                                          │   │
│  └────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                               │                                         │
│                               ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                Hardware Instruction Generation                   │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  • DAC/ADC control sequences                                     │   │
│  │  • Crossbar programming voltages                                 │   │
│  │  • Read pulse patterns                                           │   │
│  │  • Digital post-processing                                       │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                         │
│  Performance: 3.2× inference speedup over baseline (CIM-MLC)            │
│               2.1× energy efficiency improvement                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CMSwitch: Dual-Mode CIM Compilation (ASPLOS 2025)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                CMSwitch Dual-Mode Optimization                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  Arithmetic Intensity Analysis:                                         │
│                                                                         │
│      AI = Compute Operations / Memory Bytes                             │
│                                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  High AI (>10 ops/byte)        │  Low AI (<10 ops/byte)         │   │
│  │  ═══════════════════           │  ════════════════════          │   │
│  │                                │                                 │   │
│  │  ┌─────────────────┐          │  ┌─────────────────┐            │   │
│  │  │  COMPUTE MODE   │          │  │  MEMORY MODE    │            │   │
│  │  │                 │          │  │                 │            │   │
│  │  │  • Dense GEMM   │          │  │  • Embedding    │            │   │
│  │  │  • Conv layers  │          │  │  • Attention    │            │   │
│  │  │  • FC layers    │          │  │  • Pooling      │            │   │
│  │  │                 │          │  │  • Skip conn    │            │   │
│  │  │  Strategy:      │          │  │  Strategy:      │            │   │
│  │  │  - Weight stat. │          │  │  - Output stat. │            │   │
│  │  │  - Max reuse    │          │  │  - Max BW       │            │   │
│  │  │  - Tile fusion  │          │  │  - Pipeline     │            │   │
│  │  └─────────────────┘          │  └─────────────────┘            │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                         │
│  Layer-wise Mode Selection Example (ResNet-50):                         │
│                                                                         │
│  Layer      │ AI (ops/byte) │ Mode    │ Speedup                        │
│  ───────────┼───────────────┼─────────┼────────                        │
│  Conv1      │ 18.2          │ Compute │ 1.8×                           │
│  Conv2_x    │ 24.6          │ Compute │ 2.1×                           │
│  Conv3_x    │ 31.4          │ Compute │ 2.3×                           │
│  Conv4_x    │ 8.7           │ Memory  │ 1.5×                           │
│  Conv5_x    │ 4.2           │ Memory  │ 1.2×                           │
│  FC         │ 2.1           │ Memory  │ 1.1×                           │
│                                                                         │
│  Overall: 1.9× average speedup with adaptive mode switching             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### NORA: Non-Ideality Aware Training (IBM Research 2025)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              NORA: Noise-Optimized Rescaling for Analog                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  Challenge: Analog CIM suffers from:                                    │
│    • Conductance variation (σ = 5%)                                     │
│    • Programming noise (±2%)                                            │
│    • Read noise (±1%)                                                   │
│    • IR drop (up to 10%)                                                │
│    • Temperature drift                                                  │
│                                                                         │
│  NORA Solution: Per-layer noise-optimized rescaling                     │
│                                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                    NORA Training Pipeline                        │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐     │   │
│  │  │ Float   │───▶│ Quantize│───▶│ Inject  │───▶│ Forward │     │   │
│  │  │ Weights │    │ to INT4 │    │  Noise  │    │  Pass   │     │   │
│  │  └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └────┬────┘     │   │
│  │                                                     │          │   │
│  │                                                     ▼          │   │
│  │  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐     │   │
│  │  │ Update  │◀───│ Compute │◀───│ Rescale │◀───│  Loss   │     │   │
│  │  │ Weights │    │  Grads  │    │ Factors │    │  Calc   │     │   │
│  │  └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘     │   │
│  │                                                                │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                         │
│  Noise Injection Models:                                                │
│                                                                         │
│  W_noisy = W_q × (1 + ε_cond) × (1 + ε_prog) + ε_read                  │
│                                                                         │
│  where:                                                                 │
│    ε_cond ~ N(0, σ_cond²)  : Conductance variation                     │
│    ε_prog ~ N(0, σ_prog²)  : Programming noise                         │
│    ε_read ~ N(0, σ_read²)  : Read noise                                │
│                                                                         │
│  Rescaling Factor Optimization:                                         │
│                                                                         │
│    α*_l = argmin E[||y_ideal - α_l × y_noisy||²]                       │
│                                                                         │
│  Results on LLMs (Llama-2-7B):                                          │
│    • Without NORA: 15% accuracy drop on analog CIM                     │
│    • With NORA: <1% accuracy drop                                      │
│    • Energy savings: 10× vs digital baseline                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### IR Drop Compensation

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               IR Drop in Crossbar Arrays                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                         │
│  Problem: Voltage drop along metal interconnects                        │
│                                                                         │
│  V_in ───R_wire───┬───R_wire───┬───R_wire───┬───R_wire───              │
│                   │            │            │            │              │
│                   G₁           G₂           G₃           G₄             │
│                   │            │            │            │              │
│  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── GND        │
│                                                                         │
│  V_drop = I × R_wire × position                                         │
│                                                                         │
│  Worst case: 10% voltage drop at array corners                          │
│                                                                         │
│  Compensation Techniques:                                               │
│                                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  1. Position-Aware Mapping                                       │   │
│  │     - Place high-weight values near voltage source               │   │
│  │     - Place low-weight values at array edges                     │   │
│  │     - Reduces worst-case error by 40%                            │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  2. IR-Aware Training                                            │   │
│  │     - Include IR drop model in forward pass                      │   │
│  │     - Weights learn to compensate                                │   │
│  │     - 2× accuracy improvement                                    │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  3. Post-Compensation Calibration                                │   │
│  │     - Measure actual drop per position                           │   │
│  │     - Apply digital correction factors                           │   │
│  │     - Eliminates 90% of IR drop error                            │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                         │
│  IR Drop Matrix (64×64 array, R_wire = 0.1Ω):                          │
│                                                                         │
│    Position  │ (0,0) │ (31,31) │ (63,63)                               │
│    ──────────┼───────┼─────────┼─────────                              │
│    V_drop    │  0%   │   5%    │   10%                                 │
│    Error     │  0%   │  2.5%   │   5%                                  │
│    After Cal │  0%   │  0.3%   │   0.5%                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### HASTILY: Transformer Acceleration (MICRO 2025)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

│                                                                         │

│                                                                         │
│  Solution: Hybrid analog-digital architecture                           │
│                                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │

│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │   │
│  │  │                  Attention Head (Analog)                 │    │   │
│  │  │                                                          │    │   │

│  │  │      │    ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐            │    │   │
│  │  │  K ──┴───▶│   Q×Kᵀ      │───▶│   Softmax   │            │    │   │
│  │  │           │  (CIM MVU)  │    │  (Digital)  │            │    │   │
│  │  │           └─────────────┘    └──────┬──────┘            │    │   │
│  │  │                                      │                   │    │   │
│  │  │  V ─────────────────────────────────┐│                   │    │   │
│  │  │                                      ││                   │    │   │
│  │  │           ┌─────────────┐           ▼▼                   │    │   │
│  │  │  Output◀──│   Attn×V    │◀──────────┘                    │    │   │
│  │  │           │  (CIM MVU)  │                                 │    │   │
│  │  │           └─────────────┘                                 │    │   │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │   │
│  │                                                                  │   │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │   │
│  │  │                  FFN Block (Analog)                      │    │   │
│  │  │                                                          │    │   │
│  │  │  Input ──▶ Linear1 ──▶ GELU ──▶ Linear2 ──▶ Output      │    │   │
│  │  │           (CIM)       (Digital)   (CIM)                  │    │   │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                                                         │
│  Performance vs A40 GPU:                                                │
│                                                                         │
│    Model         │ Speedup │ Energy Reduction                          │
│    ──────────────┼─────────┼────────────────                           │
│    BERT-Base     │  4.4×   │     18×                                   │
│    GPT-2         │  5.2×   │     22×                                   │
│    ViT-B/16      │  6.1×   │     25×                                   │
│    Llama-2-7B    │  9.8×   │     41×                                   │
│                                                                         │
│  Key Innovation: Stationary-K attention dataflow                        │
│    - K matrix stays in crossbar                                         │
│    - Q streams through for QKᵀ                                          │
│    - Minimizes weight reprogramming                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Code Reference

```go
// From edge_codesign_cim.go

// CIM Compiler with hardware-aware optimizations
type CIMCompiler struct {
    Config          *CIMCompilerConfig
    IRGraph         *CIMComputeGraph
    MappedGraph     *MappedCIMGraph
    ScheduledOps    []*ScheduledCIMOp
}

func (c *CIMCompiler) Compile(modelPath string) error {
    // 1. Build intermediate representation
    c.buildIRGraph(modelPath)

    // 2. Apply graph optimizations
    c.optimizeGraph()  // Fusion, tiling

    // 3. Map to hardware
    c.mapToHardware()  // Tile assignment, memory alloc

    // 4. Schedule operations
    c.scheduleOperations()  // Dataflow scheduling

    return nil
}

// CMSwitch dual-mode compilation
type CMSwitchCompiler struct {
    BaseCompiler    *CIMCompiler
    ComputeMode     bool
    ModeThreshold   float64
    SwitchPoints    []int
}

func (c *CMSwitchCompiler) determineOptimalMode(node *CIMGraphNode) bool {
    // Compute-centric: high arithmetic intensity
    // Memory-centric: memory-bound operations
    arithmeticIntensity := float64(node.ComputeOps) / float64(node.MemoryBytes)
    return arithmeticIntensity > 10.0  // Threshold: 10 ops/byte
}

// Non-ideality aware training
type HardwareAwareTrainer struct {
    Config              *NonIdealityConfig
    NoiseInjector       *NoiseInjector
    RescalingFactors    []float64
}

func (n *NoiseInjector) InjectNoise(weights []float64) []float64 {
    noisy := make([]float64, len(weights))
    for i, w := range weights {
        // Conductance variation + Programming noise + Read noise
        condNoise := rand.NormFloat64() * n.Config.ConductanceVariation
        progNoise := rand.NormFloat64() * n.Config.ProgrammingNoise
        readNoise := rand.NormFloat64() * n.Config.ReadNoise

        // Temperature effect
        tempEffect := 1.0 + n.Config.TempCoefficient * tempDelta

        noisy[i] = w * tempEffect * (1 + condNoise + progNoise + readNoise)
    }
    return noisy
}
```

| PIMCOMP | DNNs | End-to-end dataflow | 2.5× | 2024 |
| HASTILY | Transformers | Stationary-K attention | 9.8× | 2025 |
| NORA | LLMs | Noise-aware rescaling | N/A | 2025 |
