## 400. Neuromorphic-CIM Hybrid Architectures

### Overview

Hybrid neuromorphic-CIM architectures combine spiking neural networks (SNNs) with conventional artificial neural networks (ANNs) to achieve superior energy efficiency through event-driven, sparse computation while maintaining accuracy through precise analog operations.

### Hybrid SNN/ANN Architecture

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           HYBRID SNN-ANN COMPUTE-IN-MEMORY                  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Input Data                                              │
│        │                                                   │
│        ▼                                                   │
│    ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐              │
│    │        SPIKE ENCODER                   │              │
│    │  • Rate coding / TTFS encoding        │              │
│    │  • Temporal feature extraction         │              │
│    └───────────────┬───────────────────────┘              │
│                    │ Spike trains                         │
│                    ▼                                       │
│    ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐              │
│    │        SNN LAYERS (CIM)               │              │
│    │  • LIF neurons with crossbar weights  │              │
│    │  • Event-driven sparse computation    │              │
│    │  • 0.1 pJ per spike event            │              │
│    └───────────────┬───────────────────────┘              │
│                    │                                       │
│                    ▼                                       │
│    ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐              │
│    │     ACCUMULATION LAYER                │              │
│    │  • Synchronize SNN → ANN interface    │              │
│    │  • Spike count to analog conversion   │              │
│    └───────────────┬───────────────────────┘              │
│                    │ Analog activations                   │
│                    ▼                                       │
│    ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐              │
│    │        ANN LAYERS (CIM)               │              │
│    │  • Dense analog MVM operations        │              │
│    │  • High-precision classification      │              │
│    │  • 1 pJ per MAC operation            │              │
│    └───────────────┬───────────────────────┘              │
│                    │                                       │
│                    ▼                                       │
│              Classification Output                         │
│                                                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│    Key Metrics:                                            │
│    • Neuro-CIM: 310.4 TOPS/W                              │
│    • NEURAL: 1.97× energy efficiency improvement          │
│    • TTFS: 5.6× lower energy vs ANN                       │
│    • TTFS: 8.2× lower EDP vs ANN                          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) Neuron Model

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              LIF NEURON DYNAMICS                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Membrane Potential Evolution:                           │
│                                                            │
│    V(t)                                                    │
│     ↑  Vth ──────────────────────────────── threshold     │
│     │       ╱╲                      ╱╲                     │
│     │      ╱  ╲                    ╱  ╲   spike!          │
│     │     ╱    ╲                  ╱    ╲     │            │
│     │    ╱      ╲                ╱      ╲    ↓            │
│     │   ╱        ╲              ╱        ╲   │            │
│     │  ╱          ╲            ╱          ╲  │            │
│     │ ╱   leak     ╲          ╱            ╲ │            │
│    ─┼╱──────────────╲────────╱──────────────╲┼──────►t    │
│     │                ╲      ╱               reset         │
│     │                 ╲────╱  refractory                  │
│                                                            │
│    Equations:                                              │
│    • dV/dt = -(V - Vrest)/τ + I(t)                        │
│    • if V ≥ Vth: spike, V → Vreset, refractory period    │
│                                                            │
│    CIM Implementation:                                     │
│    • Weights stored in crossbar array                     │
│    • Input spikes → column activation                     │
│    • Current summation → membrane potential               │
│    • Threshold comparison in peripheral circuit           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Time-to-First-Spike (TTFS) Encoding

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TTFS ENCODING SCHEME                           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Analog Value → Spike Time                               │
│                                                            │
│    High value ──────► Early spike (t=1)                   │
│    Medium value ────► Mid spike (t=8)                     │
│    Low value ───────► Late spike (t=15)                   │
│    Zero value ──────► No spike                            │
│                                                            │
│    Time axis:                                              │
│    t=0   t=4   t=8   t=12  t=16                          │
│    │─────│─────│─────│─────│                              │
│    ↓     ↓     ↓     ↓     ↓                              │
│    █           █                 Neuron 1 (high input)    │
│              █                   Neuron 2 (medium)        │
│                    █             Neuron 3 (lower)         │
│                          █       Neuron 4 (low)           │
│    ───────────────────────────   Neuron 5 (zero=no spike)│
│                                                            │
│    Advantages:                                             │
│    • Single spike per neuron (energy efficient)           │
│    • Temporal coding preserves information                │
│    • Natural for event-driven CIM                         │
│    • 5.6× energy reduction vs rate coding                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### NEURAL Architecture (2025)

The NEURAL architecture introduces hybrid data-event execution:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              NEURAL ARCHITECTURE FEATURES                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    1. Elastic FIFO Decoupling                             │
│       • Separates sparsity processing from neuron compute │
│       • Enables parallel execution paths                  │
│       • 50% resource utilization reduction                │
│                                                            │
│    2. Window-to-TTFS (W2TTFS) Mechanism                   │
│       • Converts windowed attention to spike timing       │
│       • Enables spiking QKFormer execution                │
│       • Full-spike attention without analog conversion    │
│                                                            │
│    3. Sparsity-Aware Dataflow                             │
│       • Skip zero activations                             │
│       • Event-driven weight fetching                      │
│       • Dynamic workload balancing                        │
│                                                            │
│    Performance:                                            │
│    • 1.97× energy efficiency vs existing SNN accelerators │
│    • Support for transformer attention in spike domain    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Spintronic Reservoir Computing

Spintronic devices enable hardware reservoir computing:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           SPINTRONIC RESERVOIR ARCHITECTURE                 │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Input                Reservoir              Output      │
│      │                                           ↑        │
│      ▼                                           │        │
│    ┌───┐    Win    ┌─────────────────┐   Wout  ┌───┐     │
│    │ u │ ───────►  │  ●──●──●──●──●  │ ──────► │ y │     │
│    └───┘           │  │╲ │╲ │╲ │╲ │  │         └───┘     │
│                    │  ●──●──●──●──●  │                    │
│                    │  │╲ │╲ │╲ │╲ │  │  Trained          │
│                    │  ●──●──●──●──●  │  (linear)         │
│                    └─────────────────┘                    │
│                      Fixed (random)                       │
│                      Spintronic nodes                     │
│                                                            │
│    Spintronic Device Types:                               │
│    ┌──────────────┬────────────────┬─────────────┐       │
│    │ Device       │ Nonlinearity   │ Time Scale  │       │
│    ├──────────────┼────────────────┼─────────────┤       │
│    │ MTJ          │ tanh-like      │ ~1 ns       │       │
│    │ Skyrmion     │ sigmoid        │ ~10 ns      │       │
│    │ Domain Wall  │ piecewise      │ ~1 ns       │       │
│    │ Spin Wave    │ oscillatory    │ ~0.1 ns     │       │
│    └──────────────┴────────────────┴─────────────┘       │
│                                                            │
│    Results:                                                │
│    • 96.6% accuracy on arrhythmia detection               │
│    • 97.9% accuracy on gesture recognition                │
│    • 1000× lower power than digital implementations       │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation

```go
import "demo2-inference/pkg/layers"

// Hybrid SNN-ANN accelerator
hybridConfig := &layers.HybridSNNANNConfig{
    SNNLayers:      3,
    ANNLayers:      2,
    SNNNeuronType:  "lif",
    TimeSteps:      16,
    Threshold:      1.0,
    LeakFactor:     0.9,
    EnableTTFS:     true,   // Time-to-first-spike
    CrossbarSize:   256,
}
hybrid := layers.NewHybridSNNANNAccelerator(hybridConfig)

// hybrid.TOPSW → 310.4 TOPS/W (Neuro-CIM)

// Spintronic reservoir computing
reservoirConfig := &layers.SpintronicReservoirConfig{
    ReservoirSize:    256,
    NonlinearityType: "mtj",  // MTJ dynamics
    TimeConstantNs:   1.0,
    EnableStochastic: true,
}
reservoir := layers.NewSpintronicReservoir(reservoirConfig)

// Train readout layer (only output weights trained)
reservoir.TrainReadout(stateHistory, targets, 0.01)
```

### References

- [Neuro-CIM 310.4 TOPS/W - IEEE VLSI 2022](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9839152)
- [NEURAL Architecture - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2509.15036v1)
- [Integrated SNN-ANN Hardware - npj 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44335-025-00036-2)
- [EPHA Hybrid Architecture - ACM TODAES 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/full/10.1145/3643134)
- [Spintronic Reservoir Computing - Phys Rev Research 2025](https://journals.aps.org/prresearch/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevResearch.7.013310)
- [Memristor Reservoir - Nature Electronics 2022](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-022-00838-3)

---

## 401. Emerging Memory Technologies for CIM (MRAM, STT, SOT)

### Overview

Magnetic RAM (MRAM) technologies offer unique advantages for compute-in-memory: high endurance, fast switching, non-volatility, and near-zero leakage. Key variants include STT-MRAM, SOT-MRAM, and VCMA-MRAM.

### STT-MRAM for CIM

Spin-Transfer Torque MRAM uses current through the magnetic tunnel junction (MTJ) to switch magnetization:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              STT-MRAM CELL STRUCTURE                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│         Word Line (WL)                                     │
│              │                                             │
│              ▼                                             │
│         ┌────────┐                                        │
│         │  NMOS  │ Access transistor                      │
│         └───┬────┘                                        │
│             │                                              │
│         ┌───┴───┐                                         │
│         │ Free  │ ← Switchable layer                      │
│         │ Layer │                                         │
│         ├───────┤ ← MgO tunnel barrier                    │
│         │ Fixed │ ← Reference layer                       │
│         │ Layer │                                         │
│         └───┬───┘                                         │
│             │                                              │
│         Bit Line (BL)                                     │
│                                                            │
│    States:                                                 │
│    • Parallel (P): Low resistance → Logic '0'             │
│    • Anti-parallel (AP): High resistance → Logic '1'      │
│    • TMR = (RAP - RP) / RP ≈ 100-200%                    │
│                                                            │
│    CIM Advantages:                                         │
│    • Fast read: ~1 ns                                     │
│    • Non-volatile                                         │
│    • Endurance: 10¹² cycles                              │
│    • 38% more energy efficient than standard MRAM         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### FRM-CIM: Full-Digital Recursive MAC

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              FRM-CIM ARCHITECTURE (DAC 2024)                │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Traditional Bit-Serial:                                 │
│    • 8-bit MAC = 8 × partial sums × shift-add             │
│    • Latency grows with precision                         │
│                                                            │
│    FRM (Full-digital Recursive MAC):                       │
│    • Recursive accumulation in digital domain             │
│    • Single-cycle partial product computation             │
│    • Parallel bit processing                              │
│                                                            │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐        │
│    │   Input (8-bit)                              │        │
│    │      │                                       │        │
│    │      ▼                                       │        │
│    │   ┌──────────────┐                          │        │
│    │   │ STT-MRAM     │ Weight storage           │        │
│    │   │ Crossbar     │                          │        │
│    │   └──────┬───────┘                          │        │
│    │          │                                   │        │
│    │          ▼                                   │        │
│    │   ┌──────────────┐                          │        │
│    │   │  Recursive   │ Digital accumulator      │        │
│    │   │  Accumulator │                          │        │
│    │   └──────┬───────┘                          │        │
│    │          │                                   │        │
│    │          ▼                                   │        │
│    │   Output (16-bit)                           │        │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘        │
│                                                            │
│    Performance:                                            │
│    • 8-bit MAC: 3.5 ns latency                           │
│    • 16-bit MAC: 4.0 ns latency                          │
│    • Energy efficient image recognition                   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### SOT-MRAM for CIM

Spin-Orbit Torque MRAM separates read and write paths for improved performance:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              SOT-MRAM CELL STRUCTURE (2T-1MTJ)             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│              Read WL                                       │
│                 │                                          │
│                 ▼                                          │
│            ┌────────┐                                     │
│            │  T_rd  │ Read transistor                     │
│            └───┬────┘                                     │
│                │                                           │
│            ┌───┴───┐                                      │
│            │ Free  │                                      │
│            │ Layer │                                      │
│            ├───────┤                                      │
│            │ Fixed │                                      │
│            │ Layer │                                      │
│            └───┬───┘                                      │
│                │                                           │
│    ┌───────────┴───────────┐                             │
│    │    Heavy Metal (HM)   │ ← SOT generation layer      │
│    └───────────┬───────────┘                             │
│                │                                           │
│            ┌───┴────┐                                     │
│            │  T_wr  │ Write transistor                    │
│            └────────┘                                     │
│                │                                           │
│           Write WL                                        │
│                                                            │
│    Advantages over STT-MRAM:                              │
│    • 4× faster switching                                  │
│    • Better read disturb margin                          │
│    • Higher write endurance (10¹⁵ cycles)                │
│    • Separate read/write paths                           │
│                                                            │
│    Trade-off: Larger cell (2T vs 1T)                      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### SOT-MRAM Spike-Based CIM

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           SOT-MRAM SPIKE CIM ACCELERATOR                    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│    │         Spike Input Interface          │             │
│    │  • Event-driven spike detection        │             │
│    │  • Digital-to-spike conversion         │             │
│    └───────────────┬────────────────────────┘             │
│                    │ Spike events                         │
│                    ▼                                       │
│    ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│    │       SOT-MRAM Crossbar Array          │             │
│    │  ┌───┬───┬───┬───┬───┬───┐            │             │
│    │  │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ ← Weights  │             │
│    │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┼───┼───┤            │             │
│    │  │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │            │             │
│    │  ├───┼───┼───┼───┼───┼───┤            │             │
│    │  │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │ W │            │             │
│    │  └───┴───┴───┴───┴───┴───┘            │             │
│    │  Only active columns compute (sparse)  │             │
│    └───────────────┬────────────────────────┘             │
│                    │                                       │
│                    ▼                                       │
│    ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│    │       Spike Accumulator                │             │
│    │  • Integrate currents over time        │             │
│    │  • Generate output spikes              │             │
│    └────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                            │
│    Performance:                                            │
│    • 243.6 TOPS/W at 8-bit precision                      │
│    • 96.6% energy saving vs conventional                  │
│    • Event-driven = only compute on spikes                │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### VCMA-MRAM for Logic-in-Memory

Voltage-Controlled Magnetic Anisotropy enables ultra-low-energy switching:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VCMA-MRAM PRINCIPLE                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    VCMA Effect:                                            │
│    • Electric field modulates magnetic anisotropy          │
│    • No charge transfer through barrier                    │
│    • Orders of magnitude lower energy than STT             │
│                                                            │
│         V = 0                    V > 0                     │
│    ┌───────────────┐       ┌───────────────┐              │
│    │   ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑     │       │   → → → →     │              │
│    │   Free Layer  │  ───► │   Free Layer  │              │
│    │               │       │  (rotated)    │              │
│    │   High Hk     │       │   Low Hk      │              │
│    └───────────────┘       └───────────────┘              │
│                                                            │
│    Energy Comparison:                                      │
│    • STT-MRAM: ~100 fJ per switch                         │
│    • SOT-MRAM: ~20 fJ per switch                          │
│    • VCMA-MRAM: ~1 fJ per switch                          │
│                                                            │
│    Logic Operations:                                       │
│    • NOT: Toggle single cell                              │
│    • AND/OR: Activate multiple wordlines                  │
│    • XOR/XNOR: For binary MAC operations                  │
│    • Parallel: All ops within single array cycle          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Hybrid MRAM-SRAM Accelerator

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           HYBRID MRAM-SRAM ARCHITECTURE                     │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │                NVM (MRAM)                        │    │
│    │  • Non-volatile weight storage                  │    │
│    │  • High density                                 │    │
│    │  • Near-zero leakage                            │    │
│    │  • Limited write cycles for learning            │    │
│    └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘    │
│                          │ Weight read                    │
│                          ▼                                │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │              SRAM Buffer                         │    │
│    │  • Fast volatile storage                        │    │
│    │  • Activations and gradients                    │    │
│    │  • On-device learning computations              │    │
│    │  • Higher leakage but unlimited writes          │    │
│    └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘    │
│                          │                                │
│                          ▼                                │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │          Processing Element                      │    │
│    │  • Sparsity-aware MAC operations                │    │
│    │  • Skip zero activations                        │    │
│    │  • Gradient computation (SRAM-based)            │    │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                            │
│    Benefits:                                               │
│    • Best of both worlds                                  │
│    • Weights in MRAM (non-volatile, dense)               │
│    • Activations in SRAM (fast, unlimited writes)        │
│    • On-device continual learning support                │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Technology Comparison

| Feature | STT-MRAM | SOT-MRAM | VCMA-MRAM | FeCAP |
|---------|----------|----------|-----------|-------|
| Read Speed | ~1 ns | ~1 ns | ~1 ns | ~1 ns |
| Write Speed | ~5 ns | ~1 ns | ~1 ns | ~10 ns |
| Write Energy | ~100 fJ | ~20 fJ | ~1 fJ | ~10 fJ |
| Endurance | 10¹² | 10¹⁵ | 10¹⁵ | 10¹⁴ |
| Cell Size | 1T-1MTJ | 2T-1MTJ | 1T-1MTJ | 1T-1C |
| TMR | 100-200% | 100-200% | 100-200% | N/A |
| CIM Support | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| On-chip Learning | Limited | Good | Excellent | Good |

### Implementation

```go
import "demo2-inference/pkg/layers"

// STT-MRAM CIM with FRM
sttConfig := &layers.STTMRAMConfig{
    ArraySize:   256,
    TMR:         1.0,        // 100% TMR
    Endurance:   1e12,       // 10^12 cycles
    EnableFRM:   true,       // Full-digital recursive MAC
}
sttCrossbar := layers.NewSTTMRAMCrossbar(sttConfig)

// sttCrossbar.MACLatency → 3.5 ns (8-bit FRM)
// sttCrossbar.EnergyPerMAC → 0.1 pJ

// SOT-MRAM spike CIM
sotConfig := &layers.SOTMRAMConfig{
    ArraySize:       256,
    SwitchingTime:   1.0,     // 1 ns (4× faster)
    WriteEndurance:  1e15,    // Higher than STT
    EnableSpikeCIM:  true,
}
sotCrossbar := layers.NewSOTMRAMCrossbar(sotConfig)

// sotCrossbar.TOPSW → 243.6 TOPS/W
// sotCrossbar.EnergySaving → 96.6%

// VCMA logic-in-memory
vcmaConfig := &layers.VCMAMRAMConfig{
    ArraySize:       256,
    EnergyPerSwitch: 1.0,     // 1 fJ (100× lower than STT)
    EnableLogicOps:  true,
}
vcmaUnit := layers.NewVCMALogicUnit(vcmaConfig)

// Parallel MAC using XNOR
result := vcmaUnit.ParallelMAC(inputs, weightRow)
```

- [FRM-CIM STT-MRAM - DAC 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3649329.3655937)
- [SOT-MRAM Spike CIM - arXiv 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.03203)
- [SOT-MRAM Progress Review - npj Spintronics 2024](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44306-024-00044-1)
- [VCMA-MRAM Logic - Electronics 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/11/18/2805)
- [MRAM-SRAM Hybrid - DAC 2024](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3649329.3657390)
- [Spintronic Memristors - npj Spintronics 2025](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44306-025-00078-z)
- [XOR-CiM SOT-MRAM - NSF 2023](https://par.nsf.gov/biblio/10426823)

## 402. CIM Compiler and Mapping Optimization

### Overview

CIM compilers translate neural network models to efficient hardware mappings on crossbar arrays. Key challenges include handling diverse hardware architectures, optimizing layer partitioning, and maximizing utilization through cross-layer scheduling.

### CIM-MLC: Multi-Level Compilation Stack

CIM-MLC provides a universal compilation framework for diverse CIM architectures:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CIM-MLC COMPILATION STACK                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Neural Network Model (ONNX, PyTorch)                   │
│                    │                                       │
│                    ▼                                       │
│    ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│    │      High-Level IR (Graph)       │                   │
│    │  • Layer representation          │                   │
│    │  • Data dependency analysis      │                   │
│    └──────────────┬───────────────────┘                   │
│                   │                                        │
│                   ▼                                        │
│    ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│    │    Hardware Abstraction (Abs-arch)│                   │
│    │  ┌────────────────────────────┐  │                   │
│    │  │ Chip Tier                  │  │                   │
│    │  │ • NumCores, GlobalBuffer   │  │                   │
│    │  │ • InterconnectBW           │  │                   │
│    │  └────────────────────────────┘  │                   │
│    │  ┌────────────────────────────┐  │                   │
│    │  │ Core Tier                  │  │                   │
│    │  │ • NumCrossbars, LocalBuf   │  │                   │
│    │  │ • ADC/DAC specs            │  │                   │
│    │  └────────────────────────────┘  │                   │
│    │  ┌────────────────────────────┐  │                   │
│    │  │ Crossbar Tier              │  │                   │
│    │  │ • Rows, Cols, CellBits     │  │                   │
│    │  │ • Energy, Latency          │  │                   │
│    │  └────────────────────────────┘  │                   │
│    └──────────────┬───────────────────┘                   │
│                   │                                        │
│                   ▼                                        │
│    ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│    │      Low-Level IR (Tiles)        │                   │
│    │  • Crossbar mapping              │                   │
│    │  • Data movement scheduling      │                   │
│    └──────────────┬───────────────────┘                   │
│                   │                                        │
│                   ▼                                        │
│           Hardware Execution                               │
│                                                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│    Results: 3.2× average speedup over prior CIM compilers │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### CLSA-CIM: Cross-Layer Scheduling

Cross-layer scheduling improves utilization by overlapping layer execution:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           CROSS-LAYER SCHEDULING (CLSA-CIM)                │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Traditional (Layer-by-Layer):                          │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │ XBar 0 │████████│        │████████│        │    │    │
│    │ XBar 1 │████████│        │████████│        │    │    │
│    │ XBar 2 │        │████████│        │████████│    │    │
│    │ XBar 3 │        │████████│        │████████│    │    │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│             Layer 1      Layer 2    Layer 1    Layer 2    │
│    Utilization: ~50% (pipeline bubbles)                   │
│                                                            │
│    Cross-Layer Scheduling:                                │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │ XBar 0 │████│████│████│████│████│████│████│    │    │
│    │ XBar 1 │████│████│████│████│████│████│████│    │    │
│    │ XBar 2 │████│████│████│████│████│████│████│    │    │
│    │ XBar 3 │████│████│████│████│████│████│████│    │    │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│             L1/L2 interleaved execution                   │
│    Utilization: ~95% (minimal bubbles)                    │
│                                                            │
│    Benefits:                                               │
│    • Utilization increase: up to 28.4%                   │
│    • Inference speedup: up to 21.9×                      │
│    • Works with weight duplication                        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### COMPASS: Resource-Constrained Partitioning

For models larger than on-chip capacity, COMPASS partitions layers:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           COMPASS LAYER PARTITIONING                        │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Full Model (exceeds on-chip capacity):                 │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │ L1 │ L2 │ L3 │ L4 │ L5 │ L6 │ L7 │ L8 │ L9 │ L10│    │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                          │                                 │
│                          ▼                                 │
│    Genetic Algorithm Partitioning:                        │
│    ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐            │
│    │  Partition 1     │  │  Partition 2     │            │
│    │ L1│L2│L3│L4│L5   │  │ L6│L7│L8│L9│L10  │            │
│    │                  │  │                  │            │
│    │ Weights: 512KB   │  │ Weights: 480KB   │            │
│    │ Fits on-chip ✓   │  │ Fits on-chip ✓   │            │
│    └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘            │
│              │                     │                      │
│              └───────┬─────────────┘                      │
│                      ▼                                    │
│    Pipelined Execution:                                   │
│    1. Load Partition 1 weights                           │
│    2. Execute Partition 1 (pipelined)                    │
│    3. Store intermediate activations                     │
│    4. Load Partition 2 weights                           │
│    5. Execute Partition 2 (pipelined)                    │
│                                                            │
│    Optimization targets: Throughput, EDP, Latency        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Mapping Strategies

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WEIGHT MAPPING STRATEGIES                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    1. Weight Stationary (WS):                             │
│       • Weights stay in crossbar                          │
│       • Input streamed through columns                    │
│       • Best for inference                                │
│                                                            │
│    2. Output Stationary (OS):                             │
│       • Partial sums accumulated in place                 │
│       • Weights reloaded per tile                         │
│       • Good for training                                 │
│                                                            │
│    3. Row Stationary (RS):                                │
│       • Maximize row reuse                                │
│       • Good for conv layers                              │
│                                                            │
│    4. Weight Duplication:                                 │
│       • Replicate weights across crossbars               │
│       • Enables parallel input processing                │
│       • Trades capacity for throughput                   │
│                                                            │
│    Strategy Selection:                                     │
│    ┌────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┐          │
│    │ Layer Type │ Best Strategy│ Utilization  │          │
│    ├────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┤          │
│    │ FC         │ WS           │ High         │          │
│    │ Conv 1×1   │ WS           │ High         │          │
│    │ Conv 3×3   │ RS           │ Medium       │          │
│    │ Attention  │ OS           │ Medium       │          │
│    │ Depthwise  │ WS + Dup     │ Low → High   │          │
│    └────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┘          │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation

```go
import "demo2-inference/pkg/layers"

// Hardware abstraction
hw := layers.DefaultCIMHardwareAbstraction()

// hw.Chip.NumCores = 16
// hw.Core.NumCrossbars = 64
// hw.Crossbar.Rows = 256, Cols = 256

// CIM-MLC compiler
compiler := layers.NewCIMMLCCompiler(hw)

model := layers.NewDNNModelSpec("ResNet-18")
model.AddLayer(&layers.DNNLayerSpec{
    Name:        "conv1",
    Type:        "conv",
    WeightShape: []int{64, 3, 7, 7},
    OutputShape: []int{1, 64, 112, 112},
})

compiler.CompileModel(model)
result := compiler.GetCompilationResult()
// result["speedup"] → 3.2× average

// Cross-layer scheduling
scheduler := layers.NewCrossLayerScheduler(hw)
scheduler.ScheduleLayers(compiler.Mappings)
// scheduler.SpeedupFactor → up to 21.9×
// scheduler.UtilizationGain → up to 28.4%

// COMPASS partitioning for large models
partConfig := layers.DefaultPartitionConfig()
partConfig.MaxOnChipWeights = 1024 * 1024  // 1MB
partitioner := layers.NewCOMPASSPartitioner(hw, partConfig)
partResult := partitioner.PartitionModel(model)
// partResult.NumPartitions → optimal partitioning
```

### Compiler Comparison

| Framework | Target | Speedup | Utilization | Key Feature |
|-----------|--------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| CIM-MLC | General CIM | 3.2× | 85% | Multi-level abstraction |
| CLSA-CIM | Tiled RRAM | 21.9× | 95% | Cross-layer scheduling |
| COMPASS | Resource-constrained | 5.0× | 80% | GA partitioning |
| CIM-Explorer | BNN/TNN | 2.5× | 75% | DSE flow |
| CMSwitch | Dual-mode | 4.0× | 88% | Compute-memory switch |

### References

- [CIM-MLC - ASPLOS 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2401.12428v2)
- [CLSA-CIM - DATE 2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2401.07671v2)
- [COMPASS - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.06780)
- [CIM-Explorer - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14303)
- [CMSwitch Dual-mode - arXiv 2025](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.17006)
- [CINM Compiler - ResearchGate 2025](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/390679656)

---

## 403. On-Chip Learning and Adaptation

### Overview

On-chip learning enables neural network training directly on CIM hardware, avoiding expensive weight transfers between memory and processor. Key challenges include device non-idealities, limited write endurance, and gradient computation in analog domain.

### Progressive Gradient Descent

Progressive GD avoids storing partial derivatives by updating layers sequentially:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           PROGRESSIVE GRADIENT DESCENT                      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Standard Backpropagation:                              │
│    • Store all partial derivatives (memory intensive)     │
│    • Update all layers simultaneously                     │
│    • High memory requirement                              │
│                                                            │
│    Progressive GD:                                         │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│    │ Step 1: Forward pass                             │    │
│    │    Input → L1 → L2 → L3 → Output                │    │
│    │                                                  │    │
│    │ Step 2: Compute output error                    │    │
│    │    Error = Target - Output                      │    │
│    │                                                  │    │
│    │ Step 3: Progressive layer updates               │    │
│    │    L3: Compute gradient, update in-situ         │    │
│    │    L2: Compute gradient, update in-situ         │    │
│    │    L1: Compute gradient, update in-situ         │    │
│    │                                                  │    │
│    │ No intermediate storage needed!                 │    │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                            │
│    Hardware Implementation (Science Advances 2024):       │
│    • 30 memristors, 4 neurons                            │
│    • Binary image classification                          │
│    • Training time: 2.4 seconds                          │
│    • Energy: 18.9 mJ                                     │
│    • Accuracy improvement: 3.43%                         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Error-Aware Probabilistic Update (EaPU)

EaPU addresses algorithm-device mismatch through probabilistic updates:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           ERROR-AWARE PROBABILISTIC UPDATE (EaPU)          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Problem: Device write noise conflicts with BP          │
│                                                            │
│    Standard BP:                                            │
│    w_new = w_old - η * gradient                           │
│    (Assumes deterministic write)                          │
│                                                            │
│    EaPU Solution:                                          │
│    1. Compute target weight: w_target                     │
│    2. Calculate error: e = |w_target - w_current|         │
│    3. Compute update probability:                         │
│                                                            │
│       P(update) = e / (e + σ_noise) × P_base             │
│                                                            │
│    4. If random() < P(update):                            │
│          Write w_target + noise                           │
│       Else:                                                │
│          Skip update (save energy)                        │
│                                                            │
│    Results (Nature Communications 2025):                  │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│    │ Metric              │ EaPU vs BP            │       │
│    ├─────────────────────┼───────────────────────┤       │
│    │ Weight updates      │ <1‰ (>99.9% skipped) │       │
│    │ Training energy     │ 50.54× lower         │       │
│    │ Accuracy improvement│ >60%                  │       │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### SITU: Stochastic Input Encoding

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           SITU TRAINING SCHEME                              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Components:                                             │
│                                                            │
│    1. Layer-wise Threshold (LW)                           │
│       • Different update thresholds per layer             │
│       • Deeper layers: higher threshold                   │
│       • Reduces unnecessary updates                       │
│                                                            │
│    2. Gradient Accumulation (GA)                          │
│       • Accumulate gradients over N steps                 │
│       • Batch update reduces write operations             │
│       • Improves convergence                              │
│                                                            │
│    3. Open-Loop Tuning                                    │
│       • No read-verify cycles                             │
│       • Single write pulse per update                     │
│       • Significant energy savings                        │
│                                                            │
│    Training Flow:                                          │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│    │  for step in training:                       │       │
│    │      gradient = compute_gradient(input)      │       │
│    │      accumulated += gradient                 │       │
│    │                                              │       │
│    │      if step % N == 0:  # Every N steps     │       │
│    │          for layer in layers:               │       │
│    │              if |accumulated| > threshold:  │       │
│    │                  update_weights(layer)      │       │
│    │          accumulated = 0                    │       │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                            │
│    Results: 3.43% accuracy improvement                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Noise-Aware Training

Training with simulated hardware noise improves deployment accuracy:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           NOISE-AWARE TRAINING                              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Noise Sources in CIM:                                  │
│    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│    │ Source              │ Impact    │ Mitigation │       │
│    ├─────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┤       │
│    │ Cell variation      │ ±3-5%     │ Calibration│       │
│    │ Read noise          │ ±1-2%     │ Averaging  │       │
│    │ ADC quantization    │ 0.5 LSB   │ Dithering  │       │
│    │ IR drop             │ ±2-3%     │ Compensation│      │
│    │ Temperature drift   │ ±1-5%     │ Adaptation │       │
│    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                            │
│    Training with Noise Injection:                         │
│                                                            │
│    forward_pass:                                           │
│        noisy_weights = weights + N(0, σ_schedule)        │
│        output = matmul(input, noisy_weights)             │
│        output = quantize_adc(output, bits)               │
│                                                            │
│    Noise Schedule (cosine):                               │
│                                                            │
│    σ(t) ↑                                                 │
│         │            ╭─────────                           │
│         │          ╭─╯                                    │
│         │        ╭─╯                                      │
│         │      ╭─╯                                        │
│         │────╯                                            │
│         └───────────────────────────► epoch               │
│                                                            │
│    Start low (0.01), increase to final (0.05)            │
│                                                            │
│    Results: Up to 5.3% accuracy improvement               │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Ferroelectric-Memristor Hybrid Memory

Combining ferroelectric and memristor for both training and inference:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           FERROELECTRIC-MEMRISTOR HYBRID                    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                            │
│    Device Stack:                                           │
│    ┌───────────────────────────────┐                      │
│    │     Top Electrode             │                      │
│    ├───────────────────────────────┤                      │
│    │     Ferroelectric Layer       │ ◄── Learning         │
│    │     (HfZrO, high endurance)   │     (10¹² cycles)   │
│    ├───────────────────────────────┤                      │
│    │     Memristor Layer           │ ◄── Inference        │
│    │     (TaOx, analog levels)     │     (high precision) │
│    ├───────────────────────────────┤                      │
│    │     Bottom Electrode          │                      │
│    └───────────────────────────────┘                      │
│                                                            │
│    Operation Modes:                                        │
│    • Training: Use ferroelectric (high endurance)         │
│    • Inference: Use memristor (precise analog)            │
│    • Transfer: Copy learned weights FE → memristor        │
│                                                            │
│    Advantages:                                             │
│    • Best of both technologies                            │
│    • On-chip training capability                          │
│    • High-precision inference                             │
│    • Single device stack (area efficient)                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation

```go
import "demo2-inference/pkg/layers"

// Progressive gradient descent
pgdConfig := &layers.ProgressiveGDConfig{
    LearningRate:         0.01,
    GradientAccumulation: 4,
    UseStochasticUpdate:  true,
    GradientClipping:     1.0,
}
pgdTrainer := layers.NewProgressiveGDTrainer(pgdConfig)
pgdTrainer.InitializeWeights([][]int{{784, 256}, {256, 10}})

// Train layer-by-layer
for epoch := 0; epoch < 100; epoch++ {
    for layerIdx := range model.Layers {
        pgdTrainer.AccumulateGradient(layerIdx, gradient)
        pgdTrainer.UpdateLayerProgressive(layerIdx)
    }
}

// Error-aware probabilistic update
eapuConfig := &layers.EaPUConfig{
    WriteNoiseSigma: 0.05,
    UpdateProbBase:  0.1,
    SkipRatio:       0.999,  // Skip 99.9% of updates
}
eapuTrainer := layers.NewEaPUTrainer(eapuConfig)

// eapuTrainer.EnergyReduction → 50.54×
// eapuTrainer.AccuracyImprovement → 60%

// Noise-aware training
noiseConfig := &layers.NoiseAwareConfig{
    ReadNoiseSigma:  0.02,
    WriteNoiseSigma: 0.05,
    NoiseSchedule:   "cosine",
    InitialNoise:    0.01,
    FinalNoise:      0.05,
}
noiseTrainer := layers.NewNoiseAwareTrainer(noiseConfig, 100)
noisyWeights := noiseTrainer.InjectNoise(weights)

// noiseTrainer.AccuracyGain → up to 5.3%
```

### On-Chip Learning Comparison

| Method | Accuracy Gain | Energy Reduction | Update Reduction | Hardware |
|--------|--------------|------------------|------------------|----------|
| Progressive GD | 3.43% | 50% | 30% | 30 memristors |
| EaPU | 60% | 98% (50.54×) | 99.9% | 180nm memristor |
| Siamese Learning | 2% | 98% | 98% | Crossbar array |
| Noise-Aware | 5.3% | 30% | 0% | Simulation |
| Contrastive | 4% | 60% | 50% | RRAM crossbar |
